title: The Afternoon Light on an Old Wall
text_zh: 有些事物的存在方式，是通过消逝来完成的。我住的那条巷子里有一面老墙，青砖已经风化得不成样子，砖缝里长出的苔藓倒比砖本身更有生命力。每天下午三点左右，阳光会从对面楼的缝隙间斜射过来，在墙面上投下一道狭长的光带。那道光每天都在移动，冬天偏南，夏天偏北，像一只看不见的手在墙面上缓慢地书写什么。我观察了整整两年，才意识到这道光其实是一种时间的刻度——它丈量的不是空间，而是季节的更替。墙面上那些被光反复照射的地方，苔藓退去了，露出底下更深的砖色，仿佛被时间灼伤。而光照不到的角落，苔藓越长越厚，形成一种幽暗的绿。这面墙因此成了一幅永远画不完的画：光与苔藓的对峙，其实就是时间与遗忘的拉锯。有一天我路过，发现墙被刷成了白色。施工的人说要'翻新'。施工的人在收拾工具。下午三点的阳光照在白墙上，什么痕迹都没有了。
text_pinyin: Yǒu xiē shìwù de cúnzài fāngshì, shì tōngguò xiāoshì lái wánchéng de. Wǒ zhù de nà tiáo xiàngzi lǐ yǒu yì miàn lǎo qiáng, qīngzhuān yǐjīng fēnghuà de bù chéng yàngzi, zhuānfèng lǐ zhǎng chū de táixiǎn dào bǐ zhuān běnshēn gèng yǒu shēngmìnglì. Měi tiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn zuǒyòu, yángguāng huì cóng duìmiàn lóu de fèngxì jiān xié shè guòlái, zài qiángmiàn shàng tóu xià yì dào xiácháng de guāngdài. Nà dào guāng měi tiān dōu zài yídòng, dōngtiān piān nán, xiàtiān piān běi, xiàng yì zhī kàn bú jiàn de shǒu zài qiángmiàn shàng huǎnmàn de shūxiě shénme. Wǒ guānchá le zhěngzhěng liǎng nián, cái yìshi dào zhè dào guāng qíshí shì yì zhǒng shíjiān de kèdù——tā zhàngliang de bú shì kōngjiān, ér shì jìjié de gēngtì. Qiángmiàn shàng nàxiē bèi guāng fǎnfù zhàoshè de dìfang, táixiǎn tuì qù le, lùchū dǐxia gèng shēn de zhuānsè, fǎngfú bèi shíjiān zhuóshāng. Ér guāng zhào bú dào de jiǎoluò, táixiǎn yuè zhǎng yuè hòu, xíngchéng yì zhǒng yōuàn de lǜ. Zhè miàn qiáng yīncǐ chéng le yì fú yǒngyuǎn huà bù wán de huà: guāng yǔ táixiǎn de duìzhì, qíshí jiù shì shíjiān yǔ yíwàng de lājù. Yǒu yì tiān wǒ lùguò, fāxiàn qiáng bèi shuā chéng le báisè. Shīgōng de rén shuō yào 'fānxīn'. Nà yí kè wǒ tūrán míngbai, suǒwèi xiàndàihuà, yǒu shíhou búguò shì yòng yì céng túliào mǒ qù jǐ shí nián de duìhuà.
text_en: Some things exist by way of vanishing. In the alley where I live there is an old wall. The grey bricks have weathered beyond recognition, and the moss growing from the mortar joints has more vitality than the bricks themselves. Every day around three in the afternoon, sunlight slants through the gaps between the buildings opposite and casts a long, narrow band of light on the wall. That band shifts daily—southward in winter, northward in summer—like an invisible hand slowly writing something on the surface. I observed it for two full years before I realized that this light was actually a scale of time: what it measured was not space but the rotation of seasons. Where the light repeatedly struck the wall, the moss had retreated, exposing a deeper brick color underneath, as though scorched by time. In the corners the light could not reach, the moss grew ever thicker, forming a dark green. The wall thus became a painting that could never be finished: the standoff between light and moss was really the tug-of-war between time and forgetting. One day I passed by and found the wall had been painted white. The workers said they were 'renovating.' The workers were packing up. The three o’clock sun fell on the white wall. No trace of anything.

questions.q_en: What does the author believe the afternoon band of light essentially represents?

title: The Sound of Rain on Different Roofs
title_zh: 雨落在不同屋顶上的声音
text_zh: 城市屋顶的材质决定了雨的声音。以下是五种屋顶和它们各自的雨声。第一种：老式瓦片。每一滴雨打在凹面上产生微小的回响，千百滴连在一起，形成低沉的合唱。瓦片之间的缝隙让声音有了层次——近处的雨点清晰，远处的模糊成一片嗡鸣。适合下午独坐，适合失眠。第二种：铁皮。雨点击打铁皮时发出清脆而密集的声响，像有人在急促地敲打一面巨大的锣。铁皮屋顶不允许安静——哪怕只是几滴雨，它都要放大成一场事件。修车铺的老张说他听了四十年铁皮雨声，现在闭着眼睛就能分辨出是小雨还是中雨。第三种：混凝土平顶。现代高层的标配。雨在上面落下去就消失了，不留任何回音。这种屋顶的设计初衷就是隔绝——隔绝温度，隔绝声响，隔绝一切来自天空的消息。第四种：玻璃天窗。商场和写字楼里常见。雨打在玻璃上的声音是尖锐的、短促的，没有余韵。像有人不停地用指甲弹桌面。抬头能看见雨滴撞碎的一瞬间，但听不到它完整的声音。第五种：帆布棚。夜市和大排档的临时遮雨设备。雨落在帆布上是闷响，像拍打面团。积水太多的时候帆布中央会鼓起一个大包，老板拿竹竿从下面一捅，水哗啦一声倾泻下来，溅湿最近的那桌客人。客人骂一声，老板赔笑，雨继续下。
text_pinyin: Wǒ céngjīng huā le yí zhěnggè méiyǔ jì, zhuānmén qù tīng yǔ luò zài bùtóng wūdǐng shàng de shēngyīn. Wǎpiàn wūdǐng shì zuì gǔdiǎn de, měi yì dī yǔ dǎ zài āomiàn shàng dōu huì chǎnshēng yí gè wēixiǎo de huíxiǎng, qiān bǎi dī lián zài yìqǐ jiù chéng le yì zhǒng dīchén de héchàng. Tiěpí wūdǐng wánquán bùtóng, yǔdiǎn jī dǎ tiěpí shí fāchū qīngcuì ér mìjí de shēngxiǎng, xiàng yǒu rén zài jícù de qiāodǎ yí miàn jùdà de luó. Zuì ānjìng de shì nàxiē xiàndài gāocéng de píng wūdǐng, hùnníngtǔ hé fángshuǐ céng xīshōu le jīhū suǒyǒu shēngbō, yǔ zài shàngmian luò xiàqù jiù xiāoshī le, bù liú rènhé huíyīn. Wǒ yóucǐ xiǎng dào yí gè wèntí: yí zuò chéngshì de shēngxué tèzhēng, qíshí qǔjué yú tā de wūdǐng cáizhì. Dāng zhěnggè chéngshì de wūdǐng dōu bèi tìhuàn chéng hùnníngtǔ zhīhòu, wǒmen bùjǐn shīqù le yǔshēng, hái shīqù le yì zhǒng yǔ zìrán duìhuà de píndào. Gǔrén shuō 'liú dé cánhé tīng yǔshēng', nà zhǒng shěnměi qiántí shì yǔshēng bìxū bèi tīngjiàn. Ér xiàndài jiànzhù de luójí qiàqià xiāngfǎn——tā zhìlì yú géjué yíqiè láizì wàibù de shēngxiǎng, bǎ rén fēngbì zài yí gè héngwēn héngshī de chénmò hézi lǐ. Wǒ bù quèdìng zhè suàn bú suàn yì zhǒng jìnbù. Xiàolǜ shàng dāngrán shì de, dàn zài gǎnzhī céngmiàn, wǒmen zhèngzài jiànzào yí gè yuè lái yuè ānjìng de shìjiè, ér ānjìng yǒu shíhou zhǐ shì shīcōng de lìng yì zhǒng shuōfǎ.
text_en: The material of a city's rooftops determines the sound of its rain. Here are five rooftops and their respective rain-voices. The first: old-style clay tiles. Each drop striking the concave surface produces a tiny resonance, hundreds linked together forming a low chorus. Gaps between tiles give the sound layers—nearby drops are distinct, distant ones blur into a hum. Suited for sitting alone in the afternoon, suited for insomnia. The second: sheet metal. Raindrops striking metal produce a crisp, dense clatter, like someone rapidly beating an enormous gong. A metal roof permits no quiet—even a few drops get amplified into an event. Old Zhang at the auto repair shop says he has listened to rain on metal for forty years and can now tell with his eyes closed whether it is light or moderate rain. The third: concrete flat-top. Standard for modern high-rises. Rain falls on it and vanishes, leaving no echo. This roof was designed from the start to seal things out—temperature, sound, every message from the sky. The fourth: glass skylights. Common in malls and office buildings. Rain on glass is sharp and brief, with no lingering resonance. Like someone ceaselessly flicking a tabletop with their fingernail. You can look up and see the instant a raindrop shatters, but you cannot hear its full voice. The fifth: canvas awning. The temporary rain-shelter of night markets and open-air eateries. Rain on canvas makes a muffled thud, like slapping dough. When too much water collects, the center of the canvas swells into a bulge. The owner jabs it from below with a bamboo pole, water crashes down in a rush, splashing the nearest table of customers. The customers curse, the owner grins apologetically, the rain keeps falling.

options.text: 安静的环境有益于身心健康

questions.q_en: Which of the following is closest to the author's overall assessment of modern architecture?

options.text: 完全支持现代建筑的隔音功能

options.text_en: Fully supports modern architecture's soundproofing function

questions.explanation: Only one option matches "temperature, sound, every message from the sky" — the others import ideas the passage never commits to.

title: The Weight of a Teacup
text_zh: 我父亲今年六十七岁，退休前是中学化学老师。他有一只青花瓷茶杯，据说是景德镇出的老物件，他母亲留给他的。杯壁很薄，薄到能看见手指的影子透过来。父亲每天只用这只杯子喝茶。早上六点起床，先烧水，等水凉到八十度左右——他用手背试温度，从不用温度计——把龙井放进杯子里，看叶子慢慢沉下去。洗杯子的时候他用左手托底、右手捏着杯沿转圈擦，动作和他在实验室洗烧杯一模一样。我弟弟去年给他买了一套日本的陶瓷茶具，包装精美，价格不菲。父亲道了谢，放进柜子里，再没打开过。有一次我三岁的女儿伸手去拿那只青花杯，父亲的反应速度比我见过的任何一次都快——不是愤怒，是一种条件反射式的恐惧。他把杯子挪开，然后蹲下来对女儿说：「这个杯子是太奶奶的，等你长大了爸爸讲给你听。」他没有讲。后来女儿也没有再问。每天早上父亲端起那只杯子，喝一口，放下，看窗外。杯底有一圈茶渍，褐色的，洗不掉了。他从来不提他母亲。杯子替他记着。
text_pinyin: Wǒ fùqīn yǒu yī zhī qīnghuā cí chábēi, shì tā mǔqīn liú gěi tā de, jùshuō shì Jǐngdézhèn chū de lǎo wùjiàn. Bēi bì hěn báo, báo dào kěyǐ kànjiàn shǒuzhǐ de yǐngzi tòu guòlái, dàn pěng zài shǒu lǐ què yǒu yī zhǒng bù fúhé tǐjī de chénzhòng gǎn. Wùlǐ shàng, zhè zhī bēizi dàyuē bāshí kè; qínggǎn shàng, tā kěnéng shì wǒmen jiā zuì zhòng de yī jiàn dōngxi. Fùqīn měi tiān zhǐ yòng zhè zhī bēizi hē chá. Xǐ de shíhou géwài xiǎoxīn, cónglái bú ràng biéren pèng. Yǒu yī cì wǒ de háizi shēnshǒu xiǎng ná, fùqīn de fǎnyìng sùdù bǐ wǒ jiàn guò de rènhé yī cì dōu kuài——bú shì fènnù, shì yī zhǒng jìnhū kǒngjù de bǎohù běnnéng. Shìhòu tā yǒuxiē bù hǎoyìsi de jiěshì shuō, tā mā qùshì nà tiān, zuìhòu yī jiàn zhǔfù de shì jiùshì ràng tā bǎ zhè zhī bēizi hǎohǎo liú zhe. 「Tā méi shuō wèishéme,」fùqīn shuō, 「dàn wǒ juéde tā shì xiǎng ràng wǒ měi tiān hē chá de shíhou hái néng xiǎng qǐ tā.」Wǒ hòulái zǐxì xiǎng guò zhè jiàn shì. Yī zhī bēizi wèishéme néng chéngzài zhème duō? Dàgài shì yīnwèi tā bèi fùyǔ le yī zhǒng shíjiān de gōngnéng——měi yī cì duān qǐ, měi yī cì fàng xià, měi yī cì shǒuzhǐ chùpèng bēi bì de wēndù, dōu zài chóngfù yī gè yíshì: jì zhù yī gè yǐjīng bú zài de rén. Rénlèi fāmíng le wúshù zhǒng bǎocún jìyì de jìshù——wénzì, zhàopiàn, lùxiàng. Dàn yěxǔ zuì gǔlǎo, zuì yǒuxiào de fāngshì, shì bǎ jìyì zhuāng zài yī jiàn měi tiān dōu yào chùpèng de wùpǐn lǐ. Yīnwèi jìyì rúguǒ zhǐshì bèi cúnchú, jiù huì mànmàn biànchéng dàng'àn; zhǐyǒu bèi měi tiān shǐyòng, tā cái shì huó zhe de. Nà zhī bēizi zhī suǒyǐ chénzhòng, bú shì yīnwèi cíqì běnshēn de zhòngliàng, ér shì yīnwèi tā měi tiān dōu zài bèi jìyì chóngxīn tiánmǎn.
text_en: My father is sixty-seven this year. Before retirement he was a middle-school chemistry teacher. He has a blue-and-white porcelain teacup, reportedly an antique from Jingdezhen, left to him by his mother. The cup wall is so thin you can see the shadow of your fingers through it. Father uses only this cup for tea every day. He gets up at six in the morning, boils water first, waits for it to cool to around eighty degrees—he tests the temperature with the back of his hand, never uses a thermometer—puts the Longjing in the cup, watches the leaves slowly sink. When washing the cup he cradles the bottom with his left hand and pinches the rim with his right, turning it in circles to wipe, the motion identical to how he used to wash beakers in the lab. Last year my younger brother bought him a set of Japanese ceramic tea ware, beautifully packaged, not cheap. Father thanked him, put it in the cabinet, and never opened it again. Once my three-year-old daughter reached for the blue-and-white cup. Father reacted faster than I had ever seen—not anger, but a reflex-like terror. He moved the cup aside, then crouched down and said to my daughter: 'This cup belonged to Great-Grandma. When you grow up, Daddy will tell you about it.' He never told her. She never asked again either. Every morning Father picks up that cup, takes a sip, sets it down, looks out the window. There is a ring of tea stain at the bottom of the cup, brown, impossible to wash away. He never mentions his mother. The cup remembers for him.

title: A Theory of Dust
text_zh: 搬进新居的第三周，楼下的钟阿姨敲门来借醋。她站在门口往里扫了一眼，说：「你这桌子怎么这么多灰？刚搬来的吧？」我说才半天没擦。她摆摆手：「不对，你这个桌面颜色太深了，灰一落就看得见。我跟你说，选家具要选浅色的，藏灰。」我说灰尘不是应该清理掉吗，藏起来有什么用。她看了我一眼，那种看年轻人不懂事的眼神：「你以为你擦得掉？你今天擦了明天又有。灰是擦不完的。」她说她活了六十多年，总结出一条经验：「灰尘这东西，你越在意它就越多。你不管它，它反而老实。」我笑她这是唯心主义。她不理我，借了醋就走了。后来我查了一下，一个普通家庭每年大约产生十八公斤灰尘——皮肤碎屑、衣物纤维、花粉、矿物微粒。有一次在楼道里又碰到钟阿姨，我把这个数据告诉她。她想了想说：「十八公斤，那不少了。」停了一下又说：「那也就是说，我们每个人每年都在往世界上撒十八公斤的自己。」我说没想到您还挺有哲学天赋。她说：「什么哲学，我就是懒得擦桌子，给自己找个理由。」她提着菜篮子下楼去了。楼梯拐角的窗台上落了一层薄灰，阳光正好照在上面。
text_pinyin: Bān jìn xīnjū de tóu yī gè yuè, wǒ měi tiān dōu cā zhuōzi. Bú shì yīnwèi jiépǐ, ér shì yīnwèi nà zhāng shēn sè de mù zhuō bǎ huīchén bàolù de guòyú qīngxī——jǐnjǐn gé le yī yè, zhǐjiān huá guò zhuōmiàn jiù néng liú xià yī dào hénjì. Wǒ yīncǐ kāishǐ zhùyì dào yī gè píngshí wánquán hūshì de shìshí: huīchén wú chù bú zài, wú shí bú luò, yǐ yī zhǒng rénlèi gǎnguān nányǐ chájué de sùdù, zài měi yī gè biǎomiàn chénjī. Jùshuō yī gè pǔtōng jiātíng měi nián huì chǎnshēng dàyuē shíbā gōngjīn de huīchén, qízhōng bāohán pífū suìxiè, yīwù xiānwéi, huāfěn, kuàngwù wēilì, yǐjí cóng yáoyuǎn dàlù piāo lái de shātǔ. Huàn yán zhī, huīchén shì yī bù wēixíng de dìzhìxué biānniánshǐ——nǐ zhuō shàng de nà céng báo huī, kěnéng tóngshí bāohán le nǐ zìjǐ shēntǐ de hénjì hé Sāhālā shāmò de yī lì shā. Zhège xiǎngfǎ ràng wǒ duì cā zhuōzi zhè jiàn shì chǎnshēng le yī zhǒng qíguài de yóuyù. Měi cì wǒ ná qǐ mābù, shíjì shàng shì zài cā qù yī duàn jíqí duǎnzàn dàn zhēnshí fāshēng guò de lìshǐ. Nàxiē huīchén jìlù le guòqù èrshísì xiǎoshí zhège fángjiān lǐ de yīqiè——shuí dǎkāi guò chuānghù, shuí jīngguò zhè zhāng zhuōzi, kōngqì liúdòng de fāngxiàng hé sùdù. Dāngrán, wǒ zuìzhōng háishi huì cā diào tāmen. Dàn wǒ bú zài juéde huīchén shì xūyào duìkàng de dírén le. Tā gèng xiàng shì shíjiān de píxiè——shìjiè měi shí měi kè dōu zài tuōluò yī céng jí báo de zìjǐ, ér zhèxiē tuōluòwù fùgài zài wǒmen de shēnghuó biǎomiàn, ānjìng ér jūnyún, xiàng shì wànwù zài qīng shēng shuō: wǒ lái guò.
text_en: In the third week after moving in, Aunt Zhong from downstairs knocked on my door to borrow vinegar. She stood at the entrance and swept a glance inside. 'How come your table has so much dust? Just moved in, right?' I said I had only gone half a day without wiping it. She waved her hand: 'No, your table is too dark-colored. Dust shows the moment it lands. Let me tell you—pick light-colored furniture. It hides dust.' I said dust should be cleaned away; what is the point of hiding it. She gave me a look, the kind reserved for young people who do not understand life: 'You think you can wipe it all away? You wipe today, it is back tomorrow. You can never finish wiping dust.' She said in over sixty years of life she had arrived at one conclusion: 'Dust—the more you fuss over it, the more there is. Leave it alone, and it behaves.' I laughed and called her an idealist. She ignored me, took the vinegar, and left. Later I looked it up: an average household produces about eighteen kilograms of dust per year—skin flakes, textile fibers, pollen, mineral particles. One time I ran into Aunt Zhong again in the stairwell and told her this figure. She thought for a moment and said: 'Eighteen kilograms. That is a lot.' After a pause she added: 'So that means every one of us scatters eighteen kilograms of ourselves into the world each year.' I said I had not expected such philosophical talent from her. She said: 'Philosophy nothing. I am just too lazy to wipe the table and looking for an excuse.' She went downstairs carrying her grocery basket. On the windowsill at the stairway landing lay a thin layer of dust, the sunlight falling right on it.

title: The Pedagogy of Silence
text_zh: 我读中学时有一位语文老师，姓贺，教法与众不同。别的老师讲课的时候拼命往里面塞内容，恨不得每一分钟都不浪费。贺老师不一样。他讲完一段话以后，经常会停下来，什么都不说，就那样站在讲台上看着我们。这种沉默有时候会持续一分钟甚至更长。刚开始全班都很不适应，有人小声说话，有人偷偷笑。但渐渐地，我们学会了在那个沉默中待着。贺老师后来解释过他的理由。他说，中国的课堂太满了，老师说得太多，学生听得太多，但想得太少。「知识如果没有被沉默消化过，它只是一堆别人嚼过的食物。」他最著名的一次沉默发生在讲鲁迅的《药》的那堂课上。他花了三十分钟讲完背景和文本，然后说：「现在请大家安静五分钟，想一个问题——华老栓和夏瑜住在同一座城里，但他们的世界有交集吗？」然后他就不说话了。那五分钟里，教室安静得可以听见窗外的蝉鸣。那是我第一次真正「想」一篇课文，而不只是「学」它。多年以后我才意识到贺老师教给我的不是任何具体的知识，而是一种能力：在信息的洪流中学会暂停，给思考留出空间。这种能力在今天比任何时候都珍贵——我们生活在一个害怕沉默的时代，每一秒钟都必须被声音、文字、影像填满，仿佛安静本身就是一种过错。但贺老师用他的课堂告诉我：最深的理解往往不是从别人的话语中获得的，而是从话语之间的空白中生长出来的。
text_pinyin: Wǒ dú zhōngxué shí yǒu yī wèi yǔwén lǎoshī, xìng Hè, jiàofǎ yǔzhòngbùtóng. Bié de lǎoshī jiǎngkè de shíhou pīnmìng wǎng lǐmiàn sāi nèiróng, hènbude měi yī fēnzhōng dōu bù làngfèi. Hè lǎoshī bù yīyàng. Tā jiǎng wán yī duàn huà yǐhòu, jīngcháng huì tíng xiàlái, shénme dōu bù shuō, jiù nàyàng zhàn zài jiǎngtái shàng kàn zhe wǒmen. Zhè zhǒng chénmò yǒu shíhou huì chíxù yī fēnzhōng shènzhì gèng cháng. Gāng kāishǐ quán bān dōu hěn bú shìyìng, yǒu rén xiǎo shēng shuōhuà, yǒu rén tōutōu xiào. Dàn jiànjiàn de, wǒmen xuéhuì le zài nàge chénmò zhōng dāi zhe. Hè lǎoshī hòulái jiěshì guò tā de lǐyóu. Tā shuō, Zhōngguó de kètáng tài mǎn le, lǎoshī shuō de tài duō, xuéshēng tīng de tài duō, dàn xiǎng de tài shǎo. 「Zhīshi rúguǒ méiyǒu bèi chénmò xiāohuà guò, tā zhǐshì yī duī biéren jiáo guò de shíwù.」Tā zuì zhùmíng de yī cì chénmò fāshēng zài jiǎng Lǔ Xùn de 《Yào》de nà táng kè shàng. Tā huā le sānshí fēnzhōng jiǎng wán bèijǐng hé wénběn, ránhòu shuō: 「Xiànzài qǐng dàjiā ānjìng wǔ fēnzhōng, xiǎng yī gè wèntí——Huá Lǎoshuān hé Xià Yú zhù zài tóngyī zuò chéng lǐ, dàn tāmen de shìjiè yǒu jiāojí ma?」Ránhòu tā jiù bù shuōhuà le. Nà wǔ fēnzhōng lǐ, jiàoshì ānjìng de kěyǐ tīng jiàn chuāng wài de chán míng. Nà shì wǒ dì yī cì zhēnzhèng 「xiǎng」yī piān kèwén, ér bù zhǐshì 「xué」tā. Duō nián yǐhòu wǒ cái yìshi dào Hè lǎoshī jiào gěi wǒ de bú shì rènhé jùtǐ de zhīshi, ér shì yī zhǒng nénglì: zài xìnxī de hóngliú zhōng xuéhuì zàntíng, gěi sīkǎo liú chū kōngjiān. Zhè zhǒng nénglì zài jīntiān bǐ rènhé shíhou dōu zhēnguì——wǒmen shēnghuó zài yī gè hàipà chénmò de shídài, měi yī miǎozhōng dōu bìxū bèi shēngyīn, wénzì, yǐngxiàng tiánmǎn, fǎngfú ānjìng běnshēn jiùshì yī zhǒng guòcuò. Dàn Hè lǎoshī yòng tā de kètáng gàosu wǒ: zuì shēn de lǐjiě wǎngwǎng bú shì cóng biéren de huàyǔ zhōng huòdé de, ér shì cóng huàyǔ zhī jiān de kòngbái zhōng shēngzhǎng chūlái de.
text_en: In middle school I had a Chinese teacher surnamed He whose methods were unlike anyone else's. Other teachers crammed content frantically, unwilling to waste a single minute. Teacher He was different. After finishing a passage, he would often stop, say nothing at all, and just stand at the podium looking at us. These silences sometimes lasted a minute or longer. At first the whole class was uncomfortable—some whispered, some snickered. But gradually we learned to dwell in that silence. Teacher He once explained his reasoning. He said Chinese classrooms are too full: teachers say too much, students listen too much, but think too little. 'Knowledge that hasn't been digested by silence is just food someone else has already chewed.' His most famous silence occurred during the class on Lu Xun's 'Medicine.' He spent thirty minutes covering background and text, then said: 'Now please be quiet for five minutes and think about one question—Hua Laoshuan and Xia Yu live in the same city, but do their worlds intersect?' Then he went silent. During those five minutes, the classroom was so quiet you could hear cicadas outside the window. That was the first time I truly 'thought about' a text rather than merely 'studied' it. Years later I realized what Teacher He taught me wasn't any specific knowledge but a capacity: learning to pause in the flood of information, to leave space for thinking.

questions.q_zh: 贺老师说「知识如果没有被沉默消化过，它只是一堆别人嚼过的食物」，这句话是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does Teacher He mean by 'knowledge that hasn't been digested by silence is just food someone else has already chewed'?

options.text: 学生不应该听老师讲课
options.pinyin: xuéshēng bù yīnggāi tīng lǎoshī jiǎngkè
options.text_en: Students shouldn't listen to teacher lectures

title: The City Reads Its Own Handwriting
text_zh: 一座城市的手写痕迹正在消失。我指的不是书法或信件，而是那些嵌入城市肌理中的文字：面馆门口用油漆手写的价目表，裁缝铺的玻璃上用白色广告颜料写的电话号码，菜市场摊位前用硬纸板和记号笔临时标注的当日价格。这些文字没有经过任何设计，字体歪歪扭扭，大小不一，偶尔还有错别字。但正因如此，它们有一种不可替代的真实感——你能从笔画的粗细和力度中读出写字那个人的性格、年龄、甚至心情。如今这些手写文字正在被统一制作的印刷招牌所取代。市政部门要求沿街商铺更换标准化店招，字体是统一的黑体或宋体，底色是规定的颜色。整条街看起来整齐了很多，但也失去了辨识度。我曾经可以闭着眼走进那家面馆，因为我认得老板娘的字——她的「面」字最后一笔总是拖得很长，像面条从碗里滑出来。现在，所有的店铺看起来都一样了，你必须走近去看门牌号才能区分。一位城市规划学者曾说过一个概念叫「可读性」——他指的是一座城市让人认路的能力。但我觉得「可读性」还有另一层含义：一座城市允不允许被手写，决定了它是不是一座有人情味的城市。当所有的文字都变成标准印刷体的时候，城市就不再有「笔迹」了——而一座没有笔迹的城市，就像一封没有签名的信：整洁、规范，但你不知道是谁写的。
text_pinyin: Yī zuò chéngshì de shǒuxiě hénjì zhèngzài xiāoshī. Wǒ zhǐ de bú shì shūfǎ huò xìnjiàn, ér shì nàxiē qiàn rù chéngshì jīlǐ zhōng de wénzì: miànguǎn ménkǒu yòng yóuqī shǒuxiě de jiàmùbiǎo, cáiféng pù de bōli shàng yòng báisè guǎnggào yánliào xiě de diànhuà hàomǎ, càishìchǎng tānwèi qián yòng yìng zhǐbǎn hé jìhaobǐ línshí biāozhù de dāngrì jiàgé. Zhèxiē wénzì méiyǒu jīngguò rènhé shèjì, zìtǐ wāiwāiniūniū, dàxiǎo bùyī, ǒu'ěr hái yǒu cuòbiézì. Dàn zhèng yīncǐ, tāmen yǒu yī zhǒng bùkě tìdài de zhēnshígǎn——nǐ néng cóng bǐhuà de cūxì hé lìdù zhōng dú chū xiě zì nàge rén de xìnggé, niánlíng, shènzhì xīnqíng. Rújīn zhèxiē shǒuxiě wénzì zhèngzài bèi tǒngyī zhìzuò de yìnshuā zhāopai suǒ qǔdài. Shìzhèng bùmén yāoqiú yánjiē shāngpù gēnghuàn biāozhǔnhuà diànzhāo, zìtǐ shì tǒngyī de hēitǐ huò sòngtǐ, dǐsè shì guīdìng de yánsè. Zhěng tiáo jiē kàn qǐlái zhěngqí le hěn duō, dàn yě shīqù le biànshídù. Wǒ céngjīng kěyǐ bì zhe yǎn zǒu jìn nà jiā miànguǎn, yīnwèi wǒ rèn de lǎobǎnniáng de zì——tā de 「miàn」zì zuìhòu yī bǐ zǒng shì tuō de hěn cháng, xiàng miàntiáo cóng wǎn lǐ huá chūlái. Xiànzài, suǒyǒu de diànpù kàn qǐlái dōu yīyàng le, nǐ bìxū zǒu jìn qù kàn ménpáihào cái néng qūfēn. Yī wèi chéngshì guīhuà xuézhě céng shuō guò yī gè gàiniàn jiào 「kě dúxìng」——tā zhǐ de shì yī zuò chéngshì ràng rén rèn lù de nénglì. Dàn wǒ juéde 「kě dúxìng」hái yǒu lìng yī céng hányì: yī zuò chéngshì yǔn bù yǔnxǔ bèi shǒuxiě, juédìng le tā shì bú shì yī zuò yǒu rénqíngwèi de chéngshì. Dāng suǒyǒu de wénzì dōu biànchéng biāozhǔn yìnshuātǐ de shíhou, chéngshì jiù bú zài yǒu 「bǐjì」le——ér yī zuò méiyǒu bǐjì de chéngshì, jiù xiàng yī fēng méiyǒu qiānmíng de xìn: zhěngjié, guīfàn, dàn nǐ bù zhīdào shì shuí xiě de.
text_en: The handwritten traces of a city are disappearing. I don't mean calligraphy or letters, but the text embedded in the city's fabric: the price list hand-painted on the noodle shop's door, the phone number written in white sign-paint on the tailor's glass, the day's prices scrawled on cardboard with marker at market stalls. These texts have no design—crooked characters, inconsistent sizes, the occasional wrong character. But precisely because of this, they carry an irreplaceable authenticity—you can read the personality, age, even mood of the writer from the thickness and pressure of the strokes. Today these handwritten texts are being replaced by uniformly manufactured printed signs. Municipal departments require street-facing shops to install standardized signage—fonts are uniform Heiti or Songti, background colors are mandated. The whole street looks much neater but has lost its distinctiveness. I once could walk into that noodle shop with my eyes closed, because I recognized the owner's handwriting—the last stroke of her character for 'noodle' always trailed long, like noodles sliding out of a bowl. Now all shops look the same; you have to walk up close and check the door number to tell them apart. An urban planning scholar once spoke of a concept called 'legibility'—the ability of a city to let people find their way. But I think 'legibility' has another layer of meaning: whether a city allows itself to be handwritten determines whether it is a city with human warmth. When all text becomes standardized print, the city no longer has 'handwriting'—and a city without handwriting is like an unsigned letter: neat, proper, but you don't know who wrote it.

title: The Translator's Dilemma
text_zh: 我做了十二年的文学翻译，最困扰我的不是语法或词汇的难题，而是一种更根本的焦虑：当我把一个中文的句子变成英文以后，它还是原来那个句子吗？翻译理论里有一个经典的比喻：翻译就像把一块织毯拆成线，再用另一种颜色的线重新织一遍。图案可以是相似的，但质感永远不同。我最痛苦的一次翻译经历是处理一个「意思」的问题。中文里「意思」这个词有多少种用法？「你是什么意思」「没什么意思」「这个人很有意思」「意思意思」「不好意思」——同一个词，在不同语境中指向完全不同的心理状态。英文里根本不存在一个能覆盖所有这些含义的单词。我试过meaning、intention、fun、gesture、embarrassment，但每一个都只能捕捉到原文的一个切面。翻译的本质困境在于：语言不仅仅是信息的载体，它还是一种认知框架。中文母语者说「意思」的时候，大脑中激活的不是一个定义，而是一整片由语境、语气、关系、文化记忆编织成的语义场。这片语义场无法被直接移植到另一种语言中，就像你不能把一棵树连同它脚下的土壤一起移到另一个地方。你能移的只是树，但树的根系所依赖的那片土壤——那个让它成为它自己的生态系统——是移不走的。所以每一次翻译，我都清楚地知道自己在做的是一种妥协：不是传递原文，而是为原文画一张尽可能准确的素描。而素描和原物之间的距离，就是翻译者永远无法消除的遗憾。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zuò le shí'èr nián de wénxué fānyì, zuì kùnrǎo wǒ de bú shì yǔfǎ huò cíhuì de nántí, ér shì yī zhǒng gèng gēnběn de jiāolǜ: dāng wǒ bǎ yī gè Zhōngwén de jùzi biànchéng Yīngwén yǐhòu, tā háishi yuánlái nàge jùzi ma? Fānyì lǐlùn lǐ yǒu yī gè jīngdiǎn de bǐyù: fānyì jiù xiàng bǎ yī kuài zhī tǎn chāi chéng xiàn, zài yòng lìng yī zhǒng yánsè de xiàn chóngxīn zhī yī biàn. Tú'àn kěyǐ shì xiāngsì de, dàn zhìgǎn yǒngyuǎn bùtóng. Wǒ zuì tòngkǔ de yī cì fānyì jīnglì shì chǔlǐ yī gè 「yìsi」de wèntí. Zhōngwén lǐ 「yìsi」zhège cí yǒu duōshao zhǒng yòngfǎ? 「Nǐ shì shénme yìsi」「méi shénme yìsi」「zhège rén hěn yǒu yìsi」「yìsi yìsi」「bù hǎoyìsi」——tóngyī gè cí, zài bùtóng yǔjìng zhōng zhǐ xiàng wánquán bùtóng de xīnlǐ zhuàngtài. Yīngwén lǐ gēnběn bù cúnzài yī gè néng fùgài suǒyǒu zhèxiē hányì de dāncí. Wǒ shì guò meaning, intention, fun, gesture, embarrassment, dàn měi yī gè dōu zhǐ néng bǔzhuō dào yuánwén de yī gè qiēmiàn. Fānyì de běnzhì kùnjìng zàiyú: yǔyán bù jǐnjǐn shì xìnxī de zàitǐ, tā háishi yī zhǒng rènzhī kuàngjià. Zhōngwén mǔyǔzhě shuō 「yìsi」de shíhou, dànǎo zhōng jīhuó de bú shì yī gè dìngyì, ér shì yī zhěng piàn yóu yǔjìng, yǔqì, guānxi, wénhuà jìyì biānzhī chéng de yǔyìchǎng. Zhè piàn yǔyìchǎng wúfǎ bèi zhíjiē yízhí dào lìng yī zhǒng yǔyán zhōng, jiù xiàng nǐ bù néng bǎ yī kē shù lián tóng tā jiǎo xià de tǔrǎng yīqǐ yí dào lìng yī gè dìfang. Nǐ néng yí de zhǐshì shù, dàn shù de gēnxì suǒ yīlài de nà piàn tǔrǎng——nàge ràng tā chéngwéi tā zìjǐ de shēngtài xìtǒng——shì yí bù zǒu de. Suǒyǐ měi yī cì fānyì, wǒ dōu qīngchu de zhīdào zìjǐ zài zuò de shì yī zhǒng tuǒxié: bú shì chuándì yuánwén, ér shì wèi yuánwén huà yī zhāng jǐn kěnéng zhǔnquè de sùmiáo. Ér sùmiáo hé yuánwù zhī jiān de jùlí, jiùshì fānyìzhě yǒngyuǎn wúfǎ xiāochú de yíhàn.
text_en: I've been doing literary translation for twelve years. What troubles me most isn't the difficulty of grammar or vocabulary, but a more fundamental anxiety: after I turn a Chinese sentence into English, is it still the same sentence? Translation theory has a classic metaphor: translating is like unraveling a tapestry into threads, then reweaving it with threads of a different color. The pattern can be similar, but the texture is forever different. My most agonizing translation experience involved the word 'yisi' (meaning/intention/interesting/gesture/embarrassment). How many uses does this single word have in Chinese? 'What do you mean,' 'it's boring,' 'this person is interesting,' 'just a token gesture,' 'sorry/embarrassed'—the same word, pointing to entirely different psychological states in different contexts. No single English word can cover all these meanings. I tried meaning, intention, fun, gesture, embarrassment, but each captures only one facet of the original. The essential dilemma of translation is this: language is not merely a carrier of information—it is also a cognitive framework. When a native Chinese speaker says 'yisi,' what activates in the brain isn't a definition but an entire semantic field woven from context, tone, relationship, and cultural memory. This semantic field cannot be directly transplanted into another language, just as you cannot move a tree together with the soil beneath its feet to another place. You can move only the tree, but the soil on which its root system depends—the ecosystem that makes it what it is—cannot be moved. So with every translation, I clearly know that what I'm doing is a compromise: not transmitting the original text, but drawing a sketch of it as accurately as possible. And the distance between the sketch and the original object is the regret a translator can never eliminate.

title: The Second Patron
text_zh: 常去的那家咖啡馆有一个不成文的传统：每天的第二位客人可以免费得到一杯咖啡。老板说这个规矩从他爷爷开店的时候就有了，快六十年了。为什么是第二位？他说他爷爷的解释是：第一位客人是最勇敢的，因为他走进一家空无一人的店需要勇气；但第二位客人是最重要的，因为他的到来证明了第一位客人的选择是正确的。「一家店如果只有一个客人，那是偶然；有了第二个，就开始变成某种必然。」这个解释让我想了很久。它背后有一种很东方的社会心理学：我们做决定时，真正说服我们的往往不是决定本身的合理性，而是看到有人在我们之前做了同样的决定。排队心理学证实了这一点——人们选择排长队的餐厅，不是因为食物更好，而是因为队伍本身就是一种认证。第二位客人的意义就在于此：他是第一个「认证者」。没有他，第一位客人只是一个独自做了选择的人；有了他，一家店的日常就开始了。我后来观察到一个有趣的现象：很多人知道这个传统以后，会特意在开门时间之后稍等一会儿再进去，确保自己是第二位。结果有时候第一位客人迟迟不出现，因为每个人都在等着当第二位。这件事让我觉得既幽默又深刻：人人都想做那个被验证的人，却没有人愿意做那个第一个走进空店的勇者。
text_pinyin: Cháng qù de nà jiā kāfēiguǎn yǒu yī gè bù chéngwén de chuántǒng: měi tiān de dì'èr wèi kèrén kěyǐ miǎnfèi dédào yī bēi kāfēi. Lǎobǎn shuō zhège guījǔ cóng tā yéye kāi diàn de shíhou jiù yǒu le, kuài liùshí nián le. Wèishéme shì dì'èr wèi? Tā shuō tā yéye de jiěshì shì: dì yī wèi kèrén shì zuì yǒnggǎn de, yīnwèi tā zǒu jìn yī jiā kōng wú yī rén de diàn xūyào yǒngqì; dàn dì'èr wèi kèrén shì zuì zhòngyào de, yīnwèi tā de dàolái zhèngmíng le dì yī wèi kèrén de xuǎnzé shì zhèngquè de. 「Yī jiā diàn rúguǒ zhǐ yǒu yī gè kèrén, nà shì ǒurán; yǒu le dì'èr gè, jiù kāishǐ biànchéng mǒu zhǒng bìrán.」Zhège jiěshì ràng wǒ xiǎng le hěn jiǔ. Tā bèihòu yǒu yī zhǒng hěn dōngfāng de shèhuì xīnlǐxué: wǒmen zuò juédìng shí, zhēnzhèng shuōfú wǒmen de wǎngwǎng bú shì juédìng běnshēn de hélǐxìng, ér shì kàn dào yǒu rén zài wǒmen zhīqián zuò le tóngyàng de juédìng. Páiduì xīnlǐxué zhèngshí le zhè yī diǎn——rénmen xuǎnzé pái cháng duì de cāntīng, bú shì yīnwèi shíwù gèng hǎo, ér shì yīnwèi duìwu běnshēn jiùshì yī zhǒng rènzhèng. Dì'èr wèi kèrén de yìyì jiù zàiyú cǐ: tā shì dì yī gè 「rènzhèngzhě」. Méiyǒu tā, dì yī wèi kèrén zhǐshì yī gè dúzì zuò le xuǎnzé de rén; yǒu le tā, yī jiā diàn de rìcháng jiù kāishǐ le. Wǒ hòulái guānchá dào yī gè yǒuqù de xiànxiàng: hěn duō rén zhīdào zhège chuántǒng yǐhòu, huì tèyì zài kāimén shíjiān zhīhòu shāo děng yīhuìr zài jìnqù, quèbǎo zìjǐ shì dì'èr wèi. Jiéguǒ yǒu shíhou dì yī wèi kèrén chíchí bù chūxiàn, yīnwèi měi gè rén dōu zài děng zhe dāng dì'èr wèi. Zhè jiàn shì ràng wǒ juéde jì yōumò yòu shēnkè: rénrén dōu xiǎng zuò nàge bèi yànzhèng de rén, què méiyǒu rén yuànyì zuò nàge dì yī gè zǒu jìn kōng diàn de yǒngzhě.
text_en: The coffee shop I frequent has an unwritten tradition: every day, the second customer gets a free cup of coffee. The owner says this rule has existed since his grandfather opened the shop, nearly sixty years. Why the second? He says his grandfather's explanation was: the first customer is the bravest, because walking into a completely empty shop takes courage; but the second customer is the most important, because their arrival proves the first customer's choice was correct. 'If a shop has only one customer, that's chance; with a second, it starts becoming a kind of inevitability.' This explanation stayed with me. Behind it lies a distinctly Eastern social psychology: when we make decisions, what truly convinces us is often not the rationality of the decision itself, but seeing that someone before us made the same choice. Queue psychology confirms this—people choose restaurants with long lines not because the food is better, but because the line itself is a form of certification. The significance of the second customer lies precisely here: they are the first 'certifier.' Without them, the first customer is merely someone who made a solitary choice; with them, the shop's daily routine begins. I later observed a fascinating phenomenon: many people, once they learned of this tradition, would deliberately wait a bit after opening time before entering, to ensure they were the second customer. The result was that sometimes the first customer never appeared, because everyone was waiting to be second. This struck me as both humorous and profound: everyone wants to be the one who is validated, but nobody wants to be the brave one who walks into the empty shop first.

title: The Minutes of the Neighborhood Tree Committee
title_zh: 小区绿化委员会会议记录
text_zh: 我们小区曾经成立过一个「绿化委员会」，由七位退休业主组成，每月开一次会，讨论小区内的树木花草事务。第一次会议的核心议题是：三号楼前的那棵银杏树在秋天掉落的白果气味太大，有业主投诉。委员们讨论了三个方案：一、砍树；二、摇树让果子提前掉完；三、什么都不做但发一封告业主书解释银杏果有药用价值。投票结果是四比二比一，选了第三方案。但争论并没有结束，因为负责起草告业主书的委员老钱写了一篇两千字的文章，从银杏的进化史讲到中药的药理学，被其他委员批评「太长了，没人会看」。最终由秘书杨阿姨改写成三行字：「银杏果有药用价值。味道是暂时的。请理解。」最精彩的一次会议发生在春天。有人提议在小区空地上种樱花树，理由是「日本的樱花很美」。这个提议立刻引发了激烈的争论——不是关于美学的，而是关于国族情感的。赵叔坚持应该种梅花，因为「梅花才是中国的精神」；张阿姨说应该种桃树，因为「桃子可以吃，樱花能吃吗」。最终的折中方案是种了一棵海棠——理由是海棠既有中国传统审美的典雅，又不涉及任何政治敏感性，而且「海棠花开的时候很好看，落了以后不臭」。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen xiǎoqū céngjīng chénglì guò yī gè 「lǜhuà wěiyuánhuì」, yóu qī wèi tuìxiū yèzhǔ zǔchéng, měi yuè kāi yī cì huì, tǎolùn xiǎoqū nèi de shùmù huācǎo shìwù. Wǒ zhī suǒyǐ zhīdào zhè jiàn shì de xìjié, shì yīnwèi wěiyuánhuì de mìshū——tuìxiū kuàijì Yáng āyí——bǎ měi cì de huìyì jìlù yòng gōngzhěng de kǎishū chāoxiě zài yī gè yìngpí bǐjìběn lǐ, hòulái tā bānjiā shí bǎ bǐjìběn sòng gěi le wùyè, wùyè yòu zhǎnzhuǎn dào le wǒ shǒu lǐ. Fānkàn nàxiē huìyì jìlù, jiǎnzhí jiùshì yī bù wēixíng de zhèngzhì xǐjù. Dì yī cì huìyì de héxīn yìtí shì: sān hào lóu qián de nà kē yínxìng shù zài qiūtiān diāoluò de báiguǒ qìwèi tài dà, yǒu yèzhǔ tóusù. Wěiyuánmen tǎolùn le sān gè fāng'àn: yī, kǎn shù; èr, yáo shù ràng guǒzi tíqián diào wán; sān, shénme dōu bú zuò dàn fā yī fēng gào yèzhǔ shū jiěshì yínxìng guǒ yǒu yàoyòng jiàzhí. Tóupiào jiéguǒ shì sì bǐ èr bǐ yī, xuǎn le dì sān fāng'àn. Dàn zhēnglùn bìng méiyǒu jiéshù, yīnwèi fùzé qǐcǎo gào yèzhǔ shū de wěiyuán lǎo Qián xiě le yī piān liǎng qiān zì de wénzhāng, cóng yínxìng de jìnhuàshǐ jiǎng dào zhōngyào de yàolǐxué, bèi qítā wěiyuán pīpíng 「tài cháng le, méi rén huì kàn」. Zuìzhōng yóu Yáng āyí gǎixiě chéng sān háng zì: 「Yínxìng guǒ yǒu yàoyòng jiàzhí. Wèidao shì zànshí de. Qǐng lǐjiě.」Zuì jīngcǎi de yī cì huìyì fāshēng zài chūntiān. Yǒu rén tíyì zài xiǎoqū kòngdì shàng zhòng yīnghuā shù, lǐyóu shì 「Rìběn de yīnghuā hěn měi」. Zhège tíyì lìkè yǐnfā le jīliè de zhēnglùn——bú shì guānyú měixué de, ér shì guānyú guózú qínggǎn de. Zhào shū jiānchí yīnggāi zhòng méihuā, yīnwèi 「méihuā cái shì Zhōngguó de jīngshén」; Zhāng āyí shuō yīnggāi zhòng táo shù, yīnwèi 「táozi kěyǐ chī, yīnghuā néng chī ma」; zuìzhōng de zhézhōng fāng'àn shì zhòng le yī kē hǎitáng——lǐyóu shì hǎitáng jì yǒu Zhōngguó chuántǒng shěnměi de diǎnyǎ, yòu bù shèjí rènhé zhèngzhì mǐngǎnxìng, érqiě 「hǎitáng huā kāi de shíhou hěn hǎokàn, luò le yǐhòu bù chòu」. Wǒ dú zhèxiē jìlù de shíhou yīzhí zài xiào, dàn xiào wán yǐhòu juéde yǒu yīdiǎn gǎndòng. Zhèxiē tuìxiū lǎorén yòng qiánsuǒwèiyǒu de rènzhēn tàidù tǎolùn zhe yī kē shù de qùliú. Zài yī gè suǒyǒu rén dōu máng zhe zhuànqián de shìjiè lǐ, yǒu rén yuànyì wèi yī kē shù kāi qī cì huì, xiě sānshí yè huìyì jìlù, zhēnglùn bànnián——zhè běnshēn jiùshì yī zhǒng zhēnguì de huāngdàn.
text_en: Our compound once established a 'Greening Committee,' composed of seven retired homeowners, meeting monthly to discuss the community's trees and plants. The first meeting's core agenda item: the ginkgo tree in front of Building 3 drops fruit in autumn whose smell is too strong; residents complained. The committee discussed three proposals: 1) cut the tree down; 2) shake it to make the fruit fall early; 3) do nothing but issue a letter to homeowners explaining ginkgo fruit has medicinal value. The vote was 4-2-1 in favor of the third option. But the debate didn't end, because Committee Member Old Qian, tasked with drafting the letter, wrote a two-thousand-character essay spanning the evolutionary history of ginkgo to the pharmacology of traditional Chinese medicine. Other members criticized it as 'too long—nobody will read it.' Secretary Auntie Yang ultimately rewrote it into three lines: 'Ginkgo fruit has medicinal value. The smell is temporary. Please understand.' The most spectacular meeting occurred in spring. Someone proposed planting cherry trees in the compound's open space, reasoning that 'Japanese cherry blossoms are beautiful.' This immediately triggered fierce debate—not about aesthetics but about national sentiment. Uncle Zhao insisted on plum blossoms because 'plum blossoms represent the Chinese spirit.' Auntie Zhang said they should plant peach trees because 'you can eat peaches—can you eat cherry blossoms?' The final compromise was a crabapple tree—the reasoning being that crabapples possess the elegance of traditional Chinese aesthetics without any political sensitivity, and 'crabapple blossoms look beautiful when in bloom and don't stink when they fall.'

title: The Grammar of Rain
text_zh: 中文里描述雨的七个词，从轻到重。第一个：毛毛雨。它不是在「下」，而是在「飘」，像空气中多了一层看不见的绒毛。走在毛毛雨里不需要伞，衣服表面会蒙上一层细密的水珠，但不会湿透。适合散步，适合想事情。第二个：细雨。比毛毛雨重一点，有了线条感，可以被看见但几乎没有声音。李商隐写过它，戴望舒也写过它，它是中国文学里出场率最高的天气。第三个：小雨。地面会湿，但不会积水。带不带伞都行，取决于你对「淋湿」这件事的态度。第四个：中雨。分界线。超过这个等级，不打伞就会被淋透。出门前要看天气预报的，一般就是因为它。第五个：大雨。有力量了。打在屋檐上是有节奏的，像一个不太有耐心的人在敲门。积水开始出现在地势低洼的路段。外卖员的差评率在这个等级开始上升。第六个：暴雨。不再敲门——直接闯进来。声音覆盖一切，说话要喊着说。世界变成一面白色的墙，三米以外的东西看不清楚。第七个：倾盆大雨。整个天空像一个被打翻的盆，水不是一滴一滴地落，而是一整面一整面地倾倒下来。英语里用torrential rain来翻译它，但翻译完总觉得少了什么。少的是那个「盆」——那个具体的、家常的、从厨房里拿出来的意象。中文把天空变成了一口倒扣的盆。这只有中文做得到。
text_pinyin: Zhōngwén lǐ miáoshù yǔ de cíhuì gòuchéng le yī tào jīngmì de gǎnzhī yǔfǎ. 「Máomáoyǔ」shì zuì qīng de——tā bú shì zài 「xià」, ér shì zài 「piāo」, xiàng kōngqì zhōng duō le yī céng kàn bú jiàn de róngmáo. 「Xìyǔ」bǐ máomáoyǔ zhòng yīdiǎn, yǒu le xiàntiáo gǎn, kěyǐ bèi kànjiàn dàn jīhū méiyǒu shēngyīn. 「Xiǎoyǔ」kāishǐ yǒu le cúnzàigǎn——dìmiàn huì shī, dàn bú huì jīshuǐ, sǎn kěyǐ bù dǎ. 「Zhōngyǔ」shì yī gè fēnjièxiàn——chāoguò zhège děngjí, bù dǎ sǎn jiù huì bèi lín tòu. 「Dàyǔ」yǒu le lìliàng, tā dǎ zài wūyán shàng shì yǒu jiézòu de, xiàng yī gè bú tài yǒu nàixīn de rén zài qiāo mén. 「Bàoyǔ」bú zài qiāo mén, tā zhíjiē chuǎng jìnlái, shēngyīn fùgài yīqiè, shìjiè biànchéng le yī miàn báisè de qiáng. 「Qīngpéndàyǔ」shì zuì jíduān de——zhěnggè tiānkōng xiàng yī gè bèi dǎfān de pén, shuǐ bú shì yī dī yī dī de luò, ér shì yī zhěng miàn yī zhěng miàn de qīngdǎo xiàlái. Wǒ céngjīng shìtú bǎ zhèxiē cí fānyì chéng Yīngyǔ: drizzle, light rain, moderate rain, heavy rain, downpour, torrential rain. Fānyì wán yǐhòu juéde nǎlǐ búduì. Yīngyǔ de zhèxiē cí shì ànzhào jiàngshuǐliàng de děngjí huàfēn de, běnzhì shàng shì qìxiàngxué de yǔyán; ér Zhōngwén de zhèxiē cí miáoshù de bù jǐn shì yǔ de dàxiǎo, hái yǒu yǔ de zhìgǎn, shēngyīn, sùdù, shènzhì yǔ duì rén de xīnlǐ yǐngxiǎng. 「Máomáoyǔ」ràng rén xiǎng mànmàn zǒulù, 「bàoyǔ」ràng rén xiǎng duǒ qǐlái——zhèxiē xīnlǐ ànshì shì qiàn zài cíhuì běnshēn lǐmiàn de. Yǔyán bù jǐnjǐn shì zài miáoshù shìjiè, tā hái zài jiào wǒmen yòng shénme yàng de kēlìdù qù gǎnzhī shìjiè. Yī gè yǔyán zhōng guānyú yǔ de cíhuì yuè fēngfù, shuō zhè zhǒng yǔyán de rén jiù yuè néng zài yǔ lǐ biànrèn chū gèng duō de céngcì. Zhè dàgài jiùshì wèishéme wǒ měi cì tīng yǔ de shíhou, yòng Zhōngwén xiǎng hé yòng Yīngwén xiǎng, gǎnshòu dào de shì bùtóng de yǔ.
text_en: Seven Chinese words for rain, from lightest to heaviest. The first: maomaoyv. It does not 'fall' but 'floats,' as though the air has acquired an invisible layer of down. Walking in maomaoyv requires no umbrella. A fine mist of droplets settles on the surface of your clothes but will not soak through. Suited for strolling, suited for thinking. The second: xiyu, fine rain. Slightly heavier than maomaoyv, with a sense of line—visible but nearly soundless. Li Shangyin wrote about it. Dai Wangshu wrote about it. It is the most frequently appearing weather in Chinese literature. The third: xiaoyv, light rain. The ground gets wet but does not puddle. Whether to carry an umbrella is optional, depending on your attitude toward getting damp. The fourth: zhongyv, moderate rain. The dividing line. Beyond this level, you get soaked without an umbrella. When people check the weather forecast before leaving the house, this is usually why. The fifth: dayv, heavy rain. It has force now. Striking the eaves it has rhythm, like an impatient person knocking at the door. Standing water begins to appear in low-lying sections of road. Delivery drivers' negative review rates start climbing at this level. The sixth: baoyv, rainstorm. It stops knocking—barges straight in. Its sound covers everything; you have to shout to be heard. The world becomes a white wall; anything beyond three meters is unclear. The seventh: qingpendayv, pouring rain. The entire sky like an overturned basin. Water falls not drop by drop but in entire sheets pouring down. English translates it as 'torrential rain,' but after translating, something always feels missing. What is missing is the 'basin'—that concrete, domestic, taken-from-the-kitchen image. Chinese turns the sky into an upended basin. Only Chinese can do that.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为中文雨词和英文雨词的根本区别是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author see as the fundamental difference between Chinese and English rain vocabulary?

options.text: 中文雨词更多
options.pinyin: Zhōngwén yǔ cí gèng duō
options.text_en: Chinese has more rain words

options.text: 英文按降水量分级（气象学语言），中文还包含质感、声音和心理影响
options.pinyin: Yīngwén àn jiàngshuǐliàng fēnjí (qìxiàngxué yǔyán), Zhōngwén hái bāohán zhìgǎn, shēngyīn hé xīnlǐ yǐngxiǎng
options.text_en: English classifies by precipitation volume (meteorological language); Chinese also encodes texture, sound, and psychological effect

options.text: 英文的雨词翻译不准确
options.pinyin: Yīngwén de yǔ cí fānyì bù zhǔnquè
options.text_en: English rain words are inaccurately translated

options.text: 中国的雨比外国的雨更复杂
options.pinyin: Zhōngguó de yǔ bǐ wàiguó de yǔ gèng fùzá
options.text_en: Rain in China is more complex than rain abroad

questions.explanation: "Seven Chinese words for rain" — concrete detail pins this down; the passage doesn't leave it abstract.

questions.q_zh: 「语言不仅是在描述世界，还在教我们用什么样的颗粒度去感知世界」这句话的核心论点是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the core argument of 'language not only describes the world but teaches us at what granularity to perceive it'?

options.text: 学习更多语言可以感知更多
options.pinyin: xuéxí gèng duō yǔyán kěyǐ gǎnzhī gèng duō
options.text_en: Learning more languages lets you perceive more

options.text: 语言塑造了我们的认知框架，词汇的丰富度决定了感知的精细度
options.pinyin: yǔyán sùzào le wǒmen de rènzhī kuàngjià, cíhuì de fēngfùdù juédìng le gǎnzhī de jīngxìdù
options.text_en: Language shapes our cognitive framework; vocabulary richness determines perceptual granularity

options.text: 中文是世界上最好的语言
options.pinyin: Zhōngwén shì shìjiè shàng zuì hǎo de yǔyán
options.text_en: Chinese is the best language in the world

options.text: 翻译是不可能的
options.pinyin: fānyì shì bù kěnéng de
options.text_en: Translation is impossible

questions.explanation: "visible but nearly soundless" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

questions.q_zh: 文末作者说用中文和英文想的时候感受到的是「不同的雨」，这说明了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author's observation that thinking in Chinese versus English yields 'different rain' demonstrate?

options.text: 作者的英语不够好
options.pinyin: zuòzhě de Yīngyǔ búgòu hǎo
options.text_en: The author's English isn't good enough

options.text: 不同的语言提供不同的感知滤镜，改变了同一现象在意识中的呈现方式
options.pinyin: bùtóng de yǔyán tígōng bùtóng de gǎnzhī lǜjìng, gǎibiàn le tóngyī xiànxiàng zài yìshi zhōng de chéngxiàn fāngshì
options.text_en: Different languages provide different perceptual filters, changing how the same phenomenon presents itself in consciousness

options.text: 中国的气候和外国不同
options.pinyin: Zhōngguó de qìhòu hé wàiguó bùtóng
options.text_en: China's climate differs from abroad

options.text: 他在不同语言环境中生活过
options.pinyin: tā zài bùtóng yǔyán huánjìng zhōng shēnghuó guò
options.text_en: He has lived in different language environments

questions.explanation: "visible but nearly soundless" — the contrast structure reveals the passage's actual commitment.

title: The Cartography of Silence
text_zh: 赵先生和赵太太结婚五十三年了。他们每天下午三点在小区花园的长椅上坐一个小时。赵先生坐左边，赵太太坐右边，中间隔着一个保温杯的距离。他们很少说话。赵太太织毛衣，赵先生看报纸。偶尔赵太太会停下手里的针，用下巴指一下路过的某个人。赵先生从报纸上方看一眼，点点头或者摇摇头，然后两个人继续各做各的。小区的保安小李说他观察了三年，发现这两个人有一套外人根本看不懂的信号系统。赵太太把保温杯推向赵先生那边，意思是「你该喝水了」。赵先生翻报纸的声音如果特别响，意思是「看到一条荒唐的新闻」。赵太太如果把毛衣针插进线团里不动了，是在生气。赵先生如果把报纸折起来放在膝盖上，是在道歉。最微妙的一次：赵太太忽然停止织毛衣，看了赵先生一眼。赵先生放下报纸，两个人同时站起来，没说一个字就往家走。小李后来问他们怎么回事，赵太太说：「要下雨了。」小李抬头看天，万里无云。二十分钟后下了暴雨。赵先生叠好报纸，夹在腋下。长椅上留着两个人坐出来的浅浅凹痕。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhùyì dào, měi gè rén dōu yōngyǒu yī tào shǔyú zìjǐ de chénmò xìtǒng. Zhè tào xìtǒng bìngfēi dānyī de kòngbái, ér shì yī fú jīngmì de dìtú, shàngmiàn biāozhù zhe nǎxiē huàtí kěyǐ tánlùn, nǎxiē bìxū ràoxíng, nǎxiē dìfāng shè yǒu qínggǎn de guānkǎ. Yǔ rén jiāowǎng yuè jiǔ, nǐ jiù yuè néng biànrèn chū duìfāng dìtú shàng de jìnqū——nàxiē tā cóng bù tíqǐ de míngzì, tā yǒngyuǎn bú huì zhǔdòng liáo qǐ de chéngshì, tāmen gòngtóng huíbì de nà duàn suìyuè. Chénmò bìngfēi wúshēng. Qiàqià xiāngfǎn, tā yōngyǒu jíqí fēngfù de zhìdì yǔ céngcì. Yǒuxiē chénmò róuruǎn rú mián, shì liǎng gè rén zuò zài yīqǐ bù xūyào shuōhuà yě juéde shūfu de nà zhǒng mòqì; yǒuxiē chénmò jiānyìng rú bì, shì yī zhǒng jīngnián-lěiyuè zhù qǐ de xīnlǐ fángxiàn. Zuì nàirén-xúnwèi de, shì nàxiē「jīhū shuō chūkǒu」de chénmò——huà dào zuǐ biān yòu yàn huíqù de shùnjiān, kōngqì zhōng fǎngfú cánliú zhe wèi wánchéng de yīnjié de xíngzhuàng. Wǒ rènshi yī duì lǎo fūfù, jiéhūn wǔshí yú nián, bǐcǐ zhī jiān de chénmò yǐjīng fāzhǎn chéng le yī zhǒng jìnhū yìshù de jiāoliú fāngshì. Tāmen nénggòu zài chénmò zhōng chuándì guānqiè, biǎodá bùmǎn, shènzhì wánchéng fùzá de qínggǎn xiéshāng. Pángrén kàn lái tāmen zhǐshì ānjìng de zuò zhe, dàn shíjì shàng tāmen zhī jiān zhèngzài jìnxíng yī chǎng wúshēng què mìjí de duìhuà. Huòxǔ, rén yǔ rén zhī jiān zuì shēn de lǐjiě, bú zàiyú nǐ zhīdào duìfāng huì shuō shénme, ér zàiyú nǐ dú de dǒng duìfāng xuǎnzé bù shuō shénme.
text_en: Mr. Zhao and Mrs. Zhao have been married fifty-three years. Every afternoon at three they sit for one hour on the bench in the residential compound's garden. Mr. Zhao sits on the left, Mrs. Zhao on the right, a thermos-width of space between them. They rarely speak. Mrs. Zhao knits; Mr. Zhao reads the newspaper. Occasionally Mrs. Zhao pauses her needles and uses her chin to point at someone passing by. Mr. Zhao glances over the top of his newspaper, nods or shakes his head, and then they resume their separate activities. Li, the compound's security guard, says he has observed them for three years and discovered that these two people have a signal system outsiders cannot decipher. When Mrs. Zhao pushes the thermos toward Mr. Zhao's side, it means 'you should drink some water.' When Mr. Zhao turns the newspaper pages especially loudly, it means 'I just read something absurd.' When Mrs. Zhao sticks her knitting needles into the ball of yarn and stops moving, she is angry. When Mr. Zhao folds the newspaper and places it on his knees, he is apologizing. The most subtle instance: Mrs. Zhao suddenly stopped knitting and looked at Mr. Zhao. Mr. Zhao put down the newspaper. Both stood up at the same time and walked home without saying a word. Li asked them later what had happened. Mrs. Zhao said: 'It was going to rain.' Li looked up at the sky. Not a cloud in sight. Twenty minutes later it poured. Mr. Zhao folded the newspaper and tucked it under his arm. On the bench remained the shallow impressions where two people had been sitting.

questions.q_zh: 作者把沉默比作地图，主要想表达什么观点？
questions.q_en: What is the author's main point in comparing silence to a map?

options.text: 每个人的沉默都有结构、有边界，是一套有组织的体系
options.pinyin: měi gè rén de chénmò dōu yǒu jiégòu, yǒu biānjiè, shì yī tào yǒu zǔzhī de tǐxì
options.text_en: Everyone's silence has structure and boundaries, forming an organized system

options.text: 沉默像地图一样可以被精确测量
options.pinyin: chénmò xiàng dìtú yīyàng kěyǐ bèi jīngquè cèliáng
options.text_en: Silence can be precisely measured like a map

options.text: 每个人都需要学习如何绘制沉默
options.pinyin: měi gè rén dōu xūyào xuéxí rúhé huìzhì chénmò
options.text_en: Everyone needs to learn how to draw silence

options.text: 沉默总是消极的，需要被探索和打破
options.pinyin: chénmò zǒng shì xiāojí de, xūyào bèi tànsuǒ hé dǎpò
options.text_en: Silence is always negative and needs to be explored and broken

questions.explanation: "her chin to point at someone passing" — this is where the passage shows its hand.

questions.q_zh: 关于那对老夫妇的沉默，以下哪项最符合作者的描述？
questions.q_en: Which best matches the author's description of the elderly couple's silence?

options.text: 他们因为无话可说才沉默
options.pinyin: tāmen yīnwèi wú huà kě shuō cái chénmò
options.text_en: They are silent because they have nothing to say

options.text: 他们的沉默是一种高度发展的、近乎艺术的交流形式
options.pinyin: tāmen de chénmò shì yī zhǒng gāodù fāzhǎn de, jìnhū yìshù de jiāoliú xíngshì
options.text_en: Their silence is a highly developed, near-artistic form of communication

options.text: 他们的沉默代表着五十年积累的不满
options.pinyin: tāmen de chénmò dàibiǎo zhe wǔshí nián jīlěi de bùmǎn
options.text_en: Their silence represents fifty years of accumulated dissatisfaction

options.text: 旁人能轻易理解他们沉默中的含义
options.pinyin: pángrén néng qīngyì lǐjiě tāmen chénmò zhōng de hányì
options.text_en: Outsiders can easily understand the meaning in their silence

questions.explanation: "have been married fifty-three years" narrows the field; specificity rules out vaguer alternatives.

title: The Archaeology of a Bookshelf
text_zh: 一个人的书架，往往比他的自传更加诚实。自传可以经过精心编排，把不堪的章节删去、把平庸的岁月美化，但书架不会说谎。那些被翻到书脊开裂的书，透露着某段人生的执念；那些买回来却从未拆封的书，暴露了某个未曾兑现的自我期许；而那些被藏在最角落、几乎被其他书压住的书，则暗示着某些不愿面对的曾经。我有一位老友，退休后邀我去他家整理书房。他的书架跨越了四面墙，按照年代自然排列——并非刻意为之，而是多年来随手放置的结果。从左到右走一遍，竟然像穿越了一个人的一生。最左边是大学时代的理想主义读物：存在主义哲学、革命诗歌、乌托邦小说。接下来是研究生时期的专业文献，密密麻麻地做着笔记。中间一大段是职场黄金期——管理学、心理学、演讲技巧，实用而功利。再往右，节奏明显慢了下来：旅行文学、园艺指南、古典诗词选集。最右端，也就是最近几年买的书，全是关于死亡、告别与存在意义的哲思。他看着我扫视完整面书墙，苦笑了一下说：「你看，一个人的一辈子，就这么摊在墙上了。」我想说些安慰的话，但最终只是沉默。因为那面书墙确实比任何语言都更完整地叙述了一个人从燃烧到冷却的全部过程。
text_pinyin: Yī gè rén de shūjià, wǎngwǎng bǐ tā de zìzhuàn gèngjiā chéngshí. Zìzhuàn kěyǐ jīngguò jīngxīn biānpái, bǎ bùkān de zhāngjié shān qù, bǎ píngyōng de suìyuè měihuà, dàn shūjià bú huì shuōhuǎng. Nàxiē bèi fān dào shūjǐ kāiliè de shū, tòulù zhe mǒu duàn rénshēng de zhíniàn; nàxiē mǎi huílái què cóng wèi chāifēng de shū, bàolù le mǒu gè wèicéng duìxiàn de zìwǒ qīxǔ; ér nàxiē bèi cáng zài zuì jiǎoluò, jīhū bèi qítā shū yā zhù de shū, zé ànshì zhe mǒuxiē bú yuàn miànduì de céngjīng. Wǒ yǒu yī wèi lǎoyǒu, tuìxiū hòu yāo wǒ qù tā jiā zhěnglǐ shūfáng. Tā de shūjià kuàyuè le sì miàn qiáng, ànzhào niándài zìrán páiliè——bìngfēi kèyì wéi zhī, ér shì duō nián lái suíshǒu fàngzhì de jiéguǒ. Cóng zuǒ dào yòu zǒu yī biàn, jìngrán xiàng chuānyuè le yī gè rén de yīshēng. Zuì zuǒbiān shì dàxué shídài de lǐxiǎng zhǔyì dúwù: cúnzài zhǔyì zhéxué, gémìng shīgē, wūtuōbāng xiǎoshuō. Jiē xiàlái shì yánjiūshēng shíqī de zhuānyè wénxiàn, mìmìmámá de zuò zhe bǐjì. Zhōngjiān yī dà duàn shì zhíchǎng huángjīn qī——guǎnlǐ xué, xīnlǐ xué, yǎnjiǎng jìqiǎo, shíyòng ér gōnglì. Zài wǎng yòu, jiézòu míngxiǎn màn le xiàlái: lǚxíng wénxué, yuányì zhǐnán, gǔdiǎn shīcí xuǎnjí. Zuì yòu duān, yě jiùshì zuìjìn jǐ nián mǎi de shū, quán shì guānyú sǐwáng, gàobié yǔ cúnzài yìyì de zhésī. Tā kàn zhe wǒ sǎoshì wán zhěng miàn shū qiáng, kǔxiào le yīxià shuō:「Nǐ kàn, yī gè rén de yī bèizi, jiù zhème tān zài qiáng shàng le.」Wǒ xiǎng shuō xiē ānwèi de huà, dàn zuìzhōng zhǐshì chénmò. Yīnwèi nà miàn shū qiáng quèshí bǐ rènhé yǔyán dōu gèng wánzhěng de xùshù le yī gè rén cóng ránshāo dào lěngquè de quánbù guòchéng.
text_en: A person's bookshelf is often more honest than their autobiography. An autobiography can be carefully curated—shameful chapters deleted, mediocre years embellished—but a bookshelf doesn't lie. Books worn until their spines crack reveal an obsession from some chapter of life; books bought but never unwrapped expose some unfulfilled self-promise; and books hidden in the farthest corner, nearly buried under others, hint at things once were that one would rather not face. An old friend of mine invited me over after retirement to help organize his study. His shelves spanned four walls, naturally arranged by era—not by design, but as the result of years of casually placing books. Walking from left to right felt like traversing a person's entire life. At the far left were the idealistic readings of college days: existentialist philosophy, revolutionary poetry, utopian novels. Next came the specialized literature of graduate school, densely annotated. The large middle section covered his golden professional years—management, psychology, public speaking techniques, practical and utilitarian. Further right, the pace visibly slowed: travel literature, gardening guides, classical poetry anthologies. At the far right end—books purchased in recent years—all were philosophical reflections on death, farewell, and the meaning of existence. He watched me scan the entire wall of books and smiled bitterly: 'You see, a person's whole life, just spread out on the wall like that.' I wanted to say something comforting but ultimately just stayed silent. Because that wall of books did narrate, more completely than any language could, the entire process of a person going from burning to cooling.

title: The Grammar of Rain
text_zh: 如果雨有自己的语法，那么不同季节的雨大概属于不同的语态。春雨是虚拟语气——轻柔、含蓄、带有几分试探性，仿佛在小心翼翼地征求大地的同意。夏雨则是命令句，不由分说地倾泻而下，把一切声音都淹没在它的独白里。秋雨用的是过去完成时，每一滴都像是对某段已经结束的事物的追忆，带着一种「一切都已然发生」的既定感。冬雨最为罕见，但一旦出现，它所使用的语态最为复杂——介于存在与消亡之间，随时可能凝结成雪，也随时可能蒸发殆尽。我住过一栋老宅，瓦片已经年久失修，雨天的时候整栋房子就变成了一架巨大的乐器。不同位置的漏水发出不同音高的声响：厨房角落是低沉的咚咚声，走廊尽头是清脆的滴答声，而我卧室窗台上那一片略有翘起的铁皮，被雨滴击打后会发出一种类似于古筝拨弦的共鸣。我从未修好过那些漏洞——不是因为懒惰，而是因为某个雨夜，我忽然意识到，这栋房子正在用漏雨的方式与天空对话。修好它，就等于剥夺了它说话的权利。后来我搬了家，住进了一栋隔音极好的公寓。下雨的时候，室内安静得像一个密封的容器。下雨的时候，我偶尔把窗户打开一条缝。
text_pinyin: Rúguǒ yǔ yǒu zìjǐ de yǔfǎ, nàme bùtóng jìjié de yǔ dàgài shǔyú bùtóng de yǔtài. Chūnyǔ shì xūnǐ yǔqì——qīngróu, hánxù, dài yǒu jǐ fēn shìtàn xìng, fǎngfú zài xiǎoxīn-yìyì de zhēngqiú dàdì de tóngyì. Xiàyǔ zé shì mìnglìng jù, bùyóu-fēnshuō de qīngxiè ér xià, bǎ yīqiè shēngyīn dōu yānmò zài tā de dúbái lǐ. Qiūyǔ yòng de shì guòqù wánchéng shí, měi yī dī dōu xiàng shì duì mǒu duàn yǐjīng jiéshù de shìwù de zhuīyì, dài zhe yī zhǒng「yīqiè dōu yǐrán fāshēng」de jìdìng gǎn. Dōngyǔ zuìwéi hǎnjiàn, dàn yīdàn chūxiàn, tā suǒ shǐyòng de yǔtài zuìwéi fùzá——jiè yú cúnzài yǔ xiāowáng zhī jiān, suíshí kěnéng níngjié chéng xuě, yě suíshí kěnéng zhēngfā dài jìn. Wǒ zhù guò yī dòng lǎozhái, wǎpiàn yǐjīng niánjiǔ-shīxiū, yǔtiān de shíhou zhěng dòng fángzi jiù biànchéng le yī jià jùdà de yuèqì. Bùtóng wèizhì de lòushuǐ fāchū bùtóng yīngāo de shēngxiǎng: chúfáng jiǎoluò shì dīchén de dōngdōng shēng, zǒuláng jìntóu shì qīngcuì de dīdā shēng, ér wǒ wòshì chuāngtái shàng nà yī piàn lüè yǒu qiào qǐ de tiěpí, bèi yǔdī jīdǎ hòu huì fāchū yī zhǒng lèisì yú gǔzhēng bō xián de gòngmíng. Wǒ cóng wèi xiūhǎo guò nàxiē lòudòng——bú shì yīnwèi lǎnduò, ér shì yīnwèi mǒu gè yǔ yè, wǒ hūrán yìshí dào, zhè dòng fángzi zhèngzài yòng lòuyǔ de fāngshì yǔ tiānkōng duìhuà. Xiūhǎo tā, jiù děngyú bōduó le tā shuōhuà de quánlì. Hòulái wǒ bān le jiā, zhù jìn le yī dòng géyīn jí hǎo de gōngyù. Xiàyǔ de shíhou, shìnèi ānjìng de xiàng yī gè mìfēng de róngqì. Wǒ cái huǎngrán míngbái, yuánlái wǒ huáiniàn de bú shì nà dòng lǎozhái, ér shì nà zhǒng yǔ tiānqì zhī jiān háo wú gé hé de gòngchǔ fāngshì.
text_en: If rain had its own grammar, then rain in different seasons would probably belong to different moods. Spring rain is the subjunctive—gentle, reserved, with a hint of tentativeness, as if carefully seeking the earth's consent. Summer rain is the imperative, pouring down peremptorily, drowning all other sounds in its monologue. Autumn rain uses the past perfect tense, each drop seemingly a reminiscence of something already ended, carrying a sense of 'everything has already occurred.' Winter rain is the rarest, but once it appears, the mood it employs is the most complex—hovering between existence and dissolution, liable at any moment to crystallize into snow or evaporate entirely. I once lived in an old house whose tiles had long fallen into disrepair. On rainy days the entire house became a massive instrument. Leaks in different locations produced sounds of different pitches: the kitchen corner gave a deep thudding, the end of the hallway a crisp dripping, and the slightly curled piece of tin on my bedroom windowsill, when struck by raindrops, produced a resonance resembling a guzheng string being plucked. I never fixed those leaks—not out of laziness, but because one rainy night this house was conversing with the sky through its leaks. To repair it would be to strip it of its right to speak. Later I moved into an apartment with excellent soundproofing. When it rained, the interior was as quiet as a sealed container. When it rains, I sometimes crack the window open.

title: The Patience of Dust
text_zh: 灰尘是世界上最有耐心的观察者。它落在书页上，落在窗台上，落在你以为自己再也不会碰的那个抽屉的把手上。它不催促你做任何事情，只是安静地沉积，用厚度丈量着你的忽略。如果说时间是一条河流，那么灰尘就是它的沉积物——你无法直接看到时间流过，但你可以用手指在积灰的表面划出一道痕迹，然后惊觉：原来已经过了这么久。我母亲去世后，我一直没有整理她的房间。门关着，钥匙挂在走廊的钩子上，我每天经过都会下意识地看一眼，但从未伸手去取。一年后我终于推开门，发现一切都和她离开时一模一样，只是所有表面都覆上了一层均匀的灰。那层灰让我感到一种奇异的安慰——它证明了这个房间在我缺席的这一年里，一直在忠实地等待。灰尘没有擅自移动任何东西、没有改变任何陈设，它只是温柔地覆盖了一切，像一床极薄的被子，把母亲留下的世界小心翼翼地保护起来。我花了整整一个下午来打扫，每擦去一层灰，就像揭开一层时间的封印。当母亲的梳妆台重新露出原来的颜色时，我忽然有一种错觉——仿佛她只是出了一趟远门，现在快要回来了。灰尘走了，但它留下的时间感，比任何日历都更加沉重。
text_pinyin: Huīchén shì shìjiè shàng zuì yǒu nàixīn de guānchá zhě. Tā luò zài shūyè shàng, luò zài chuāngtái shàng, luò zài nǐ yǐwéi zìjǐ zài yě bú huì pèng de nàge chōutì de bǎshou shàng. Tā bù cuīcù nǐ zuò rènhé shìqíng, zhǐshì ānjìng de chénjī, yòng hòudù zhàngliang zhe nǐ de hūlüè. Rúguǒ shuō shíjiān shì yī tiáo héliú, nàme huīchén jiùshì tā de chénjī wù——nǐ wúfǎ zhíjiē kàn dào shíjiān liú guò, dàn nǐ kěyǐ yòng shǒuzhǐ zài jī huī de biǎomiàn huà chū yī dào hénjì, ránhòu jīngjué: yuánlái yǐjīng guò le zhème jiǔ. Wǒ mǔqīn qùshì hòu, wǒ yīzhí méiyǒu zhěnglǐ tā de fángjiān. Mén guān zhe, yàoshi guà zài zǒuláng de gōuzi shàng, wǒ měi tiān jīngguò dōu huì xiàyìshí de kàn yī yǎn, dàn cóng wèi shēnshǒu qù qǔ. Yī nián hòu wǒ zhōngyú tuī kāi mén, fāxiàn yīqiè dōu hé tā líkāi shí yīmó-yīyàng, zhǐshì suǒyǒu biǎomiàn dōu fù shàng le yī céng jūnyún de huī. Nà céng huī ràng wǒ gǎndào yī zhǒng qíyì de ānwèi——tā zhèngmíng le zhège fángjiān zài wǒ quēxí de zhè yī nián lǐ, yīzhí zài zhōngshí de děngdài. Huīchén méiyǒu shànzì yídòng rènhé dōngxi, méiyǒu gǎibiàn rènhé chénshè, tā zhǐshì wēnróu de fùgài le yīqiè, xiàng yī chuáng jí báo de bèizi, bǎ mǔqīn liú xià de shìjiè xiǎoxīn-yìyì de bǎohù qǐlái. Wǒ huā le zhěngzhěng yī gè xiàwǔ lái dǎsǎo, měi cā qù yī céng huī, jiù xiàng jiē kāi yī céng shíjiān de fēngyìn. Dāng mǔqīn de shūzhuāng tái chóngxīn lùchū yuánlái de yánsè shí, wǒ hūrán yǒu yī zhǒng cuòjué——fǎngfú tā zhǐshì chū le yī tàng yuǎn mén, xiànzài kuài yào huílái le. Huīchén zǒu le, dàn tā liú xià de shíjiān gǎn, bǐ rènhé rìlì dōu gèngjiā chénzhòng.
text_en: Dust is the world's most patient observer. It settles on book pages, on windowsills, on the handle of that drawer you thought you'd never touch again. It doesn't rush you to do anything; it just quietly accumulates, measuring your neglect with its thickness. If time is a river, then dust is its sediment—you can't directly see time flowing past, but you can draw a line with your finger across a dusty surface and realize with a start: it's already been this long. After my mother passed away, I never organized her room. The door stayed closed, the key hung on a hook in the hallway. Every day I'd pass by and unconsciously glance at it, but never reached out to take it. A year later I finally pushed the door open and found everything exactly as it was when she left, except that every surface was covered with an even layer of dust. That layer of dust gave me a strange kind of comfort—it proved that this room, in the year of my absence, had been faithfully waiting. The dust had not presumed to move anything or alter any arrangement; it had simply covered everything gently, like an extremely thin blanket, carefully protecting the world my mother had left behind. I spent an entire afternoon cleaning. Every layer of dust I wiped away felt like breaking a seal of time. When my mother's vanity table revealed its original color again, I suddenly had the illusion—as if she had merely gone on a long trip and was about to come back. The dust was gone, but the sense of time it left behind was heavier than any calendar.

options.text: 灰尘让失去的时间变得可触可感，比抽象的日期更能唤起对逝去时光的沉痛感受
options.pinyin: huīchén ràng shīqù de shíjiān biàn de kě chù kě gǎn, bǐ chōuxiàng de rìqī gèng néng huànqǐ duì shìqù shíguāng de chéntòng gǎnshòu

questions.explanation: "The dust was gone" — the physical image does conceptual work that paraphrase would flatten.

title: The Frequency of Old Clocks
text_zh: 外公家有一座落地钟，据说是民国年间从一个德国传教士手中辗转流传而来的。钟壳是深色胡桃木，经过近百年的氧化，已经呈现出一种近乎黑色的深棕。那座钟每天慢两分钟。一个月下来就差了整整一个小时。外公从不校正它。外公是退休邮递员，六十二岁退休，之后的日子全部按照那座慢钟来过。他的下午茶比邻居晚两分钟。他的晚餐比别人家迟一点端上桌。他出门散步的时间也比其他老人晚。楼下的王伯伯说：「老陈，你那个钟不准，你知道吧？」外公说：「知道。它慢。」王伯伯说：「那你怎么不调？」外公说：「让它慢吧。我跟着它走，每天多赚两分钟。」王伯伯没听懂，摆了摆手走了。外公每天给那座钟上发条。周日上一次，固定在早饭后。他站在钟前面，用一把小铜钥匙插进钟面下方的孔里，顺时针转十二圈——不多不少，十二圈。转的时候很慢，像某种仪式。我小时候问他为什么不买一个电子钟，又准又方便。他没回答。外公去世是在一个星期四。星期天没有人给钟上发条。星期二钟停了。母亲后来把钟搬到了她家客厅。她买了一块新电池想改成石英机芯。拆开后盖的时候发现里面夹着一张纸条，外公的字：「不用修。让它慢。」
text_pinyin: Wǒ wàigōng jiā yǒu yī zuò luòdì zhōng, jùshuō shì Mínguó niánjiān cóng yī gè Déguó chuánjiàoshì shǒuzhōng zhǎnzhuǎn liúchuán ér lái de. Zhōng ké shì shēn sè hútáo mù, jīngguò jìn bǎi nián de yǎnghuà, yǐjīng chéngxiàn chū yī zhǒng jìnhū hēisè de shēn zōng. Bǎichuí de měi yī cì bǎidòng dōu dài yǒu yī zhǒng chéndiàndiàn de yíshì gǎn, fǎngfú tā bú shì zài jìsuàn shíjiān, ér shì zài chēngliáng shíjiān de zhòngliàng. Xiàndài de shíyīng zhōng zhuīqiú de shì jīngquè——měi miǎo wùchā bù chāoguò líng diǎn jǐ háomiǎo, shì yī zhǒng gōngyèhuà de shíjiān guān. Dàn nà zuò lǎo zhōng cónglái bù zhǔn. Tā měi tiān dàyuē màn liǎng fēnzhōng, yī gè yuè xiàlái jiù chà le zhěngzhěng yī gè xiǎoshí. Wàigōng cóng bù jiàozhèng tā, fǎn'ér lè zài qí zhōng. Tā shuō:「Ràng tā màn ba, wǒ gēn zhe tā de jiézòu zǒu, měi tiān jiù duō zhuàn le liǎng fēnzhōng.」Zhè jù huà tīng qǐlái xiàng shì wánxiào, dàn xì xiǎng zhī xià, jìng yǒu yī zhǒng shēnkè de shēnghuó zhéxué. Dāng suǒyǒu rén dōu bèi jīngquè de shíjiān bǎngjià——jīngquè dào fēnzhōng de huìyì, jīngquè dào miǎo de lièchē shíkèbiǎo, jīngquè dào háomiǎo de jiāoyì xìtǒng——wàigōng què xuǎnzé shēnghuó zài yī gè lüè xiǎn chíhuǎn de shíjiān wéidù lǐ. Tā de xiàwǔ chá yǒngyuǎn bǐ biéren wǎn liǎng fēnzhōng kāishǐ, tā de wǎncān yǒngyuǎn bǐ línjū chí yīdiǎn duān shàng zhuō. Zhè liǎng fēnzhōng de yánchí, gěi le tā yī zhǒng wéimiào de cóngróng——tā yǒngyuǎn bú shì zài「gǎn」shíjiān, ér shì zài「děng」shíjiān zhuī shàng lái. Wàigōng qùshì hòu, nà zuò zhōng tíng le. Méiyǒu rén zài gěi tā shàng fātiáo. Wǒ yǒu shíhou huì xiǎng, yěxǔ nà zuò zhōng bìng bú shì yīnwèi méi rén shàng fātiáo cái tíng de, ér shì yīnwèi wéiyī pèi de shàng tā nà màn liǎng fēnzhōng jiézòu de rén yǐjīng bú zài le.
text_en: Grandfather had a grandfather clock, said to have passed through several hands since the Republican era, originally from a German missionary. The clock case was dark walnut, oxidized over nearly a century into a brown so deep it approached black. That clock ran two minutes slow every day. After a month the gap amounted to a full hour. Grandfather never corrected it. Grandfather was a retired postman. He retired at sixty-two, and lived every day afterward according to that slow clock. His afternoon tea started two minutes later than the neighbors'. His dinner was set on the table a bit after everyone else's. His evening walk began later than the other old men's. Old Wang downstairs said: 'Old Chen, that clock of yours is off. You know that, right?' Grandfather said: 'I know. It runs slow.' Old Wang said: 'So why don't you adjust it?' Grandfather said: 'Let it run slow. I follow its rhythm and gain two extra minutes every day.' Old Wang did not understand, waved his hand, and walked away. Grandfather wound that clock every Sunday, always after breakfast. He stood before it, inserted a small brass key into the hole below the clock face, and turned it clockwise twelve times—no more, no less, twelve turns. He turned slowly, like performing some kind of ritual. When I was small I asked him why he did not buy a digital clock, accurate and convenient. He did not answer. Grandfather passed away on a Thursday. On Sunday no one wound the clock. By Tuesday the clock had stopped. Mother later moved the clock to her living room. She bought a new battery intending to convert it to a quartz movement. When she opened the back panel she found a slip of paper tucked inside, in Grandfather's handwriting: 'No need to fix it. Let it run slow.'

questions.q_zh: 外公不校正老钟的原因体现了什么哲学？
questions.q_en: What philosophy does the grandfather's refusal to correct the old clock reflect?

options.text: 与其被精确的时间绑架，不如选择从容的节奏
options.pinyin: yǔqí bèi jīngquè de shíjiān bǎngjià, bùrú xuǎnzé cóngróng de jiézòu
options.text_en: Rather than being held hostage by precise time, choose a more composed rhythm

options.text: 老年人不需要精确的时间管理
options.pinyin: lǎoniánrén bù xūyào jīngquè de shíjiān guǎnlǐ
options.text_en: Elderly people don't need precise time management

options.text: 古董钟不应该被修改
options.pinyin: gǔdǒng zhōng bù yīnggāi bèi xiūgǎi
options.text_en: Antique clocks shouldn't be modified

options.text: 他不知道如何校正这种钟
options.pinyin: tā bù zhīdào rúhé jiàozhèng zhè zhǒng zhōng
options.text_en: He didn't know how to correct this type of clock

questions.explanation: "waved his hand" — definition by exclusion: what it is NOT clarifies what it IS.

questions.q_zh: 文末为什么说钟停下来可能不是因为没人上发条？
questions.q_en: Why does the ending suggest the clock may not have stopped because no one wound it?

options.text: 钟的机械已经损坏了
options.pinyin: zhōng de jīxiè yǐjīng sǔnhuài le
options.text_en: The clock's mechanism was already broken

options.text: 作者用拟人手法表达钟与外公之间独特的默契已随人而逝
options.pinyin: zuòzhě yòng nǐrén shǒufǎ biǎodá zhōng yǔ wàigōng zhī jiān dútè de mòqì yǐ suí rén ér shì
options.text_en: The author uses personification to express that the unique understanding between the clock and grandfather vanished with him

options.text: 其他家人也可以给钟上发条
options.pinyin: qítā jiārén yě kěyǐ gěi zhōng shàng fātiáo
options.text_en: Other family members could also wind the clock

options.text: 作者觉得钟太旧了应该被淘汰
options.pinyin: zuòzhě juéde zhōng tài jiù le yīnggāi bèi táotài
options.text_en: The author felt the clock was too old and should be retired

questions.explanation: "originally from a German missionary" grounds the reason in lived detail rather than abstract logic.

title: The Weight of a Glance
text_zh: 上周四的地铁上，我和一个陌生人对视了大约两秒钟。她大概三十出头，穿深灰色大衣，手里拿着一本翻到中间的书。车厢很挤，我们面对面站着，中间隔了三个人的距离。不知道为什么，在某个瞬间我们的目光同时落在了对方脸上。两秒钟。然后我们都移开了。我看向车门上方的站点图，她低头继续看书。什么也没有发生。但那两秒钟里发生了很多事情。第一秒：确认。我的视觉系统在这一秒内完成了一次高速扫描——性别、年龄、表情、是否构成威胁。她的系统大概也在同步运行。第二秒：判断。这一秒的内容更复杂——我注意到她在读的是一本西班牙语的小说，书脊上有一行我看不懂的字。她的手指夹在第一百二十页左右。书页有折痕，说明她不用书签。与此同时，我知道她也在用同样的速度读取我的信息——我的衣着、我的疲惫程度、我手里拎着的超市塑料袋。两秒钟结束。社交协议启动：双方同时收回目光，恢复「礼貌性忽视」的默认状态。她在下一站下了车。车门关上之前，她回头看了一眼——不是看我，是看车厢里还有没有空座。或者，是看我。我不知道。门关了。列车启动。我低头看了看手里的超市塑料袋，里面是两根黄瓜和一盒鸡蛋。
text_pinyin: Rén de mùguāng shì yǒu zhòngliàng de. Zhè bú shì wùlǐ xué yìyì shàng de zhòngliàng, ér shì yī zhǒng shèhuì xìng de, qínggǎn xìng de yāqiáng. Zǒu zài jiē shàng, bèi yī gè mòshēng rén de mùguāng sǎo guò, nǐ huì gǎndào duǎnzàn de bú zìzai; zài huìyì shì lǐ, bèi suǒyǒu rén zhùshì de nà yī kè, nǐ huì juéde kōngqì dōu biàn de chóumì le. Mùguāng zhī suǒyǐ yǒu zhòngliàng, shì yīnwèi tā xiédài zhe pànduàn. Měi yī cì zhùshì dōu shì yī cì wúshēng de pínggū——nǐ de yīzhuó, nǐ de biǎoqíng, nǐ de shèhuì wèizhì, dōu zài bèi duìfāng de mùguāng kuàisù sǎomiáo bìng guīlèi. Shèhuì xué jiā shuō, chéngshì shēnghuó xùnliàn rénmen fāzhǎn chū yī zhǒng「lǐmào xìng hūshì」de jìnéng——zài gōnggòng kōngjiān lǐ, bǐcǐ xīnzhào-bùxuān de shōuhuí zhùshì, gěi mòshēng rén liú xià yī céng báobáo de yǐnsī píngzhàng. Dàn ǒu'ěr, zhè céng píngzhàng huì bèi dǎpò. Bǐrú zài dìtiě shàng, dāng nǐ wúyì zhōng yǔ yī gè mòshēng rén de mùguāng xiāngyù, shuāngfāng dōu huì lìkè yí kāi——zhè zhǒng tiáojiàn fǎnshè bān de huíbì, qiàqià bàolù le wǒmen duìyú bèi「kànjiàn」de shēncéng jiāolǜ. Rán'ér yě cúnzài lìng yī zhǒng mùguāng, tā de zhòngliàng shì wēnnuǎn de. Wǒ jìde xiǎo shíhou fāshāo wò chuáng, bàn mèng bàn xǐng jiān gǎnjué dào mǔqīn zuò zài chuáng biān kàn zhe wǒ. Nà zhǒng mùguāng méiyǒu rènhé yāoqiú, méiyǒu rènhé píngjuàn, zhǐshì ānjìng de fùgài zài wǒ shēn shàng, xiàng yī tiáo wúxíng de tǎnzi. Rújīn huíyì qǐlái, wǒ jīhū néng jīngquè de huányuán nà zhǒng mùguāng de wēndù——dàyuē sānshíqī dù, héngwēn de, bú huì tàng yě bú huì liáng. Shìjiè shàng zuì zhēnguì de mùguāng, dàgài jiùshì nà zhǒng bù xiédài rènhé mùdì, zhǐ chéngzài zhe chúncuì guānqiè de zhùshì ba.
text_en: Last Thursday on the subway, I made eye contact with a stranger for about two seconds. She was perhaps in her early thirties, wearing a dark gray coat, holding a book open to somewhere in the middle. The car was packed. We stood facing each other with three people between us. For some reason, at a certain instant our gazes simultaneously landed on each other's face. Two seconds. Then we both looked away. I looked at the route map above the door; she lowered her head and went back to her book. Nothing happened. But a great deal happened in those two seconds. The first second: identification. My visual system completed a high-speed scan in that second—gender, age, expression, threat assessment. Her system was probably running in parallel. The second second: judgment. This one was more complex—I noticed she was reading a novel in Spanish, with a line on the spine I could not understand. Her finger was wedged in around page one hundred and twenty. The pages had creases, meaning she did not use a bookmark. At the same time, I knew she was reading my information at the same speed—my clothes, my level of fatigue, the supermarket plastic bag in my hand. Two seconds ended. Social protocol activated: both parties simultaneously withdrew their gazes, restoring the default state of civil inattention. She got off at the next stop. Before the doors closed, she glanced back—not at me, but to check whether any seats had opened up in the car. Or maybe at me. I do not know. The doors closed. The train started. I looked down at the supermarket bag in my hand. Inside were two cucumbers and a carton of eggs.

questions.q_zh: 文中提到的「礼貌性忽视」指的是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the 'civil inattention' mentioned in the text refer to?

options.text: 对他人的事情完全不关心
options.pinyin: duì tārén de shìqíng wánquán bù guānxīn
options.text_en: Complete indifference to others' affairs

options.text: 公共空间中人们心照不宣地收回注视，为陌生人保留隐私
options.pinyin: gōnggòng kōngjiān zhōng rénmen xīnzhào-bùxuān de shōuhuí zhùshì, wèi mòshēng rén bǎoliú yǐnsī
options.text_en: In public spaces, people tacitly withdraw their gazes to preserve strangers' privacy

options.text: 假装没有看到别人的困难
options.pinyin: jiǎzhuāng méiyǒu kàn dào biéren de kùnnán
options.text_en: Pretending not to see others' difficulties

options.text: 城市人之间缺乏基本的礼貌
options.pinyin: chéngshì rén zhī jiān quēfá jīběn de lǐmào
options.text_en: A lack of basic politeness among city dwellers

questions.explanation: "restoring the default state of civil inattention" — the passage stakes its claim here; less precise readings miss it.

questions.q_zh: 作者用「三十七度恒温」来形容母亲的目光，想表达什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author want to express by describing the mother's gaze as 'constant thirty-seven degrees'?

options.text: 母亲发烧时体温正好是三十七度
options.pinyin: mǔqīn fāshāo shí tǐwēn zhènghǎo shì sānshíqī dù
options.text_en: The mother's temperature was exactly thirty-seven degrees when she had a fever

options.text: 母亲的关注像体温一样恒定、温和、不过分，给人最自然的温暖
options.pinyin: mǔqīn de guānzhù xiàng tǐwēn yīyàng héngdìng, wēnhé, bú guòfèn, gěi rén zuì zìrán de wēnnuǎn
options.text_en: The mother's attention is constant, gentle, and not excessive—like body temperature, giving the most natural warmth

options.text: 房间的温度正好是三十七度
options.pinyin: fángjiān de wēndù zhènghǎo shì sānshíqī dù
options.text_en: The room temperature was exactly thirty-seven degrees

options.text: 母亲的目光让他感到紧张和压力
options.pinyin: mǔqīn de mùguāng ràng tā gǎndào jǐnzhāng hé yālì
options.text_en: The mother's gaze made him feel nervous and pressured

questions.explanation: "This one was more complex" — concrete detail pins this down; the passage doesn't leave it abstract.

title: The Dialect of Hands
text_zh: 外婆是裁缝，做了五十二年衣服。她的双手已经无法完全伸直——长期握剪刀和缝纫的动作让她的手指保持了一种永久性的微弯。退休十五年了，那双手还是那个形状，仿佛随时准备拿起一把并不存在的剪刀。她的左手食指侧面有一道白色的细疤，是四十年前被剪刀划的。右手拇指和食指的指腹磨出了两块对称的硬茧，那是几万次捻针的结果。她的掌纹比同龄人深得多，线头和布料纤维在五十年间反复摩擦，把掌心的纹路刻成了沟壑。外婆量布料的时候不用尺子。她把布料展开，用手臂一拃一拃地量——从拇指尖到中指尖是她自己的一拃，大约二十二厘米。量了五十年，她的一拃比卷尺还准。给人量体的时候她也不用软尺。她用手在你肩膀上一搭、腰上一卡、胳膊上一划，数字就出来了。老客户都信她的手，不信软尺。最后几年她眼睛不好了，穿针要对着窗户，有时候对半天才穿进去。但她的手依然稳。线穿过针眼之后，她会把线头在指尖绕一圈、捻紧、咬断。这套动作她做了几十万次，快得像一个不需要思考的呼吸。外婆去世后整理遗物，在她缝纫箱的最底层找到一副老花镜。镜片上全是细小的划痕——那是针尖留下的。她一边戴着老花镜一边缝衣服，针偶尔会碰到镜片。她从来没跟任何人提过这件事。
text_pinyin: Měi gè rén de shǒu dōu shuō zhe yī zhǒng fāngyán. Bú shì yǔyán xué yìyì shàng de fāngyán, ér shì yī zhǒng tōngguò chángnián-lěiyuè de shǐyòng ér xíngchéng de dútè xíngtài yǔyán. Mùjiàng de shǒu zhǎngxīn yǒu yī céng hòu jiǎn, zhǐ guānjié cūdà, xiàng shùgēn yīyàng pánjù zài mùliào biǎomiàn; gāngqín jiā de shǒuzhǐ xiūcháng qiě línghuó, zhǐjiān yǒu yī zhǒng mǐngǎn de húdù, fǎngfú suíshí zhǔnbèi zài qínjiàn shàng qǐfēi; ér chángnián zuò zhēnxiàn huó de lǎorén de shǒu, zhǐ fù shàng mì bù zhe xìxiǎo de zhēnkǒng bāhén, nà shì yī bèizi de féngbǔ zài pífū shàng liú xià de wéixíng dìxíng tú. Wǒ wàipó de shǒu ràng wǒ zhìjīn nánwàng. Tā shì yī gè cáiféng, zuò le wǔshí duō nián de yīfu. Tā de shuāng shǒu yǐjīng wúfǎ wánquán shēn zhí——chángqī wò jiǎndāo hé féngrèn de dòngzuò ràng tā de shǒuzhǐ bǎochí le yī zhǒng yǒngjiǔ xìng de wēi wān. Tuìxiū duō nián hòu, tā de shǒu yīrán wéichí zhe nà zhǒng zītài, fǎngfú suíshí zhǔnbèi ná qǐ yī bǎ bìng bù cúnzài de jiǎndāo. Yīxué shàng zhè jiào zuò「zhíyè xìng jīròu jìyì」, dàn wǒ gèng yuànyì bǎ tā lǐjiě wéi yī zhǒng zhōngchéng——tā de shǒu duì bàn gè shìjì de gōngzuò rúcǐ zhōngchéng, yǐzhìyú jíbiàn gōngzuò jiéshù le, tāmen yīrán bǎochí zhe zhìjìng de zīshì. Zuì ràng wǒ chùdòng de shì, měi cì wàipó wò zhù wǒ de shǒu shí, nà wēi wān de shǒuzhǐ huì zìrán'ér'rán de tiēhé wǒ shǒuzhǎng de húdù, xiàng liàngtǐ-cáiyī yīyàng jīngquè. Wǒ hòulái cái yìshí dào, nà shì yī shuāng xíguàn le wèi biéren liàngshēn-dìngzuò dōngxi de shǒu——jíbiàn shì wòshǒu zhème jiǎndān de dòngzuò, tāmen yě huì zìdòng cèliáng bìng tiáozhěng dào zuì héshì de lìdù yǔ jiǎodù.
text_en: Grandmother was a tailor. She made clothes for fifty-two years. Her hands could no longer fully straighten—decades of gripping scissors and sewing had given her fingers a permanent slight curve. Fifteen years into retirement, those hands still held that shape, as if ready at any moment to pick up a pair of scissors that no longer existed. On the side of her left index finger was a thin white scar from a scissors cut forty years ago. On the pads of her right thumb and index finger, two symmetrical calluses had formed from tens of thousands of times threading needles. Her palm lines were far deeper than those of people her age—thread ends and fabric fibers rubbing back and forth over fifty years had carved the creases of her palms into grooves. When Grandmother measured fabric she did not use a ruler. She spread the cloth out and measured it span by span with her arm—from thumb tip to middle fingertip was her own span, about twenty-two centimeters. After fifty years of measuring, her span was more accurate than a tape measure. When measuring a person's body she did not use a soft tape either. She placed one hand on your shoulder, braced at your waist, drew a line down your arm, and the numbers were there. Longtime customers trusted her hands over any tape measure. In her final years her eyesight was failing. To thread a needle she had to hold it up to the window, sometimes trying for a long time before the thread went through. But her hands were still steady. After the thread passed through the eye of the needle she would loop the thread end once around her fingertip, twist it tight, and bite it off. She had performed this sequence hundreds of thousands of times, so fast it was like a breath that required no thought. After Grandmother passed away, while sorting through her belongings, we found a pair of reading glasses at the bottom of her sewing box. The lenses were covered in tiny scratches—left by needle tips.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么把外婆手指无法伸直理解为「忠诚」而非「职业病」？

options.text_en: Because 'loyalty' sounds better than 'occupational condition'

questions.explanation: "Her hands could no longer fully straighten" — negation reveals the reason: what is ruled out clarifies what drives the action.

questions.q_zh: 外婆握手时手指自动贴合手掌弧度，作者认为这说明了什么？

options.pinyin: tā de shǒu yǐjīng xíguàn le wèi tārén liàngshēn dìngzuò, lián wòshǒu dōu zì dài cèliáng hé tiáoshì de běnnéng

questions.explanation: "rubbing back and forth over fifty years" — the passage stakes its claim here; less precise readings miss it.

title: The Temperature of Letters
text_zh: 在电子邮件和即时通讯统治通信世界的今天，纸质书信几乎已经退化成了一种仪式性的文物。但正因为如此，它反而获得了一种数字时代难以企及的珍贵品质——温度。一封手写的信，从字迹的力度可以推测书写者的情绪状态：用力过猛的笔画可能意味着激动或愤怒，轻柔细腻的笔触则暗示着温情或犹豫。信纸上偶尔出现的墨水晕染，可能是写到某个段落时停下来思考太久，笔尖在纸面上留下的犹豫的痕迹。甚至信纸本身的选择——是素净的白纸还是带有花纹的信笺——也在无声地传递着写信人的审美与心境。我保留着一沓父亲年轻时写给母亲的信。信封上的邮戳记录了从寄出到收到之间的时间差——通常是三到五天。三到五天，在今天看来近乎于永恒。但正是这种延迟，赋予了每一封信一种沉甸甸的分量。因为你知道，对方在寄出这封信之后，需要在不确定中等待好几天，不知道你是否已经收到、不知道你读后是什么反应。这种不确定性催生了一种独特的真诚——当你不知道对方会不会立即回复时，你会更加认真地斟酌每一个字。如今我们可以在三秒钟内发送一条消息，但也正因为如此，我们对每一条消息的珍重程度，大概只有三秒钟那么多。速度杀死了郑重，即时性消灭了期待。那沓泛黄的旧信，每一封都像一颗琥珀，把某个瞬间的情感凝固在了纸纤维之间。
text_pinyin: Zài diànzǐ yóujiàn hé jíshí tōngxùn tǒngzhì tōngxìn shìjiè de jīntiān, zhǐzhì shūxìn jīhū yǐjīng tuìhuà chéng le yī zhǒng yíshì xìng de wénwù. Dàn zhèng yīnwèi rúcǐ, tā fǎn'ér huòdé le yī zhǒng shùzì shídài nányǐ qǐjí de zhēnguì pǐnzhì——wēndù. Yī fēng shǒuxiě de xìn, cóng zìjì de lìdù kěyǐ tuīcè shūxiě zhě de qíngxù zhuàngtài: yònglì guò měng de bǐhuà kěnéng yìwèi zhe jīdòng huò fènnù, qīngróu xìnì de bǐchù zé ànshì zhe wēnqíng huò yóuyù. Xìnzhǐ shàng ǒu'ěr chūxiàn de mòshuǐ yūnrǎn, kěnéng shì xiě dào mǒu gè duànluò shí tíng xiàlái sīkǎo tài jiǔ, bǐjiān zài zhǐmiàn shàng liú xià de yóuyù de hénjì. Shènzhì xìnzhǐ běnshēn de xuǎnzé——shì sùjìng de bái zhǐ háishi dài yǒu huāwén de xìnjiān——yě zài wúshēng de chuándì zhe xiě xìn rén de shěnměi yǔ xīnjìng. Wǒ bǎoliú zhe yī dá fùqīn niánqīng shí xiě gěi mǔqīn de xìn. Xìnfēng shàng de yóuchuō jìlù le cóng jì chū dào shōu dào zhī jiān de shíjiān chā——tōngcháng shì sān dào wǔ tiān. Sān dào wǔ tiān, zài jīntiān kàn lái jìnhū yú yǒnghéng. Dàn zhèng shì zhè zhǒng yánchí, fùyǔ le měi yī fēng xìn yī zhǒng chéndiàndiàn de fènliàng. Yīnwèi nǐ zhīdào, duìfāng zài jì chū zhè fēng xìn zhīhòu, xūyào zài bú quèdìng zhōng děngdài hǎo jǐ tiān, bù zhīdào nǐ shìfǒu yǐjīng shōu dào, bù zhīdào nǐ dú hòu shì shénme fǎnyìng. Zhè zhǒng bú quèdìng xìng cuīshēng le yī zhǒng dútè de zhēnchéng——dāng nǐ bù zhīdào duìfāng huì bú huì lìjí huífù shí, nǐ huì gèngjiā rènzhēn de zhēnzhuó měi yī gè zì. Rújīn wǒmen kěyǐ zài sān miǎo zhōng nèi fāsòng yī tiáo xiāoxi, dàn yě zhèng yīnwèi rúcǐ, wǒmen duì měi yī tiáo xiāoxi de zhēnzhòng chéngdù, dàgài zhǐyǒu sān miǎo zhōng nàme duō. Sùdù shā sǐ le zhèngzhòng, jíshí xìng xiāomiè le qīdài. Nà dá fànhuáng de jiù xìn, měi yī fēng dōu xiàng yī kē hǔpò, bǎ mǒu gè shùnjiān de qínggǎn nínggù zài le zhǐ xiānwéi zhī jiān.
text_en: In today's world dominated by email and instant messaging, paper letters have nearly devolved into a kind of ceremonial artifact. But precisely because of this, they have acquired a precious quality that the digital age can hardly match—temperature. From a handwritten letter, you can infer the writer's emotional state from the pressure of their strokes: overly forceful strokes might signify excitement or anger, while gentle, delicate brushwork hints at tenderness or hesitation. Occasional ink bleeds on the stationery might mark where the writer paused too long in thought at a certain passage, the pen tip leaving traces of hesitation on the paper. Even the choice of paper itself—plain white or patterned stationery—silently conveys the writer's aesthetic sense and state of mind. I've kept a stack of letters my father wrote to my mother when he was young. The postmarks on the envelopes record the time gap between sending and receiving—usually three to five days. Three to five days, which today seems close to eternity. But it was precisely this delay that gave each letter its substantial weight. Because you know that after sending this letter, the other person must wait several days in uncertainty, not knowing whether you've received it, not knowing your reaction after reading it. This uncertainty gave birth to a unique sincerity—when you don't know whether the other person will reply immediately, you deliberate more carefully over every word. Today we can send a message in three seconds, but precisely because of this, the weight we give each message is probably only about three seconds' worth. Speed has killed solemnity; immediacy has extinguished anticipation. Each letter in that stack of yellowed old correspondence is like a piece of amber, solidifying a moment's emotion between paper fibers.

options.pinyin: rénmen yīnggāi tíngzhǐ shǐyòng diànzǐ tōngxùn

options.text: 通讯的便捷化让人们不再珍惜每一次交流，等待中的期待感也随之消失
