title: The Metabolism of a Street
text_zh: 我住的那条巷子，每隔三五年就会换一次面孔。最早是一家卖豆浆油条的早餐铺，门口永远排着长队，蒸汽从锅里升起来的时候，整条巷子都裹在一层暖烘烘的雾气里。后来那家铺子关了，换成了一间精品咖啡馆，原来装豆浆的大桶被玻璃罐装的手冲咖啡豆取代了。再后来咖啡馆也撑不住了，变成了一家直播带货的仓库，每天晚上灯火通明，但白天大门紧闭。这种更替不是衰败，而是城市的新陈代谢。每一个倒下的店铺都像一个被代谢掉的细胞，它的消失不代表死亡，而是为新的功能腾出了空间。问题在于，新陈代谢的速度越来越快。过去一家店可以经营二三十年，如今能撑过三年就算长寿。街道的记忆因此变得稀薄——当一个地方每隔两年就彻底改头换面，住在这里的人便失去了建立情感锚点的可能。我偶尔会和巷口的老大爷聊天，他在这条巷子里住了四十多年，亲眼看着它从一条朴素的居民区变成了一个不断自我更新的消费场景。他说：「以前我能闭着眼睛在这条巷子里走，现在我睁着眼睛反而不认识路了。」城市的活力需要新陈代谢，但当代谢的速度快到让居民来不及与任何事物建立联系，街道就不再是家园，而变成了一条不断流动的河——你住在河边，但河水每天都是新的，你永远无法踏入同一条河流。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de nà tiáo xiàngzi, měi gé sān wǔ nián jiù huì huàn yī cì miànkǒng. Zuì zǎo shì yī jiā mài dòujiāng yóutiáo de zǎocān pù, ménkǒu yǒngyuǎn pái zhe cháng duì, zhēngqì cóng guō lǐ shēng qǐlái de shíhou, zhěng tiáo xiàngzi dōu guǒ zài yī céng nuǎn hōnghōng de wùqì lǐ. Hòulái nà jiā pùzi guān le, huàn chéng le yī jiān jīngpǐn kāfēi guǎn, yuánlái zhuāng dòujiāng de dà tǒng bèi bōli guàn zhuāng de shǒu chōng kāfēi dòu qǔdài le. Zài hòulái kāfēi guǎn yě chēng bú zhù le, biànchéng le yī jiā zhíbō dàihuò de cāngkù, měi tiān wǎnshang dēnghuǒ-tōngmíng, dàn báitiān dàmén jǐn bì. Zhè zhǒng gēngtì bú shì shuāibài, ér shì chéngshì de xīnchén-dàixiè. Měi yī gè dǎo xià de diànpù dōu xiàng yī gè bèi dàixiè diào de xìbāo, tā de xiāoshī bú dàibiǎo sǐwáng, ér shì wèi xīn de gōngnéng téng chū le kōngjiān. Wèntí zàiyú, xīnchén-dàixiè de sùdù yuèláiyuè kuài. Guòqù yī jiā diàn kěyǐ jīngyíng èr sānshí nián, rújīn néng chēng guò sān nián jiù suàn chángshòu. Jiēdào de jìyì yīncǐ biàn de xībáo——dāng yī gè dìfāng měi gé liǎng nián jiù chèdǐ gǎitóu-huànmiàn, zhù zài zhèlǐ de rén biàn shīqù le jiànlì qínggǎn máodiǎn de kěnéng. Wǒ ǒu'ěr huì hé xiàngkǒu de lǎo dàyé liáotiān, tā zài zhè tiáo xiàngzi lǐ zhù le sìshí duō nián, qīnyǎn kàn zhe tā cóng yī tiáo pǔsù de jūmínqū biànchéng le yī gè búduàn zìwǒ gēngxīn de xiāofèi chǎngjǐng. Tā shuō:「Yǐqián wǒ néng bì zhe yǎnjīng zài zhè tiáo xiàngzi lǐ zǒu, xiànzài wǒ zhēng zhe yǎnjīng fǎn'ér bú rènshi lù le.」Chéngshì de huólì xūyào xīnchén-dàixiè, dàn dāng dàixiè de sùdù kuài dào ràng jūmín lái bú jí yǔ rènhé shìwù jiànlì liánxì, jiēdào jiù bú zài shì jiāyuán, ér biànchéng le yī tiáo búduàn liúdòng de hé——nǐ zhù zài hé biān, dàn héshuǐ měi tiān dōu shì xīn de, nǐ yǒngyuǎn wúfǎ tà rù tóng yī tiáo héliú.
text_en: The alley where I live changes its face every three to five years. First there was a breakfast stall selling soy milk and fried dough sticks, with a perpetual queue at the door. When steam rose from the pot, the whole alley was wrapped in a warm haze. Later that stall closed and was replaced by a boutique coffee shop—the big vats that once held soy milk were replaced by glass jars of pour-over coffee beans. Then the coffee shop couldn't hold on either and became a livestream sales warehouse, blazing with lights every night but its doors shut tight during the day. This turnover is not decay but the city's metabolism. Every shop that falls is like a metabolized cell—its disappearance doesn't represent death but clears space for new functions. The problem is that the rate of metabolism keeps accelerating. In the past a shop could operate for twenty or thirty years; today, lasting three years counts as longevity. Street memory therefore becomes thin—when a place completely reinvents itself every two years, the people living here lose the possibility of establishing emotional anchor points. I occasionally chat with the old man at the mouth of the alley. He has lived in this alley for over forty years, watching it transform from a simple residential lane into a perpetually self-updating consumption scene. He says: 'Before, I could walk this alley with my eyes closed. Now I keep my eyes open and still don't recognize the road.' A city's vitality requires metabolism, but when metabolism speeds up so much that residents cannot form connections with anything, the street is no longer a home but becomes a constantly flowing river—you live on the riverbank, but the water is new every day, and you can never step into the same stream twice.

options.pinyin: chéngshì de shuǐ zīyuán hěn fēngfù
options.text_en: The city has abundant water resources

options.text: 当更新速度快到无法建立归属感时，城市就不再是家园而是永恒的过境之地
options.pinyin: dāng gēngxīn sùdù kuài dào wúfǎ jiànlì guīshǔ gǎn shí, chéngshì jiù bú zài shì jiāyuán ér shì yǒnghéng de guòjìng zhī dì

questions.explanation: "but the water is new every day" — this is where the passage shows its hand.

title: The Acoustics of a Neighborhood
text_zh: 一个老街区的声音，按时间排列。凌晨五点：环卫工的竹扫帚划过水泥地面。沙哑的、有节奏的，像给还在沉睡的街道做按摩。偶尔有一声咳嗽。五点四十：第一班公交车经过。发动机的低吼在空旷的街道上回荡很远。六点十分：早餐铺拉开卷帘门。金属碰撞金属，清脆、果断。油锅开始响。六点半到八点：早高峰。电动车喇叭声、自行车铃声、家长送孩子上学的叮嘱声、校门口执勤老师的哨子声。所有声音叠在一起，形成一面嗡嗡作响的墙。十点：中场休息。早高峰退去，午间还没到。能听见的只有空调外机的低频嗡鸣和远处某栋楼装修的电钻声。偶尔从某个窗口飘出电视节目的声音。十一点半：菜市场进入尾声。摊贩开始降价叫卖。「最后几把了啊！」「便宜卖了便宜卖了！」下午三点半：放学。笑声、叫喊声、篮球拍地声。声学频谱骤然升高。四点：麻将声从二楼某户人家的窗口传出来。噼啪噼啪，规律得像节拍器。傍晚六点：各家厨房同时运作。油锅滋啦声、切菜的笃笃声、抽油烟机的呼呼声。混合成一首关于「回家」的交响曲。晚上九点半：一切慢慢安静下来。最后消失的声音是某一家的电视，正在播新闻联播的片尾曲。
text_pinyin: Měi gè jiēqū dōu yǒu zìjǐ de shēngxué qiānmíng——yī tào yóu rìcháng zàoyīn biānzhī ér chéng de dútè shēngjǐng, xiàng zhǐwén yīyàng bùkě fùzhì. Wǒ zhù guò de nàge lǎo jiēqū, qīngchén de shēngxué zhǔ xuánlǜ shì niǎomíng yǔ sàozhou shēng de èr chóngzòu. Huánwèi gōngrén zài tiān hái méi liàng de shíhou jiù kāishǐ gōngzuò, zhú sàozhou huà guò shuǐní dìmiàn de shēngyīn yǒu yī zhǒng shāyǎ ér yǒu jiézòu de zhìgǎn, xiàng shì zài gěi jíjiāng sūxǐng de chéngshì zuò ànmó. Ránhòu shì zǎocān pù lā kāi juǎnlián mén de shēngxiǎng——jīnshǔ yǔ jīnshǔ pèngzhuàng, qīngcuì ér guǒduàn, xuāngào zhe yī tiān shāngyè huódòng de kāishǐ. Shàngwǔ shí diǎn zuǒyòu, jiēqū de shēngyīn jìnrù yī gè duǎnzàn de zhōngchǎng xiūxi. Zǎo gāofēng de xuānxiāo yǐjīng tuìqù, wǔjiān de rènao hái méiyǒu dàolái, zhěng gè jiēqū xiàn rù yī zhǒng dài yǒu juànyì de ānjìng. Zhège shíduàn néng tīng dào de, shì kōngtiáo wàijī de dīpín wēngmíng, yuǎnchù mǒu dòng lóu zhuāngxiū de diànzuàn shēng, yǐjí ǒu'ěr cóng mǒu gè chuāngkǒu piāo chūlái de diànshì jiémù shēng. Xiàwǔ sān sì diǎn, háizimen fàngxué le, jiēqū de shēngxué pínpǔ zhòurán shēnggāo. Xiàoshēng, jiàohǎn shēng, qiúpāi jī qiú shēng, xiàng yī qún xiǎoxíng de shēngbō zhàdàn zài xiàngdào jiān cǐqǐ-bǐfú de bàozhà. Dào le bàngwǎn, gè jiā chúfáng kāishǐ yùnzuò, yóu guō zīlā shēng, qiē cài de dūdū shēng, chōu yóuyān jī de hūhū shēng, hùnhé chéng yī shǒu guānyú「guī jiā」de jiāoxiǎng yuè. Zhèxiē shēngyīn zài bān zǒu zhīhòu wǒ cái zhēnzhèng yìshí dào tāmen de cúnzài. Xīn gōngyù tài ānjìng le——ānjìng dào wǒ fǎn'ér shīmián le. Wǒ zhè cái míngbái, suǒwèi「jiā」de gǎnjué, hěn dà yī bùfen qíshí shì yóu shēngyīn gòuchéng de. Nǐ yǐwéi nǐ xíguàn de shì yī gè kōngjiān, dàn nǐ zhēnzhèng yīlài de, shì nàge kōngjiān tèyǒu de shēngxué huánjìng.
text_en: The sounds of an old neighborhood, arranged by time. Five a.m.: a sanitation worker's bamboo broom scraping cement. Husky, rhythmic, like massaging a street still asleep. Occasionally a cough. Five forty: the first bus passes. The engine's low growl echoes far down the empty street. Six ten: a breakfast stall rolls up its metal shutter. Metal striking metal, crisp, decisive. The oil in the wok begins to sizzle. Six thirty to eight: morning rush. Electric scooter horns, bicycle bells, parents reminding children on their way to school, the whistle of the teacher on duty at the school gate. All sounds layered together forming a humming wall. Ten: intermission. The rush-hour clamor has retreated, the noon bustle has not yet arrived. All you can hear is the low-frequency hum of air conditioning units and the distant drill of a renovation somewhere. Occasionally the sound of a TV program drifts from a window. Eleven thirty: the wet market nears its end. Vendors start slashing prices. 'Last few bunches!' 'Selling cheap, selling cheap!' Three thirty p.m.: school lets out. Laughter, shouts, the thud of a basketball on pavement. The acoustic spectrum spikes. Four: the clatter of mahjong tiles drifts from a second-floor window. Click-clack, click-clack, regular as a metronome. Six p.m.: kitchens across the neighborhood fire up simultaneously. The sizzle of oil in woks, the rhythmic thunk of chopping, the whir of range hoods. Blending into a symphony of 'coming home.' Nine thirty p.m.: everything gradually quiets. The last sound to disappear is a television in one of the apartments, playing the closing theme of the evening news broadcast.

questions.q_en: What feeling does the metaphor of bamboo brooms 'massaging the city awake' convey?

options.text: 环卫工作很辛苦

options.text_en: Sanitation work is very hard

options.text: 清晨的清扫有一种温柔的、唤醒式的节奏感
options.pinyin: qīngchén de qīngsǎo yǒu yī zhǒng wēnróu de, huànxǐng shì de jiézòu gǎn
options.text_en: The morning sweeping has a gentle, awakening rhythmic quality

title: The Underground Library
text_zh: 地铁系统是一座城市最坦诚的自画像。它不像地面上的建筑那样可以被精心设计成展示给游客的门面，它是纯粹的功能性空间——为了把人从A点移动到B点而存在。正因如此，它暴露了一座城市最真实的质地。我在北京的地铁里看到过一个中年男人，利用换乘的七分钟站在通道里背英语单词。他的嘴唇微微翕动，目光紧盯着手机屏幕上的单词表，完全沉浸在自己微型的学习时空里。七分钟到了，他收起手机，快步走向站台，动作干净利落，显然这是一套经过无数次练习的程序。在上海的地铁里，我注意到一位老太太每天在同一节车厢、同一个位置坐下，从包里拿出一本厚厚的俄语小说继续阅读。她的书签是一片压干的银杏叶。一个月后我再坐同一班车，她已经换了一本书，但书签依然是那片银杏叶。在广州的地铁里，我见过一个年轻的母亲，单手抱着熟睡的婴儿，另一只手在手机上处理工作邮件。她的拇指在屏幕上飞速滑动，脸上的表情在「温柔」和「专业」之间无缝切换——每隔几秒低头看一眼孩子，确认他还在沉睡，然后继续打字。地铁是一座流动的图书馆，记录着城市居民最无防备的片刻。在这个拥挤的地下空间里，人们卸下了社交面具，展露出最真实的自己——疲惫的、专注的、温柔的、孤独的。
text_pinyin: Dìtiě xìtǒng shì yī zuò chéngshì zuì tǎnchéng de zìhuà xiàng. Tā bú xiàng dìmiàn shàng de jiànzhù nàyàng kěyǐ bèi jīngxīn shèjì chéng zhǎnshì gěi yóukè de ménmiàn, tā shì chúncuì de gōngnéng xìng kōngjiān——wèile bǎ rén cóng A diǎn yídòng dào B diǎn ér cúnzài. Zhèng yīncǐ, tā bàolù le yī zuò chéngshì zuì zhēnshí de zhìdì. Wǒ zài Běijīng de dìtiě lǐ kàn dào guò yī gè zhōngnián nánrén, lìyòng huànchéng de qī fēnzhōng zhàn zài tōngdào lǐ bèi Yīngyǔ dāncí. Tā de zuǐchún wēiwēi xīdòng, mùguāng jǐn dīng zhe shǒujī píngmù shàng de dāncí biǎo, wánquán chénjìn zài zìjǐ wēixíng de xuéxí shíkōng lǐ. Qī fēnzhōng dào le, tā shōu qǐ shǒujī, kuàibù zǒu xiàng zhàntái, dòngzuò gānjìng lìluo, xiǎnrán zhè shì yī tào jīngguò wúshù cì liànxí de chéngxù. Zài Shànghǎi de dìtiě lǐ, wǒ zhùyì dào yī wèi lǎo tàitai měi tiān zài tóng yī jié chēxiāng, tóng yī gè wèizhì zuò xià, cóng bāo lǐ ná chū yī běn hòuhòu de Éyǔ xiǎoshuō jìxù yuèdú. Tā de shūqiān shì yī piàn yā gān de yínxìng yè. Yī gè yuè hòu wǒ zài zuò tóng yī bān chē, tā yǐjīng huàn le yī běn shū, dàn shūqiān yīrán shì nà piàn yínxìng yè. Zài Guǎngzhōu de dìtiě lǐ, wǒ jiàn guò yī gè niánqīng de mǔqīn, dān shǒu bào zhe shúshuì de yīng'ér, lìng yī zhī shǒu zài shǒujī shàng chǔlǐ gōngzuò yóujiàn. Tā de mǔzhǐ zài píngmù shàng fēisù huádòng, liǎn shàng de biǎoqíng zài「wēnróu」hé「zhuānyè」zhī jiān wúfèng qiēhuàn——měi gé jǐ miǎo dītóu kàn yī yǎn háizi, quèrèn tā hái zài chénshuì, ránhòu jìxù dǎzì. Dìtiě shì yī zuò liúdòng de túshūguǎn, jìlù zhe chéngshì jūmín zuì wú fángbèi de piànkè. Zài zhège yōngjǐ de dìxià kōngjiān lǐ, rénmen xiè xià le shèjiāo miànjù, zhǎnlù chū zuì zhēnshí de zìjǐ——píbèi de, zhuānzhù de, wēnróu de, gūdú de.
text_en: A subway system is a city's most candid self-portrait. Unlike surface architecture, which can be carefully designed as a facade for tourists, it is a purely functional space—existing to move people from point A to point B. Precisely because of this, it reveals a city's truest texture. In Beijing's subway, I once saw a middle-aged man using a seven-minute transfer window to stand in the corridor memorizing English vocabulary. His lips moved slightly, his gaze locked on the word list on his phone screen, completely immersed in his own micro-universe of study. When the seven minutes were up, he pocketed his phone and walked briskly toward the platform, his movements clean and practiced—clearly a routine rehearsed countless times. In Shanghai's subway, I noticed an elderly woman who sat every day in the same car, in the same spot, taking out a thick Russian novel from her bag to continue reading. Her bookmark was a pressed ginkgo leaf. A month later when I rode the same train, she had changed books, but the bookmark was still that same ginkgo leaf. In Guangzhou's subway, I saw a young mother holding a sleeping baby with one arm while handling work emails on her phone with the other. Her thumb flew across the screen, her expression switching seamlessly between 'tender' and 'professional'—glancing down at the child every few seconds to confirm he was still asleep, then continuing to type. The subway is a mobile library, recording city residents' most unguarded moments. In this crowded underground space, people shed their social masks and reveal their most authentic selves—exhausted, focused, tender, solitary.

title: The Nocturnal Cartography
text_zh: 一座城市在夜间会变成另一座城市。白天的城市属于秩序——交通信号灯、上下班的人潮、写字楼里整齐排列的格子间。但当最后一班地铁驶离车站，当路灯把行道树的影子拉得又细又长，城市的另一张面孔就慢慢浮现出来了。凌晨两点的便利店是夜间城市的锚点。荧光灯管发出惨白的光，照亮了货架上色彩过于鲜艳的包装。来这里的人都有一种共同的气质——不是失眠就是加班，或者两者兼而有之。收银台后面的店员已经进入了一种半自动状态，扫码、报价、找零的动作行云流水，面无表情却精准无误。凌晨三点的出租车司机是城市的夜间叙事者。他们比任何人都更了解这座城市深夜的脉搏——哪条路在这个时间段最荒凉，哪个路口容易出事故，哪家医院的急诊室门口总有人徘徊。一位开了二十年夜班的老司机跟我说：「白天的城市是假的，夜里的才是真的。白天大家都在演，夜里演不动了，说的话就实在多了。」凌晨四点是城市最脆弱的时刻。夜班的人还没下班，早起的人还没出门，整座城市像一个正在换气的潜水员——上一口氧气快要用完，下一口还没吸进来。在这个短暂的真空里，你能听到城市自身的呼吸：管道里水流的声音、变压器的低频嗡鸣、远处某辆垃圾清运车的引擎声。这些声音白天完全被喧嚣淹没，只有在四点钟的寂静中才会浮出水面，像海底生物趁退潮的时候露出身体。
text_pinyin: Yī zuò chéngshì zài yèjiān huì biànchéng lìng yī zuò chéngshì. Báitiān de chéngshì shǔyú zhìxù——jiāotōng xìnhào dēng, shàngxiàbān de réncháo, xiězì lóu lǐ zhěngqí páiliè de gézi jiān. Dàn dāng zuìhòu yī bān dìtiě shǐ lí chēzhàn, dāng lùdēng bǎ xíngdào shù de yǐngzi lā de yòu xì yòu cháng, chéngshì de lìng yī zhāng miànkǒng jiù mànmàn fúxiàn chūlái le. Língchén liǎng diǎn de biànlì diàn shì yèjiān chéngshì de máodiǎn. Yíngguāng dēngguǎn fāchū cǎnbái de guāng, zhào liàng le huòjià shàng sècǎi guòyú xiānyàn de bāozhuāng. Lái zhèlǐ de rén dōu yǒu yī zhǒng gòngtóng de qìzhì——bú shì shīmián jiùshì jiābān, huòzhě liǎng zhě jiān ér yǒu zhī. Shōuyín tái hòumiàn de diànyuán yǐjīng jìnrù le yī zhǒng bàn zìdòng zhuàngtài, sǎomǎ, bàojià, zhǎolíng de dòngzuò xíngyún-liúshuǐ, miàn wú biǎoqíng què jīngzhǔn wúwù. Língchén sān diǎn de chūzūchē sījī shì chéngshì de yèjiān xùshì zhě. Tāmen bǐ rènhé rén dōu gèng liǎojiě zhè zuò chéngshì shēnyè de màibó——nǎ tiáo lù zài zhège shíjiān duàn zuì huāngliáng, nǎge lùkǒu róngyì chū shìgù, nǎ jiā yīyuàn de jízhěn shì ménkǒu zǒng yǒu rén páihuái. Yī wèi kāi le èrshí nián yèbān de lǎo sījī gēn wǒ shuō:「Báitiān de chéngshì shì jiǎ de, yèlǐ de cái shì zhēn de. Báitiān dàjiā dōu zài yǎn, yèlǐ yǎn bú dòng le, shuō de huà jiù shízài duō le.」Língchén sì diǎn shì chéngshì zuì cuìruò de shíkè. Yèbān de rén hái méi xiàbān, zǎoqǐ de rén hái méi chūmén, zhěng zuò chéngshì xiàng yī gè zhèngzài huànqì de qiánshuǐ yuán——shàng yī kǒu yǎngqì kuàiyào yòng wán, xià yī kǒu hái méi xī jìnlái. Zài zhège duǎnzàn de zhēnkōng lǐ, nǐ néng tīng dào chéngshì zìshēn de hūxī: guǎndào lǐ shuǐliú de shēngyīn, biànyāqì de dīpín wēngmíng, yuǎnchù mǒu liàng lājī qīngyùn chē de yǐnqíng shēng. Zhèxiē shēngyīn báitiān wánquán bèi xuānxiāo yānmò, zhǐyǒu zài sì diǎn zhōng de jìjìng zhōng cái huì fú chū shuǐmiàn, xiàng hǎidǐ shēngwù chèn tuìcháo de shíhou lùchū shēntǐ.
text_en: A city at night becomes another city entirely. The daytime city belongs to order—traffic lights, commuting crowds, cubicles arrayed in neat rows inside office buildings. But when the last subway pulls out of the station and streetlamps stretch tree shadows thin and long, the city's other face slowly emerges. The convenience store at two in the morning is the nighttime city's anchor point. Fluorescent tubes emit a pallid light, illuminating packaging on shelves whose colors are too vivid. Everyone who comes here shares a common quality—either insomnia or overtime, or both. The clerk behind the register has entered a semi-automatic state, scanning, quoting prices, making change with fluid motions, expressionless yet precisely accurate. The taxi driver at three in the morning is the city's nocturnal narrator. They understand the city's late-night pulse better than anyone—which road is most desolate at this hour, which intersection is prone to accidents, which hospital's emergency room entrance always has people lingering. A veteran driver who had worked night shifts for twenty years told me: 'The daytime city is fake; the nighttime one is real. During the day everyone's performing. At night they can't keep it up anymore, and what they say becomes much more genuine.' Four in the morning is the city's most vulnerable moment. Night-shift workers haven't clocked out yet; early risers haven't left home. The entire city is like a diver between breaths—the last gulp of oxygen nearly used up, the next not yet inhaled. In this brief vacuum, you can hear the city's own breathing: water flowing through pipes, the low-frequency hum of transformers, the engine of a distant garbage truck. These sounds are completely drowned out by daytime clamor; only in the silence of four o'clock do they surface, like deep-sea creatures showing themselves when the tide retreats.

title: The Patience of a Bridge
text_zh: 那座老石桥横跨一条并不宽阔的河，我每天上下班都走。石栏杆已经被无数双手摩挲得光滑如镜，有些位置甚至被磨出了凹陷。上个月的一个傍晚，我照常走到桥中央，把手搭在栏杆上。手指正好嵌入了一个光滑的凹痕——不知道是谁的手掌，在不知道多少年里，反复停留在这个位置，把石头磨成了这个形状。那天有一个老人也停在桥上，白头发，穿灰色夹克，站在我旁边大概两米的地方。他也把手放在栏杆上，看着水面。我们没有说话。站了大概五分钟，他忽然开口：「这个栏杆，我摸了四十年了。」我转头看他。他没看我，继续说：「我1986年搬到这边来的。那时候这条河还臭，夏天走过桥要捂鼻子。后来治理了，水变清了，桥也修过两次。但这个栏杆没换。」他拍了拍栏杆：「你摸的那个坑，有一部分是我磨出来的。」我低头看了看自己手下的那个凹痕。它已经很深了，光滑得像一个小碗。四十年。这个人每天走过这座桥，每天把手放在同一个位置。他的手掌和石头之间进行了几万次的接触，每一次都带走了一点点石头的表面。他说：「我退休以后没什么事，有时候就来桥上站一会儿。看看水。」说完他拍了拍栏杆，转身走了。我也走了。第二天我又从桥上经过，手又搭在了那个凹痕上。石头的温度和前一天一样。
text_pinyin: Chéngshì lǐ de qiáoliáng shì zuì bèi dī gū de gōnggòng jiànzhù. Rénmen zǒu guò tā, kāichē niǎn guò tā, zài tā shàngmiàn pāizhào liúniàn, dàn hěn shǎo yǒu rén zhēnzhèng zhùyì dào tā běnshēn. Qiáo shì yī zhǒng yǐnxíng de cúnzài——tā de chénggōng qiàqià zàiyú nǐ yìshí bú dào tā de cúnzài. Dāng nǐ zǒu zài qiáo shàng de shíhou, nǐ kàn de shì liǎng àn de fēngjǐng, shuǐmiàn shàng de dàoyǐng, tiānjì xiàn de lúnkuò. Qiáo běnshēn zhǐshì yī gè guòdù, yī gè jiè yú「zhè biān」hé「nà biān」zhī jiān de zhōngjiān dìdài. Dàn rúguǒ nǐ yuànyì tíng xiàlái zǐxì guānchá, qiáo qíshí shì chéngshì lǐ zuì nàixīn de qīngtīng zhě. Tā chéngshòu zhe měi yī liàng jīngguò de chē de zhòngliàng, xīshōu zhe měi yī gè xíngrén jiǎobù de zhèndòng, mòmò rěnshòu zhe rìshài-yǔlín, fēngchuī-shuāngdòng. Yī zuò jiànchéng wǔshí nián de qiáo, yǐjīng jīnglì le shù shí wàn dūn de lěijī zàihè, dàn tā yīrán zhàn zài nàlǐ, xiàng yī gè cóng bù bàoyuàn de lǎo púrén. Wǒ cháng zǒu de nà zuò lǎo shí qiáo héngkuà yī tiáo bìng bù kuānkuò de hé. Qiáo shēn de shí lángān yǐjīng bèi wúshù shuāng shǒu mōsuō de guānghuá rú jìng. Yǒuxiē wèizhì shènzhì bèi mó chū le āoxiàn——nà shì jǐ dài rén shǒuzhǎng de lúnkuò diéjiā zài yīqǐ de jiéguǒ. Měi cì wǒ zǒu guò nà zuò qiáo, bǎ shǒu dā zài shí lángān shàng, zǒng juéde zìjǐ de shǒu zhènghǎo qiàn rù le mǒu gè qiánrén liú xià de zhǎngyìn lǐ, fǎngfú zài jìnxíng yī cì kuàyuè shíjiān de wòshǒu. Qiáo liánjiē de bù jǐn shì liǎng àn, yě liánjiē zhe cǐkè yǔ guòqù. Dāng nǐ de shǒuzhǐ chùpèng dào bèi qiánrén mó guāng de shí miàn shí, nǐ jiù yǔ suǒyǒu zǒu guò zhè zuò qiáo de rén jiànlì le yī zhǒng wúshēng de liánxì——jǐnguǎn nǐmen sù wèi móumiàn, dàn nǐmen céngjīng bǎ shǒu fàng zài le tóng yī gè wèizhì, wàng xiàng le tóng yī piàn shuǐmiàn, tīng guò tóng yī zhǒng liúshuǐ de shēngyīn.
text_en: The old stone bridge spans a river that is not particularly wide. I cross it every day commuting to and from work. The stone railings have been rubbed mirror-smooth by countless hands, some spots even worn into concavities. One evening last month, I walked to the middle of the bridge as usual and placed my hand on the railing. My fingers settled right into a smooth indentation—worn into this shape by someone's palm, resting repeatedly in this exact spot over who knows how many years. That day an old man was also standing on the bridge. White hair, gray jacket, about two meters from me. He too had his hand on the railing, watching the water. We did not speak. After about five minutes he suddenly said: 'This railing—I have been touching it for forty years.' I turned to look at him. He was not looking at me. He continued: 'I moved to this area in 1986. Back then this river still stank. In summer you had to hold your nose crossing the bridge. Later they cleaned it up, the water cleared, and the bridge was repaired twice. But this railing was never replaced.' He patted the railing: 'That depression you are touching—part of it was worn down by me.' I looked down at the indentation under my hand. It was already quite deep, smooth like a small bowl. Forty years. This man crossing this bridge every day, placing his hand in the same spot every day. Tens of thousands of contacts between his palm and the stone, each one carrying away a tiny bit of the stone's surface. He said: 'After I retired, not much to do. Sometimes I just come stand on the bridge for a while. Watch the water.' Then he patted the railing, turned, and walked away. I left too. The next day I crossed the bridge again, my hand settling into that same indentation. The temperature of the stone was the same as the day before.

title: The Archaeology of Walls
text_zh: 老城区的墙壁是城市记忆的断面。如果你仔细观察一面经历了几十年风雨的外墙，你会发现它上面层层叠叠地记录着不同年代的痕迹。最底层可能是某个年代的标语，红色大字已经褪色成了淡粉色，但依稀可辨。上面覆盖着一层八十年代的白灰，白灰又被九十年代的商业广告覆盖——某家已经倒闭的饭店、某个早已停产的电器品牌。再上面是新世纪初贴上去的瓷砖，有些已经脱落，露出下面的历史地层。这面墙像一本被反复书写的羊皮卷——每一个时代都在上面留下自己的文字，但从未完全抹去前一个时代的痕迹。城市规划者们总想把旧墙推倒重建，用统一的风格取代杂乱的历史积层。但这种「整洁」的代价是记忆的清零。当一面承载了五十年历史的墙被一块崭新的铝塑板覆盖，消失的不仅是几层油漆和灰泥，而是一个社区与自身过去的物质连接。我认识一位老居民，她能对着自家门口的一面旧墙讲出二十分钟的故事——哪个年代刷了什么颜色、邻居的孩子曾在这里画过什么涂鸦、某年暴雨后这面墙渗水留下的水痕至今还在。对她来说，这面墙不是需要翻新的老旧建材，而是她人生故事的物质载体。拆掉这面墙，等于删掉了她记忆的一个章节。
text_pinyin: Lǎo chéngqū de qiángbì shì chéngshì jìyì de duànmiàn. Rúguǒ nǐ zǐxì guānchá yī miàn jīnglì le jǐ shí nián fēngyǔ de wàiqiáng, nǐ huì fāxiàn tā shàngmiàn céngcéng-diédié de jìlù zhe bùtóng niándài de hénjì. Zuì dǐcéng kěnéng shì mǒu gè niándài de biāoyǔ, hóngsè dà zì yǐjīng tuìsè chéng le dàn fěnsè, dàn yīxī kě biàn. Shàngmiàn fùgài zhe yī céng bāshí niándài de báihuī, báihuī yòu bèi jiǔshí niándài de shāngyè guǎnggào fùgài——mǒu jiā yǐjīng dǎobì de fàndiàn, mǒu gè zǎoyǐ tíngchǎn de diànqì pǐnpái. Zài shàngmiàn shì xīn shìjì chū tiē shàngqù de cízhuān, yǒuxiē yǐjīng tuōluò, lùchū xiàmiàn de lìshǐ dìcéng. Zhè miàn qiáng xiàng yī běn bèi fǎnfù shūxiě de yángpí juǎn——měi yī gè shídài dōu zài shàngmiàn liú xià zìjǐ de wénzì, dàn cóng wèi wánquán mǒ qù qián yī gè shídài de hénjì. Chéngshì guīhuà zhě men zǒng xiǎng bǎ jiù qiáng tuīdǎo chóngjiàn, yòng tǒngyī de fēnggé qǔdài záluàn de lìshǐ jīcéng. Dàn zhè zhǒng「zhěngjié」de dàijià shì jìyì de qīnglíng. Dāng yī miàn chéngzài le wǔshí nián lìshǐ de qiáng bèi yī kuài zhǎnxīn de lǚ sù bǎn fùgài, xiāoshī de bù jǐn shì jǐ céng yóuqī hé huīní, ér shì yī gè shèqū yǔ zìshēn guòqù de wùzhì liánjiē. Wǒ rènshi yī wèi lǎo jūmín, tā néng duì zhe zìjiā ménkǒu de yī miàn jiù qiáng jiǎng chū èrshí fēnzhōng de gùshi——nǎge niándài shuā le shénme yánsè, línjū de háizi céng zài zhèlǐ huà guò shénme túyā, mǒu nián bàoyǔ hòu zhè miàn qiáng shènshuǐ liú xià de shuǐhén zhìjīn hái zài. Duì tā lái shuō, zhè miàn qiáng bú shì xūyào fānxīn de lǎojiù jiàncái, ér shì tā rénshēng gùshi de wùzhì zàitǐ. Chāi diào zhè miàn qiáng, děngyú shān diào le tā jìyì de yī gè zhāngjié.
text_en: The walls of an old city district are cross-sections of urban memory. If you carefully observe an exterior wall that has weathered decades of wind and rain, you'll find it records traces of different eras in accumulated layers. The bottommost layer might be a slogan from some bygone era, its red characters faded to pale pink but still faintly discernible. Over it lies a coat of whitewash from the eighties, which in turn was covered by nineties commercial advertisements—a restaurant long since closed, an electronics brand long out of production. Above that are tiles affixed in the early new century, some already fallen off, exposing the historical strata beneath. This wall is like a palimpsest—every era leaves its own writing on it, but never completely erases the traces of the previous one. Urban planners always want to tear down old walls and rebuild, replacing the messy historical layers with a unified style. But the cost of this 'neatness' is the zeroing out of memory. When a wall bearing fifty years of history is covered by a brand-new aluminum composite panel, what vanishes is not merely a few coats of paint and plaster, but a community's material connection to its own past. I know a longtime resident who can tell twenty minutes of stories while facing an old wall outside her front door—what color it was painted in which decade, what graffiti the neighbors' children once drew here, the water stains still visible from when the wall leaked after a rainstorm one year. For her, this wall is not outdated building material in need of renovation; it is the material vessel of her life story. Tearing down this wall would be like deleting a chapter of her memory.

questions.explanation: Tone condenses at "rebuild, replacing the messy historical layers" — the author lets image do the evaluative work.

title: The Democracy of Rain
text_zh: 雨是城市里最民主的现象。它不区分富人区和贫民窟，不关心你住的是别墅还是筒子楼。它落在金融中心的玻璃幕墙上，也落在城中村的铁皮屋顶上，以完全相同的角度和力度。但雨的民主只停留在降落的那一刻。一旦落地，它的命运立刻被城市的阶层结构所决定。在高档社区，雨水被精心设计的排水系统迅速吞没，路面几乎看不到积水。在老旧的棚户区，雨水无处可去，漫过破损的路面，灌进低洼处的住宅。同样的雨，落在不同的城市肌理上，产生了截然不同的后果。一场暴雨，对于住在高层公寓的人来说，不过是窗外一幅水墨画般的景致，配上一杯热茶就是一个惬意的下午。但对于住在地下室的人来说，同一场雨可能意味着一夜不眠的排水作业。雨暴露了城市最不愿意承认的真相：基础设施的质量，是用来丈量社会公平的最精确的标尺。当一座城市的不同区域对同一场雨有着天壤之别的抵御能力时，所谓「人人平等」不过是一句客套话。雨后的城市，积水退去的速度画出了一幅隐形的社会地图——水退得最快的地方，往往就是权力与资源集中的地方。
text_pinyin: Yǔ shì chéngshì lǐ zuì mínzhǔ de xiànxiàng. Tā bù qūfēn fùrén qū hé pínmín kū, bù guānxīn nǐ zhù de shì biéshù háishi tǒngzi lóu. Tā luò zài jīnróng zhōngxīn de bōli mùqiáng shàng, yě luò zài chéngzhōngcūn de tiěpí wūdǐng shàng, yǐ wánquán xiāngtóng de jiǎodù hé lìdù. Dàn yǔ de mínzhǔ zhǐ tíngliú zài jiàngluò de nà yī kè. Yīdàn luòdì, tā de mìngyùn lìkè bèi chéngshì de jiēcéng jiégòu suǒ juédìng. Zài gāodàng shèqū, yǔshuǐ bèi jīngxīn shèjì de páishuǐ xìtǒng xùnsù tūnmò, lùmiàn jīhū kàn bú dào jīshuǐ. Zài lǎojiù de pénghu qū, yǔshuǐ wú chù kě qù, màn guò pòsǔn de lùmiàn, guàn jìn dīwā chù de zhùzhái. Tóngyàng de yǔ, luò zài bùtóng de chéngshì jīlǐ shàng, chǎnshēng le jiérán bùtóng de hòuguǒ. Yī chǎng bàoyǔ, duìyú zhù zài gāocéng gōngyù de rén lái shuō, búguò shì chuāng wài yī fú shuǐmòhuà bān de jǐngzhì, pèi shàng yī bēi rè chá jiùshì yī gè qièyì de xiàwǔ. Dàn duìyú zhù zài dìxiàshì de rén lái shuō, tóng yī chǎng yǔ kěnéng yìwèi zhe yī yè bù mián de páishuǐ zuòyè. Yǔ bàolù le chéngshì zuì bú yuànyì chéngrèn de zhēnxiàng: jīchǔ shèshī de zhìliàng, shì yòng lái zhàngliang shèhuì gōngpíng de zuì jīngquè de biāochǐ. Dāng yī zuò chéngshì de bùtóng qūyù duì tóng yī chǎng yǔ yǒu zhe tiānrǎng zhī bié de dǐyù nénglì shí, suǒwèi「rénrén píngděng」búguò shì yī jù kètào huà. Yǔ hòu de chéngshì, jīshuǐ tuìqù de sùdù huà chū le yī fú yǐnxíng de shèhuì dìtú——shuǐ tuì de zuì kuài de dìfāng, wǎngwǎng jiùshì quánlì yǔ zīyuán jízhōng de dìfāng.
text_en: Rain is the most democratic phenomenon in a city. It doesn't distinguish between wealthy neighborhoods and slums, doesn't care whether you live in a villa or a tenement. It falls on the glass curtain walls of financial centers and on the corrugated metal roofs of urban villages with exactly the same angle and force. But rain's democracy lasts only in the moment of falling. Once it lands, its fate is immediately determined by the city's class structure. In upscale communities, rainwater is swiftly swallowed by meticulously engineered drainage systems, with barely any puddles visible on the roads. In dilapidated shanty areas, rainwater has nowhere to go, overflowing damaged road surfaces and flooding into low-lying residences. The same rain, falling on different urban fabrics, produces utterly different consequences. A rainstorm, for someone living in a high-rise apartment, is merely an ink-painting-like scene outside the window, paired with a cup of hot tea for a pleasant afternoon. But for someone living in a basement, the same storm might mean an all-night water-pumping operation. Rain exposes the truth cities are most reluctant to admit: the quality of infrastructure is the most precise ruler for measuring social equity. When different areas of a city have vastly different capacities to withstand the same rain, so-called 'equality for all' is nothing more than a pleasantry. After the rain, the speed at which standing water recedes draws an invisible social map—the places where water retreats fastest are usually where power and resources are concentrated.

questions.q_zh: 作者说「雨的民主只停留在降落的那一刻」，这是什么意思？
questions.q_en: What does the author mean by 'rain's democracy lasts only in the moment of falling'?

options.text: 雨在下落过程中是均匀的
options.pinyin: yǔ zài xiàluò guòchéng zhōng shì jūnyún de
options.text_en: Rain is uniform during the falling process

options.text: 雨对所有地方一视同仁地降落，但落地后因基础设施差异而产生完全不同的后果
options.pinyin: yǔ duì suǒyǒu dìfāng yīshì-tóngrén de jiàngluò, dàn luòdì hòu yīn jīchǔ shèshī chāyì ér chǎnshēng wánquán bùtóng de hòuguǒ
options.text_en: Rain falls impartially on all places, but after landing produces completely different consequences due to infrastructure disparities

options.text: 雨停了之后就不再公平了
options.pinyin: yǔ tíng le zhīhòu jiù bú zài gōngpíng le
options.text_en: Rain is no longer fair after it stops

options.text: 只有下雨的时候人们才是平等的
options.pinyin: zhǐyǒu xiàyǔ de shíhou rénmen cái shì píngděng de
options.text_en: People are equal only when it rains

questions.explanation: "in the moment of falling" — the passage stakes its claim here; less precise readings miss it.

questions.q_zh: 文末「积水退去的速度画出了一幅隐形的社会地图」表达了什么核心思想？
questions.q_en: What core idea does the final line about receding water drawing an invisible social map express?

options.text: 城市需要更好的排水系统
options.pinyin: chéngshì xūyào gèng hǎo de páishuǐ xìtǒng
options.text_en: Cities need better drainage systems

options.text: 基础设施的质量直接映射了社会资源和权力的分配不均
options.pinyin: jīchǔ shèshī de zhìliàng zhíjiē yìngshè le shèhuì zīyuán hé quánlì de fēnpèi bù jūn
options.text_en: The quality of infrastructure directly maps the uneven distribution of social resources and power

options.text: 下雨后应该尽快清理积水
options.pinyin: xiàyǔ hòu yīnggāi jǐnkuài qīnglǐ jīshuǐ
options.text_en: Standing water should be cleared quickly after rain

options.text: 富人区的地势比较高
options.pinyin: fùrén qū de dìshì bǐjiào gāo
options.text_en: Wealthy areas are at higher elevations

questions.explanation: "map—the places where water retreats fastest are" — concrete detail pins this down; the passage doesn't leave it abstract.

title: The Language of Neon
text_zh: 霓虹灯正在从城市的视觉词汇中消失，被LED屏幕和数字广告牌悄然取代。但两者的质感截然不同。LED屏幕发出的光是均匀的、扁平的、高清的，它追求的是信息的精确传达——每一个像素都被严格控制，不允许有任何模糊地带。而霓虹灯的光是温暖的、弥散的、带有轻微颤动的，它周围总是笼罩着一圈柔和的光晕，像一个词语说出口之后在空气中慢慢扩散的余韵。我小时候家附近有一条商业街，两侧密密麻麻地挂满了霓虹灯招牌。红色、绿色、蓝色、紫色的光交织在一起，把整条街映照得如梦似幻。那些灯并不是同时亮起的——有些闪烁、有些恒亮、有些若明若暗，形成了一种无序却和谐的视觉节奏。雨夜是霓虹灯最动人的时刻。湿漉漉的路面变成了一面巨大的镜子，把头顶的灯光加倍反射回来。走在那样的街上，你仿佛同时走在两个世界里——抬头是真实的霓虹，低头是倒映的霓虹，而你自己则悬浮在两者之间。如今那条街已经完成了「改造升级」，所有霓虹灯都被换成了统一规格的LED灯箱。街道更亮了、更整洁了、更现代了，但同时也更冰冷了。那种弥散的、有温度的、会呼吸的光消失了，取而代之的是一种工业化的、无菌的明亮。有时候我会想：我们是不是在追求效率和清晰度的过程中，把城市的诗意也一并清除掉了？
text_pinyin: Nóhóng dēng zhèngzài cóng chéngshì de shìjué cíhuì zhōng xiāoshī, bèi LED píngmù hé shùzì guǎnggào pái qiǎorán qǔdài. Dàn liǎng zhě de zhìgǎn jiérán bùtóng. LED píngmù fāchū de guāng shì jūnyún de, biǎnpíng de, gāoqīng de, tā zhuīqiú de shì xìnxī de jīngquè chuándá——měi yī gè xiàngsù dōu bèi yángé kòngzhì, bù yǔnxǔ yǒu rènhé móhu dìdài. Ér nóhóng dēng de guāng shì wēnnuǎn de, mísàn de, dài yǒu qīngwéi chàndòng de, tā zhōuwéi zǒng shì lǒngzhào zhe yī quān róuhé de guāngyùn, xiàng yī gè cíyǔ shuō chūkǒu zhīhòu zài kōngqì zhōng mànmàn kuòsàn de yúyùn. Wǒ xiǎo shíhou jiā fùjìn yǒu yī tiáo shāngyè jiē, liǎng cè mìmìmámá de guà mǎn le nóhóng dēng zhāopai. Hóngsè, lǜsè, lánsè, zǐsè de guāng jiāozhī zài yīqǐ, bǎ zhěng tiáo jiē yìngzhào de rú mèng sì huàn. Nàxiē dēng bìng bú shì tóngshí liàng qǐ de——yǒuxiē shǎnshuò, yǒuxiē héng liàng, yǒuxiē ruò míng ruò àn, xíngchéng le yī zhǒng wúxù què héxié de shìjué jiézòu. Yǔ yè shì nóhóng dēng zuì dòngrén de shíkè. Shīlūlū de lùmiàn biànchéng le yī miàn jùdà de jìngzi, bǎ tóudǐng de dēngguāng jiābèi fǎnshè huílái. Zǒu zài nàyàng de jiē shàng, nǐ fǎngfú tóngshí zǒu zài liǎng gè shìjiè lǐ——táitóu shì zhēnshí de nóhóng, dītóu shì dàoyìng de nóhóng, ér nǐ zìjǐ zé xuánfú zài liǎng zhě zhī jiān. Rújīn nà tiáo jiē yǐjīng wánchéng le「gǎizào shēngjí」, suǒyǒu nóhóng dēng dōu bèi huàn chéng le tǒngyī guīgé de LED dēngxiāng. Jiēdào gèng liàng le, gèng zhěngjié le, gèng xiàndài le, dàn tóngshí yě gèng bīnglěng le. Nà zhǒng mísàn de, yǒu wēndù de, huì hūxī de guāng xiāoshī le, qǔ ér dài zhī de shì yī zhǒng gōngyèhuà de, wújūn de míngliàng. Yǒu shíhou wǒ huì xiǎng: wǒmen shì bú shì zài zhuīqiú xiàolǜ hé qīngxī dù de guòchéng zhōng, bǎ chéngshì de shīyì yě yībìng qīngchú diào le?
text_en: Neon lights are vanishing from the city's visual vocabulary, quietly replaced by LED screens and digital billboards. But the texture of the two is utterly different. The light from LED screens is even, flat, and high-definition, pursuing precise information transmission—every pixel strictly controlled, no blurriness permitted. Neon light, however, is warm, diffuse, with a slight tremor, always enveloped in a soft halo, like the afterglow of a spoken word slowly spreading through the air. Near my childhood home there was a commercial street, both sides densely hung with neon signs. Red, green, blue, and purple light intertwined, casting the entire street in a dreamlike glow. The lights didn't all illuminate simultaneously—some flickered, some glowed steadily, some dimmed and brightened—forming a disordered yet harmonious visual rhythm. Rainy nights were neon's most moving moments. The wet road surface became a giant mirror, reflecting the overhead lights back doubled. Walking on such a street, you seemed to walk in two worlds at once—look up and see real neon, look down and see reflected neon, while you yourself floated between the two. Now that street has completed its 'renovation upgrade,' and all neon has been replaced by standardized LED light boxes. The street is brighter, neater, more modern, but also colder. That diffuse, warm, breathing light has vanished, replaced by an industrialized, sterile brightness.

questions.q_zh: 作者把霓虹灯的光比作「词语的余韵」，想表达什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author want to express by comparing neon light to 'the afterglow of a spoken word'?

options.text: 霓虹灯的广告效果比LED差
options.pinyin: nóhóng dēng de guǎnggào xiàoguǒ bǐ LED chà
options.text_en: Neon's advertising effect is worse than LED

options.text: 霓虹灯的光线有一种弥散的、有韵味的、富有生命感的质感
options.pinyin: nóhóng dēng de guāngxiàn yǒu yī zhǒng mísàn de, yǒu yùnwèi de, fù yǒu shēngmìng gǎn de zhìgǎn
options.text_en: Neon light has a diffuse, resonant, living texture

options.text: 霓虹灯会发出声音
options.pinyin: nóhóng dēng huì fāchū shēngyīn
options.text_en: Neon lights make sounds

options.text: 霓虹灯可以传递语言信息
options.pinyin: nóhóng dēng kěyǐ chuándì yǔyán xìnxī
options.text_en: Neon lights can transmit linguistic information

questions.explanation: "always enveloped in a soft halo" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

questions.q_zh: 文末作者提出的反思是什么？
questions.q_en: What reflection does the author raise at the end?

options.text: LED灯应该被禁止使用
options.pinyin: LED dēng yīnggāi bèi jìnzhǐ shǐyòng
options.text_en: LED lights should be banned

options.text: 城市应该回到过去的样子
options.pinyin: chéngshì yīnggāi huí dào guòqù de yàngzi
options.text_en: Cities should return to how they were in the past

options.text: 在追求效率和清晰度的现代化进程中，城市可能失去了某种难以量化的诗意
options.pinyin: zài zhuīqiú xiàolǜ hé qīngxī dù de xiàndàihuà jìnchéng zhōng, chéngshì kěnéng shīqù le mǒu zhǒng nányǐ liànghuà de shīyì
options.text_en: In the modernization process of pursuing efficiency and clarity, cities may have lost a certain unquantifiable poetry

options.text: 霓虹灯比LED更节能
options.pinyin: nóhóng dēng bǐ LED gèng jiénéng
options.text_en: Neon lights are more energy-efficient than LED

questions.explanation: "forming a disordered yet harmonious visual rhythm" — the contrast structure reveals the passage's actual commitment.

title: The Geometry of Laundry
text_zh: 「你把竹竿往回收一点，你的床单挡住我的衣服了。」三楼的周姐站在窗口朝对面喊。四楼的小刘探出头：「我没挡啊，风吹的。」周姐说：「风吹的？那你晾的时候就不能算一下风向吗？」小刘没吭声，把竹竿往回缩了半米。楼下路过的快递员老马抬头看了一眼，对我说：「这两家每个月都要吵一次。」我搬到这个老小区半年了，已经摸清了晾衣绳的地缘政治。二楼的张阿姨占据着最佳位置——正南朝向，日照最长。她的竹竿伸得最远，上面永远挂着各种颜色的被套和床单，像万国旗。三楼的周姐在张阿姨正上方，两家的晾衣空间有部分重叠。多年博弈之后形成了一个默契：上午十点以前是张阿姨的时间，她先晾；十点以后周姐可以晾，但不能超过张阿姨竹竿的长度。四楼小刘是新搬来的，不懂这些规矩。第一周就把竹竿伸出去两米，直接挡住了周姐的阳光。周姐没有直接说，而是把自己的衣服全部挂到最高处，阴影正好落在小刘的衣服上。小刘三天后发现自己的衣服总是干不透，才反应过来。快递员老马说他送了十年快递，光是看各家晾的衣服就能猜出这栋楼的人口结构。「五楼那家只晾男士衬衫和西裤，一个人住的。六楼那家婴儿衣服晾了一整排——二胎。」他骑上电动车走了。楼上又传来周姐的声音：「小刘，你那条裤子滴水了，滴到我被子上了！」
text_pinyin: Zài lǎo chéngqū de xiàngnòng lǐ táitóu wàng qù, nǐ huì kàn dào yī fú yóu liàng yī shéng biānzhī ér chéng de kōngzhōng túpǔ. Zhúgān cóng chuānghù shēn chūlái, kuàyuè xiàngdào de shàngkōng, xiàng yī zuò zuò wēixíng de qiáoliáng. Shàngmiàn guà zhe de yīwù suí fēng bǎidòng——báisè de chuángdān rú fān, cǎisè de qúnzi rú qí, shēnsè de gōngzhuāng kù chéndiàndiàn de chuí zhe, xiàng yī pái píbèi de shìbīng zài xiūxi. Zhèxiē liàng yī shéng bù jǐnjǐn shì gōngnéng xìng de shēnghuó shèshī, tāmen shì yī gè shèqū zuì tǎnshuài de zìwǒ zhǎnshì. Nǐ bù xūyào qiāo kāi rènhé yī shàn mén, zhǐyào táitóu kàn kan nàxiē yīwù, jiù néng dàzhì gōulè chū zhù zài zhèlǐ de rén de shēnghuó lúnkuò. Nà jiā guà zhe hǎo jǐ jiàn yīng'ér liántǐ yī de, xiǎnrán gāng tiān le xiǎo bǎobao; nà jiā quán shì sùsè miánmá yīshang de, dàgài zhù zhe yī wèi shěnměi kèzhì de zhōngnián rén; nà jiā de liàng yī shéng shàng zhǐyǒu jǐ jiàn shēn lánsè de gōngzuò fú, yī kàn jiùshì yī gè rén zhù de dújū zhě. Zuì yǒu yìsi de shì, bùtóng lóucéng de liàng yī shéng zhī jiān wǎngwǎng cúnzài yī zhǒng wúshēng de lǐngtǔ xiéshāng. Shuí de zhúgān kěyǐ shēn chū duō yuǎn, shuí jiā de yīwù kěyǐ zhànjù duōshǎo kōngjiān, dōu shì jīngguò chángqī bóyì hòu xíngchéng de mòqì. Ǒu'ěr yǒu xīn zhùhù bān lái, bù dǒng zhèxiē qián guīzé, bǎ zhúgān shēn de tài yuǎn huòzhě yīfu shài de tài mì, jiù huì yǐnfā yī chǎng wēixíng de wàijiāo shìjiàn——tōngcháng bú shì zhèngmiàn jiāofēng, ér shì tōngguò yīxiē wéimiào de xìnhào lái biǎodá bùmǎn: gùyì bǎ zìjǐ de yīwù guà de gèng gāo yǐ zhēdǎng duìfāng de yángguāng, huòzhě zài liàng yī shéng de biānjiè xiàn shàng duō liàng yī jiàn yīfu yǐ shì zhǔquán. Zhè zhǒng wúshēng de bóyì, shì chéngshì mìjí jūzhù shēnghuó zhōng zuì shēngdòng de shèhuì xué yàngběn.
text_en: 'Pull your pole back a bit—your bed sheet is blocking my clothes.' Sister Zhou on the third floor shouted from her window toward the opposite building. Xiao Liu on the fourth floor leaned out: 'I am not blocking anything. It is the wind.' Sister Zhou said: 'The wind? Then can you not calculate the wind direction when you hang things out?' Xiao Liu said nothing and retracted her pole by half a meter. Old Ma the delivery driver, passing below, looked up and said to me: 'Those two fight about once a month.' I have lived in this old compound for six months now and have already mapped out the geopolitics of the clotheslines. Aunt Zhang on the second floor occupies the prime position—due south-facing, longest sun exposure. Her bamboo pole extends the farthest, perpetually hung with bed covers and sheets of various colors, like flags of all nations. Sister Zhou on the third floor is directly above Aunt Zhang; their drying spaces partially overlap. After years of maneuvering, a tacit agreement formed: before ten a.m. Is Aunt Zhang's time—she hangs first. After ten, Sister Zhou may hang her laundry, but her pole must not extend beyond the length of Aunt Zhang's. Xiao Liu on the fourth floor moved in recently and does not know these rules. In her first week she extended her pole two meters out, directly blocking Sister Zhou's sunlight. Sister Zhou did not say anything directly, but hung all her own clothes at maximum height so the shadow fell precisely on Xiao Liu's laundry. Three days later Xiao Liu realized her clothes never fully dried and figured it out. Old Ma the delivery driver said he has been delivering packages for ten years and can guess a building's demographic structure just from the laundry. 'Fifth floor only hangs men's dress shirts and trousers—lives alone. Sixth floor has a whole row of baby clothes—second child.' He rode off on his electric scooter. From upstairs came Sister Zhou's voice again: 'Xiao Liu, your pants are dripping water—dripping onto my comforter!'

title: The Memory of Manholes
text_zh: 城市的井盖是最容易被忽略的城市文本。每天有成千上万的人踩过它们，但几乎没有人低头仔细看过上面铸造的文字和图案。一块井盖上通常标注着它的归属——「给水」「排水」「电信」「燃气」——这些简短的词语勾勒出了一个隐藏在地面之下的平行世界。我们脚下的这座看不见的城市，其实比头顶上的那座更加复杂：纵横交错的管道、密如蛛网的电缆、大大小小的检修通道，它们共同构成了现代城市的地下神经系统。有些城市的老井盖上还保留着早已不存在的地名或机构名称。我在一座南方城市的旧城区，见过一块上面铸着「××人民公社」的井盖。那个公社早在四十多年前就已经撤销了，但这块铸铁的记忆载体依然嵌在路面上，每天被无数双脚反复踩踏，却固执地保存着那个年代的印记。它比任何历史教科书都更直接地证明了某段历史的物质存在。日本的一些城市把井盖设计成了艺术品——彩色的、精美的、带有地方特色图案的装饰性井盖。这种做法固然赏心悦目，但我更喜欢那些朴素的、带有锈迹的、刻着年份的老井盖。因为它们的美不在于设计，而在于使用——每一道被磨平的纹路、每一块被踩掉的棱角、每一片生锈的区域，都是这座城市几十年来行走痕迹的累积。井盖是城市的脚底板——最不起眼，却承受着全部的重量。
text_pinyin: Chéngshì de jǐnggài shì zuì róngyì bèi hūlüè de chéngshì wénběn. Měi tiān yǒu chéng qiān shàng wàn de rén cǎi guò tāmen, dàn jīhū méiyǒu rén dītóu zǐxì kàn guò shàngmiàn zhùzào de wénzì hé tú'àn. Yī kuài jǐnggài shàng tōngcháng biāozhù zhe tā de guīshǔ——「gěishuǐ」「páishuǐ」「diànxìn」「ránqì」——zhèxiē jiǎnduǎn de cíyǔ gōulè chū le yī gè yǐncáng zài dìmiàn zhī xià de píngxíng shìjiè. Wǒmen jiǎo xià de zhè zuò kàn bú jiàn de chéngshì, qíshí bǐ tóudǐng shàng de nà zuò gèngjiā fùzá: zònghéng-jiāocuò de guǎndào, mì rú zhūwǎng de diànlǎn, dàdà-xiǎoxiǎo de jiǎnxiū tōngdào, tāmen gòngtóng gòuchéng le xiàndài chéngshì de dìxià shénjīng xìtǒng. Yǒuxiē chéngshì de lǎo jǐnggài shàng hái bǎoliú zhe zǎoyǐ bù cúnzài de dìmíng huò jīgòu míngchēng. Wǒ zài yī zuò nánfāng chéngshì de jiù chéngqū, jiàn guò yī kuài shàngmiàn zhù zhe「××rénmín gōngshè」de jǐnggài. Nàge gōngshè zǎo zài sìshí duō nián qián jiù yǐjīng chèxiāo le, dàn zhè kuài zhùtiě de jìyì zàitǐ yīrán qiàn zài lùmiàn shàng, měi tiān bèi wúshù shuāng jiǎo fǎnfù cǎità, què gùzhí de bǎocún zhe nàge niándài de yìnjì. Tā bǐ rènhé lìshǐ jiàokēshū dōu gèng zhíjiē de zhèngmíng le mǒu duàn lìshǐ de wùzhì cúnzài. Rìběn de yīxiē chéngshì bǎ jǐnggài shèjì chéng le yìshùpǐn——cǎisè de, jīngměi de, dài yǒu dìfāng tèsè tú'àn de zhuāngshì xìng jǐnggài. Zhè zhǒng zuòfǎ gùrán shǎngxīn-yuèmù, dàn wǒ gèng xǐhuān nàxiē pǔsù de, dài yǒu xiùjì de, kè zhe niánfèn de lǎo jǐnggài. Yīnwèi tāmen de měi bú zàiyú shèjì, ér zàiyú shǐyòng——měi yī dào bèi mó píng de wénlù, měi yī kuài bèi cǎi diào de léngjiǎo, měi yī piàn shēngxiù de qūyù, dōu shì zhè zuò chéngshì jǐ shí nián lái xíngzǒu hénjì de lěijī. Jǐnggài shì chéngshì de jiǎodǐ bǎn——zuì bù qǐyǎn, què chéngshòu zhe quánbù de zhòngliàng.
text_en: Manhole covers are the most easily overlooked urban text. Thousands of people step on them every day, but almost no one looks down to examine the cast words and patterns on their surface. A manhole cover typically indicates its affiliation—'Water Supply,' 'Drainage,' 'Telecom,' 'Gas'—these brief words sketch a parallel world hidden beneath the surface. The invisible city beneath our feet is actually more complex than the one above our heads: intersecting pipes, cables dense as spider webs, maintenance passages of all sizes—together they constitute the underground nervous system of a modern city. Some older manhole covers still bear place names or institutional names that no longer exist. In the old district of a southern city, I once saw a cover cast with the name of a 'People's Commune' that had been dissolved over forty years ago. Yet this cast-iron vessel of memory remains embedded in the road surface, trampled daily by countless feet, stubbornly preserving the imprint of that era. It proves the material existence of a period of history more directly than any textbook. Some Japanese cities have designed manhole covers as works of art—colorful, exquisite, decorative covers bearing locally distinctive patterns. This approach is certainly pleasing to the eye, but I prefer the plain, rust-stained old covers stamped with dates.

title: The Sociology of Elevators
text_zh: 电梯是现代城市中最浓缩的社交实验室。在不到三平方米的空间里，几个互不相识的人被迫短暂共处——通常不超过两分钟。这两分钟内发生的微观社会行为，浓缩了人类社交的几乎所有基本模式。首先是站位。人们在电梯里的站位遵循着一套不成文的空间语法。第一个进入电梯的人通常会站到角落或者靠近操作面板的位置，然后每多进来一个人，空间就会被重新分配——人们下意识地调整位置，试图在有限的面积内最大化彼此之间的距离。这种行为在动物行为学中叫做「个人空间维护」，但在电梯这个极端环境中，它展现出了一种近乎舞蹈编排般的精确性。然后是目光。电梯里的人几乎从不对视。大多数人会选择盯着楼层显示器——那个每隔几秒变化一次的数字成了所有人共同的视觉避难所。还有人会低头看手机，或者抬头看天花板上并不存在的东西。这些行为的本质都是一样的：在一个无法逃离的密闭空间中，用视线的回避来制造心理上的距离感。最值得玩味的是声音。电梯里的交谈有一种特殊的声学品质——人们会不自觉地压低音量，仿佛怕打扰这个微型空间里脆弱的社交平衡。偶尔有人在电梯里大声打电话，所有人都会感到不适，但没有人会出声制止。这种集体沉默中的共同不适，本身就构成了一种无声的团结。
text_pinyin: Diàntī shì xiàndài chéngshì zhōng zuì nóngsuō de shèjiāo shíyàn shì. Zài bú dào sān píngfāng mǐ de kōngjiān lǐ, jǐ gè hù bù xiāngshí de rén bèi pò duǎnzàn gòngchǔ——tōngcháng bù chāoguò liǎng fēnzhōng. Zhè liǎng fēnzhōng nèi fāshēng de wēiguān shèhuì xíngwéi, nóngsuō le rénlèi shèjiāo de jīhū suǒyǒu jīběn móshì. Shǒuxiān shì zhànwèi. Rénmen zài diàntī lǐ de zhànwèi zūnxún zhe yī tào bù chéngwén de kōngjiān yǔfǎ. Dì yī gè jìnrù diàntī de rén tōngcháng huì zhàn dào jiǎoluò huòzhě kàojìn cāozuò miànbǎn de wèizhì, ránhòu měi duō jìnlái yī gè rén, kōngjiān jiù huì bèi chóngxīn fēnpèi——rénmen xiàyìshí de tiáozhěng wèizhì, shìtú zài yǒuxiàn de miànjī nèi zuìdàhuà bǐcǐ zhī jiān de jùlí. Zhè zhǒng xíngwéi zài dòngwù xíngwéi xué zhōng jiào zuò「gèrén kōngjiān wéihù」, dàn zài diàntī zhège jíduān huánjìng zhōng, tā zhǎnxiàn chū le yī zhǒng jìnhū wǔdǎo biānpái bān de jīngquè xìng. Ránhòu shì mùguāng. Diàntī lǐ de rén jīhū cóng bú duìshì. Dàduōshù rén huì xuǎnzé dīng zhe lóucéng xiǎnshì qì——nàge měi gé jǐ miǎo biànhuà yī cì de shùzì chéng le suǒyǒu rén gòngtóng de shìjué bìnàn suǒ. Hái yǒu rén huì dītóu kàn shǒujī, huòzhě táitóu kàn tiānhuā bǎn shàng bìng bù cúnzài de dōngxi. Zhèxiē xíngwéi de běnzhì dōu shì yīyàng de: zài yī gè wúfǎ táolí de mìbì kōngjiān zhōng, yòng shìxiàn de huíbì lái zhìzào xīnlǐ shàng de jùlí gǎn. Zuì zhíde wánwèi de shì shēngyīn. Diàntī lǐ de jiāotán yǒu yī zhǒng tèshū de shēngxué pǐnzhì——rénmen huì bù zìjué de yā dī yīnliàng, fǎngfú pà dǎrǎo zhège wēixíng kōngjiān lǐ cuìruò de shèjiāo pínghéng. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén zài diàntī lǐ dàshēng dǎ diànhuà, suǒyǒu rén dōu huì gǎndào bú shì, dàn méiyǒu rén huì chūshēng zhìzhǐ. Zhè zhǒng jítǐ chénmò zhōng de gòngtóng bú shì, běnshēn jiù gòuchéng le yī zhǒng wúshēng de tuánjié.
text_en: An elevator is the most concentrated social laboratory in a modern city. In a space of less than three square meters, several strangers are forced into brief coexistence—usually no more than two minutes. The micro-social behaviors occurring in those two minutes condense nearly all basic patterns of human sociality. First, positioning. People's positioning in an elevator follows an unwritten spatial grammar. The first person to enter usually stands in a corner or near the control panel; then with each additional person, the space is redistributed—people unconsciously adjust their positions, attempting to maximize the distance between one another within the limited area. This behavior is called 'personal space maintenance' in animal behavioral science, but in the extreme environment of an elevator, it displays an almost choreographic precision. Then, gaze. People in elevators almost never make eye contact. Most choose to stare at the floor indicator—that number changing every few seconds becomes everyone's shared visual refuge. Others look down at their phones or up at something nonexistent on the ceiling. The essence of all these behaviors is the same: in an inescapable enclosed space, using gaze avoidance to create a psychological sense of distance. Most interesting is sound. Conversation in elevators has a peculiar acoustic quality—people unconsciously lower their volume, as if afraid of disturbing the fragile social equilibrium in this micro-space. Occasionally someone talks loudly on a phone in the elevator, and everyone feels uncomfortable, but no one speaks up to stop it. This shared discomfort within collective silence itself constitutes a kind of wordless solidarity.

questions.q_zh: 有人在电梯里大声打电话时，「集体沉默中的共同不适」为什么被称为一种团结？
questions.q_en: Why is the 'shared discomfort within collective silence' when someone talks loudly in an elevator called a form of solidarity?

options.text: 因为大家会一起去制止那个人
options.pinyin: yīnwèi dàjiā huì yīqǐ qù zhìzhǐ nàge rén
options.text_en: Because everyone stops that person together

options.text: 因为虽然无人出声，但所有人共享同一种不适感，形成了一种无形的共识
options.pinyin: yīnwèi suīrán wú rén chūshēng, dàn suǒyǒu rén gòngxiǎng tóng yī zhǒng bú shì gǎn, xíngchéng le yī zhǒng wúxíng de gòngshí
options.text_en: Because though no one speaks, everyone shares the same discomfort, forming an invisible consensus

options.text: 因为大家都在默默支持那个打电话的人
options.pinyin: yīnwèi dàjiā dōu zài mòmò zhīchí nàge dǎ diànhuà de rén
options.text_en: Because everyone is silently supporting the person on the phone

options.text: 因为沉默比语言更有力量
options.pinyin: yīnwèi chénmò bǐ yǔyán gèng yǒu lìliàng
options.text_en: Because silence is more powerful than words

questions.explanation: "itself constitutes a kind of wordless solidarity" — narrative sequence does the causal work; the reason unfolds in the telling.

title: The Dialect of Storefronts
text_zh: 三个城市的招牌，三种说话方式。第一个城市：京都。一块深色木板，烫金的字，三个汉字加一个小小的家纹。没有图片，没有电话号码，没有营业时间。整块招牌传递的信息是：「如果你不知道我们是做什么的，那你大概不需要知道。」木板上有雨水渗透的痕迹，字的边缘微微发毛，看起来已经挂了三十年以上。招牌和店面一样旧，和老板一样沉默。第二个城市：曼谷。一块招牌上挤了四种文字——泰文、中文、英文、日文。底色是大红配金黄，上面贴满了菜品照片，每张照片旁边标着价格。招牌的面积比店铺的门面还大。老板娘站在门口，穿着和招牌一样鲜艳的围裙。招牌上有一行手写的字：「AC inside」。在曼谷四十度的高温里，这三个字可能比任何菜品照片都更有说服力。第三个城市：成都。原本每家店都有自己的招牌——火锅店用红底金字，茶馆用竹编框配毛笔字，苍蝇馆子什么都不挂，就在玻璃窗上用红油漆写几个歪歪扭扭的大字。去年整条街统一换了招牌：白底黑字，宋体，规格统一，间距统一。第一个月看着确实整洁。第三个月开始觉得哪里不对。第六个月意识到问题在哪里了——你站在街头往前看，分不清哪家是火锅哪家是茶馆哪家是药店。所有的店铺都穿上了同一套校服。整齐是整齐了，但街道不说话了。
text_pinyin: Měi gè chéngshì de diànpù zhāopai dōu shuō zhe yī zhǒng shǔyú zìjǐ de fāngyán. Bú shì zhǐ zhāopai shàng xiě de wénzì yǔyán, ér shì zhǐ zhāopai de shèjì yǔyán——zìtǐ de xuǎnzé, yánsè de dāpèi, cáizhì de qīngxiàng, xìnxī de mìdù, zhèxiē yuánsù gòngtóng gòuchéng le yī gè chéngshì dútè de shāngyè shìjué yǔfǎ. Rìběn de diànpù zhāopai piān'ài liúbái. Yī kuài shēnsè de mùbǎn shàng, yòng tàngjīn huò fúdiāo de shǒufǎ xiě jǐ gè zì, xìnxī jíqí jīngjiǎn, fǎngfú zài shuō:「Rúguǒ nǐ bù zhīdào wǒmen shì zuò shénme de, nà nǐ dàgài bú shì wǒmen de mùbiāo kèhù.」Zhè zhǒng kèzhì de měixué bèihòu, shì yī zhǒng shēnhòu de shāngyè zìxìn. Ér Dōngnányà chéngshì de zhāopai zé rènao de xiàng yī chǎng shìjué de jiānián huá——dà hóng dà lǜ de pèisè, duō zhǒng yǔyán bìngliè de wénzì, jǐn kěnéng duō de shāngpǐn túpiàn, hènbude bǎ suǒyǒu xìnxī dōu sāi jìn yī kuài zhāopai lǐ. Zhè bú shì shěnměi de cūcāo, ér shì yī zhǒng shíyòng zhǔyì de zhìhuì——zài jìngzhēng jīliè de shāngyè huánjìng zhōng, xìnxī mìdù jiùshì shēngcún cèlüè. Zhōngguó chéngshì de zhāopai zhèngzài jīnglì yī chǎng jìng qiāoqiāo de biāozhǔnhuà yùndòng. Yuèláiyuè duō de chéngshì kāishǐ tǒngyī yán jiē diànpù de zhāopai yàngshì——xiāngtóng de chǐcùn, xiāngtóng de dǐsè, xiāngtóng de zìtǐ. Biǎomiàn shàng kàn, zhè ràng jiēdào biàn de zhěngjié huáyī le, dàn dàijià shì shāngyè shēngtài de duōyàng xìng bèi mǒ píng le. Dāng yī tiáo jiē shàng suǒyǒu de zhāopai dōu zhǎng de yīmó-yīyàng, nǐ jiù shīqù le jǐn píng zhāopai jiù néng pànduàn yī jiā diàn tèsè de nénglì. Biāozhǔnhuà de zhāopai xiàng shì gěi měi gè diànpù chuān shàng le xiàofú——suīrán zhěngqí, dàn mǒshā le gèxìng. Ér yī tiáo hǎo de shāngyè jiē, yīnggāi xiàng yī gè hǎo de jiāoxiǎng yuètuán: měi jiàn yuèqì dōu yǒu zìjǐ de shēngyīn, dàn tāmen gòngtóng yǎnzòu chū héxié de xuánlǜ.
text_en: Three cities' shop signs, three ways of speaking. The first city: Kyoto. A dark wooden board, gold-leaf characters, three kanji plus a small family crest. No photographs, no phone number, no business hours. The entire sign communicates: 'If you do not know what we do, you probably do not need to know.' The board shows traces of rainwater seepage; the edges of the characters are slightly fuzzy, looking as though it has hung there for over thirty years. The sign is as worn as the shopfront, as silent as the owner. The second city: Bangkok. Four scripts crammed onto one sign—Thai, Chinese, English, Japanese. Background of bold red and gold, plastered with dish photographs, each with a price beside it. The sign is bigger than the shop's storefront. The owner's wife stands at the entrance wearing an apron as bright as the sign. One line is handwritten on the board: 'AC inside.' In Bangkok's forty-degree heat, those three characters may be more persuasive than any food photo. The third city: Chengdu. Originally every shop had its own sign—hot pot restaurants used red backgrounds with gold characters, teahouses used bamboo frames with brush calligraphy, hole-in-the-wall joints hung nothing at all, just scrawled a few crooked characters in red paint on the glass window. Last year the entire street was given uniform signs: white background, black text, Song typeface, standardized dimensions, standardized spacing. The first month it did look tidy. By the third month something felt wrong. By the sixth month the problem became clear—standing at the head of the street and looking forward, you could not tell which shop was the hot pot place, which was the teahouse, which was the pharmacy. Every shop had been dressed in the same school uniform. Orderly, yes. But the street had stopped talking.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为东南亚招牌信息密度高的原因是什么？
questions.q_en: According to the author, why do Southeast Asian signs have high information density?

options.text: 因为东南亚人的审美品味较低
options.pinyin: yīnwèi Dōngnányà rén de shěnměi pǐnwèi jiào dī
options.text_en: Because Southeast Asians have lower aesthetic taste

options.text: 因为在激烈竞争的商业环境中，高信息密度是一种实用的生存策略
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zài jīliè jìngzhēng de shāngyè huánjìng zhōng, gāo xìnxī mìdù shì yī zhǒng shíyòng de shēngcún cèlüè
options.text_en: Because in a fiercely competitive commercial environment, high information density is a practical survival strategy

options.text: 因为东南亚的招牌材料比较便宜
options.pinyin: yīnwèi Dōngnányà de zhāopai cáiliào bǐjiào piányi
options.text_en: Because Southeast Asian sign materials are cheaper

options.text: 因为法律规定必须包含多种语言
options.pinyin: yīnwèi fǎlǜ guīdìng bìxū bāohán duō zhǒng yǔyán
options.text_en: Because the law requires multiple languages

questions.explanation: "could not tell which shop was" — narrative sequence does the causal work; the reason unfolds in the telling.

questions.q_zh: 作者把统一招牌比作「给店铺穿校服」，想表达什么观点？
questions.q_en: What viewpoint does the author express by comparing uniform signs to 'putting shops in school uniforms'?

options.text: 校服让学生更有纪律性
options.pinyin: xiàofú ràng xuéshēng gèng yǒu jìlǜ xìng
options.text_en: School uniforms give students more discipline

options.text: 统一招牌虽然整齐但扼杀了个性和商业多样性
options.pinyin: tǒngyī zhāopai suīrán zhěngqí dàn èshā le gèxìng hé shāngyè duōyàng xìng
options.text_en: Uniform signs are orderly but kill individuality and commercial diversity

options.text: 店铺应该像学校一样规范管理
options.pinyin: diànpù yīnggāi xiàng xuéxiào yīyàng guīfàn guǎnlǐ
options.text_en: Shops should be regulated like schools

options.text: 校服设计应该更加多样化
options.pinyin: xiàofú shèjì yīnggāi gèngjiā duōyànghuà
options.text_en: School uniform designs should be more diverse

questions.explanation: "in the same school uniform" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

title: The Topography of Smells
text_zh: 每座城市都有一幅用气味绘制的隐形地图。这张地图无法用眼睛看见，但你的鼻子能毫不费力地读取它。走进菜市场，湿润的泥土气息混合着生鲜的腥味和香料的辛辣，构成了一种浓烈而有层次的嗅觉和弦。转过街角来到烘焙坊附近，空气突然变得温暖而甜腻，黄油和面粉在高温中释放出的焦香飘荡在方圆五十米内。再往前走，经过一家中药铺，陈皮、当归、甘草的药香从半掩的门缝里渗出来，有一种沉稳而古老的气息，仿佛时间本身也有味道。我曾经认识一个盲人按摩师，他靠气味建立了自己的城市认知地图。他说他不需要知道路名和门牌号，只要闻到空气中的味道就能判断自己走到了哪里。「面包店那个路口是甜的，修车铺那段路是油腻的，幼儿园门口有一种特殊的奶粉和消毒液混合的味道。」对他来说，城市不是由建筑和道路构成的，而是由一个个气味的节点串联而成的。这种认知方式让我意识到，我们大多数人其实浪费了一种强大的感官通道。视觉太过强势，以至于我们几乎把全部的注意力都给了眼睛，而忽略了鼻子传递给我们的那些微妙的、无法言说的、却直通记忆深处的信息。一种气味能在瞬间把你拽回二十年前的某个场景——这种穿越时间的能力，是任何照片或视频都做不到的。
text_pinyin: Měi zuò chéngshì dōu yǒu yī fú yòng qìwèi huìzhì de yǐnxíng dìtú. Zhè zhāng dìtú wúfǎ yòng yǎnjīng kànjiàn, dàn nǐ de bízi néng háo bù fèilì de dúqǔ tā. Zǒu jìn cài shìchǎng, shīrùn de nítǔ qìxī hùnhé zhe shēngxiān de xīngwèi hé xiāngliào de xīnlà, gòuchéng le yī zhǒng nóngliè ér yǒu céngcì de xiùjué héxián. Zhuǎn guò jiējiǎo lái dào hōngbèi fáng fùjìn, kōngqì tūrán biàn de wēnnuǎn ér tián nì, huángyóu hé miànfěn zài gāowēn zhōng shìfàng chū de jiāo xiāng piāodàng zài fāngyuán wǔshí mǐ nèi. Zài wǎng qián zǒu, jīngguò yī jiā zhōngyào pù, chénpí, dāngguī, gāncǎo de yào xiāng cóng bàn yǎn de mén fèng lǐ shèn chūlái, yǒu yī zhǒng chénwěn ér gǔlǎo de qìxī, fǎngfú shíjiān běnshēn yě yǒu wèidao. Wǒ céngjīng rènshi yī gè mángrén ànmó shī, tā kào qìwèi jiànlì le zìjǐ de chéngshì rènzhī dìtú. Tā shuō tā bù xūyào zhīdào lùmíng hé ménpái hào, zhǐyào wén dào kōngqì zhōng de wèidao jiù néng pànduàn zìjǐ zǒu dào le nǎlǐ.「Miànbāo diàn nàge lùkǒu shì tián de, xiū chē pù nà duàn lù shì yóunì de, yòu'éryuán ménkǒu yǒu yī zhǒng tèshū de nǎifěn hé xiāodú yè hùnhé de wèidao.」Duì tā lái shuō, chéngshì bú shì yóu jiànzhù hé dàolù gòuchéng de, ér shì yóu yī gè gè qìwèi de jiédiǎn chuànlián ér chéng de. Zhè zhǒng rènzhī fāngshì ràng wǒ yìshí dào, wǒmen dàduōshù rén qíshí làngfèi le yī zhǒng qiángdà de gǎnguān tōngdào. Shìjué tài guò qiángshì, yǐzhìyú wǒmen jīhū bǎ quánbù de zhùyìlì dōu gěi le yǎnjīng, ér hūlüè le bízi chuándì gěi wǒmen de nàxiē wéimiào de, wúfǎ yánshuō de, què zhí tōng jìyì shēnchù de xìnxī. Yī zhǒng qìwèi néng zài shùnjiān bǎ nǐ zhuāi huí èrshí nián qián de mǒu gè chǎngjǐng——zhè zhǒng chuānyuè shíjiān de nénglì, shì rènhé zhàopiàn huò shìpín dōu zuò bú dào de.
text_en: Every city has an invisible map drawn with smells. This map cannot be seen with eyes, but your nose reads it effortlessly. Walk into a wet market and the moist earthen aroma mingles with the fishiness of fresh seafood and the bite of spices, forming a rich, layered olfactory chord. Turn the corner near a bakery and the air suddenly becomes warm and sweet—the caramelized fragrance of butter and flour releasing at high temperatures drifts within a fifty-meter radius. Walk further past a traditional Chinese medicine shop, and the medicinal scent of dried tangerine peel, angelica root, and licorice seeps through the half-open door, carrying a steady, ancient aura, as if time itself had a smell. I once knew a blind massage therapist who built his own cognitive map of the city through smells. He said he didn't need to know street names or building numbers—he could determine his location just by the smell in the air. 'The intersection by the bakery is sweet, the stretch by the auto repair shop is oily, the kindergarten entrance has a distinctive smell of formula milk mixed with disinfectant.' For him, the city was not constituted by buildings and roads but strung together by nodes of scent. This way of perceiving made me realize that most of us actually waste a powerful sensory channel. Vision is so dominant that we give almost all our attention to our eyes, neglecting the subtle, inexpressible information our noses transmit to us—information that connects directly to the depths of memory. A smell can snatch you back to a scene from twenty years ago in an instant—this ability to traverse time is something no photograph or video can achieve.

questions.q_zh: 盲人按摩师的城市认知方式给了作者什么启发？
questions.q_en: What insight did the blind massage therapist's way of knowing the city give the author?

options.text: 盲人比正常人更聪明
options.pinyin: mángrén bǐ zhèngcháng rén gèng cōngming
options.text_en: Blind people are smarter than sighted people

options.text: 大多数人过于依赖视觉，忽略了嗅觉这个直通记忆深处的强大感官通道
options.pinyin: dàduōshù rén guòyú yīlài shìjué, hūlüè le xiùjué zhège zhí tōng jìyì shēnchù de qiángdà gǎnguān tōngdào
options.text_en: Most people rely too much on vision, neglecting smell as a powerful sensory channel that connects directly to the depths of memory

options.text: 城市应该为盲人设计更好的导航系统
options.pinyin: chéngshì yīnggāi wèi mángrén shèjì gèng hǎo de dǎoháng xìtǒng
options.text_en: Cities should design better navigation systems for blind people

options.text: 气味可以用来制作真正的地图
options.pinyin: qìwèi kěyǐ yòng lái zhìzuò zhēnzhèng de dìtú
options.text_en: Smells can be used to make real maps

questions.explanation: "inexpressible information our noses transmit to us" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说气味穿越时间的能力超过照片和视频？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say smell's ability to traverse time exceeds that of photos and videos?

options.text: 因为照片和视频的技术不够先进
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhàopiàn hé shìpín de jìshù bú gòu xiānjìn
options.text_en: Because photo and video technology is not advanced enough

options.text: 因为气味能在瞬间直接触发深层记忆，唤回完整的场景体验
options.pinyin: yīnwèi qìwèi néng zài shùnjiān zhíjiē chùfā shēncéng jìyì, huàn huí wánzhěng de chǎngjǐng tǐyàn
options.text_en: Because smell can instantly trigger deep memories directly, recalling a complete scene experience

options.text: 因为照片和视频容易丢失
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zhàopiàn hé shìpín róngyì diūshī
options.text_en: Because photos and videos are easily lost

options.text: 因为气味保存的时间更长
options.pinyin: yīnwèi qìwèi bǎocún de shíjiān gèng cháng
options.text_en: Because smells are preserved longer

questions.explanation: "an instant—this ability to traverse time is" — negation reveals the reason: what is ruled out clarifies what drives the action.

title: The Architecture of Waiting
text_zh: 公交车站是城市中专门为「等待」这种行为设计的建筑。但大多数公交车站在设计上并不尊重等待。一块勉强遮雨的顶棚、一排硬邦邦的塑料座椅、一块LED显示屏——这些标准配置仿佛在说：等待不值得被认真对待，因为它只是「到达」之前的过渡。但实际上，对于每天通勤的人来说，等待可能占据了他们清醒时间的一个不小的比例。如果你每天等两次公交车，每次等十五分钟，一年下来就是一百八十多个小时——超过七天七夜。我们花了七天的时间站在一个被粗暴设计的空间里，这难道不值得反思吗？我见过一些把等待变成享受的公交车站。有一个小城市的车站，顶棚下种了一棵真正的树，树荫洒在长椅上，等车的人坐在斑驳的光影里，神态安详得像是在公园休息而非等车。另一个车站的设计者在座位之间放了一个小型的免费图书架，来来往往的乘客可以随手取一本书翻几页，下次来的时候再放回去——一种基于信任的微型公共图书馆。这些设计不需要太多预算，但它们传递了一个重要的信号：你的等待是有价值的，你的时间值得被温柔地对待。当一个社会开始关注等待的品质——而不仅仅是效率——的时候，某种关于公共生活的深层理念就悄悄发生了变化。
text_pinyin: Gōngjiāo chēzhàn shì chéngshì zhōng zhuānmén wèi「děngdài」zhè zhǒng xíngwéi shèjì de jiànzhù. Dàn dàduōshù gōngjiāo chēzhàn zài shèjì shàng bìng bù zūnzhòng děngdài. Yī kuài miǎnqiáng zhē yǔ de dǐngpéng, yī pái yìngbāngbāng de sùliào zuòyǐ, yī kuài LED xiǎnshì píng——zhèxiē biāozhǔn pèizhì fǎngfú zài shuō: děngdài bù zhíde bèi rènzhēn duìdài, yīnwèi tā zhǐshì「dàodá」zhīqián de guòdù. Dàn shíjì shàng, duìyú měi tiān tōngqín de rén lái shuō, děngdài kěnéng zhànjù le tāmen qīngxǐng shíjiān de yī gè bù xiǎo de bǐlì. Rúguǒ nǐ měi tiān děng liǎng cì gōngjiāo chē, měi cì děng shíwǔ fēnzhōng, yī nián xiàlái jiùshì yībǎi bāshí duō gè xiǎoshí——chāoguò qī tiān qī yè. Wǒmen huā le qī tiān de shíjiān zhàn zài yī gè bèi cūbào shèjì de kōngjiān lǐ, zhè nándào bù zhíde fǎnsī ma? Wǒ jiàn guò yīxiē bǎ děngdài biànchéng xiǎngshòu de gōngjiāo chēzhàn. Yǒu yī gè xiǎo chéngshì de chēzhàn, dǐngpéng xià zhǒng le yī kē zhēnzhèng de shù, shùyīn sǎ zài chángyǐ shàng, děng chē de rén zuò zài bānbó de guāngyǐng lǐ, shéntài ānxiáng de xiàng shì zài gōngyuán xiūxi ér fēi děng chē. Lìng yī gè chēzhàn de shèjì zhě zài zuòwèi zhī jiān fàng le yī gè xiǎoxíng de miǎnfèi túshū jià, láilái-wǎngwǎng de chéngkè kěyǐ suíshǒu qǔ yī běn shū fān jǐ yè, xià cì lái de shíhou zài fàng huíqù——yī zhǒng jīyú xìnrèn de wēixíng gōnggòng túshūguǎn. Zhèxiē shèjì bù xūyào tài duō yùsuàn, dàn tāmen chuándì le yī gè zhòngyào de xìnhào: nǐ de děngdài shì yǒu jiàzhí de, nǐ de shíjiān zhíde bèi wēnróu de duìdài. Dāng yī gè shèhuì kāishǐ guānzhù děngdài de pǐnzhì——ér bù jǐnjǐn shì xiàolǜ——de shíhou, mǒu zhǒng guānyú gōnggòng shēnghuó de shēncéng lǐniàn jiù qiāoqiāo fāshēng le biànhuà.
text_en: A bus stop is architecture specifically designed for the act of 'waiting' in a city. But most bus stops do not respect waiting in their design. A canopy that barely blocks rain, a row of hard plastic seats, an LED display—these standard features seem to say: waiting doesn't deserve to be taken seriously because it's merely a transition before 'arriving.' But in reality, for daily commuters, waiting may occupy a significant portion of their waking hours. If you wait for the bus twice a day, fifteen minutes each time, over a year that amounts to over a hundred and eighty hours—more than seven full days and nights. We spend seven days standing in a crudely designed space—doesn't that merit reflection? I've seen bus stops that transform waiting into enjoyment. One small city's stop had a real tree planted under the canopy, its shade dappling the bench below. People waiting sat in mottled light and shadow, looking as serene as if resting in a park rather than waiting for a bus. Another stop's designer placed a small free bookshelf between the seats—passing riders could pick up a book, flip through a few pages, and put it back next time—a trust-based micro public library. These designs don't require large budgets, but they send an important signal: your waiting has value; your time deserves to be treated gently. When a society begins to care about the quality of waiting—not merely efficiency—some deep-seated concept about public life quietly shifts.

questions.q_zh: 作者计算了每年等车的总时间，目的是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the author's purpose in calculating the total time spent waiting for buses each year?

questions.q_en: What deeper social shift does the author believe 'caring about the quality of waiting' reflects?

options.text: 经济发展水平的提高
options.pinyin: jīngjì fāzhǎn shuǐpíng de tígāo

options.pinyin: cóng dānchún zhuīqiú xiàolǜ zhuǎn xiàng duì rén de shíjiān hé zūnyán de wēnróu guānhuái

title: The Calendar of Trees
text_zh: 城市里的行道树是最可靠的日历。当你生活在一个被钢筋混凝土包围的环境中，四季的更替很容易变得模糊——空调让室内永远保持二十二度，商场里全年播放着同一种背景音乐，办公室的荧光灯管不分昼夜地发出相同的光。但只要你抬头看一眼窗外的那棵树，季节就立刻变得真实了。春天的行道树是含蓄的。嫩芽从枝条上冒出来，小心翼翼的，像是在试探世界是否准备好了接受它们。这个过程通常发生在你注意不到的时候——有一天你忽然发现，那棵枝条光秃秃的树不知何时已经披上了一层淡绿色的薄纱。夏天的行道树是慷慨的。树冠完全展开，遮蔽了大片天空，在地面投下浓密的阴影。走在行道树下的人不自觉地会选择走在阴影里——这是人类最古老的趋利避害本能在城市环境中的微型体现。秋天是行道树最富戏剧性的季节。叶子变色——从绿到黄，从黄到红，从红到棕，每一片叶子都在上演自己的小型告别仪式。落叶堆积在路面上，被行人的脚步和汽车的轮胎碾碎，混入泥土，完成从生到死再到归的循环。冬天的行道树是诚实的。失去了叶子的遮掩，树的骨骼结构完全暴露出来——每一个分叉、每一根枝条的走向、每一个疤节的位置，都清清楚楚。这是一棵树最真实的样子，也是它最脆弱的时刻。但正是这种脆弱，让来年的新芽显得格外珍贵。
text_pinyin: Chéngshì lǐ de xíngdào shù shì zuì kěkào de rìlì. Dāng nǐ shēnghuó zài yī gè bèi gāngjīn hùnníngtǔ bāowéi de huánjìng zhōng, sìjì de gēngtì hěn róngyì biàn de móhu——kōngtiáo ràng shìnèi yǒngyuǎn bǎochí èrshí'èr dù, shāngchǎng lǐ quánnián bōfàng zhe tóng yī zhǒng bèijǐng yīnyuè, bàngōngshì de yíngguāng dēngguǎn bù fēn zhòuyè de fāchū xiāngtóng de guāng. Dàn zhǐyào nǐ táitóu kàn yī yǎn chuāng wài de nà kē shù, jìjié jiù lìkè biàn de zhēnshí le. Chūntiān de xíngdào shù shì hánxù de. Nènyá cóng zhītiáo shàng mào chūlái, xiǎoxīn-yìyì de, xiàng shì zài shìtàn shìjiè shìfǒu zhǔnbèi hǎo le jiēshòu tāmen. Zhège guòchéng tōngcháng fāshēng zài nǐ zhùyì bú dào de shíhou——yǒu yī tiān nǐ hūrán fāxiàn, nà kē zhītiáo guāngtūtū de shù bù zhī hé shí yǐjīng pī shàng le yī céng dàn lǜsè de báoshā. Xiàtiān de xíngdào shù shì kāngkǎi de. Shùguān wánquán zhǎnkāi, zhēbì le dà piàn tiānkōng, zài dìmiàn tóu xià nóngmì de yīnyǐng. Zǒu zài xíngdào shù xià de rén bù zìjué de huì xuǎnzé zǒu zài yīnyǐng lǐ——zhè shì rénlèi zuì gǔlǎo de qūlì-bìhài běnnéng zài chéngshì huánjìng zhōng de wēixíng tǐxiàn. Qiūtiān shì xíngdào shù zuì fù xìjù xìng de jìjié. Yèzi biànsè——cóng lǜ dào huáng, cóng huáng dào hóng, cóng hóng dào zōng, měi yī piàn yèzi dōu zài shàngyǎn zìjǐ de xiǎoxíng gàobié yíshì. Luòyè duījī zài lùmiàn shàng, bèi xíngrén de jiǎobù hé qìchē de lúntāi niǎn suì, hùn rù nítǔ, wánchéng cóng shēng dào sǐ zài dào guī de xúnhuán. Dōngtiān de xíngdào shù shì chéngshí de. Shīqù le yèzi de zhēyǎn, shù de gǔgé jiégòu wánquán bàolù chūlái——měi yī gè fēnchà, měi yī gēn zhītiáo de zǒuxiàng, měi yī gè bā jié de wèizhì, dōu qīngqīng-chǔchǔ. Zhè shì yī kē shù zuì zhēnshí de yàngzi, yě shì tā zuì cuìruò de shíkè. Dàn zhèng shì zhè zhǒng cuìruò, ràng lái nián de xīnyá xiǎn de géwài zhēnguì.
text_en: Street trees in a city are the most reliable calendar. When you live in an environment surrounded by reinforced concrete, the changing of seasons easily blurs—air conditioning keeps indoors forever at twenty-two degrees, malls play the same background music year-round, office fluorescent lights emit identical light regardless of day or night. But the moment you look up at the tree outside your window, the season instantly becomes real. Spring street trees are reserved. Tender buds emerge from branches cautiously, as if testing whether the world is ready to receive them. This process usually happens when you're not paying attention—one day you suddenly discover that the tree with bare branches has somehow draped itself in a veil of pale green. Summer street trees are generous. Their canopies fully unfurl, shielding large swaths of sky, casting dense shadows on the ground. People walking beneath them unconsciously choose to walk in the shade—a micro-manifestation of humanity's oldest instinct for seeking benefit and avoiding harm in an urban environment. Autumn is street trees' most dramatic season. Leaves change color—from green to yellow, yellow to red, red to brown—each leaf staging its own small farewell ceremony. Fallen leaves accumulate on the road, crushed by pedestrian feet and car tires, mixed into soil, completing the cycle from life to death to return. Winter street trees are honest. Stripped of their leaf cover, the tree's skeletal structure is fully exposed—every fork, the direction of every branch, the position of every knot, all perfectly clear. This is a tree's most authentic form, and also its most vulnerable moment. But it is precisely this vulnerability that makes next year's new buds seem especially precious.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说冬天的行道树是「诚实的」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author call winter street trees 'honest'?

options.text_en: Because winter trees don't bloom

options.text: 因为失去叶子后树的真实骨骼结构完全暴露，无处隐藏
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shīqù yèzi hòu shù de zhēnshí gǔgé jiégòu wánquán bàolù, wú chù yǐncáng
options.text_en: Because after losing leaves, the tree's true skeletal structure is fully exposed with nowhere to hide

options.text: 因为冬天的树不会移动
options.pinyin: yīnwèi dōngtiān de shù bú huì yídòng
options.text_en: Because winter trees don't move

options.text: 因为冬天的温度是真实的
options.pinyin: yīnwèi dōngtiān de wēndù shì zhēnshí de
options.text_en: Because winter temperatures are real

questions.explanation: "Winter street trees are honest" — cause is shown through concrete sequence, not stated as principle.

questions.q_zh: 文中把行道树称为「最可靠的日历」，原因是什么？
questions.q_en: Why does the text call street trees 'the most reliable calendar'?

options.text: 因为树的年轮可以计算年龄
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shù de niánlún kěyǐ jìsuàn niánlíng
options.text_en: Because tree rings can be used to calculate age

options.text: 因为在人工环境模糊了四季界限的城市中，树的变化忠实地标记着季节更替
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zài réngōng huánjìng móhu le sìjì jièxiàn de chéngshì zhōng, shù de biànhuà zhōngshí de biāojì zhe jìjié gēngtì
options.text_en: Because in a city where artificial environments blur seasonal boundaries, trees' changes faithfully mark the changing seasons

options.text: 因为每棵树上都标注了种植日期
options.pinyin: yīnwèi měi kē shù shàng dōu biāozhù le zhòngzhí rìqī
options.text_en: Because every tree is labeled with its planting date

options.text: 因为城市规划者按季节种植不同的树
options.pinyin: yīnwèi chéngshì guīhuà zhě àn jìjié zhòngzhí bùtóng de shù
options.text_en: Because urban planners plant different trees according to seasons

questions.explanation: "are the most reliable calendar" — the passage traces motive through action; the why sits inside the what.

title: The Palimpsest of Footsteps
text_zh: 如果一座城市的路面能够记录下所有曾经走过它的脚步，那它一定是世界上最复杂的文本。每一双脚都在上面写下了自己的句子——有些匆忙而急促，是早高峰赶地铁的通勤者；有些缓慢而沉重，是提着菜篮子走回家的老人；有些轻盈而跳跃，是放学后追逐嬉闹的孩子。这些脚步交叠在一起，形成了一个不断被覆写的文本——每天都有新的句子写上去，但旧的句子并没有真正消失，只是被埋在了更新的层次之下。城市规划者可以通过一条路面的磨损程度来判断这条路被使用的频率。石板路上被踩得最光滑的地方，通常就是最多人行走的路线。有些古镇的石阶中间有一道明显的凹陷，那是几百年来无数双脚在同一位置反复踩踏的结果。每一个踩过这个凹陷的人，都无形中参与了一场持续数百年的集体雕塑。我在一座古城的城墙上散步时，注意到某些石砖比其他的光滑得多。导游告诉我，那是因为旧时守城的士兵每天在固定的位置巡逻，日复一日，年复一年，他们的脚底把石砖磨出了一条近乎于光滑的轨迹。那些士兵早已化为尘土，但他们日复一日的行走，至今还刻在石头上。路面是一个没有作者署名的集体创作——每个人都贡献了微不足道的一步，但亿万步叠加在一起，便形成了一部无声的城市史诗。
text_pinyin: Rúguǒ yī zuò chéngshì de lùmiàn nénggòu jìlù xià suǒyǒu céngjīng zǒu guò tā de jiǎobù, nà tā yīdìng shì shìjiè shàng zuì fùzá de wénběn. Měi yī shuāng jiǎo dōu zài shàngmiàn xiě xià le zìjǐ de jùzi——yǒuxiē cōngmáng ér jícù, shì zǎo gāofēng gǎn dìtiě de tōngqín zhě; yǒuxiē huǎnmàn ér chénzhòng, shì tí zhe cài lánzi zǒu huí jiā de lǎorén; yǒuxiē qīngyíng ér tiàoyuè, shì fàngxué hòu zhuīzhú xīnào de háizi. Zhèxiē jiǎobù jiāodié zài yīqǐ, xíngchéng le yī gè búduàn bèi fùxiě de wénběn——měi tiān dōu yǒu xīn de jùzi xiě shàngqù, dàn jiù de jùzi bìng méiyǒu zhēnzhèng xiāoshī, zhǐshì bèi mái zài le gēngxīn de céngcì zhī xià. Chéngshì guīhuà zhě kěyǐ tōngguò yī tiáo lùmiàn de mósǔn chéngdù lái pànduàn zhè tiáo lù bèi shǐyòng de pínlǜ. Shíbǎn lù shàng bèi cǎi de zuì guānghuá de dìfāng, tōngcháng jiùshì zuì duō rén xíngzǒu de lùxiàn. Yǒuxiē gǔzhèn de shíjiē zhōngjiān yǒu yī dào míngxiǎn de āoxiàn, nà shì jǐ bǎi nián lái wúshù shuāng jiǎo zài tóng yī wèizhì fǎnfù cǎità de jiéguǒ. Měi yī gè cǎi guò zhège āoxiàn de rén, dōu wúxíng zhōng cānyù le yī chǎng chíxù shù bǎi nián de jítǐ diāosù. Wǒ zài yī zuò gǔchéng de chéngqiáng shàng sànbù shí, zhùyì dào mǒuxiē shízhuān bǐ qítā de guānghuá de duō. Dǎoyóu gàosù wǒ, nà shì yīnwèi jiùshí shǒu chéng de shìbīng měi tiān zài gùdìng de wèizhì xúnluó, rì fù yī rì, nián fù yī nián, tāmen de jiǎodǐ bǎ shízhuān mó chū le yī tiáo jìnhū yú guānghuá de guǐjì. Nàxiē shìbīng zǎoyǐ huà wéi chéntǔ, dàn tāmen rì fù yī rì de xíngzǒu, zhìjīn hái kè zài shítou shàng. Lùmiàn shì yī gè méiyǒu zuòzhě shǔmíng de jítǐ chuàngzuò——měi gè rén dōu gòngxiàn le wēi bù zúdào de yī bù, dàn yì wàn bù diéjiā zài yīqǐ, biàn xíngchéng le yī bù wúshēng de chéngshì shǐshī.
text_en: If a city's road surface could record every footstep that ever crossed it, it would certainly be the world's most complex text. Each pair of feet writes its own sentence on it—some hurried and rushed, the morning commuters racing for the subway; some slow and heavy, the elderly carrying vegetable baskets walking home; some light and bouncing, children chasing and playing after school. These footsteps overlap, forming a continuously overwritten text—new sentences are written every day, but old ones haven't truly disappeared, merely buried beneath newer layers. Urban planners can judge how frequently a road is used by its degree of wear. The smoothest spots on a stone-paved road are usually the most-walked routes. Some ancient towns have a visible concavity in the middle of stone steps—the result of countless feet stepping in the same position over hundreds of years. Everyone who has stepped into that concavity has unknowingly participated in a collective sculpture spanning centuries. While walking on the city wall of an ancient city, I noticed that certain stone bricks were much smoother than others. The guide told me it was because soldiers guarding the walls in the old days patrolled fixed positions daily, day after day, year after year, their feet wearing a nearly smooth track into the brick. Those soldiers have long since turned to dust, but their daily walking is still inscribed in the stone. A road surface is a collective creation without any author's signature—each person contributes an insignificant step, but billions of steps layered together form a silent urban epic.

questions.q_zh: 古镇石阶上的凹陷被作者理解为什么？
questions.q_en: How does the author interpret the concavity in the stone steps of ancient towns?

options.text: 几百年来无数人在同一位置行走形成的集体雕塑

options.text: 建筑设计的缺陷

options.text_en: A defect in architectural design

options.text: 地震造成的裂缝
options.pinyin: dìzhèn zàochéng de lièfèng
options.text_en: Cracks caused by earthquakes
