title: The Loneliness of Breakfast
text_zh: 所有的餐食中，早餐是最孤独的一顿。午餐和晚餐往往是社交性的——同事一起去食堂，家人围坐在桌前——但早餐通常是一个人的事。你刚从睡眠中醒来，还没有完全进入社会角色，意识仍然处于私人和公共之间的灰色地带。在这个短暂的窗口期里，你与食物之间的关系是最纯粹的——不是为了社交，不是为了仪式，只是一个身体在对另一个清晨的交代。中国城市的早餐摊位是观察社会最好的窗口。每天凌晨四点，卖豆浆油条的摊主就已经开始准备了。他们在路灯还亮着的街头支起摊位，在昏暗中开始揉面、炸油条、磨豆浆。当城市的其余部分还在沉睡时，他们已经完成了一天中最重要的工作。排队买早餐的人们形成了一种奇特的临时社区。平时互不相识的邻居在等待油条出锅的几分钟里交换着关于天气和物价的简短评论。这种对话没有任何深度，却有一种令人安慰的重复性——它确认了这个世界还在正常运转，今天和昨天一样，明天大概也是如此。一碗热豆浆在清晨的冷空气中冒着蒸汽。你站在街边，用塑料勺搅动着它，看着白色的蒸汽被晨风吹散。整个城市正在你周围慢慢醒来——远处传来第一班公交车的引擎声，路灯一盏一盏地熄灭，天空从深蓝变成浅灰。你在这个巨大的苏醒过程中只是一个微小的参与者，却因为手里这碗热豆浆而感到与整个世界有了联系。
text_pinyin: Suǒyǒu de cānshí zhōng, zǎocān shì zuì gūdú de yī dùn. Wǔcān hé wǎncān wǎngwǎng shì shèjiāo xìng de——tóngshì yīqǐ qù shítáng, jiārén wéi zuò zài zhuō qián——dàn zǎocān tōngcháng shì yī gè rén de shì. Nǐ gāng cóng shuìmián zhōng xǐng lái, hái méiyǒu wánquán jìnrù shèhuì juésè, yìshi réngrán chǔ yú sīrén hé gōnggòng zhī jiān de huīsè dìdài. Zài zhège duǎnzàn de chuāngkǒu qī lǐ, nǐ yǔ shíwù zhī jiān de guānxì shì zuì chúncuì de——bú shì wèile shèjiāo, bú shì wèile yíshì, zhǐ shì yī gè shēntǐ zài duì lìng yī gè qīngchén de jiāodài. Zhōngguó chéngshì de zǎocān tānwèi shì guānchá shèhuì zuì hǎo de chuāngkǒu. Měi tiān língchén sì diǎn, mài dòujiāng yóutiáo de tānzhǔ jiù yǐjīng kāishǐ zhǔnbèi le. Tāmen zài lùdēng hái liàng zhe de jiētóu zhī qǐ tānwèi, zài hūn'àn zhōng kāishǐ róu miàn, zhá yóutiáo, mó dòujiāng. Dāng chéngshì de qíyú bùfen hái zài chénshuì shí, tāmen yǐjīng wánchéng le yī tiān zhōng zuì zhòngyào de gōngzuò. Páiduì mǎi zǎocān de rénmen xíngchéng le yī zhǒng qítè de línshí shèqū. Píngshí hù bù xiāngshí de línjū zài děngdài yóutiáo chū guō de jǐ fēnzhōng lǐ jiāohuàn zhe guānyú tiānqì hé wùjià de jiǎnduǎn pínglùn. Zhè zhǒng duìhuà méiyǒu rènhé shēndù, què yǒu yī zhǒng lìng rén ānwèi de chóngfù xìng——tā quèrèn le zhège shìjiè hái zài zhèngcháng yùnzhuǎn, jīntiān hé zuótiān yīyàng, míngtiān dàgài yě shì rúcǐ. Yī wǎn rè dòujiāng zài qīngchén de lěng kōngqì zhōng mào zhe zhēngqì. Nǐ zhàn zài jiēbiān, yòng sùliào sháo jiǎodòng zhe tā, kàn zhe bái sè de zhēngqì bèi chénfēng chuī sàn. Zhěnggè chéngshì zhèngzài nǐ zhōuwéi mànmàn xǐng lái——yuǎn chù chuán lái dì yī bān gōngjiāo chē de yǐnqíng shēng, lùdēng yī zhǎn yī zhǎn de xīmiè, tiānkōng cóng shēn lán biànchéng qiǎn huī. Nǐ zài zhège jùdà de sūxǐng guòchéng zhōng zhǐ shì yī gè wēixiǎo de cānyù zhě, què yīnwèi shǒu lǐ zhè wǎn rè dòujiāng ér gǎn dào yǔ zhěnggè shìjiè yǒu le liánxì.
text_en: Of all meals, breakfast is the loneliest. Lunch and dinner tend to be social—colleagues heading to the cafeteria together, family gathered around the table—but breakfast is usually a solitary affair. You have just woken from sleep, not yet fully entered your social role; your consciousness still lingers in the gray zone between private and public. In this brief window, your relationship with food is at its purest—not for socializing, not for ceremony, just one body accounting for another morning. Breakfast stalls in Chinese cities are the best windows for observing society. Every day at four in the morning, the vendors selling soy milk and fried dough sticks have already begun preparations. They set up their stalls under still-lit streetlamps, kneading dough, frying youtiao, and grinding soybeans in the dimness. While the rest of the city still sleeps, they have already completed the day's most important work. The people lining up for breakfast form a peculiar temporary community. Neighbors who normally don't know each other exchange brief comments about weather and prices during the few minutes waiting for youtiao to come out of the oil. These conversations have no depth whatsoever, yet possess a comforting repetitiveness—they confirm that the world is still running normally, that today is like yesterday, and tomorrow will likely be the same. A bowl of hot soy milk steams in the cold morning air. You stand by the roadside, stirring it with a plastic spoon, watching the white steam scatter in the morning breeze. The entire city is slowly waking around you—the engine sound of the first bus comes from the distance, streetlights go out one by one, the sky shifts from deep blue to pale gray. You are merely a tiny participant in this immense awakening, yet because of this bowl of hot soy milk in your hands, you feel connected to the entire world.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说早餐是所有餐食中最孤独的一顿？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say breakfast is the loneliest of all meals?

title: The Memory of Spice
text_zh: 花椒的味道不是辣，而是麻——一种独特的、令舌头发颤的触电感。第一次吃花椒的人往往会被这种感觉吓到：嘴唇开始微微发麻，舌尖像被一千根极细的针同时轻触，整个口腔的感知系统仿佛被重新校准了一遍。这不是味觉体验，而是触觉体验——花椒激活的不是味蕾，而是皮肤上感知振动的神经末梢。四川人对花椒的依赖几乎可以上升到哲学层面。在这片潮湿闷热的盆地里，麻辣不仅仅是一种口味偏好，更是一种对抗气候的生存策略。花椒和辣椒的刺激能够驱散体内的湿气，让人在溽暑中保持一种清醒而亢奋的状态。久而久之，这种生理需求内化为文化基因——四川人的性格和他们的食物一样，热烈、直接、不拐弯抹角。我的四川朋友每次出远门都会随身携带一小罐花椒面。他说这不是因为外地的菜不好吃，而是因为花椒的味道里藏着他的整个童年——母亲做的麻婆豆腐、街角那家面馆的红油抄手、夏天夜晚在路边吃串串时满桌的花椒油香。一种调料可以是一把打开记忆之门的钥匙。你闻到某种气味的瞬间，时间会突然折叠，把你直接送回十年前、二十年前的某个具体场景。普鲁斯特用一块玛德琳蛋糕打开了记忆的闸门，而中国人用的是一粒花椒。
text_pinyin: Huājiāo de wèidào bú shì là, ér shì má——yī zhǒng dútè de, lìng shétou fā chàn de chùdiàn gǎn. Dì yī cì chī huājiāo de rén wǎngwǎng huì bèi zhè zhǒng gǎnjué xià dào: zuǐchún kāishǐ wēiwēi fā má, shéjiān xiàng bèi yī qiān gēn jí xì de zhēn tóngshí qīng chù, zhěnggè kǒuqiāng de gǎnzhī xìtǒng fǎngfú bèi chóngxīn jiàozhǔn le yī biàn. Zhè bú shì wèijué tǐyàn, ér shì chùjué tǐyàn——huājiāo jīhuó de bú shì wèilěi, ér shì pífū shàng gǎnzhī zhèndòng de shénjīng mòshāo. Sìchuān rén duì huājiāo de yīlài jīhū kěyǐ shàngshēng dào zhéxué céngmiàn. Zài zhè piàn cháoshī mēnrè de péndì lǐ, málà bù jǐnjǐn shì yī zhǒng kǒuwèi piānhǎo, gèng shì yī zhǒng duìkàng qìhòu de shēngcún cèlüè. Huājiāo hé làjiāo de cìjī nénggòu qūsàn tǐnèi de shīqì, ràng rén zài rùshǔ zhōng bǎochí yī zhǒng qīngxǐng ér kàngfèn de zhuàngtài. Jiǔ ér jiǔ zhī, zhè zhǒng shēnglǐ xūqiú nèihuà wéi wénhuà jīyīn——Sìchuān rén de xìnggé hé tāmen de shíwù yīyàng, rèliè, zhíjiē, bù guǎiwān-mòjiǎo. Wǒ de Sìchuān péngyou měi cì chū yuǎnmén dōu huì suíshēn xiédài yī xiǎo guàn huājiāo miàn. Tā shuō zhè bú shì yīnwèi wàidì de cài bù hǎochī, ér shì yīnwèi huājiāo de wèidào lǐ cáng zhe tā de zhěnggè tóngnián——mǔqīn zuò de mápó dòufu, jiējiǎo nà jiā miànguǎn de hóngyóu chāoshǒu, xiàtiān yèwǎn zài lùbiān chī chuànchuàn shí mǎn zhuō de huājiāo yóu xiāng. Yī zhǒng tiáoliào kěyǐ shì yī bǎ dǎkāi jìyì zhī mén de yàoshi. Nǐ wén dào mǒu zhǒng qìwèi de shùnjiān, shíjiān huì tūrán zhédié, bǎ nǐ zhíjiē sòng huí shí nián qián, èrshí nián qián de mǒu gè jùtǐ chǎngjǐng. Pǔlǔsītè yòng yī kuài mǎdélín dàngāo dǎkāi le jìyì de zhámén, ér Zhōngguó rén yòng de shì yī lì huājiāo.
text_en: The taste of Sichuan peppercorn is not spicy but numbing—a unique, electric tingling that makes the tongue tremble. People eating it for the first time are often startled by this sensation: the lips begin to tingle faintly, the tip of the tongue feels as if touched simultaneously by a thousand impossibly fine needles, and the entire oral perception system seems to be recalibrated. This is not a taste experience but a tactile one—Sichuan peppercorn activates not taste buds but the nerve endings in the skin that perceive vibration. Sichuanese dependence on Sichuan peppercorn can almost be elevated to a philosophical level. In this humid, sweltering basin, the numbing-spicy combination is not merely a flavor preference but a survival strategy against the climate. The stimulation of peppercorn and chili can dispel internal dampness, keeping one alert and invigorated in the oppressive heat. Over time, this physiological need internalized into cultural DNA—Sichuanese character, like their food, is passionate, direct, and without beating around the bush. My Sichuanese friend always carries a small jar of ground peppercorn whenever traveling far. He says this is not because food elsewhere is bad, but because the taste of peppercorn holds his entire childhood—his mother's mapo tofu, the red-oil wontons at the noodle shop on the street corner, the peppercorn oil fragrance covering the table while eating skewers on summer nights by the roadside. A single seasoning can be a key that opens the door to memory. The instant you smell a certain scent, time suddenly folds, sending you directly back to a specific scene ten or twenty years ago. Proust used a madeleine cake to open the floodgates of memory; Chinese people use a single Sichuan peppercorn.

questions.q_en: What does the author explain the 'numbing' of Sichuan peppercorn to be physiologically?

options.text: 为了展示自己的文学知识
options.pinyin: wèile zhǎnshì zìjǐ de wénxué zhīshi
options.text_en: To display literary knowledge

questions.explanation: "Chinese people use a single Sichuan peppercorn" — the passage traces motive through action; the why sits inside the what.

title: The Ethics of Waste
text_zh: 在中国的传统观念中，浪费食物是一种近乎原罪式的行为。「谁知盘中餐，粒粒皆辛苦」——这首几乎每个中国孩子都会背诵的唐诗，在童年时代就将食物与劳动之间的因果链条刻进了我们的道德基底。浪费食物不仅仅是「可惜」，而是对种植者、收割者、运输者、烹饪者整条生产链上所有劳动者的一种轻慢。然而现实中，中国的餐桌浪费却是世界上最触目惊心的之一。这种矛盾源于一个更深层的文化逻辑：在中国的待客之道中，「让客人吃不完」才是慷慨的标志。如果桌上的菜恰好被吃光了，主人会觉得自己点少了、招待不周；只有剩下大量的菜肴，才能证明主人的诚意和排场。于是，每一场中国式的宴请都成了一场浪费与面子之间的博弈。客人明知吃不完也不好意思说够了，主人明知会浪费也不敢少点——因为在面子的计量体系中，少即是失。我曾在一个乡村婚宴上目睹了一幕令我至今难忘的场景。宴席结束后，新娘的奶奶一个人悄悄地拿着塑料袋，把每张桌子上没吃完的菜一样一样地装起来。她的动作很轻、很快，仿佛不想被任何人看到。这个经历过饥荒年代的老人，身体里住着一种比面子更古老的记忆——那是对饥饿的恐惧，一种骨子里明白每一粒粮食的重量的人才有的、近乎虔诚的节俭。
text_pinyin: Zài Zhōngguó de chuántǒng guānniàn zhōng, làngfèi shíwù shì yī zhǒng jìnhū yuánzuì shì de xíngwéi.「Shuí zhī pán zhōng cān, lìlì jiē xīnkǔ」——zhè shǒu jīhū měi gè Zhōngguó háizi dōu huì bèisòng de Táng shī, zài tóngnián shídài jiù jiāng shíwù yǔ láodòng zhī jiān de yīnguǒ liántiáo kè jìn le wǒmen de dàodé jīdǐ. Làngfèi shíwù bù jǐnjǐn shì「kěxī」, ér shì duì zhòngzhí zhě, shōugē zhě, yùnshū zhě, pēngrèn zhě zhěng tiáo shēngchǎn liàn shàng suǒyǒu láodòng zhě de yī zhǒng qīngmàn. Rán'ér xiànshí zhōng, Zhōngguó de cānzhuō làngfèi què shì shìjiè shàng zuì chùmù-jīngxīn de zhī yī. Zhè zhǒng máodùn yuán yú yī gè gèng shēncéng de wénhuà luójí: zài Zhōngguó de dàikè zhī dào zhōng,「ràng kèrén chī bù wán」cái shì kāngkǎi de biāozhì. Rúguǒ zhuō shàng de cài qiàhǎo bèi chī guāng le, zhǔrén huì juéde zìjǐ diǎn shǎo le, zhāodài bù zhōu; zhǐ yǒu shèng xià dàliàng de càiyáo, cái néng zhèngmíng zhǔrén de chéngyì hé páichǎng. Yúshì, měi yī chǎng Zhōngguó shì de yànqǐng dōu chéng le yī chǎng làngfèi yǔ miànzi zhī jiān de bóyì. Kèrén míng zhī chī bù wán yě bù hǎoyìsi shuō gòu le, zhǔrén míng zhī huì làngfèi yě bù gǎn shǎo diǎn——yīnwèi zài miànzi de jìliàng tǐxì zhōng, shǎo jí shì shī. Wǒ céng zài yī gè xiāngcūn hūnyàn shàng mùdǔ le yī mù lìng wǒ zhìjīn nánwàng de chǎngjǐng. Yànxí jiéshù hòu, xīnniáng de nǎinai yī gè rén qiāoqiāo de ná zhe sùliào dài, bǎ měi zhāng zhuōzi shàng méi chī wán de cài yī yàng yī yàng de zhuāng qǐlái. Tā de dòngzuò hěn qīng, hěn kuài, fǎngfú bù xiǎng bèi rènhé rén kàn dào. Zhège jīnglì guò jīhuāng niándài de lǎorén, shēntǐ lǐ zhù zhe yī zhǒng bǐ miànzi gèng gǔlǎo de jìyì——nà shì duì jī'è de kǒngjù, yī zhǒng gǔzi lǐ míngbai měi yī lì liángshi de zhòngliàng de rén cái yǒu de, jìnhū qiánchéng de jiéjiǎn.
text_en: In traditional Chinese thought, wasting food is an act bordering on original sin. 'Who knows the food on the plate, every grain comes from bitter toil'—this Tang dynasty poem, memorized by nearly every Chinese child, etches the causal chain between food and labor into our moral foundation from childhood. Wasting food is not merely 'a pity' but a slight against every laborer along the entire production chain—planter, harvester, transporter, cook. Yet in reality, Chinese table waste is among the most staggering in the world. This contradiction springs from a deeper cultural logic: in Chinese hospitality, 'making sure guests cannot finish' is the mark of generosity. If the dishes happen to be eaten clean, the host feels they ordered too little and showed poor hospitality; only a large quantity of remaining dishes can prove the host's sincerity and grandeur. Thus every Chinese-style banquet becomes a game between waste and face. Guests know they cannot finish but are too embarrassed to say enough; hosts know it will be wasted but dare not order less—because in the accounting system of face, less equals loss. I once witnessed a scene at a rural wedding banquet that I still cannot forget. After the feast ended, the bride's grandmother quietly went around alone with plastic bags, packing up leftover dishes from every table one by one. Her movements were light and quick, as if she didn't want anyone to see. This old woman who had lived through famine years carried within her body a memory older than face—a fear of hunger, a nearly devout frugality that belongs only to those who understand in their bones the weight of every grain.

options.text: 中国菜太好吃了所以总是点太多

questions.q_zh: 新娘奶奶悄悄打包剩菜的行为代表了什么？
questions.q_en: What does the bride's grandmother quietly packing leftovers represent?

options.text: 她很节俭想省钱
options.pinyin: tā hěn jiéjiǎn xiǎng shěng qián
options.text_en: She is frugal and wants to save money

options.text: 一种比面子更古老的记忆——经历过饥荒的人对每粒粮食的虔诚珍惜
options.pinyin: yī zhǒng bǐ miànzi gèng gǔlǎo de jìyì——jīnglì guò jīhuāng de rén duì měi lì liángshi de qiánchéng zhēnxī
options.text_en: A memory older than face—the devout cherishing of every grain by someone who lived through famine

options.text: 她对婚宴的铺张感到不满
options.pinyin: tā duì hūnyàn de pūzhāng gǎn dào bùmǎn

title: The Tea That Waited
text_zh: 泡一壶好茶需要等待——但和煲汤不同，茶的等待是以秒来计量的。第一泡十五秒，第二泡二十秒，第三泡三十秒……每一泡的时间都在延长，因为茶叶中容易溶出的成分已经在前几泡中释放完毕，剩下的需要更多的时间来萃取。这种精确到秒的时间控制，让泡茶变成了一种接近于冥想的行为——你必须全神贯注，因为多一秒就苦了，少一秒就淡了。中国的茶文化中有一个概念叫「留根」——泡茶时不把茶汤倒尽，而是留下三分之一在壶中，作为下一泡的底味。这个做法的智慧在于：它让每一泡之间保持了连续性，前一泡的余韵自然地过渡到下一泡中，形成了一种味觉上的叙事连贯性。这让我想到人与人之间的关系也需要「留根」。每一次交谈结束时，不要把话说尽——留一些未尽之意，让对方在下次见面时有了继续的理由。把话说满了，关系反而就到头了。我最喜欢的泡茶时刻是第五泡或第六泡——这时茶叶已经完全舒展开来，味道从最初的浓烈变得澄澈而透明，如同一段音乐从激昂的主题进入了安静的尾声。有经验的茶客把这个阶段叫做「水路开了」，意思是茶与水之间终于找到了平衡——不是茶压过了水，也不是水稀释了茶，而是两者融为一体，你分不清你喝下的是茶还是水。这种「不分彼此」的状态，或许就是所有关系的最高境界。
text_pinyin: Pào yī hú hǎo chá xūyào děngdài——dàn hé bāo tāng bùtóng, chá de děngdài shì yǐ miǎo lái jìliàng de. Dì yī pào shíwǔ miǎo, dì èr pào èrshí miǎo, dì sān pào sānshí miǎo……měi yī pào de shíjiān dōu zài yáncháng, yīnwèi cháyè zhōng róngyì róng chū de chéngfèn yǐjīng zài qián jǐ pào zhōng shìfàng wánbì, shèng xià de xūyào gèng duō de shíjiān lái cuìqǔ. Zhè zhǒng jīngquè dào miǎo de shíjiān kòngzhì, ràng pào chá biànchéng le yī zhǒng jiējìn yú míngxiǎng de xíngwéi——nǐ bìxū quánshén-guànzhù, yīnwèi duō yī miǎo jiù kǔ le, shǎo yī miǎo jiù dàn le. Zhōngguó de chá wénhuà zhōng yǒu yī gè gàiniàn jiào「liú gēn」——pào chá shí bù bǎ chá tāng dào jìn, ér shì liú xià sān fēn zhī yī zài hú zhōng, zuòwéi xià yī pào de dǐ wèi. Zhège zuòfǎ de zhìhuì zàiyú: tā ràng měi yī pào zhī jiān bǎochí le liánxù xìng, qián yī pào de yúyùn zìrán de guòdù dào xià yī pào zhōng, xíngchéng le yī zhǒng wèijué shàng de xùshì liánguàn xìng. Zhè ràng wǒ xiǎng dào rén yǔ rén zhī jiān de guānxì yě xūyào「liú gēn」. Měi yī cì jiāotán jiéshù shí, bú yào bǎ huà shuō jìn——liú yīxiē wèi jìn zhī yì, ràng duìfāng zài xià cì jiànmiàn shí yǒu le jìxù de lǐyóu. Bǎ huà shuō mǎn le, guānxì fǎn'ér jiù dàotóu le. Wǒ zuì xǐhuan de pào chá shíkè shì dì wǔ pào huò dì liù pào——zhè shí cháyè yǐjīng wánquán shūzhǎn kāilái, wèidào cóng zuìchū de nóngliè biàn de chéngchè ér tòumíng, rútóng yī duàn yīnyuè cóng jī'áng de zhǔtí jìnrù le ānjìng de wěishēng. Yǒu jīngyàn de chákè bǎ zhège jiēduàn jiàozuò「shuǐlù kāi le」, yìsi shì chá yǔ shuǐ zhī jiān zhōngyú zhǎo dào le pínghéng——bú shì chá yā guò le shuǐ, yě bú shì shuǐ xīshì le chá, ér shì liǎng zhě róng wéi yītǐ, nǐ fēn bù qīng nǐ hē xià de shì chá háishi shuǐ. Zhè zhǒng「bù fēn bǐcǐ」de zhuàngtài, huòxǔ jiù shì suǒyǒu guānxì de zuìgāo jìngjiè.
text_en: Brewing a good pot of tea requires waiting—but unlike soup-making, tea's waiting is measured in seconds. The first steep: fifteen seconds. The second: twenty. The third: thirty. Each infusion's time grows longer, because the easily soluble components in the tea leaves have already been released in earlier steeps, and what remains needs more time to extract. This second-precise time control turns tea-brewing into an act approaching meditation—you must be utterly focused, because one second too many and it turns bitter, one second too few and it's bland. Chinese tea culture has a concept called 'leaving the root'—when brewing, you do not pour out all the tea liquor but leave about a third in the pot as the base flavor for the next steep. The wisdom of this practice is that it maintains continuity between each infusion; the lingering notes of the previous steep naturally transition into the next, forming a narrative coherence of flavor. This makes me think that relationships between people also need 'leaving the root.' At the end of every conversation, don't say everything—leave some meaning unfinished, giving the other person a reason to continue when you next meet. When everything has been said, the relationship has actually reached its end. My favorite moment in tea-brewing is the fifth or sixth steep—by then the leaves have fully unfurled, and the taste has transformed from its initial intensity to something clear and transparent, like a piece of music moving from its passionate theme into a quiet coda. Experienced tea drinkers call this stage 'the water path has opened,' meaning tea and water have finally found balance—not tea overpowering water, nor water diluting tea, but the two merging into one, and you cannot tell whether what you're drinking is tea or water.

title: The Geography of Noodles
text_zh: 中国的面条版图是一张比任何政治地图都更真实的文化地图。从北到南、从东到西，面条的宽窄厚薄、汤底的清浊浓淡、浇头的丰简繁约，忠实地记录了每一片土地上的气候、物产和人的性格。兰州拉面的汤底清亮如镜，萝卜片漂浮其上，几缕牛肉丝和一撮翠绿的蒜苗构成了全部的点缀。这种近乎禁欲的简洁，映射的是西北大地的辽阔与粗犷——在那里，人们不需要太多修饰，一碗面能填饱肚子就够了。而武汉的热干面则完全是另一种哲学——芝麻酱浓稠地裹住每一根面条，酸豆角和葱花混杂其中，吃起来需要大口大口地搅拌，那种混沌而浓烈的口感，恰如这座长江与汉水交汇的城市的脾气——热情、杂糅、不怕混乱。苏州的汤面是文人的面。那碗汤清得像一面镜子，浇头精致得如同微雕——一只大虾、两片笋尖、几根翡翠般的菜心，每一样都摆放得恰到好处。苏州人吃面讲究「宽汤窄面」，面要少，汤要多，吃的不是饱腹感，而是一种审美体验。这种精致的矜持和上海的浓油赤酱形成了有趣的对照——两座相距不过一百公里的城市，在面条上却体现出了截然不同的文化性格。一碗面条就是一本浓缩的地方志。你不需要翻阅任何文献，只要吃上一口当地的面，就能大致推断出这片土地上的人是内敛还是豪放，是精致还是粗犷，是喜欢确定性还是拥抱混乱。
text_pinyin: Zhōngguó de miàntiáo bǎntú shì yī zhāng bǐ rènhé zhèngzhì dìtú dōu gèng zhēnshí de wénhuà dìtú. Cóng běi dào nán, cóng dōng dào xī, miàntiáo de kuān zhǎi hòu báo, tāng dǐ de qīng zhuó nóng dàn, jiāotou de fēng jiǎn fán yuē, zhōngshí de jìlù le měi yī piàn tǔdì shàng de qìhòu, wùchǎn hé rén de xìnggé. Lánzhōu lāmiàn de tāng dǐ qīngliàng rú jìng, luóbo piàn piāofú qí shàng, jǐ lǚ niúròu sī hé yī cuō cuìlǜ de suànmiáo gòuchéng le quánbù de diǎnzhuì. Zhè zhǒng jìnhū jìnyù de jiǎnjié, yìngshè de shì xīběi dàdì de liáokuò yǔ cūguǎng——zài nàlǐ, rénmen bù xūyào tài duō xiūshì, yī wǎn miàn néng tián bǎo dùzi jiù gòu le. Ér Wǔhàn de rè gān miàn zé wánquán shì lìng yī zhǒng zhéxué——zhīmajiàng nóngchóu de guǒ zhù měi yī gēn miàntiáo, suān dòujiǎo hé cōnghuā hùnzá qízhōng, chī qǐlái xūyào dà kǒu dà kǒu de jiǎobàn, nà zhǒng hùndùn ér nóngliè de kǒugǎn, qià rú zhè zuò Chángjiāng yǔ Hànshuǐ jiāohuì de chéngshì de píqi——rèqíng, záróu, bú pà hùnluàn. Sūzhōu de tāng miàn shì wénrén de miàn. Nà wǎn tāng qīng de xiàng yī miàn jìngzi, jiāotou jīngzhì de rútóng wēidiāo——yī zhī dà xiā, liǎng piàn sǔn jiān, jǐ gēn fěicuì bān de càixīn, měi yī yàng dōu bǎifàng de qiàdào-hǎochù. Sūzhōu rén chī miàn jiǎngjiū「kuān tāng zhǎi miàn」, miàn yào shǎo, tāng yào duō, chī de bú shì bǎofù gǎn, ér shì yī zhǒng shěnměi tǐyàn. Zhè zhǒng jīngzhì de jīnchí hé Shànghǎi de nóng yóu chì jiàng xíngchéng le yǒuqù de duìzhào——liǎng zuò xiāngjù bú guò yī bǎi gōnglǐ de chéngshì, zài miàntiáo shàng què tǐxiàn chū le jiérán bùtóng de wénhuà xìnggé. Yī wǎn miàntiáo jiù shì yī běn nóngsuō de dìfāng zhì. Nǐ bù xūyào fānyuè rènhé wénxiàn, zhǐ yào chī shàng yī kǒu dāngdì de miàn, jiù néng dàzhì tuīduàn chū zhè piàn tǔdì shàng de rén shì nèiliǎn háishi háofàng, shì jīngzhì háishi cūguǎng, shì xǐhuan quèdìng xìng háishi yōngbào hùnluàn.
text_en: China's noodle map is a cultural map more authentic than any political one. From north to south, east to west, the width and thickness of noodles, the clarity and richness of broth, the abundance or simplicity of toppings faithfully record each region's climate, produce, and human temperament. Lanzhou hand-pulled noodle broth is clear as a mirror, with radish slices floating on top, a few strands of beef and a pinch of bright green garlic sprouts composing all the garnish. This near-ascetic simplicity mirrors the vastness and ruggedness of the northwest—where people need little adornment; a bowl of noodles that fills the belly is enough. Wuhan's hot-dry noodles embody an entirely different philosophy—thick sesame paste coats every strand, pickled green beans and scallions mixed throughout, requiring vigorous stirring with big mouthfuls. That chaotic and intense flavor matches this city at the confluence of the Yangtze and Han rivers—passionate, blended, unafraid of disorder. Suzhou's soup noodles are the literati's noodles. The broth is clear as a mirror, the toppings refined as miniature carvings—one large prawn, two bamboo shoot tips, a few jade-like vegetable hearts, each placed just so. Suzhou people insist on 'generous broth, restrained noodles'—less noodle, more soup. What you eat is not fullness but an aesthetic experience. This refined restraint contrasts interestingly with Shanghai's rich-oil-red-sauce style—two cities barely a hundred kilometers apart, yet their noodles reveal utterly different cultural characters. A bowl of noodles is a condensed local gazetteer. You need not consult any text; just take one bite of the local noodles and you can roughly infer whether the people of this land are reserved or bold, refined or rugged, fond of certainty or embracing of chaos.

title: The Fermentation of Time
text_zh: 发酵是中国最古老的烹饪智慧之一——它的本质是把时间变成一种调料。酱油需要半年到一年的日晒夜露，豆腐乳需要数月的霉菌培养，老陈醋需要至少五年的陈酿。在一个追求速度的时代，这些食物的存在本身就是一种挑衅——它们在说：「你可以加速一切，但你加速不了我。」发酵的过程中发生了什么？简单来说，微生物正在对原材料进行一场漫长而精密的分解与重组。大豆中的蛋白质被分解成氨基酸，产生了「鲜味」；糖分被转化为有机酸和醇类，创造了复杂的香气层次。这些变化如此缓慢，以至于你每天去看都觉得什么也没发生——但三个月后、半年后，当你打开那个密封的坛子，扑面而来的浓郁醇香会告诉你：时间一直在工作，只是它选择了沉默。我外婆会做一种古法酱油。每年春天，她把煮熟的大豆摊在竹匾上，搬到阳台上晒。然后封进陶缸里，每天打开缸盖翻搅一次，让阳光均匀地照到每一粒大豆。这个过程需要持续一整个夏天。秋天时，陶缸里的大豆已经变成了深褐色的浓稠液体——那就是酱油。整个过程没有添加任何化学物质，唯一的添加剂是时间。每次我用超市买来的瓶装酱油时，总觉得少了什么——不是少了味道，而是少了那个「等待了一整个夏天」的仪式感。工业生产可以在几天内模拟出发酵的化学结果，但它无法模拟的是那个过程中包含的耐心、期待和人与自然节律之间的默契。
text_pinyin: Fājiào shì Zhōngguó zuì gǔlǎo de pēngrèn zhìhuì zhī yī——tā de běnzhì shì bǎ shíjiān biànchéng yī zhǒng tiáoliào. Jiàngyóu xūyào bàn nián dào yī nián de rì shài yè lù, dòufu rǔ xūyào shù yuè de méijūn péiyǎng, lǎo chéncù xūyào zhìshǎo wǔ nián de chénniàng. Zài yī gè zhuīqiú sùdù de shídài, zhèxiē shíwù de cúnzài běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng tiǎoxìn——tāmen zài shuō:「Nǐ kěyǐ jiāsù yīqiè, dàn nǐ jiāsù bù liǎo wǒ.」Fājiào de guòchéng zhōng fāshēng le shénme? Jiǎndān lái shuō, wēishēngwù zhèngzài duì yuán cáiliào jìnxíng yī chǎng màncháng ér jīngmì de fēnjiě yǔ chóngzǔ. Dàdòu zhōng de dànbáizhì bèi fēnjiě chéng ānjīsuān, chǎnshēng le「xiān wèi」; tángfèn bèi zhuǎnhuà wéi yǒujī suān hé chún lèi, chuàngzào le fùzá de xiāngqì céngcì. Zhèxiē biànhuà rúcǐ huǎnmàn, yǐzhìyú nǐ měi tiān qù kàn dōu juéde shénme yě méi fāshēng——dàn sān gè yuè hòu, bàn nián hòu, dāng nǐ dǎkāi nàge mìfēng de tánzi, pū miàn ér lái de nóngyù chúnxiāng huì gàosu nǐ: shíjiān yīzhí zài gōngzuò, zhǐ shì tā xuǎnzé le chénmò. Wǒ wàipó huì zuò yī zhǒng gǔfǎ jiàngyóu. Měi nián chūntiān, tā bǎ zhǔ shú de dàdòu tān zài zhúbiǎn shàng, bān dào yángtái shàng shài. Ránhòu fēng jìn táo gāng lǐ, měi tiān dǎkāi gāng gài fānjiǎo yī cì, ràng yángguāng jūnyún de zhào dào měi yī lì dàdòu. Zhège guòchéng xūyào chíxù yī zhěnggè xiàtiān. Qiūtiān shí, táo gāng lǐ de dàdòu yǐjīng biànchéng le shēn hè sè de nóngchóu yètǐ——nà jiù shì jiàngyóu. Zhěnggè guòchéng méiyǒu tiānjiā rènhé huàxué wùzhì, wéiyī de tiānjiā jì shì shíjiān. Měi cì wǒ yòng chāoshì mǎi lái de píngzhuāng jiàngyóu shí, zǒng juéde shǎo le shénme——bú shì shǎo le wèidào, ér shì shǎo le nàge「děngdài le yī zhěnggè xiàtiān」de yíshì gǎn. Gōngyè shēngchǎn kěyǐ zài jǐ tiān nèi mónǐ chū fājiào de huàxué jiéguǒ, dàn tā wúfǎ mónǐ de shì nàge guòchéng zhōng bāohán de nàixīn, qīdài hé rén yǔ zìrán jiélǜ zhī jiān de mòqì.
text_en: Fermentation is one of China's oldest culinary wisdoms—its essence is turning time into a seasoning. Soy sauce needs half a year to a year of sun and dew exposure; fermented tofu requires months of mold cultivation; aged Shanxi vinegar needs at least five years of aging. In an era obsessed with speed, the very existence of these foods is a provocation—they say: 'You can accelerate everything, but you cannot accelerate me.' What happens during fermentation? Simply put, microorganisms conduct a prolonged and precise decomposition and reassembly of raw materials. Proteins in soybeans break down into amino acids, producing umami; sugars convert into organic acids and alcohols, creating complex layers of aroma. These changes are so slow that checking daily you feel nothing has happened—but three months later, half a year later, when you open that sealed jar, the rich, mellow fragrance that hits you announces: time has been working all along; it simply chose silence. My maternal grandmother knew how to make traditional soy sauce. Every spring she spread cooked soybeans on bamboo trays and carried them to the balcony to sun-dry. Then she sealed them in clay urns, opening the lid once daily to stir so sunlight reached every bean evenly. This process lasted an entire summer. By autumn, the soybeans in the clay urn had become a deep brown, thick liquid—that was soy sauce. The entire process added no chemical substances; the only additive was time. Whenever I use bottled soy sauce from the supermarket, I always feel something is missing—not flavor, but the ritual feeling of 'having waited an entire summer.' Industrial production can simulate fermentation's chemical results in days, but what it cannot simulate is the patience, anticipation, and the rapport between human beings and nature's rhythm contained in that process.

title: The Silence of Tofu
text_zh: 豆腐是中国食材中最沉默的一种。它没有颜色——白得近乎透明；没有味道——淡得几乎等于无味；没有形状——软得可以被切成任何形态。它是一块空白的画布，等待着厨师的赋予。正是这种「无」，让它成为了中国烹饪中最具可塑性的食材——它可以是麻婆豆腐中的火辣主角，也可以是鲫鱼汤中的温柔配角，还可以被冻成冻豆腐，拥有完全不同的多孔质地。豆腐的制作过程本身就是一个关于「转化」的寓言。大豆——坚硬、粗糙、难以消化——经过浸泡、研磨、过滤、煮沸，变成了豆浆——流动、柔滑、易于吸收。然后加入卤水或石膏，蛋白质在凝固剂的作用下结成絮状，再经过压制脱水，成为我们看到的方方正正的豆腐。这个过程是一次从硬到软、从粗到细、从固到液再到固的漫长旅程——它让我想到，一个人的成长或许也是如此：你需要先被打碎，再被重新凝聚，才能变成更好的质地。日本人把豆腐的美学推到了极致。在京都的百年老铺里，师傅们用泉水磨豆浆，用天然卤水点豆腐，追求的是一种「入口即化」的极致柔嫩。他们说，最好的豆腐应该让你忘记你在吃豆腐——它只是一种口感，一种温度，一种若有若无的大豆清香。这让我想起老子说的「大象无形，大音希声」——最高级的存在，往往是最不引人注目的存在。
text_pinyin: Dòufu shì Zhōngguó shícái zhōng zuì chénmò de yī zhǒng. Tā méiyǒu yánsè——bái de jìnhū tòumíng; méiyǒu wèidào——dàn de jīhū děngyú wú wèi; méiyǒu xíngzhuàng——ruǎn de kěyǐ bèi qiē chéng rènhé xíngtài. Tā shì yī kuài kòngbái de huàbù, děngdài zhe chúshī de fùyǔ. Zhèng shì zhè zhǒng「wú」, ràng tā chéngwéi le Zhōngguó pēngrèn zhōng zuì jù kěsù xìng de shícái——tā kěyǐ shì mápó dòufu zhōng de huǒlà zhǔjué, yě kěyǐ shì jìyú tāng zhōng de wēnróu pèijué, hái kěyǐ bèi dòng chéng dòng dòufu, yōngyǒu wánquán bùtóng de duōkǒng zhìdì. Dòufu de zhìzuò guòchéng běnshēn jiù shì yī gè guānyú「zhuǎnhuà」de yùyán. Dàdòu——jiānyìng, cūcāo, nányǐ xiāohuà——jīngguò jìnpào, yánmó, guòlǜ, zhǔfèi, biànchéng le dòujiāng——liúdòng, róuhuá, yìyú xīshōu. Ránhòu jiārù lǔshuǐ huò shígāo, dànbáizhì zài nínggù jì de zuòyòng xià jié chéng xù zhuàng, zài jīngguò yāzhì tuōshuǐ, chéngwéi wǒmen kàn dào de fāngfāng-zhèngzhèng de dòufu. Zhège guòchéng shì yī cì cóng yìng dào ruǎn, cóng cū dào xì, cóng gù dào yè zài dào gù de màncháng lǚchéng——tā ràng wǒ xiǎng dào, yī gè rén de chéngzhǎng huòxǔ yě shì rúcǐ: nǐ xūyào xiān bèi dǎ suì, zài bèi chóngxīn níngjù, cái néng biànchéng gèng hǎo de zhìdì. Rìběn rén bǎ dòufu de měixué tuī dào le jízhì. Zài Jīngdū de bǎi nián lǎo pù lǐ, shīfumen yòng quánshuǐ mó dòujiāng, yòng tiānrán lǔshuǐ diǎn dòufu, zhuīqiú de shì yī zhǒng「rùkǒu jí huà」de jízhì róunèn. Tāmen shuō, zuì hǎo de dòufu yīnggāi ràng nǐ wàngjì nǐ zài chī dòufu——tā zhǐ shì yī zhǒng kǒugǎn, yī zhǒng wēndù, yī zhǒng ruò yǒu ruò wú de dàdòu qīngxiāng. Zhè ràng wǒ xiǎng qǐ Lǎozǐ shuō de「dà xiàng wú xíng, dà yīn xī shēng」——zuì gāojí de cúnzài, wǎngwǎng shì zuì bù yǐn rén zhùmù de cúnzài.
text_en: Tofu is the most silent of Chinese ingredients. It has no color—white to the point of near-transparency; no flavor—so bland as to be nearly tasteless; no form—so soft it can be cut into any shape. It is a blank canvas, awaiting the cook's endowment. It is precisely this 'nothingness' that makes it the most malleable ingredient in Chinese cooking—it can be the fiery protagonist of mapo tofu, the gentle supporting player in crucian carp soup, or be frozen into a completely different porous texture. The process of making tofu is itself a parable about 'transformation.' Soybeans—hard, rough, difficult to digest—are soaked, ground, filtered, and boiled into soy milk—fluid, silky, easily absorbed. Then bittern or gypsum is added; proteins coagulate into curds under the agent's effect, and after pressing and draining, they become the neat square of tofu we know. This process is a long journey from hard to soft, coarse to fine, solid to liquid to solid again—it makes me think that perhaps a person's growth is similar: you need to be broken apart first, then re-coagulated, before you can become a better texture. The Japanese have pushed tofu aesthetics to their ultimate expression. In Kyoto's century-old shops, masters grind soy milk with spring water and set tofu with natural bittern, pursuing an ultimate tenderness that 'melts the moment it enters the mouth.' They say the best tofu should make you forget you are eating tofu—it is merely a texture, a temperature, a barely perceptible soybean fragrance.

title: The Storyteller's Dumpling
text_zh: 包饺子是中国家庭中为数不多的需要全家人围坐在一起的集体劳动。和面、擀皮、调馅、包制、煮熟——每一个环节都可以分配给不同的人，让每个家庭成员都有参与感。这种分工协作的结构，让包饺子天然地成为了家庭叙事的载体：人们在手上忙着的时候，嘴巴反而放松了，那些平时不好意思说出口的话，在擀面杖来回滚动的节奏中自然地流淌出来。我记得小时候每年除夕包饺子的场景。外婆坐在桌子最中央，负责调馅——这是最核心的工序，因为馅的味道决定了一切。她一边往肉馅里加盐和酱油，一边讲述那些我已经听过无数遍的家族往事。母亲在旁边擀皮，偶尔纠正外婆记忆中的细节偏差。父亲负责包——他的手法很笨拙，包出来的饺子歪歪扭扭，但他坚持认为味道和形状无关。我和妹妹则负责在每个饺子底下撒一层薄薄的面粉，防止粘连。每个人都有自己的位置，每个人都不可或缺。饺子皮里包的不仅仅是肉馅，还有那些在包的过程中说出来的故事、争论、笑话和叹息。每一个饺子都是一个微型的时间胶囊，封存了那个特定的除夕夜里发生的一切。当你咬开饺子的那一刻，你同时咬开了一段记忆。这也许就是为什么，无论在世界的哪个角落，中国人过年都要吃饺子——不是因为饺子本身有多好吃，而是因为包饺子的那个过程，才是真正的年味。
text_pinyin: Bāo jiǎozi shì Zhōngguó jiātíng zhōng wéishù bù duō de xūyào quán jiā rén wéi zuò zài yīqǐ de jítǐ láodòng. Huó miàn, gǎn pí, tiáo xiàn, bāozhì, zhǔ shú——měi yī gè huánjié dōu kěyǐ fēnpèi gěi bùtóng de rén, ràng měi gè jiātíng chéngyuán dōu yǒu cānyù gǎn. Zhè zhǒng fēngōng-xiézuò de jiégòu, ràng bāo jiǎozi tiānrán de chéngwéi le jiātíng xùshì de zàitǐ: rénmen zài shǒu shàng máng zhe de shíhou, zuǐba fǎn'ér fàngsōng le, nàxiē píngshí bù hǎoyìsi shuō chūkǒu de huà, zài gǎnmiànzhàng láihuí gǔndòng de jiézòu zhōng zìrán de liútǎng chūlái. Wǒ jìde xiǎo shíhou měi nián Chúxī bāo jiǎozi de chǎngjǐng. Wàipó zuò zài zhuōzi zuì zhōngyāng, fùzé tiáo xiàn——zhè shì zuì héxīn de gōngxù, yīnwèi xiàn de wèidào juédìng le yīqiè. Tā yībiān wǎng ròu xiàn lǐ jiā yán hé jiàngyóu, yībiān jiǎngshù nàxiē wǒ yǐjīng tīng guò wúshù biàn de jiāzú wǎngshì. Mǔqīn zài pángbiān gǎn pí, ǒu'ěr jiūzhèng wàipó jìyì zhōng de xìjié piānchā. Fùqīn fùzé bāo——tā de shǒufǎ hěn bènzhuō, bāo chūlái de jiǎozi wāiwāi-niǔniǔ, dàn tā jiānchí rènwéi wèidào hé xíngzhuàng wúguān. Wǒ hé mèimei zé fùzé zài měi gè jiǎozi dǐxia sā yī céng báobáo de miànfěn, fángzhǐ zhānlián. Měi gè rén dōu yǒu zìjǐ de wèizhì, měi gè rén dōu bùkě-huòquē. Jiǎozi pí lǐ bāo de bù jǐnjǐn shì ròu xiàn, hái yǒu nàxiē zài bāo de guòchéng zhōng shuō chūlái de gùshi, zhēnglùn, xiàohua hé tànxī. Měi yī gè jiǎozi dōu shì yī gè wēixíng de shíjiān jiāonáng, fēngcún le nàge tèdìng de Chúxī yè lǐ fāshēng de yīqiè. Dāng nǐ yǎo kāi jiǎozi de nà yī kè, nǐ tóngshí yǎo kāi le yī duàn jìyì. Zhè yěxǔ jiù shì wèishénme, wúlùn zài shìjiè de nǎge jiǎoluò, Zhōngguó rén guònián dōu yào chī jiǎozi——bú shì yīnwèi jiǎozi běnshēn yǒu duō hǎochī, ér shì yīnwèi bāo jiǎozi de nàge guòchéng, cái shì zhēnzhèng de nián wèi.
text_en: Making dumplings is one of the few collective labors in a Chinese family that requires everyone to sit together. Mixing dough, rolling wrappers, preparing filling, wrapping, and cooking—each step can be assigned to different people, giving every family member a sense of participation. This structure of division and collaboration naturally makes dumpling-making a vehicle for family narrative: when hands are busy, mouths relax, and words normally too embarrassing to speak flow naturally to the rhythm of the rolling pin going back and forth. I remember the scene of making dumplings every New Year's Eve as a child. Grandmother sat at the center of the table, in charge of the filling—the core process, because the filling's flavor determined everything. She added salt and soy sauce to the meat filling while narrating family stories I had heard countless times. Mother rolled wrappers beside her, occasionally correcting details in grandmother's memory. Father was in charge of wrapping—his technique was clumsy, producing lopsided dumplings, but he insisted taste had nothing to do with shape. My sister and I were responsible for sprinkling a thin layer of flour beneath each dumpling to prevent sticking. Everyone had their position; everyone was indispensable. What the dumpling wrappers enclosed was not just meat filling, but also the stories, arguments, jokes, and sighs spoken during the wrapping process. Every dumpling is a miniature time capsule, preserving everything that happened on that particular New Year's Eve. The moment you bite into a dumpling, you simultaneously bite into a memory.

options.text: 因为手忙的时候嘴巴反而放松，平时不好说的话在劳动节奏中自然流淌

options.text_en: When hands are busy, mouths relax; words normally hard to say flow naturally in the rhythm of labor

options.text: 因为中国家庭平时很少在一起吃饭

questions.q_zh: 作者认为中国人过年一定要吃饺子的真正原因是什么？
questions.q_en: What does the author believe is the real reason Chinese people must eat dumplings for New Year?

options.text: 饺子的味道特别好吃
options.pinyin: jiǎozi de wèidào tèbié hǎochī
options.text_en: Dumplings taste especially good

title: The Night Market's Grammar
text_zh: 夜市有自己的语法。它的主语是烟火气，谓语是吆喝声，宾语是那些无法在任何正式餐厅里找到的、只属于街头的食物。夜市不是餐饮的补充，而是正规餐饮体系之外的另一个平行世界——在这里，所有关于卫生、装潢、服务的标准都被暂时搁置，唯一的标准是味道本身。夜市的食物之所以好吃，有一个很少有人提到的原因：它们是在你面前现做的。你能看到面团在案板上被拍打的过程，能看到肉串在炭火上翻转时油脂滴落引起的小型火焰，能看到调料一勺一勺地被撒上去。这种透明性本身就是一种信任的建立——你知道你吃的是什么，它从哪里来，经过了怎样的处理。在一个食品安全焦虑弥漫的时代，这种「眼见为实」的确定感具有一种独特的安慰力量。夜市也是社会阶层最民主的空间之一。在这里，穿着西装的白领和穿着拖鞋的工人站在同一个摊位前，吃着同样的烤串，擦着同样的塑料桌子。食物在这一刻成为了一个伟大的平等器——它不在乎你的收入、学历和社会地位，它只在乎你是否饿了。我最怀念的夜市味道不是任何一道具体的食物，而是所有食物的味道混合在一起之后形成的那种独特的「夜市气味」——烤肉的焦香、臭豆腐的浓烈、糖葫芦的甜腻、啤酒的苦涩——这些互相矛盾的气味神奇地融合在了夜晚的空气里，形成了一种只有在夜市才能闻到的、无法被任何人工香精复制的味道。
text_pinyin: Yèshì yǒu zìjǐ de yǔfǎ. Tā de zhǔyǔ shì yānhuǒ qì, wèiyǔ shì yāohe shēng, bīnyǔ shì nàxiē wúfǎ zài rènhé zhèngshì cāntīng lǐ zhǎo dào de, zhǐ shǔyú jiētóu de shíwù. Yèshì bú shì cānyǐn de bǔchōng, ér shì zhèngguī cānyǐn tǐxì zhī wài de lìng yī gè píngxíng shìjiè——zài zhèlǐ, suǒyǒu guānyú wèishēng, zhuānghuáng, fúwù de biāozhǔn dōu bèi zànshí gēzhì, wéiyī de biāozhǔn shì wèidào běnshēn. Yèshì de shíwù zhī suǒyǐ hǎochī, yǒu yī gè hěn shǎo yǒu rén tí dào de yuányīn: tāmen shì zài nǐ miànqián xiàn zuò de. Nǐ néng kàn dào miàntuán zài ànbǎn shàng bèi pāidǎ de guòchéng, néng kàn dào ròu chuàn zài tàn huǒ shàng fānzhuǎn shí yóuzhī dī luò yǐnqǐ de xiǎoxíng huǒyàn, néng kàn dào tiáoliào yī sháo yī sháo de bèi sā shàng qù. Zhè zhǒng tòumíng xìng běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng xìnrèn de jiànlì——nǐ zhīdào nǐ chī de shì shénme, tā cóng nǎlǐ lái, jīngguò le zěnyàng de chǔlǐ. Zài yī gè shípǐn ānquán jiāolǜ mímàn de shídài, zhè zhǒng「yǎnjiàn-wéishí」de quèdìng gǎn jùyǒu yī zhǒng dútè de ānwèi lìliàng. Yèshì yě shì shèhuì jiēcéng zuì mínzhǔ de kōngjiān zhī yī. Zài zhèlǐ, chuān zhe xīzhuāng de báilǐng hé chuān zhe tuōxié de gōngrén zhàn zài tóng yī gè tānwèi qián, chī zhe tóngyàng de kǎo chuàn, cā zhe tóngyàng de sùliào zhuōzi. Shíwù zài zhè yī kè chéngwéi le yī gè wěidà de píngděng qì——tā bú zàihu nǐ de shōurù, xuélì hé shèhuì dìwèi, tā zhǐ zàihu nǐ shìfǒu è le. Wǒ zuì huáiniàn de yèshì wèidào bú shì rènhé yī dào jùtǐ de shíwù, ér shì suǒyǒu shíwù de wèidào hùnhé zài yīqǐ zhīhòu xíngchéng de nà zhǒng dútè de「yèshì qìwèi」——kǎoròu de jiāo xiāng, chòu dòufu de nóngliè, tánghúlu de tián nì, píjiǔ de kǔsè——zhèxiē hùxiāng máodùn de qìwèi shénqí de rónghé zài le yèwǎn de kōngqì lǐ, xíngchéng le yī zhǒng zhǐ yǒu zài yèshì cái néng wén dào de, wúfǎ bèi rènhé réngōng xiāngjīng fùzhì de wèidào.
text_en: Night markets have their own grammar. Their subject is the smoky vitality of cooking fires, the predicate is hawkers' calls, and the object is food that cannot be found in any formal restaurant—food that belongs only to the street. A night market is not a supplement to dining but a parallel world outside the formal dining system—where all standards of hygiene, decor, and service are temporarily set aside, and the only standard is taste itself. There is a seldom-mentioned reason night market food tastes so good: it is made right before your eyes. You can watch dough being slapped on the cutting board, meat skewers turning over charcoal as dripping fat ignites small flames, and seasonings being sprinkled on spoonful by spoonful. This transparency itself establishes trust—you know what you're eating, where it came from, how it was handled. In an era pervaded by food-safety anxiety, this 'seeing is believing' certainty carries a unique power of reassurance. Night markets are also among the most democratic spaces across social classes. Here, white-collar workers in suits and laborers in flip-flops stand before the same stall, eating the same skewers, wiping the same plastic tables. Food in this moment becomes a great equalizer—it cares nothing for your income, education, or social status; it cares only whether you are hungry. The night market scent I miss most is not any specific dish, but the unique 'night market smell' formed when all foods' aromas blend together—the char of grilled meat, the pungent intensity of stinky tofu, the cloying sweetness of candied hawthorn, the bitterness of beer—these contradictory scents magically fuse in the night air, forming a flavor that can only be smelled at a night market and cannot be replicated by any artificial fragrance.

title: The Inheritance of Flavor
text_zh: 每个人的味觉都有一个「原点」——那是你在童年时期形成的口味基准线，此后你一生中遇到的所有味道，都会被不自觉地拿来和这个原点比较。你觉得某家餐厅的红烧肉「不够好」，往往不是因为它客观上不好吃，而是因为它和你妈妈做的红烧肉不一样。这个味觉原点的力量之大，大到可以跨越几十年的时间和几千公里的距离。我认识一个在法国生活了三十年的中国厨师。他在巴黎开了一家融合餐厅，菜单上是精致的法式烹饪技巧与中国食材的结合。但每周日休息的时候，他会一个人在厨房里做一碗最简单的西红柿鸡蛋面——用最普通的挂面，加上两个鸡蛋和一个西红柿，连味精都不放。他说：「这碗面没有任何技术含量，但它是我十岁时放学回家后我妈给我做的，是我所有烹饪记忆的起点。我可以做出米其林三星级别的菜，但这碗面是我永远无法超越的——因为它包含的不是技术，而是一种无法复制的「对的感觉」。」味觉的传承比任何文化传承都更加脆弱。一道菜的做法可以写成食谱流传下去，但那些无法量化的东西——盐应该「随手抓一把」还是「用拇指和食指捏一撮」？油温应该「冒小泡」还是「冒大泡」时下锅？——这些模糊的、依赖于身体感知的知识，只能从一双手传到另一双手，从一个灶台传到另一个灶台。当最后一个会做某道菜的人离去时，那道菜就永远地消失了——不是消失在文字记录里，而是消失在了人类味觉记忆的版图上。
text_pinyin: Měi gè rén de wèijué dōu yǒu yī gè「yuándiǎn」——nà shì nǐ zài tóngnián shíqī xíngchéng de kǒuwèi jīzhǔn xiàn, cǐhòu nǐ yīshēng zhōng yùdào de suǒyǒu wèidào, dōu huì bèi bú zìjué de ná lái hé zhège yuándiǎn bǐjiào. Nǐ juéde mǒu jiā cāntīng de hóngshāo ròu「bú gòu hǎo」, wǎngwǎng bú shì yīnwèi tā kèguān shàng bù hǎochī, ér shì yīnwèi tā hé nǐ māma zuò de hóngshāo ròu bù yīyàng. Zhège wèijué yuándiǎn de lìliàng zhī dà, dà dào kěyǐ kuàyuè jǐ shí nián de shíjiān hé jǐ qiān gōnglǐ de jùlí. Wǒ rènshi yī gè zài Fǎguó shēnghuó le sānshí nián de Zhōngguó chúshī. Tā zài Bālí kāi le yī jiā rónghé cāntīng, càidān shàng shì jīngzhì de Fǎshì pēngrèn jìqiǎo yǔ Zhōngguó shícái de jiéhé. Dàn měi zhōurì xiūxi de shíhou, tā huì yī gè rén zài chúfáng lǐ zuò yī wǎn zuì jiǎndān de xīhóngshì jīdàn miàn——yòng zuì pǔtōng de guàmiàn, jiā shàng liǎng gè jīdàn hé yī gè xīhóngshì, lián wèijīng dōu bú fàng. Tā shuō:「Zhè wǎn miàn méiyǒu rènhé jìshù hánliàng, dàn tā shì wǒ shí suì shí fàngxué huíjiā hòu wǒ mā gěi wǒ zuò de, shì wǒ suǒyǒu pēngrèn jìyì de qǐdiǎn. Wǒ kěyǐ zuò chū Mǐqílín sān xīng jíbié de cài, dàn zhè wǎn miàn shì wǒ yǒngyuǎn wúfǎ chāoyuè de——yīnwèi tā bāohán de bú shì jìshù, ér shì yī zhǒng wúfǎ fùzhì de「duì de gǎnjué」.」Wèijué de chuánchéng bǐ rènhé wénhuà chuánchéng dōu gèng jiā cuìruò. Yī dào cài de zuòfǎ kěyǐ xiě chéng shípǔ liúchuán xiàqù, dàn nàxiē wúfǎ liànghuà de dōngxi——yán yīnggāi「suíshǒu zhuā yī bǎ」háishi「yòng mǔzhǐ hé shízhǐ niē yī cuō」? Yóu wēn yīnggāi「mào xiǎo pào」háishi「mào dà pào」shí xià guō?——zhèxiē móhu de, yīlài yú shēntǐ gǎnzhī de zhīshi, zhǐ néng cóng yī shuāng shǒu chuán dào lìng yī shuāng shǒu, cóng yī gè zàotái chuán dào lìng yī gè zàotái. Dāng zuìhòu yī gè huì zuò mǒu dào cài de rén lí qù shí, nà dào cài jiù yǒngyuǎn de xiāoshī le——bú shì xiāoshī zài wénzì jìlù lǐ, ér shì xiāoshī zài le rénlèi wèijué jìyì de bǎntú shàng.
text_en: Everyone's palate has an 'origin point'—a taste baseline formed during childhood against which every flavor encountered throughout life is unconsciously compared. When you feel a restaurant's braised pork 'isn't quite right,' it is often not because it is objectively bad, but because it differs from how your mother made it. The power of this taste origin is so great it can span decades and thousands of kilometers. I know a Chinese chef who has lived in France for thirty years. He runs a fusion restaurant in Paris, its menu combining refined French techniques with Chinese ingredients. But every Sunday on his day off, he makes himself the simplest bowl of tomato-egg noodles alone in the kitchen—using the most ordinary dried noodles, two eggs, and one tomato, not even adding MSG. He says: 'This bowl has zero technical sophistication, but it is what my mother made me when I came home from school at age ten; it is the starting point of all my cooking memories. I can produce Michelin three-star dishes, but this bowl of noodles is something I can never surpass—because what it contains is not technique, but an irreplicable feeling of rightness.' The inheritance of flavor is more fragile than any cultural inheritance. A dish's method can be written into a recipe and passed on, but those unquantifiable things—should salt be 'grabbed by the handful' or 'pinched between thumb and forefinger'? Should ingredients go in when the oil shows 'small bubbles' or 'large bubbles'?—this fuzzy knowledge dependent on bodily perception can only pass from one pair of hands to another, from one stove to another. When the last person who knows how to make a certain dish departs, that dish disappears forever—not from written records, but from the map of human taste memory.

title: The Alchemy of Leftovers
text_zh: 蛋炒饭是中国烹饪中最朴素的炼金术——它把最不起眼的剩饭变成了一道独立的、有尊严的菜肴。这个转化的关键在于「隔夜」二字。新鲜的米饭含水量太高，下锅后会粘成一团；只有经过一夜的风干，米饭表面失去了多余的水分，每一粒都变得独立而干爽，才能在猛火的翻炒中粒粒分明。换句话说，蛋炒饭的灵魂不是鸡蛋，不是葱花，而是时间——那个让米饭从「剩余」变成「恰好」的隔夜过程。这种「化腐朽为神奇」的思维方式贯穿了中国烹饪的许多角落。昨天的馒头蒸硬了？切片炸一炸就是金黄酥脆的炸馒头片。前天的米饭吃不完？加水煮成粥，配上一碟酱菜，就是一顿温暖的早餐。剩下的面条凉了？用油煎到两面焦黄，就是一份酥脆的煎面。中国人的厨房里几乎没有「废弃物」的概念——每一样东西都有第二次生命的可能。我曾问过一位老厨师，中国烹饪最核心的精神是什么。他想了很久，说了两个字：「不舍」。不是舍不得扔，而是舍不得辜负——辜负食材本身蕴含的可能性。每一粒米都来之不易，你有责任让它以最好的方式被食用。如果今天没被吃完，那就给它一个明天变得更好的机会。蛋炒饭就是这种哲学的最佳证明：昨天的剩饭，加上今天的鸡蛋和猛火，成为了明天的经典。
text_pinyin: Dàn chǎofàn shì Zhōngguó pēngrèn zhōng zuì pǔsù de liànjīn shù——tā bǎ zuì bù qǐyǎn de shèng fàn biànchéng le yī dào dúlì de, yǒu zūnyán de càiyáo. Zhège zhuǎnhuà de guānjiàn zàiyú「géyè」èr zì. Xīnxiān de mǐfàn hánshuǐ liàng tài gāo, xià guō hòu huì zhān chéng yī tuán; zhǐ yǒu jīngguò yī yè de fēnggān, mǐfàn biǎomiàn shīqù le duōyú de shuǐfèn, měi yī lì dōu biàn de dúlì ér gānshuǎng, cái néng zài měng huǒ de fānchǎo zhōng lìlì-fēnmíng. Huàn jù huà shuō, dàn chǎofàn de línghún bú shì jīdàn, bú shì cōnghuā, ér shì shíjiān——nàge ràng mǐfàn cóng「shèngyú」biànchéng「qiàhǎo」de géyè guòchéng. Zhè zhǒng「huà fǔxiǔ wéi shénqí」de sīwéi fāngshì guànchuān le Zhōngguó pēngrèn de xǔduō jiǎoluò. Zuótiān de mántou zhēng yìng le? Qiē piàn zhá yī zhá jiù shì jīnhuáng sūcuì de zhá mántou piàn. Qiántiān de mǐfàn chī bù wán? Jiā shuǐ zhǔ chéng zhōu, pèi shàng yī dié jiàngcài, jiù shì yī dùn wēnnuǎn de zǎocān. Shèng xià de miàntiáo liáng le? Yòng yóu jiān dào liǎng miàn jiāo huáng, jiù shì yī fèn sūcuì de jiān miàn. Zhōngguó rén de chúfáng lǐ jīhū méiyǒu「fèiqì wù」de gàiniàn——měi yī yàng dōngxi dōu yǒu dì èr cì shēngmìng de kěnéng. Wǒ céng wèn guò yī wèi lǎo chúshī, Zhōngguó pēngrèn zuì héxīn de jīngshén shì shénme. Tā xiǎng le hěn jiǔ, shuō le liǎng gè zì:「Bù shě」. Bú shì shěbudé rēng, ér shì shěbudé gūfù——gūfù shícái běnshēn yùnhán de kěnéng xìng. Měi yī lì mǐ dōu lái zhī bù yì, nǐ yǒu zérèn ràng tā yǐ zuì hǎo de fāngshì bèi shíyòng. Rúguǒ jīntiān méi bèi chī wán, nà jiù gěi tā yī gè míngtiān biàn de gèng hǎo de jīhuì. Dàn chǎofàn jiù shì zhè zhǒng zhéxué de zuìjiā zhèngmíng: zuótiān de shèng fàn, jiā shàng jīntiān de jīdàn hé měng huǒ, chéngwéi le míngtiān de jīngdiǎn.
text_en: Egg fried rice is the most humble alchemy in Chinese cooking—it transforms the most unassuming leftover rice into an independent, dignified dish. The key to this transformation lies in two words: 'overnight.' Fresh rice has too high a moisture content and clumps when wok-fried; only after a night of air-drying, when the rice surface has lost excess moisture and each grain has become independent and dry, can it achieve that grain-by-grain clarity in the fierce heat of the wok. In other words, the soul of egg fried rice is not the egg, not the scallion, but time—that overnight process that transforms rice from 'leftover' to 'just right.' This 'turning the mundane into the magical' mentality runs through many corners of Chinese cooking. Yesterday's steamed bun went stale? Slice it, fry it, and you have golden crispy fried bun slices. Day-before-yesterday's rice unfinished? Add water, cook it into congee, pair it with a small dish of pickles, and it's a warm breakfast. Leftover noodles gone cold? Pan-fry until both sides are golden, and you have crispy fried noodles. In a Chinese kitchen there is almost no concept of 'waste'—everything has the possibility of a second life. I once asked a veteran chef what the core spirit of Chinese cooking is. He thought for a long time and said two characters: 'bu she'—reluctance to let go. Not reluctance to throw things away, but reluctance to betray—to betray the possibilities inherent in an ingredient. Every grain of rice came at a cost; you bear the responsibility to ensure it is consumed in the best way possible. If it wasn't eaten today, give it the chance to become something better tomorrow. Egg fried rice is the finest proof of this philosophy: yesterday's leftovers, plus today's eggs and fierce fire, become tomorrow's classic.

questions.q_zh: 为什么蛋炒饭必须用隔夜饭而不能用新鲜米饭？
questions.q_en: Why must egg fried rice use overnight rice rather than fresh rice?

options.text: 隔夜饭更有营养
options.pinyin: géyè fàn gèng yǒu yíngyǎng
options.text_en: Overnight rice is more nutritious

options.text: 新鲜米饭含水量太高会粘成一团，隔夜风干后每粒独立干爽才能粒粒分明
options.pinyin: xīnxiān mǐfàn hánshuǐ liàng tài gāo huì zhān chéng yī tuán, géyè fēnggān hòu měi lì dúlì gānshuǎng cái néng lìlì-fēnmíng
options.text_en: Fresh rice is too moist and clumps; after overnight drying each grain becomes independent and dry for distinct separation

options.text: 隔夜饭的味道更好
options.pinyin: géyè fàn de wèidào gèng hǎo
options.text_en: Overnight rice tastes better

options.text: 为了节约食材不浪费
options.pinyin: wèile jiéyuē shícái bù làngfèi
options.text_en: To save ingredients and avoid waste

questions.explanation: "only after a night of air-drying" grounds the reason in lived detail rather than abstract logic.

questions.q_zh: 老厨师说中国烹饪最核心的精神是「不舍」，这里的「不舍」指什么？
questions.q_en: When the veteran chef says the core spirit of Chinese cooking is 'bu she' (reluctance to let go), what does this mean?

options.text: 不舍得花钱买新食材
options.pinyin: shěbudé huā qián mǎi xīn shícái
options.text_en: Reluctance to spend money on new ingredients

options.text: 不舍得辜负食材本身蕴含的可能性，有责任让它以最好的方式被食用
options.pinyin: shěbudé gūfù shícái běnshēn yùnhán de kěnéng xìng, yǒu zérèn ràng tā yǐ zuì hǎo de fāngshì bèi shíyòng
options.text_en: Reluctance to betray the possibilities in an ingredient; responsibility to ensure it's consumed in the best way

options.text: 不舍得扔掉任何东西的节俭习惯
options.pinyin: shěbudé rēng diào rènhé dōngxi de jiéjiǎn xíguàn
options.text_en: A frugal habit of being unable to throw anything away

options.text: 不舍得让传统烹饪方法消失
options.pinyin: shěbudé ràng chuántǒng pēngrèn fāngfǎ xiāoshī
options.text_en: Reluctance to let traditional cooking methods disappear

questions.explanation: "Not reluctance to throw things away" — this is where the passage shows its hand.

title: The Banquet of Absence
text_zh: 中国人最重要的饭局，往往有一个空着的座位。那个座位属于一个不在场的人——可能是远在异国的孩子，可能是已经去世的长辈，也可能是因为某种原因无法到场的家人。没有人会坐到那个位子上去，也没有人会把它撤走。它就那样空着，安静地参与着整场聚餐。在中国的团年饭上，这种「为缺席者留位」的习俗尤其动人。一双筷子，一个碗，一杯酒，整整齐齐地摆在那里——和其他人的餐具毫无区别，仿佛那个人随时可能推门走进来，在自己的位置上坐下，然后像什么都没发生过一样开始吃饭。这不是仪式，这是一种拒绝接受缺席的倔强——我不管你在不在，你的位置永远在这里。食物的温度是有时间性的。一碗刚盛好的米饭会在二十分钟内从滚烫变成温热，再变成冰凉。那个空座位上的饭菜也会经历同样的冷却过程——这个过程残忍地提醒着在场的每一个人：他真的不在了。但即便如此，下一次团年饭上，那个位置依然会被留出来，那副碗筷依然会被摆好。因为在中国人的情感逻辑里，爱不会因为缺席而过期。一个人可以不在场，但他在这个家庭中的位置是永恒的——就像那把空着的椅子，它的空本身就是一种在场。也许世界上最动人的款待，不是为到来的人准备盛宴，而是为不会来的人留一个位置。
text_pinyin: Zhōngguó rén zuì zhòngyào de fànjú, wǎngwǎng yǒu yī gè kōng zhe de zuòwèi. Nàge zuòwèi shǔyú yī gè bú zàichǎng de rén——kěnéng shì yuǎn zài yìguó de háizi, kěnéng shì yǐjīng qùshì de zhǎngbèi, yě kěnéng shì yīnwèi mǒu zhǒng yuányīn wúfǎ dàochǎng de jiārén. Méiyǒu rén huì zuò dào nàge wèizi shàng qù, yě méiyǒu rén huì bǎ tā chè zǒu. Tā jiù nàyàng kōng zhe, ānjìng de cānyù zhe zhěng chǎng jùcān. Zài Zhōngguó de tuánnián fàn shàng, zhè zhǒng「wèi quēxí zhě liú wèi」de xísú yóuqí dòngrén. Yī shuāng kuàizi, yī gè wǎn, yī bēi jiǔ, zhěngzhěng-qíqí de bǎi zài nàlǐ——hé qítā rén de cānjù háo wú qūbié, fǎngfú nàge rén suíshí kěnéng tuī mén zǒu jìnlái, zài zìjǐ de wèizhì shàng zuò xià, ránhòu xiàng shénme dōu méi fāshēng guò yīyàng kāishǐ chīfàn. Zhè bú shì yíshì, zhè shì yī zhǒng jùjué jiēshòu quēxí de juéjiàng——wǒ bùguǎn nǐ zài bú zài, nǐ de wèizhì yǒngyuǎn zài zhèlǐ. Shíwù de wēndù shì yǒu shíjiān xìng de. Yī wǎn gāng chéng hǎo de mǐfàn huì zài èrshí fēnzhōng nèi cóng gǔntàng biànchéng wēnrè, zài biànchéng bīngliáng. Nàge kōng zuòwèi shàng de fàncài yě huì jīnglì tóngyàng de lěngquè guòchéng——zhège guòchéng cánrěn de tíxǐng zhe zàichǎng de měi yī gè rén: tā zhēn de bú zài le. Dàn jíbiàn rúcǐ, xià yī cì tuánnián fàn shàng, nàge wèizhì yīrán huì bèi liú chūlái, nà fù wǎn kuài yīrán huì bèi bǎi hǎo. Yīnwèi zài Zhōngguó rén de qínggǎn luójí lǐ, ài bú huì yīnwèi quēxí ér guòqī. Yī gè rén kěyǐ bú zàichǎng, dàn tā zài zhège jiātíng zhōng de wèizhì shì yǒnghéng de——jiù xiàng nà bǎ kōng zhe de yǐzi, tā de kōng běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng zàichǎng. Yěxǔ shìjiè shàng zuì dòngrén de kuǎndài, bú shì wèi dàolái de rén zhǔnbèi shèngyàn, ér shì wèi bú huì lái de rén liú yī gè wèizhì.
text_en: At Chinese people's most important meals, there is often an empty seat. That seat belongs to someone who is not present—perhaps a child far away in another country, perhaps a deceased elder, perhaps a family member unable to attend for some reason. No one will sit in that seat, and no one will remove it. It simply remains empty, quietly participating in the entire gathering. At the Chinese New Year reunion dinner, this custom of 'keeping a place for the absent' is especially moving. A pair of chopsticks, a bowl, a cup of wine, arranged neatly—indistinguishable from everyone else's place setting, as though that person might push through the door at any moment, sit in their place, and begin eating as if nothing had happened. This is not ritual; it is a stubborn refusal to accept absence—I don't care whether you are here or not; your place is always here. The temperature of food has temporality. A freshly served bowl of rice will go from scalding to warm to cold within twenty minutes. The food at that empty seat undergoes the same cooling process—cruelly reminding everyone present: they truly are gone. Yet even so, at the next reunion dinner, that spot will still be kept, those chopsticks will still be laid out. Because in the emotional logic of Chinese people, love does not expire because of absence. A person may not be present, but their position in the family is eternal—like that empty chair, its emptiness itself is a form of presence.

questions.q_zh: 作者提到食物冷却的过程在文中起什么作用？
questions.q_en: What role does the description of food cooling play in the text?

options.text: 说明应该趁热吃饭
options.pinyin: shuōmíng yīnggāi chèn rè chīfàn
options.text_en: To explain that food should be eaten while hot

options.text: 残忍地提醒在场者缺席是真实的——那个人真的不会来了
options.pinyin: cánrěn de tíxǐng zàichǎng zhě quēxí shì zhēnshí de——nàge rén zhēn de bú huì lái le
options.text_en: Cruelly reminding those present that the absence is real—that person truly will not come

options.text: 强调中国人重视食物的温度
options.pinyin: qiángdiào Zhōngguó rén zhòngshì shíwù de wēndù
options.text_en: Emphasizing that Chinese people value food temperature

options.text: 暗示聚餐的时间太长了
options.pinyin: ànshì jùcān de shíjiān tài cháng le
options.text_en: Implying the dinner lasted too long

questions.explanation: "A person may not be present" links evidence to thesis — remove it and the argument collapses.

questions.q_zh: 「空本身就是一种在场」这句话表达了什么深层含义？
questions.q_en: What deeper meaning does the phrase 'emptiness itself is a form of presence' express?

options.text: 空椅子有象征意义
options.pinyin: kōng yǐzi yǒu xiàngzhēng yìyì
options.text_en: The empty chair has symbolic meaning

options.text: 爱不会因为缺席而过期，一个人虽然不在场但在家庭中的位置是永恒的
options.pinyin: ài bú huì yīnwèi quēxí ér guòqī, yī gè rén suīrán bú zàichǎng dàn zài jiātíng zhōng de wèizhì shì yǒnghéng de
options.text_en: Love doesn't expire with absence; a person may be gone but their position in the family is eternal

options.text: 中国人很迷信不愿意撤走座位
options.pinyin: Zhōngguó rén hěn míxìn bú yuànyì chè zǒu zuòwèi
options.text_en: Chinese people are superstitious and unwilling to remove seats

options.text: 空着的座位让人感到不舒服
options.pinyin: kōng zhe de zuòwèi ràng rén gǎn dào bù shūfu
options.text_en: The empty seat makes people feel uncomfortable

questions.explanation: "A person may not be present" — the physical analogy controls the conceptual range.

title: The Bookshop That Vanished
text_zh: 城南有一家书店，我确信自己去过，但没有任何人能证实它的存在。那是一个秋天的傍晚，我在一条从未走过的巷子里迷了路，然后看到了一扇半开的木门，门楣上挂着一块褪色的匾额，写着「不期而遇书屋」。店内的灯光昏黄，书架从地面一直延伸到天花板，每一层都塞满了旧书。空气中弥漫着陈年纸张特有的微甜的霉味——那种味道让人想到地下室、阁楼和被遗忘的时间。店主是一个头发花白的老人，戴着一副圆框眼镜，正坐在柜台后面读一本已经翻得很旧的书。他抬头看了我一眼，没有说话，只是微微点了点头——那个动作的含义似乎是「随便看看吧」，又似乎是「我知道你会来的」。我在那里待了大约一个小时，翻了很多书，最后买了一本薄薄的诗集。付钱的时候我注意到，老人用的不是计算器，而是一把老式的算盘。他算得很慢，珠子碰撞发出清脆的声响，在安静的书店里格外分明。第二天我想再去的时候，那条巷子还在，但书店消失了——那扇门变成了一面完整的砖墙，仿佛从来不曾有过任何开口。我至今保留着那本诗集，但版权页上印着的出版社名字，我在任何数据库里都查不到。
text_pinyin: Chéng nán yǒu yī jiā shūdiàn, wǒ quèxìn zìjǐ qù guò, dàn méiyǒu rènhé rén néng zhèngshí tā de cúnzài. Nà shì yī gè qiūtiān de bàngwǎn, wǒ zài yī tiáo cóng wèi zǒu guò de xiàngzi lǐ mí le lù, ránhòu kàn dào le yī shàn bàn kāi de mù mén, ménméi shàng guà zhe yī kuài tuì sè de biǎn'é, xiě zhe「Bùqī'éryù Shūwū」. Diàn nèi de dēngguāng hūnhuáng, shūjià cóng dìmiàn yīzhí yánshēn dào tiānhuābǎn, měi yī céng dōu sāi mǎn le jiù shū. Kōngqì zhōng mímàn zhe chénnián zhǐzhāng tèyǒu de wēi tián de méi wèi——nà zhǒng wèidào ràng rén xiǎng dào dìxiàshì, gélóu hé bèi yíwàng de shíjiān. Diànzhǔ shì yī gè tóufa huābái de lǎorén, dài zhe yī fù yuán kuàng yǎnjìng, zhèng zuò zài guìtái hòumiàn dú yī běn yǐjīng fān de hěn jiù de shū. Tā táitóu kàn le wǒ yī yǎn, méiyǒu shuōhuà, zhǐ shì wēiwēi diǎn le diǎn tóu——nàge dòngzuò de hányì sìhū shì「suíbiàn kànkan ba」, yòu sìhū shì「wǒ zhīdào nǐ huì lái de」. Wǒ zài nàlǐ dāi le dàyuē yī gè xiǎoshí, fān le hěn duō shū, zuìhòu mǎi le yī běn báobáo de shījí. Fù qián de shíhou wǒ zhùyì dào, lǎorén yòng de bú shì jìsuànqì, ér shì yī bǎ lǎoshì de suànpán. Tā suàn de hěn màn, zhūzi pèngzhuàng fāchū qīngcuì de shēngxiǎng, zài ānjìng de shūdiàn lǐ géwài fēnmíng. Dì èr tiān wǒ xiǎng zài qù de shíhou, nà tiáo xiàngzi hái zài, dàn shūdiàn xiāoshī le——nà shàn mén biànchéng le yī miàn wánzhěng de zhuān qiáng, fǎngfú cóng lái bù céng yǒu guò rènhé kāikǒu. Wǒ zhìjīn bǎoliú zhe nà běn shījí, dàn bǎnquán yè shàng yìn zhe de chūbǎnshè míngzì, wǒ zài rènhé shùjùkù lǐ dōu chá bú dào.
text_en: In the south of the city there was a bookshop I am certain I visited, yet no one can confirm its existence. It was an autumn evening; I had gotten lost in an alley I'd never walked before, when I noticed a half-open wooden door with a faded signboard above it reading 'Serendipity Books.' The interior lighting was dim amber, bookshelves stretched from floor to ceiling, each shelf packed with old books. The air was thick with the slightly sweet mustiness unique to aged paper—a smell evoking basements, attics, and forgotten time. The owner was a white-haired old man wearing round spectacles, sitting behind the counter reading a well-worn book. He glanced up at me, said nothing, and merely nodded slightly—a gesture that seemed to mean 'browse as you like,' or perhaps 'I knew you would come.' I stayed about an hour, leafed through many books, and finally bought a slim poetry collection. When paying I noticed the old man used not a calculator but an old-fashioned abacus. He calculated slowly, the beads clicking crisply, uncommonly distinct in the quiet shop. The next day when I tried to return, the alley was still there, but the bookshop had vanished—the door had become a solid brick wall, as if no opening had ever existed. I still have that poetry collection, but the publisher's name printed on the copyright page cannot be found in any database.

questions.q_zh: 作者第二天再去找那家书店时发现了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the author find when trying to return to the bookshop the next day?

options.text: 书店关门了，门上挂着「暂停营业」的牌子
options.pinyin: shūdiàn guānmén le, mén shàng guà zhe「zàntíng yíngyè」de páizi
options.text_en: The bookshop was closed with a 'temporarily closed' sign

options.text: 巷子还在，但那扇门变成了一面完整的砖墙，仿佛从未有过开口
options.pinyin: xiàngzi hái zài, dàn nà shàn mén biànchéng le yī miàn wánzhěng de zhuān qiáng, fǎngfú cóng wèi yǒu guò kāikǒu
options.text_en: The alley was still there, but the door had become a solid brick wall, as if no opening ever existed

options.text: 书店搬走了，旁边邻居告诉他新地址
options.pinyin: shūdiàn bān zǒu le, pángbiān línjū gàosu tā xīn dìzhǐ
options.text_en: The bookshop had moved and a neighbor gave him the new address

options.text: 整条巷子都找不到了
options.pinyin: zhěng tiáo xiàngzi dōu zhǎo bú dào le
options.text_en: The entire alley could not be found

questions.explanation: "as if no opening had ever existed" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

questions.q_zh: 那本诗集至今留下了什么无法解释的谜团？
questions.q_en: What unexplainable mystery does the poetry collection still hold?

options.text: 书里的诗都是用古文写的无人能读
options.pinyin: shū lǐ de shī dōu shì yòng gǔwén xiě de wú rén néng dú
options.text_en: The poems are all written in classical Chinese that no one can read

options.text: 版权页上的出版社名字在任何数据库里都查不到
options.pinyin: bǎnquán yè shàng de chūbǎnshè míngzì zài rènhé shùjùkù lǐ dōu chá bú dào
options.text_en: The publisher's name on the copyright page cannot be found in any database

options.text: 书的页码是倒着印的
options.pinyin: shū de yèmǎ shì dào zhe yìn de
options.text_en: The page numbers are printed backwards

options.text: 每次翻开书内容都不一样
options.pinyin: měi cì fān kāi shū nèiróng dōu bù yīyàng
options.text_en: The content changes every time the book is opened

questions.explanation: "I still have that poetry collection" — this is where the passage shows its hand.

title: The Pianist in the Rain
text_zh: 每到雨天，小区花园里就会传来钢琴声。这件事本身并不奇怪——奇怪的是，花园里没有钢琴，也没有任何能够发出钢琴声的设备。我第一次注意到它是在一个梅雨季节的午后。雨淅淅沥沥地下着，我站在窗边看雨，突然听到了一段旋律——是德彪西的《雨中花园》。琴声不是从哪栋楼里传出来的，而是像从雨水本身中流淌出来的，仿佛每一滴雨落在地面、树叶和金属扶手上时，都被一只看不见的手编排成了音符。我下楼去寻找声音的来源。花园里空无一人，只有雨在下。但那段旋律依然清晰——我甚至能分辨出弹奏者在某些段落使用了踏板，因为音符之间有一种特殊的延绵感。我问了小区里的很多邻居。有些人说他们也听到过，但各人描述的曲目却不一样——有人听到的是肖邦，有人听到的是舒伯特，有人听到的是一首完全不认识的曲子。唯一的共同点是：只在雨天出现，只在花园附近能听到，而且每个人都觉得那个旋律恰好是自己最喜欢的那首。我后来查了小区的历史档案，发现这里在建成住宅之前是一所音乐学校，据说有一位年轻的钢琴教师在一个雨夜里突发心脏病去世了。但这或许只是巧合。也或许雨水本身就懂得音乐——毕竟，水滴击打不同材质的表面时产生的频率组合，在某些条件下确实可能形成类似旋律的声学现象。但这个解释太过理性，让人觉得遗憾——我更愿意相信花园里住着一个弹钢琴的幽灵，他只在雨天演奏，而且总是弹每个听众最喜欢的曲子。
text_pinyin: Měi dào yǔtiān, xiǎoqū huāyuán lǐ jiù huì chuán lái gāngqín shēng. Zhè jiàn shì běnshēn bìng bù qíguài——qíguài de shì, huāyuán lǐ méiyǒu gāngqín, yě méiyǒu rènhé nénggòu fāchū gāngqín shēng de shèbèi. Wǒ dì yī cì zhùyì dào tā shì zài yī gè méiyǔ jìjié de wǔhòu. Yǔ xīxī-lìlì de xià zhe, wǒ zhàn zài chuāng biān kàn yǔ, tūrán tīng dào le yī duàn xuánlǜ——shì Débīxī de《Yǔ Zhōng Huāyuán》. Qín shēng bú shì cóng nǎ dòng lóu lǐ chuán chūlái de, ér shì xiàng cóng yǔshuǐ běnshēn zhōng liútǎng chūlái de, fǎngfú měi yī dī yǔ luò zài dìmiàn, shùyè hé jīnshǔ fúshǒu shàng shí, dōu bèi yī zhī kàn bú jiàn de shǒu biānpái chéng le yīnfú. Wǒ xià lóu qù xúnzhǎo shēngyīn de láiyuán. Huāyuán lǐ kōng wú yī rén, zhǐ yǒu yǔ zài xià. Dàn nà duàn xuánlǜ yīrán qīngxī——wǒ shènzhì néng fēnbiàn chū tánzòu zhě zài mǒuxiē duànluò shǐyòng le tàbǎn, yīnwèi yīnfú zhī jiān yǒu yī zhǒng tèshū de yánmián gǎn. Wǒ wèn le xiǎoqū lǐ de hěn duō línjū. Yǒuxiē rén shuō tāmen yě tīng dào guò, dàn gè rén miáoshù de qǔmù què bù yīyàng——yǒu rén tīng dào de shì Xiāobāng, yǒu rén tīng dào de shì Shūbótè, yǒu rén tīng dào de shì yī shǒu wánquán bú rènshi de qǔzi. Wéiyī de gòngtóng diǎn shì: zhǐ zài yǔtiān chūxiàn, zhǐ zài huāyuán fùjìn néng tīng dào, érqiě měi gè rén dōu juéde nàge xuánlǜ qiàhǎo shì zìjǐ zuì xǐhuan de nà shǒu. Wǒ hòulái chá le xiǎoqū de lìshǐ dàng'àn, fāxiàn zhèlǐ zài jiànchéng zhùzhái zhīqián shì yī suǒ yīnyuè xuéxiào, jùshuō yǒu yī wèi niánqīng de gāngqín jiàoshī zài yī gè yǔ yè lǐ tūfā xīnzàng bìng qùshì le. Dàn zhè huòxǔ zhǐ shì qiǎohé. Yě huòxǔ yǔshuǐ běnshēn jiù dǒngde yīnyuè——bìjìng, shuǐdī jīdǎ bùtóng cáizhì de biǎomiàn shí chǎnshēng de pínlǜ zǔhé, zài mǒuxiē tiáojiàn xià quèshí kěnéng xíngchéng lèisì xuánlǜ de shēngxué xiànxiàng. Dàn zhège jiěshì tài guò lǐxìng, ràng rén juéde yíhàn——wǒ gèng yuànyì xiāngxìn huāyuán lǐ zhù zhe yī gè tán gāngqín de yōulíng, tā zhǐ zài yǔtiān yǎnzòu, érqiě zǒng shì tán měi gè tīngzhòng zuì xǐhuan de qǔzi.
text_en: Every rainy day, piano music drifts through the neighborhood garden. This itself is unremarkable—what is strange is that the garden has no piano, nor any equipment capable of producing piano sound. I first noticed it on a plum-rain-season afternoon. The rain pattered gently; I stood by the window watching it when I suddenly heard a melody—Debussy's 'Jardins sous la pluie.' The piano sound did not come from any building but seemed to flow from the rain itself, as if every drop striking the ground, leaves, and metal railings was arranged into notes by an invisible hand. I went downstairs to find the source. The garden was deserted; only the rain was falling. Yet the melody remained clear—I could even tell the performer used the sustain pedal in certain passages, because there was a special lingering quality between notes. I asked many neighbors. Some said they had heard it too, but each described a different piece—one heard Chopin, another Schubert, another a completely unfamiliar composition. The only common thread: it appeared only on rainy days, could only be heard near the garden, and every person felt the melody happened to be their favorite piece. I later searched the neighborhood's historical records and discovered that before the residential complex was built, this had been a music school, and reportedly a young piano teacher had died of a sudden heart attack on a rainy night. But this is perhaps merely coincidence. Or perhaps rain itself understands music—after all, the frequency combinations produced when water drops strike surfaces of different materials can, under certain conditions, genuinely form acoustic phenomena resembling melody. But this explanation is too rational, leaving one with a sense of regret—I would rather believe a piano-playing ghost lives in the garden, performing only in the rain, always playing each listener's favorite piece.

questions.q_zh: 关于花园里的钢琴声，邻居们的描述有什么共同特点和不同之处？
questions.q_en: What commonalities and differences exist among the neighbors' descriptions of the garden's piano music?

options.text: 都只在雨天花园附近听到，但每人听到的曲目不同且恰好是各自最喜欢的
options.pinyin: dōu zhǐ zài yǔtiān huāyuán fùjìn tīng dào, dàn měi rén tīng dào de qǔmù bùtóng qiě qiàhǎo shì gèzì zuì xǐhuan de

options.pinyin: dàjiā dōu tīng dào le Débīxī de qǔzi dàn zài bùtóng shíjiān
options.text_en: Everyone heard Debussy but at different times

questions.q_zh: 面对理性解释和浪漫解释，作者最终倾向于哪一种？为什么？
questions.q_en: Between the rational and romantic explanations, which does the author lean toward and why?

options.text: 理性解释，因为声学现象更有科学依据
options.pinyin: lǐxìng jiěshì, yīnwèi shēngxué xiànxiàng gèng yǒu kēxué yījù
options.text_en: The rational explanation, because the acoustic phenomenon has more scientific basis

options.text: 浪漫解释——宁愿相信有弹钢琴的幽灵，因为理性解释虽然合理但让人觉得遗憾
options.pinyin: làngmàn jiěshì——nìngyuàn xiāngxìn yǒu tán gāngqín de yōulíng, yīnwèi lǐxìng jiěshì suīrán hélǐ dàn ràng rén juéde yíhàn
options.text_en: The romantic explanation—preferring to believe in a piano-playing ghost, because the rational one, though reasonable, feels regrettable

options.text: 两者都不相信，认为是自己的幻觉
options.pinyin: liǎng zhě dōu bù xiāngxìn, rènwéi shì zìjǐ de huànjué
options.text_en: Believes neither, thinking it's their own hallucination

options.text: 没有表态，保持中立
options.pinyin: méiyǒu biǎotài, bǎochí zhōnglì
options.text_en: Does not take a position, remaining neutral

questions.explanation: "always playing each listener's favorite piece" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

title: The Map That Drew Itself
text_zh: 我在祖父的遗物中发现了一张手绘地图。地图画在一张泛黄的宣纸上，用毛笔勾勒出了一个我完全不认识的地方——蜿蜒的河流、密集的等高线、几个用小圆圈标注的村庄，以及一条用虚线标出的、似乎通向某个终点的小路。地图的右下角标注着一个日期：一九四七年十一月初八。我尝试用各种方法辨认这个地方的位置。我对照了当地的县志，查阅了民国时期的测绘资料，甚至请教了一位退休的地理学教授——但没有任何一处真实的地形与这张地图吻合。地图上标注的那些村庄名字，在任何历史记录中都不存在。起初我以为这是祖父凭想象画的，但地图的精确程度令人震惊。等高线的间距完全符合专业测绘规范，河流的弯曲度遵循了自然地貌中的侵蚀力学原理，甚至连北斗标志的朝向都严格按照了制图学的标准。一个虚构的地方不需要如此精确。最令我困惑的是那条虚线小路。它从地图左上角的一个村庄出发，蜿蜒穿过山谷和溪流，最终在地图的正中央戛然而止——虚线在那里断开了，旁边用很小的字写着两个字：「未至」。祖父一生都住在同一个小镇上，从未远行。这张地图描绘的到底是一个他去过但我找不到的地方，还是一个他想去但终究没有到达的地方？「未至」二字，是一个事实的陈述，还是一个未完成的愿望？
text_pinyin: Wǒ zài zǔfù de yíwù zhōng fāxiàn le yī zhāng shǒuhuì dìtú. Dìtú huà zài yī zhāng fànhuáng de xuānzhǐ shàng, yòng máobǐ gōulè chū le yī gè wǒ wánquán bú rènshi de dìfāng——wānyán de héliú, mìjí de děnggāo xiàn, jǐ gè yòng xiǎo yuánquān biāozhù de cūnzhuāng, yǐjí yī tiáo yòng xūxiàn biāo chū de, sìhū tōng xiàng mǒu gè zhōngdiǎn de xiǎo lù. Dìtú de yòu xià jiǎo biāozhù zhe yī gè rìqī: yī jiǔ sì qī nián shíyī yuè chū bā. Wǒ chángshì yòng gè zhǒng fāngfǎ biànrèn zhège dìfāng de wèizhì. Wǒ duìzhào le dāngdì de xiànzhì, cháyuè le Mínguó shíqī de cèhuì zīliào, shènzhì qǐngjiào le yī wèi tuìxiū de dìlǐ xué jiàoshòu——dàn méiyǒu rènhé yī chù zhēnshí de dìxíng yǔ zhè zhāng dìtú wěnhé. Dìtú shàng biāozhù de nàxiē cūnzhuāng míngzì, zài rènhé lìshǐ jìlù zhōng dōu bù cúnzài. Qǐchū wǒ yǐwéi zhè shì zǔfù píng xiǎngxiàng huà de, dàn dìtú de jīngquè chéngdù lìng rén zhènjīng. Děnggāo xiàn de jiānjù wánquán fúhé zhuānyè cèhuì guīfàn, héliú de wānqū dù zūnxún le zìrán dìmào zhōng de qīnshí lìxué yuánlǐ, shènzhì lián Běidǒu biāozhì de cháoxiàng dōu yángé ànzhào le zhìtú xué de biāozhǔn. Yī gè xūgòu de dìfāng bù xūyào rúcǐ jīngquè. Zuì lìng wǒ kùnhuò de shì nà tiáo xūxiàn xiǎo lù. Tā cóng dìtú zuǒ shàng jiǎo de yī gè cūnzhuāng chūfā, wānyán chuānguò shāngǔ hé xīliú, zuìzhōng zài dìtú de zhèng zhōngyāng jiárán ér zhǐ——xūxiàn zài nàlǐ duàn kāi le, pángbiān yòng hěn xiǎo de zì xiě zhe liǎng gè zì:「Wèi zhì」. Zǔfù yīshēng dōu zhù zài tóng yī gè xiǎo zhèn shàng, cóng wèi yuǎn xíng. Zhè zhāng dìtú miáohuì de dàodǐ shì yī gè tā qù guò dàn wǒ zhǎo bú dào de dìfāng, háishi yī gè tā xiǎng qù dàn zhōngjiù méiyǒu dàodá de dìfāng?「Wèi zhì」èr zì, shì yī gè shìshí de chénshù, háishi yī gè wèi wánchéng de yuànwàng?
text_en: Among my grandfather's possessions I found a hand-drawn map. It was rendered on yellowed rice paper, brush-inked outlines of a place I completely did not recognize—winding rivers, dense contour lines, several villages marked with small circles, and a path marked in dotted lines that seemed to lead toward some destination. In the lower right corner was a date: November 8th, 1947. I tried every method to identify this location. I cross-referenced local county gazetteers, consulted Republican-era survey materials, even sought the expertise of a retired geography professor—but no real terrain matched this map. The village names marked on it exist in no historical record. At first I assumed grandfather had drawn it from imagination, but the map's precision was astonishing. The contour line intervals perfectly met professional surveying standards, the river curvature followed the erosion mechanics of natural landforms, and even the compass rose orientation strictly adhered to cartographic conventions. An imaginary place does not require such precision. Most puzzling was the dotted path. Starting from a village in the upper left corner, it wound through valleys and streams, only to stop abruptly in the map's exact center—the dotted line broke off there, and beside it, in small characters, were written two words: 'Not yet arrived.' My grandfather lived his entire life in the same small town and never traveled far. Does this map depict a place he visited that I cannot find, or a place he wished to go but ultimately never reached? Are the words 'not yet arrived' a statement of fact, or an unfulfilled wish?

questions.q_zh: 为什么作者排除了地图是祖父凭想象画的这种可能性？
questions.q_en: Why does the author rule out the possibility that the grandfather drew the map from imagination?

options.text: 因为祖父不会画画
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zǔfù bú huì huà huà
options.text_en: Because the grandfather couldn't draw

options.text: 因为地图精确度极高——等高线、河流弯曲度和罗盘标志都符合专业标准
options.pinyin: yīnwèi dìtú jīngquè dù jí gāo——děnggāo xiàn, héliú wānqū dù hé luópán biāozhì dōu fúhé zhuānyè biāozhǔn
options.text_en: Because the map's precision is extremely high—contour lines, river curvature, and compass rose all meet professional standards

options.text: 因为地图上有具体的日期
options.pinyin: yīnwèi dìtú shàng yǒu jùtǐ de rìqī
options.text_en: Because the map has a specific date

options.text: 因为祖父告诉过家人他画过地图
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zǔfù gàosu guò jiārén tā huà guò dìtú
options.text_en: Because the grandfather told family members he drew a map

questions.explanation: "mechanics of natural landforms, and even" — cause is shown through concrete sequence, not stated as principle.

questions.q_zh: 「未至」二字在文中留下了什么悬念？
questions.q_en: What suspense do the words 'not yet arrived' create in the text?

options.text: 祖父是否还活着
options.pinyin: zǔfù shìfǒu hái huó zhe
options.text_en: Whether the grandfather is still alive

options.text: 地图描绘的是祖父去过但找不到的地方，还是他想去但终究没到达的地方
options.pinyin: dìtú miáohuì de shì zǔfù qù guò dàn zhǎo bú dào de dìfāng, háishi tā xiǎng qù dàn zhōngjiù méi dàodá de dìfāng
options.text_en: Whether the map depicts a place he visited but can't be found, or a place he wished to go but never reached

options.text: 虚线小路通向什么宝藏
options.pinyin: xūxiàn xiǎo lù tōng xiàng shénme bǎozàng
options.text_en: What treasure the dotted path leads to

options.text: 地图是不是别人画的
options.pinyin: dìtú shì bu shì biéren huà de
options.text_en: Whether the map was drawn by someone else

questions.explanation: "Not yet arrived" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

title: The Clock That Gained a Minute
text_zh: 我家客厅里挂着一座老式挂钟，是曾祖父从上海带回来的。那座钟已经走了将近一百年，几乎从未出过差错——唯一的例外是每年的同一天，七月十四日，它会在午夜十二点的时候多走整整一分钟。也就是说，在那一天，从十一点五十九分到零点零一分之间，挂钟上实际经过了六十一分钟。这个异常是我父亲最先发现的。他是一个对时间极其敏感的人——那种会为了一列火车晚了三十秒而不安的人。有一年他偶然在七月十四日的午夜注意到挂钟和手表之间出现了一分钟的误差，从此之后他年年都会守在钟前观察。他做了详细的记录，持续了二十三年——无一例外，每年七月十四日午夜，挂钟都会多走一分钟。我曾经请一位钟表修理师检查过这座钟。他把整个机芯拆开，每一个齿轮、每一根弹簧都仔细检查过，然后告诉我一切正常，没有任何机械原因能解释这个现象。他甚至开玩笑说：「也许这座钟记住了某个特别的夜晚，每年都想让那个夜晚多延续一会儿。」我后来查了家族的旧记录，发现曾祖父去世的日期正是七月十四日。他在上海的一家医院里去世，死亡时间记录为午夜零点整。也许那座钟并没有出错——也许它只是在用自己的方式，每年为它的第一个主人多保留一分钟的时间。一分钟很短，短到在日常生活中几乎可以忽略不计。但对于一座忠诚的钟来说，那一分钟是它能给出的最长的告别。
text_pinyin: Wǒ jiā kètīng lǐ guà zhe yī zuò lǎoshì guàzhōng, shì zēngzǔfù cóng Shànghǎi dài huílái de. Nà zuò zhōng yǐjīng zǒu le jiāngjìn yī bǎi nián, jīhū cóng wèi chū guò chācuò——wéiyī de lìwài shì měi nián de tóng yī tiān, qī yuè shísì rì, tā huì zài wǔyè shí'èr diǎn de shíhou duō zǒu zhěngzhěng yī fēnzhōng. Yě jiù shì shuō, zài nà yī tiān, cóng shíyī diǎn wǔshíjiǔ fēn dào líng diǎn líng yī fēn zhī jiān, guàzhōng shàng shíjì jīngguò le liùshí yī fēnzhōng. Zhège yìcháng shì wǒ fùqīn zuì xiān fāxiàn de. Tā shì yī gè duì shíjiān jíqí mǐngǎn de rén——nà zhǒng huì wèile yī liè huǒchē wǎn le sānshí miǎo ér bù'ān de rén. Yǒu yī nián tā ǒurán zài qī yuè shísì rì de wǔyè zhùyì dào guàzhōng hé shǒubiǎo zhī jiān chūxiàn le yī fēnzhōng de wùchā, cóngcǐ zhīhòu tā nián nián dōu huì shǒu zài zhōng qián guānchá. Tā zuò le xiángxì de jìlù, chíxù le èrshísān nián——wú yī lìwài, měi nián qī yuè shísì rì wǔyè, guàzhōng dōu huì duō zǒu yī fēnzhōng. Wǒ céngjīng qǐng yī wèi zhōngbiǎo xiūlǐ shī jiǎnchá guò zhè zuò zhōng. Tā bǎ zhěnggè jīxīn chāi kāi, měi yī gè chǐlún, měi yī gēn tánhuáng dōu zǐxì jiǎnchá guò, ránhòu gàosu wǒ yīqiè zhèngcháng, méiyǒu rènhé jīxiè yuányīn néng jiěshì zhège xiànxiàng. Tā shènzhì kāi wánxiào shuō:「Yěxǔ zhè zuò zhōng jì zhù le mǒu gè tèbié de yèwǎn, měi nián dōu xiǎng ràng nàge yèwǎn duō yánxù yīhuìr.」Wǒ hòulái chá le jiāzú de jiù jìlù, fāxiàn zēngzǔfù qùshì de rìqī zhèng shì qī yuè shísì rì. Tā zài Shànghǎi de yī jiā yīyuàn lǐ qùshì, sǐwáng shíjiān jìlù wéi wǔyè líng diǎn zhěng. Yěxǔ nà zuò zhōng bìng méiyǒu chū cuò——yěxǔ tā zhǐ shì zài yòng zìjǐ de fāngshì, měi nián wèi tā de dì yī gè zhǔrén duō bǎoliú yī fēnzhōng de shíjiān. Yī fēnzhōng hěn duǎn, duǎn dào zài rìcháng shēnghuó zhōng jīhū kěyǐ hūlüè bù jì. Dàn duìyú yī zuò zhōngchéng de zhōng lái shuō, nà yī fēnzhōng shì tā néng gěi chū de zuì cháng de gàobié.
text_en: In our living room hangs an old wall clock, brought back from Shanghai by my great-grandfather. That clock has run for nearly a hundred years and has almost never been wrong—with one exception: every year on the same day, July 14th, it gains exactly one minute at midnight. That is, on that day, between 11:59 PM and 12:01 AM, the clock actually registers sixty-one minutes. My father first noticed this anomaly. He was a person extremely sensitive to time—the kind who would be unsettled by a train arriving thirty seconds late. One year he happened to notice at midnight on July 14th that the wall clock and his wristwatch had developed a one-minute discrepancy. From then on, he stood vigil before the clock every year. He kept detailed records for twenty-three years—without exception, every July 14th at midnight, the clock gained one minute. I once had a clockmaker inspect it. He disassembled the entire movement, examined every gear and spring carefully, then told me everything was normal; no mechanical cause could explain the phenomenon. He even joked: 'Perhaps this clock remembers a particular night and every year wants that night to last a little longer.' I later checked old family records and discovered that my great-grandfather's date of death was precisely July 14th. He died in a hospital in Shanghai; the recorded time of death was exactly midnight. Perhaps the clock has not been wrong—perhaps it has merely been using its own method to preserve one extra minute of time each year for its first owner. One minute is short, so short it can be almost ignored in daily life. But for a loyal clock, that minute is the longest farewell it can offer.

questions.q_zh: 父亲是如何发现挂钟异常的？他后来做了什么？
questions.q_en: How did the father discover the clock's anomaly, and what did he do afterward?
