title: The Patience of Stone
text_zh: 河边有一个老人，每天下午坐在同一块石头旁边。他叫什么没人知道，小区里的人都叫他「石头老赵」。他大概七十多岁，穿一件洗得发白的深蓝色外套，戴一顶灰色鸭舌帽，帽檐已经卷了边。他来的时候会先弯腰，从河滩上捡一块鹅卵石握在手心。然后坐下，看水。他可以一个人坐两个小时不说话。手里一直攥着那块石头，偶尔翻转一下，用拇指摩挲石头的表面。有人试过跟他搭话。遛狗的陈大爷问过他：「老哥，你天天坐这儿看什么呢？」他说：「看水。」陈大爷说：「水有什么好看的？」他没回答。陈大爷也就不再问了。带孩子的年轻妈妈有时候会把小孩指给他看：「叫爷爷。」他对小孩点点头，继续看水。走的时候他会把手里的石头放回河滩上——不是随便丢，是弯下腰轻轻放下去的，放在一个和周围石头差不多的位置。好像在把它送回家。保安老吴说石头老赵以前是水利局的工程师，老伴三年前走了。老吴说他老伴以前每天下午也来河边坐着，两个人并排坐在石头上，也不怎么说话。老伴走了以后，他还是每天来。位置没变，时间没变。只是旁边空了一个人的位置。他走了以后，石头上还留着他坐过的温度。
text_pinyin: Hé biān yǒu yī kuài shítou, bèi shuǐ chōngshuā le bù zhīdào duōshǎo nián. Tā de biǎomiàn yǐjīng bèi mó de yìcháng guānghuá, mō shàng qù yǒu yī zhǒng jìnhū sīchóu de zhìgǎn. Měi yī cùn guānghuá dōu shì shuǐ yòng shíjiān huàn lái de——bú shì yī cì jùdà de lìliàng, ér shì wúshù cì wēixiǎo de fǔmō. Shuǐ cóng lái bú huì xián fán, shítou cóng lái bú huì hǎn téng. Tāmen zhī jiān yǒu yī zhǒng wúxiàn nàixīn de mòqì: shuǐ bù jí bù xú de liú guò, shítou bù jí bù xú de gǎibiàn. Jǐ bǎi wàn nián hòu, yī kuài yuánběn léngjiǎo-fēnmíng de yánshí biànchéng le yī kē yuánrùn de éluǎnshí——ér shuǐ yīrán shì shuǐ, fǎngfú shénme dōu méi zuò guò. Wǒ yǒu shíhou huì jiǎn yī kuài hé biān de éluǎnshí wò zài shǒuxīn. Tā bǐ kōngqì lüè liáng, bǐ jīnshǔ lüè nuǎn. Tā de wēndù qiàhǎo chǔ yú「wú gǎn」de qūjiān——nǐ zhīdào shǒu lǐ wò zhe yī gè dōngxi, dàn tā de wēndù bú huì ràng nǐ tèbié zhùyì dào tā. Zhè zhǒng「qiàhǎo」bú shì ǒurán de——shítou de bǐ rè róng juédìng le tā jì bú huì xiàng jīnshǔ yīyàng xùnsù biàn liáng ràng nǐ gǎn dào bīnglěng, yě bú huì xiàng mùtou yīyàng xùnsù shēng wēn ràng nǐ gǎn dào wēnrè. Tā zhǐ shì ānjìng de dāi zài nǐ de shǒuxīn lǐ, bù dǎrǎo nǐ de tǐwēn, yě bú bèi nǐ de tǐwēn yǐngxiǎng tài duō. Zhè dàgài jiù shì zuì hǎo de péibàn fāngshì——zàichǎng, dàn bù dǎrǎo; kàojìn, dàn bù qīnrù. Xiàng yī kuài bèi shuǐ mó yuán de shítou, qù diào le suǒyǒu jiānruì de léngjiǎo, zhǐ shèng xià yī zhǒng guānghuá de, bù shāng rén de zàichǎng gǎn. Zuì hǎo de péngyou, zuì hǎo de àirén, zuì hǎo de jiārén, dàgài dōu shì zhèyàng de——nǐ zhīdào tāmen zài, dàn nǐ bù xūyào shíkè zhùyì tāmen. Tāmen de cúnzài bú huì ràng nǐ jǐnzhāng, tāmen de chénmò bú huì ràng nǐ bù'ān. Tāmen jiù shì nǐ shǒuxīn lǐ de nà kuài éluǎnshí——wēndù gānghǎo, zhòngliàng gānghǎo, xíngzhuàng gānghǎo.
text_en: By the river there is an old man who sits beside the same stone every afternoon. Nobody knows his real name; everyone in the compound calls him 'Stone Old Zhao.' He is perhaps in his seventies, wearing a deep blue jacket faded from washing and a gray flat cap with the brim curling at the edges. When he arrives he first bends down, picks up a pebble from the riverbank, and holds it in his palm. Then he sits and watches the water. He can sit alone for two hours without speaking. The stone stays in his hand the whole time; occasionally he turns it over and rubs its surface with his thumb. People have tried talking to him. Old Chen, who walks his dog there, once asked: 'Brother, you sit here every day—what are you looking at?' He said: 'The water.' Old Chen said: 'What is so interesting about water?' He did not answer. Old Chen did not ask again. Young mothers with children sometimes point their kids toward him: 'Say hi to Grandpa.' He nods at the child and goes back to watching the water. When he leaves he places the stone back on the riverbank—not tossing it, but bending down and setting it gently, in a spot that blends with the surrounding stones. As if returning it home. Wu, the security guard, says Stone Old Zhao used to be an engineer at the Water Resources Bureau. His wife passed away three years ago. Wu says his wife used to come sit by the river every afternoon too. The two of them sat side by side on the stones, not saying much either. After she passed, he still comes every day. Same spot, same time. Just an empty space beside him where a person used to be. After he leaves, the stone still holds the warmth of where he sat.

title: The Language of Rain on a Tin Roof
text_zh: 小时候家里的屋顶是铁皮的。每次下雨，雨水打在铁皮上会发出一种特别的声音——不是「滴答滴答」那种温柔的节奏，而是一种密集的、急促的、带着金属共鸣的喧哗。这种声音在大多数人听来是吵闹的、令人烦躁的，但对我而言，它是世界上最好的安眠曲。也许这就是所谓的「白噪音」效应——一种均匀的、持续的、没有信息含量的声音，恰好能够屏蔽掉所有其他的杂音，让大脑进入一种类似于冥想的放空状态。但我觉得不仅仅是这样。雨打铁皮屋顶之所以让我安心，是因为它意味着一件事：外面在下雨，而我在屋里。这个简单的事实构成了一种最基本的安全感——恶劣的天气在外面，而我被一层铁皮（虽然薄得可怜）保护着。这种「外面很糟糕但我很安全」的感觉，是所有安全感的原型。成年后我搬了好几次家，再也没有住过铁皮屋顶的房子。现代住宅的隔音越来越好，下再大的雨也听不到什么声音。从舒适度的角度来说，这当然是进步。但从某种情感的角度来说，我们失去了一样东西——失去了和天气之间的那层直接的、物理性的联系。我们不再被雨声包裹，不再因为一阵突然的暴风而感到屋子的微微颤抖。我们变得更安全了，但也更孤立了——和自然之间隔了太多层墙壁、玻璃和保温材料，以至于我们几乎忘记了自己其实一直活在天空下面。
text_pinyin: Xiǎo shíhou jiā lǐ de wūdǐng shì tiěpí de. Měi cì xiàyǔ, yǔshuǐ dǎ zài tiěpí shàng huì fāchū yī zhǒng tèbié de shēngyīn——bú shì「dīdā dīdā」nà zhǒng wēnróu de jiézòu, ér shì yī zhǒng mìjí de, jícù de, dài zhe jīnshǔ gòngmíng de xuānhuá. Zhè zhǒng shēngyīn zài dà duōshù rén tīng lái shì chǎonào de, lìng rén fánzào de, dàn duì wǒ ér yán, tā shì shìjiè shàng zuì hǎo de ānmián qǔ. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì suǒwèi de「bái zàoyīn」xiàoyìng——yī zhǒng jūnyún de, chíxù de, méiyǒu xìnxī hánliàng de shēngyīn, qiàhǎo nénggòu píngbì diào suǒyǒu qítā de záyīn, ràng dànǎo jìnrù yī zhǒng lèisì yú míngxiǎng de fàngkōng zhuàngtài. Dàn wǒ juéde bù jǐnjǐn shì zhèyàng. Yǔ dǎ tiěpí wūdǐng zhī suǒyǐ ràng wǒ ānxīn, shì yīnwèi tā yìwèi zhe yī jiàn shì: wàimiàn zài xiàyǔ, ér wǒ zài wūlǐ. Zhège jiǎndān de shìshí gòuchéng le yī zhǒng zuì jīběn de ānquán gǎn——è'liè de tiānqì zài wàimiàn, ér wǒ bèi yī céng tiěpí (suīrán báo de kělián) bǎohù zhe. Zhè zhǒng「wàimiàn hěn zāogāo dàn wǒ hěn ānquán」de gǎnjué, shì suǒyǒu ānquán gǎn de yuánxíng. Chéngnián hòu wǒ bān le hǎo jǐ cì jiā, zài yě méiyǒu zhù guò tiěpí wūdǐng de fángzi. Xiàndài zhùzhái de géyīn yuèláiyuè hǎo, xià zài dà de yǔ yě tīng bú dào shénme shēngyīn. Cóng shūshì dù de jiǎodù lái shuō, zhè dāngrán shì jìnbù. Dàn cóng mǒu zhǒng qínggǎn de jiǎodù lái shuō, wǒmen shīqù le yī yàng dōngxi——shīqù le hé tiānqì zhī jiān de nà céng zhíjiē de, wùlǐ xìng de liánxì. Wǒmen bú zài bèi yǔ shēng bāoguǒ, bú zài yīnwèi yī zhèn tūrán de bàofēng ér gǎn dào wūzi de wēiwēi chàndǒu. Wǒmen biàn de gèng ānquán le, dàn yě gèng gūlì le——hé zìrán zhī jiān gé le tài duō céng qiángbì, bōli hé bǎowēn cáiliào, yǐzhìyú wǒmen jīhū wàngjì le zìjǐ qíshí yīzhí huó zài tiānkōng xiàmiàn.
text_en: When I was small, our roof was made of tin. Whenever it rained, water striking the tin produced a distinctive sound—not the gentle 'drip-drop' rhythm, but a dense, rapid, metallic-resonance clamor. Most people find this sound noisy and irritating, but for me, it was the world's best lullaby. Perhaps this is the so-called 'white noise' effect—an even, continuous, information-free sound that happens to screen out all other noise, letting the brain enter a meditative blank state. But I think it is more than that. The reason rain on a tin roof brings me peace is that it signifies one thing: it is raining outside, and I am inside. This simple fact constitutes the most basic sense of security—harsh weather is outside, and I am protected by a sheet of tin (pitifully thin though it is). This feeling of 'outside is terrible but I am safe' is the prototype of all security. As an adult I moved several times and never again lived in a house with a tin roof. Modern housing's soundproofing keeps improving; even heavy rain produces barely any sound. From a comfort standpoint, this is certainly progress. But from a certain emotional standpoint, we have lost something—the direct, physical connection with the weather. We are no longer wrapped in the sound of rain, no longer feel the house slightly tremble from a sudden squall. We have become safer, but also more isolated—separated from nature by too many layers of walls, glass, and insulation, so much so that we have nearly forgotten we actually live beneath the sky.

title: The Weight of a Cup of Water
text_zh: 一杯水有多重？大约二百五十克——不到半斤。但如果你把它举在手里不放下，一分钟后它变成了半公斤，十分钟后变成了两公斤，一个小时后你的手臂会因为疼痛而颤抖。水的重量没有变，变的是你举着它的时间。一杯水很轻，但一杯被举了一个小时的水很重。这个简单的物理现象是我见过的关于压力最好的隐喻。我们每个人手里都举着各种各样的「水杯」——工作的压力、人际关系的焦虑、对未来的不确定感、对过去的遗憾。每一个单独拿起来都不算重，但如果你一直举着不放下，时间会让它们变得难以承受。问题不在于杯子有多重，而在于你举了多久。解决的办法不是把杯子扔掉——很多时候你扔不掉，因为那是你的责任、你的牵挂、你的爱——而是学会时不时地把它放在桌上歇一会儿，让手臂恢复一下，然后再重新拿起。一位心理学家用另一种方式解释了这个道理：他说，人类的焦虑系统本来是为了应对短期威胁而设计的——比如一只老虎冲过来的时候。这个系统会在几分钟内释放大量的肾上腺素，让你做好战斗或逃跑的准备。但现代人的焦虑来源不是老虎，而是房贷、绩效考核和子女教育——这些「老虎」永远不会冲过来，但也永远不会消失。于是我们的焦虑系统被迫全天候运行，就像一个人举着一杯水不放，直到手臂开始颤抖。学会放下那杯水——哪怕只是放下五分钟——也许是这个时代最重要的一项技能。
text_pinyin: Yī bēi shuǐ yǒu duō zhòng? Dàyuē èrbǎi wǔshí kè——bú dào bàn jīn. Dàn rúguǒ nǐ bǎ tā jǔ zài shǒu lǐ bú fàng xià, yī fēnzhōng hòu tā biànchéng le bàn gōngjīn, shí fēnzhōng hòu biànchéng le liǎng gōngjīn, yī gè xiǎoshí hòu nǐ de shǒubì huì yīnwèi téngtòng ér chàndǒu. Shuǐ de zhòngliàng méiyǒu biàn, biàn de shì nǐ jǔ zhe tā de shíjiān. Yī bēi shuǐ hěn qīng, dàn yī bēi bèi jǔ le yī gè xiǎoshí de shuǐ hěn zhòng. Zhège jiǎndān de wùlǐ xiànxiàng shì wǒ jiàn guò de guānyú yālì zuì hǎo de yǐnyù. Wǒmen měi gè rén shǒu lǐ dōu jǔ zhe gè zhǒng gè yàng de「shuǐ bēi」——gōngzuò de yālì, rénjì guānxì de jiāolǜ, duì wèilái de bú quèdìng gǎn, duì guòqù de yíhàn. Měi yī gè dāndú ná qǐlái dōu bú suàn zhòng, dàn rúguǒ nǐ yīzhí jǔ zhe bú fàng xià, shíjiān huì ràng tāmen biàn de nányǐ chéngshòu. Wèntí bú zàiyú bēizi yǒu duō zhòng, ér zàiyú nǐ jǔ le duō jiǔ. Jiějué de bànfǎ bú shì bǎ bēizi rēng diào——hěn duō shíhou nǐ rēng bú diào, yīnwèi nà shì nǐ de zérèn, nǐ de qiānguà, nǐ de ài——ér shì xué huì shí bù shí de bǎ tā fàng zài zhuō shàng xiē yīhuìr, ràng shǒubì huīfù yīxià, ránhòu zài chóngxīn ná qǐ. Yī wèi xīnlǐ xuéjiā yòng lìng yī zhǒng fāngshì jiěshì le zhège dàolǐ: tā shuō, rénlèi de jiāolǜ xìtǒng běnlái shì wèile yìngduì duǎnqī wēixié ér shèjì de——bǐrú yī zhī lǎohǔ chōng guòlái de shíhou. Zhège xìtǒng huì zài jǐ fēnzhōng nèi shìfàng dàliàng de shènshàngxiàn sù, ràng nǐ zuò hǎo zhàndòu huò táopǎo de zhǔnbèi. Dàn xiàndài rén de jiāolǜ láiyuán bú shì lǎohǔ, ér shì fángdài, jìxiào kǎohé hé zǐnǚ jiàoyù——zhèxiē「lǎohǔ」yǒngyuǎn bú huì chōng guòlái, dàn yě yǒngyuǎn bú huì xiāoshī. Yúshì wǒmen de jiāolǜ xìtǒng bèi pò quántiānhòu yùnxíng, jiù xiàng yī gè rén jǔ zhe yī bēi shuǐ bú fàng, zhídào shǒubì kāishǐ chàndǒu. Xué huì fàng xià nà bēi shuǐ——nǎpà zhǐ shì fàng xià wǔ fēnzhōng——yěxǔ shì zhège shídài zuì zhòngyào de yī xiàng jìnéng.
text_en: How heavy is a cup of water? About 250 grams—less than half a jin. But if you hold it up without putting it down, after one minute it feels like half a kilogram, after ten minutes like two kilograms, and after an hour your arm trembles with pain. The water's weight hasn't changed; what changed is how long you've held it. A cup of water is light, but a cup held for an hour is heavy. This simple physical phenomenon is the best metaphor for stress I have ever seen. Each of us holds various 'cups'—work pressure, relationship anxiety, uncertainty about the future, regret about the past. Each alone is not heavy, but if you keep holding them without putting them down, time makes them unbearable. The problem is not how heavy the cup is, but how long you've held it. The solution is not throwing the cup away—often you cannot, because it is your responsibility, your concern, your love—but learning to set it on the table occasionally for a rest, letting your arm recover, then picking it up again. A psychologist explained this differently: he said humanity's anxiety system was originally designed to handle short-term threats—like a tiger charging at you. The system releases massive adrenaline within minutes, preparing you to fight or flee. But modern anxiety sources are not tigers but mortgages, performance reviews, and children's education—these 'tigers' will never charge at you, but will also never disappear. So our anxiety system is forced to run around the clock, like a person holding a cup of water and never putting it down, until the arm starts trembling.

questions.q_en: What truth about stress does the 'weight of a cup of water' metaphor illustrate?

options.text_en: Greater stress requires more physical exercise

options.text_en: The problem isn't how heavy the stress is, but how long you've borne it—you need to learn to put it down and rest occasionally

options.text_en: All stress should be eliminated

options.text_en: Modern people have much more stress than ancient people

questions.explanation: "letting your arm recover" — the physical analogy controls the conceptual range.

questions.q_zh: 心理学家用「老虎」来比喻什么？现代的「老虎」和真正的老虎有什么不同？
questions.q_en: What does the psychologist use 'tigers' to metaphorize, and how do modern 'tigers' differ from real ones?

options.text: 老虎代表自然灾害，现代的老虎代表人为灾害
options.pinyin: lǎohǔ dàibiǎo zìrán zāihài, xiàndài de lǎohǔ dàibiǎo rénwéi zāihài
options.text_en: Tigers represent natural disasters, modern tigers represent man-made disasters

options.text: 老虎代表焦虑来源；现代的「老虎」（房贷等）永远不会冲过来但也永远不会消失，导致焦虑系统全天候运行
options.pinyin: lǎohǔ dàibiǎo jiāolǜ láiyuán; xiàndài de「lǎohǔ」(fángdài děng) yǒngyuǎn bú huì chōng guòlái dàn yě yǒngyuǎn bú huì xiāoshī, dǎozhì jiāolǜ xìtǒng quántiānhòu yùnxíng

options.text_en: Modern tigers are more dangerous because they're invisible

questions.explanation: "these 'tigers' will never charge at you" — the physical analogy controls the conceptual range.

title: The Last Light
text_zh: 每天傍晚，太阳落到地平线以下之后，天空会有大约二十分钟的时间处于一种特殊的光照状态。太阳已经看不到了，但它的光线仍然在大气层中折射和散射，给天空染上了从金色到粉色到紫色的渐变色谱。这段时间被摄影师们称为「黄金时刻」或「蓝色时刻」，是一天中光线最柔和、色彩最丰富的阶段。但我更喜欢叫它「最后的光」。这种光之所以特别，是因为它是间接的。太阳不再直接照耀任何东西，但它的影响力仍然通过大气层中的无数次折射传播着——就像一个已经离开房间的人，他的香水味还留在空气中。这种「离场后的存在感」也许是世界上最温柔的光——它不刺眼、不灼热，只是安静地给一切事物镀上了一层薄薄的暖色。在这二十分钟里，城市会呈现出它最美的面貌。水泥建筑变成了琥珀色，玻璃幕墙变成了镜子，连街边最普通的行道树都被镶上了金色的轮廓。这不是日出时那种充满期待的光，也不是正午时分那种毫无遮拦的曝晒——这是一种告别的光，柔和而不舍。也许人生中最好的时刻也是这样的——不是那些万众瞩目的高光，而是那些即将结束的、安静的、带着一点温暖的余晖的时刻。一顿和老友的晚餐快要结束时的最后一杯酒，一个漫长假期的最后一个傍晚，一段感情在成熟后散发出的那种不再炽烈但持久温暖的光。最后的光不是最亮的，但它是最美的——因为它知道自己即将消失，所以把所有的温柔都集中在了这最后的二十分钟里。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān bàngwǎn, tàiyáng luò dào dìpíngxiàn yǐxià zhīhòu, tiānkōng huì yǒu dàyuē èrshí fēnzhōng de shíjiān chǔ yú yī zhǒng tèshū de guāngzhào zhuàngtài. Tàiyáng yǐjīng kàn bú dào le, dàn tā de guāngxiàn réngrán zài dàqìcéng zhōng zhéshè hé sànshe, gěi tiānkōng rǎn shàng le cóng jīnsè dào fěnsè dào zǐsè de jiànbiàn sèpǔ. Zhè duàn shíjiān bèi shèyǐng shīmen chēngwéi「huángjīn shíkè」huò「lán sè shíkè」, shì yī tiān zhōng guāngxiàn zuì róuhé, sècǎi zuì fēngfù de jiēduàn. Dàn wǒ gèng xǐhuan jiào tā「zuìhòu de guāng」. Zhè zhǒng guāng zhī suǒyǐ tèbié, shì yīnwèi tā shì jiànjiē de. Tàiyáng bú zài zhíjiē zhàoyào rènhé dōngxi, dàn tā de yǐngxiǎng lì réngrán tōngguò dàqìcéng zhōng de wúshù cì zhéshè chuánbō zhe——jiù xiàng yī gè yǐjīng líkāi fángjiān de rén, tā de xiāngshuǐ wèi hái liú zài kōngqì zhōng. Zhè zhǒng「lí chǎng hòu de cúnzài gǎn」yěxǔ shì shìjiè shàng zuì wēnróu de guāng——tā bú cìyǎn, bú zhuórè, zhǐ shì ānjìng de gěi yīqiè shìwù dù shàng le yī céng báobáo de nuǎn sè. Zài zhè èrshí fēnzhōng lǐ, chéngshì huì chéngxiàn chū tā zuì měi de miànmào. Shuǐní jiànzhú biànchéng le hǔpò sè, bōli mùqiáng biànchéng le jìngzi, lián jiē biān zuì pǔtōng de xíngdào shù dōu bèi xiāng shàng le jīnsè de lúnkuò. Zhè bú shì rìchū shí nà zhǒng chōngmǎn qīdài de guāng, yě bú shì zhèngwǔ shí fēn nà zhǒng háo wú zhēlán de pùshài——zhè shì yī zhǒng gàobié de guāng, róuhé ér bù shě. Yěxǔ rénshēng zhōng zuì hǎo de shíkè yě shì zhèyàng de——bú shì nàxiē wànzhòng-zhǔmù de gāoguāng, ér shì nàxiē jíjiāng jiéshù de, ānjìng de, dài zhe yīdiǎn wēnnuǎn de yúhuī de shíkè. Yī dùn hé lǎoyǒu de wǎncān kuàiyào jiéshù shí de zuìhòu yī bēi jiǔ, yī gè màncháng jiàqī de zuìhòu yī gè bàngwǎn, yī duàn gǎnqíng zài chéngshú hòu sànfā chū de nà zhǒng bú zài chìliè dàn chíjiǔ wēnnuǎn de guāng. Zuìhòu de guāng bú shì zuì liàng de, dàn tā shì zuì měi de——yīnwèi tā zhīdào zìjǐ jíjiāng xiāoshī, suǒyǐ bǎ suǒyǒu de wēnróu dōu jízhōng zài le zhè zuìhòu de èrshí fēnzhōng lǐ.
text_en: Every evening, after the sun drops below the horizon, the sky spends about twenty minutes in a special state of illumination. The sun is no longer visible, but its rays still refract and scatter through the atmosphere, painting the sky in a gradient spectrum from gold to pink to purple. Photographers call this the 'golden hour' or 'blue hour'—the time of day when light is softest and colors richest. But I prefer to call it 'the last light.' This light is special because it is indirect. The sun no longer directly illuminates anything, yet its influence still propagates through countless refractions in the atmosphere—like a person who has left the room, their perfume still lingering in the air. This 'presence after departure' is perhaps the world's most gentle light—not glaring, not scorching, simply and quietly gilding everything in a thin layer of warmth. During these twenty minutes, the city shows its most beautiful face. Concrete buildings turn amber, glass curtain walls become mirrors, even the most ordinary roadside trees are outlined in gold. This is not the hopeful light of sunrise, nor the unshielded blaze of high noon—it is a farewell light, gentle and reluctant. Perhaps life's best moments are like this—not the spotlight moments of public glory, but those quiet, nearly-ending moments carrying a little warm afterglow. The last glass of wine as dinner with an old friend draws to a close, the final evening of a long holiday, the light that a mature relationship radiates—no longer fierce but enduringly warm.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么把日落后的光称为「最后的光」而不是「黄金时刻」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author call the post-sunset light 'the last light' rather than 'the golden hour'?

options.text_en: Because it is a farewell light—knowing it will vanish, concentrating all tenderness into the final moments

options.text_en: Because the sun has completely set

title: The Color of Silence
text_zh: 如果寂静有颜色，它会是什么样的？我曾经认为是白色——纯粹的、无瑕的、像一张没有被书写过的纸。但后来我意识到，白色其实是喧闹的。它太过明亮，太过刺眼，像一束聚光灯打在空荡荡的舞台上，反而让人紧张。真正的寂静应该是一种介于灰色和蓝色之间的颜色——不浓不淡，不冷不暖，像一个阴天的午后、一条被薄雾笼罩的河流、一件洗了太多次而褪色的旧衬衫。这种颜色不引人注目，也不试图表达什么。它只是安静地存在着，像一个不需要被理解就能令人感到安心的老朋友。我家的墙壁原本是白色的，搬进来的第一年我就把它重新粉刷了。不是刷成别的鲜艳颜色，而是一种很难用语言描述的灰蓝色。油漆店的店员问我要什么颜色的时候，我说：「给我雨后天空还没有完全放晴时的那种颜色。」他愣了一下，然后从色卡里抽出了一张。颜色的名字叫「晨雾」。我看了一眼就知道是它了。刷完之后的房间变得不一样了——不是变暗了，而是变「软」了。光线不再在墙壁上弹跳，而是被轻轻地吸收，像声音沉入水中。坐在这个房间里，我感到一种莫名的放松，仿佛整个世界都把音量调小了几格。有时候我觉得，人之所以焦虑，不是因为生活中的声音太多，而是因为所有的颜色都太饱和了。一切都在争夺你的注意力，一切都在大声宣告自己的存在。也许我们需要的不是安静的环境，而是安静的色彩——一种让眼睛可以休息、让心灵可以喘息的颜色。
text_pinyin: Rúguǒ jìjìng yǒu yánsè, tā huì shì shénme yàng de? Wǒ céngjīng rènwéi shì bái sè——chúncuì de, wúxiá de, xiàng yī zhāng méiyǒu bèi shūxiě guò de zhǐ. Dàn hòulái wǒ yìshí dào, bái sè qíshí shì xuānnào de. Tā tài guò míngliàng, tài guò cìyǎn, xiàng yī shù jùguāngdēng dǎ zài kōngdàngdàng de wǔtái shàng, fǎn'ér ràng rén jǐnzhāng. Zhēnzhèng de jìjìng yīnggāi shì yī zhǒng jiè yú huī sè hé lán sè zhī jiān de yánsè——bù nóng bú dàn, bù lěng bù nuǎn, xiàng yī gè yīntiān de wǔhòu, yī tiáo bèi bó wù lǒngzhào de héliú, yī jiàn xǐ le tài duō cì ér tuìsè de jiù chènshān. Zhè zhǒng yánsè bù yǐn rén zhùmù, yě bù shìtú biǎodá shénme. Tā zhǐ shì ānjìng de cúnzài zhe, xiàng yī gè bù xūyào bèi lǐjiě jiù néng lìng rén gǎndào ānxīn de lǎo péngyǒu. Wǒ jiā de qiángbì yuánběn shì bái sè de, bān jìn lái de dì yī nián wǒ jiù bǎ tā chóngxīn fěnshuā le. Bú shì shuā chéng bié de xiānyàn yánsè, ér shì yī zhǒng hěn nán yòng yǔyán miáoshù de huī lán sè. Yóuqī diàn de diànyuán wèn wǒ yào shénme yánsè de shíhòu, wǒ shuō:「Gěi wǒ yǔ hòu tiānkōng hái méiyǒu wánquán fàngqíng shí de nà zhǒng yánsè.」Tā lèng le yīxià, ránhòu cóng sèkǎ lǐ chōu chū le yī zhāng. Yánsè de míngzì jiào「Chénwù」. Wǒ kàn le yī yǎn jiù zhīdào shì tā le. Shuā wán zhīhòu de fángjiān biàn de bù yīyàng le——bú shì biàn àn le, ér shì biàn「ruǎn」le. Guāngxiàn bú zài zài qiángbì shàng tántiào, ér shì bèi qīngqīng de xīshōu, xiàng shēngyīn chén rù shuǐ zhōng. Zuò zài zhège fángjiān lǐ, wǒ gǎndào yī zhǒng mòmíng de fàngsōng, fǎngfú zhěnggè shìjiè dōu bǎ yīnliàng tiáo xiǎo le jǐ gé. Yǒu shíhòu wǒ juéde, rén zhī suǒyǐ jiāolǜ, bú shì yīnwèi shēnghuó zhōng de shēngyīn tài duō, ér shì yīnwèi suǒyǒu de yánsè dōu tài bǎohé le. Yīqiè dōu zài zhēngduó nǐ de zhùyìlì, yīqiè dōu zài dàshēng xuāngào zìjǐ de cúnzài. Yěxǔ wǒmen xūyào de bú shì ānjìng de huánjìng, ér shì ānjìng de sècǎi——yī zhǒng ràng yǎnjīng kěyǐ xiūxī, ràng xīnlíng kěyǐ chuǎnxī de yánsè.
text_en: If silence had a color, what would it look like? I once thought it would be white—pure, flawless, like a page never written upon. But later I realized that white is actually noisy. It is too bright, too glaring, like a spotlight on an empty stage, making one tense instead. True silence should be a color between gray and blue—neither dense nor light, neither cold nor warm, like an overcast afternoon, a river veiled in thin mist, an old shirt faded from too many washes. This color draws no attention and does not try to express anything. It simply exists quietly, like an old friend who puts you at ease without needing to be understood. The walls of my home were originally white. In the first year after moving in, I repainted them. Not to some other vivid color, but a gray-blue difficult to describe in words. When the paint shop clerk asked what color I wanted, I said: 'Give me the color of the sky after rain, before it has fully cleared.' He paused for a moment, then drew a card from the color chart. The color was called 'Morning Mist.' One glance and I knew it was the one. After painting, the room felt different—not darker, but 'softer.' Light no longer bounced off the walls but was gently absorbed, like sound sinking into water. Sitting in this room, I felt an inexplicable relaxation, as if the whole world had turned its volume down a few notches. Sometimes I think the reason people feel anxious is not that there are too many sounds in life, but that all the colors are too saturated. Everything competes for your attention, everything loudly proclaims its existence.

title: The Conversation of Trees
text_zh: 公园管理员老方在这儿干了二十二年了。他说园里最大的那棵银杏有一百四十岁，旁边那棵槐树大概九十岁。我问他怎么知道的。他说：「树牌上写着呢。不过那是三十年前挂的牌子了，你得自己加三十。」我问他这两棵树之间是不是真的会通过地下的菌丝网络交换营养。他抬头看了我一眼：「你看过那个纪录片吧？什么'木质互联网'。」我说是的。他蹲下来，用手扒开银杏树根部的一层落叶，露出下面的泥土。泥土表面有一层白色的细丝，像蛛网。「你看，这些就是菌丝。连着呢。你挖下去一尺，两棵树的根搅在一起，分不清谁是谁的。」我问他树真的会把营养送给旁边的树吗。他站起来拍了拍手上的土：「我不是科学家，我不知道什么营养不营养的。但我跟你说一件事。」他指了指那棵槐树：「十五年前那棵槐树生了一场虫害，差点死了。叶子全掉光了，树皮都开裂了。我跟局里打报告要砍掉，局里批了。结果第二年春天，它又发芽了。我干这行二十多年，那种程度的虫害能活过来的，我就见过这一次。」他又指了指银杏：「你注意看——那年槐树虫害的时候，银杏的叶子比往年黄得早。平时十一月底才黄，那年十月中就开始落叶了。像是提前把什么东西交出去了。」他掏出烟点上，吸了一口：「当然了，你要说这是巧合，也行。反正树不会说话。但你看它们的根。」他用脚跺了跺地面。「二十年了，谁也没挪开过。」
text_pinyin: Kēxuéjiā fāxiàn, shùmù zhī jiān tōngguò dìxià de jūnsī wǎngluò hùxiāng chuándì yíngyǎng hé xìnxī. Yī kē lǎo shù kěyǐ tōngguò gēnxì bǎ tànshuǐ huàhéwù shūsòng gěi yī kē shēngzhǎng zài yīnyǐng zhōng de yòumiáo, bāngzhù tā zài guāngzhào bùzú de tiáojiàn xià cúnhuó. Zhè zhǒng xiànxiàng bèi chēngwéi「mùzhì hùliánwǎng」. Dàn wǒ gèng yuànyì bǎ tā lǐjiě wéi yī zhǒng duìhuà——yī zhǒng méiyǒu shēngyīn, méiyǒu yǔyán, shènzhì méiyǒu yìshí de duìhuà. Yī kē shù bú huì「juédìng」qù bāngzhù lìng yī kē shù. Tā zhǐ shì ànzhào zìrán de luójí yùnxíng zhe: duōyú de yǎngfèn liú xiàng quēfá de dìfāng, qiángzhuàng de gètǐ zhīchēng zhe xūruò de qúntǐ. Zhè bú shì císhàn, bú shì cèlüè, shènzhì bú shì běnnéng——tā zhǐ shì shēngmìng zuì jīběn de yùnzuò fāngshì. Wǒ shícháng xiǎng, rénlèi shìfǒu zài jìnhuà guòchéng zhōng diūshī le zhè zhǒng nénglì. Wǒmen fāzhǎn chū le yǔyán, wénzì, wǎngluò——zhèxiē jīngmì fùzá de gōutōng gōngjù, dàn wǒmen shìfǒu yīncǐ biàn de gèng shànyú qīngtīng le? Kǒngpà qiàqià xiāngfǎn. Yǔyán gěi le wǒmen biǎodá de nénglì, tóngshí yě gěi le wǒmen yǐncáng, qīpiàn hé qūjiě de nénglì. Wǒmen kěyǐ yòng yī qiān zhǒng fāngshì shuōhuà, què jīngcháng lián yī jù zhēnxīn huà dōu shuō bù chūkǒu. Shùmù méiyǒu zhèxiē kùnrǎo. Tāmen de「duìhuà」shì chúncuì de wùzhì jiāohuàn——méiyǒu xiūcí, méiyǒu cèlüè, méiyǒu xiánwài zhī yīn. Yī kē mǔ shù bǎ yǎngfèn sòng gěi yòumiáo, bù qiú gǎnjī, bù qiú huíbào, bù qiú bèi jì zhù. Děng dào lǎo shù kūsǐ dǎo xià, tā de qūgàn fǔlàn chéng nítǔ, jìxù zīyǎng zhe xià yī dài. Zài sēnlín lǐ, sǐwáng bú shì zhōngjié, ér shì lìng yī zhǒng xíngshì de jǐyǔ. Wǒ yǒu shíhòu huì zài gōngyuán lǐ zuò hěn jiǔ, kàn zhe liǎng kē xiānglín de shù. Tāmen de zhīyè zài fēng zhōng qīngqīng mócā, fāchū shāshā de shēngxiǎng. Wǒ zhīdào zài kàn bú jiàn de dìxià, tāmen de gēnxì huòxǔ zhèng zài jiāohuàn zhe shénme. Nà zhǒng jiāohuàn shì ānjìng de, huǎnmàn de, bù xūyào bèi rènhé rén jiànzhèng de. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì zuì lǐxiǎng de rénjì guānxì——bú shì bù tíng de shuōhuà, ér shì mòmò de jǐyǔ.
text_en: Old Fang, the park groundskeeper, has worked here for twenty-two years. He says the biggest ginkgo in the park is a hundred and forty years old, and the scholar tree next to it is about ninety. I asked how he knew. He said: 'It is on the tree plaques. Though those plaques were hung thirty years ago, so you have to add thirty yourself.' I asked whether the two trees exchange nutrients through underground fungal networks. He looked up at me: 'You watched that documentary, right? The "Wood Wide Web" thing.' I said yes. He crouched down and brushed away a layer of fallen ginkgo leaves from the base of the tree, revealing the soil underneath. On the surface of the soil was a layer of fine white threads, like spider silk. 'See, these are the hyphae. They are connected. Dig down a foot and the two trees' roots are tangled together—you cannot tell whose is whose.' I asked whether trees send nutrients to neighboring trees. He stood up and brushed the dirt from his hands: 'I am not a scientist. I do not know about nutrients and all that. But let me tell you something.' He pointed at the scholar tree: 'Fifteen years ago that scholar tree had a severe insect infestation, nearly died. Lost every leaf. The bark was splitting. I filed a report to the bureau recommending removal. The bureau approved it. Then the following spring, it sprouted new leaves. In over twenty years of this work, I have seen a tree survive that level of infestation exactly once.' He pointed at the ginkgo: 'Pay attention—the year the scholar tree had the infestation, the ginkgo's leaves turned yellow earlier than usual. Normally they do not yellow until late November. That year the leaves started falling in mid-October. As if it handed something over ahead of schedule.' He pulled out a cigarette and lit it, took a drag: 'you could call it a coincidence. Trees do not talk, after all. But look at their roots.' He stamped the ground with his foot. 'Twenty years now.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么认为人类的沟通能力在某种意义上不如树木？
questions.q_en: Why does the author believe human communication is in some sense inferior to that of trees?

options.text: 因为人类的语言太复杂难以学习
options.pinyin: yīnwèi rénlèi de yǔyán tài fùzá nán yǐ xuéxí
options.text_en: Because human language is too complex to learn

options.text: 因为语言在赋予表达能力的同时也带来了隐藏和欺骗的能力
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yǔyán zài fùyǔ biǎodá nénglì de tóngshí yě dài lái le yǐncáng hé qīpiàn de nénglì
options.text_en: Because language brings the ability to conceal and deceive alongside the ability to express

options.text: 因为人类已经不使用面对面的交流方式
options.pinyin: yīnwèi rénlèi yǐjīng bù shǐyòng miàn duì miàn de jiāoliú fāngshì
options.text_en: Because humans no longer use face-to-face communication

options.text: 因为树木的地下网络传输速度比互联网快
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shùmù de dìxià wǎngluò chuánshū sùdù bǐ hùliánwǎng kuài
options.text_en: Because trees' underground networks transmit faster than the internet

questions.explanation: "tell whose is whose.' I asked whether" — narrative sequence does the causal work; the reason unfolds in the telling.

questions.q_zh: 文章中「在森林里，死亡不是终结，而是另一种形式的给予」这句话表达了什么含义？
questions.q_en: What does the sentence 'In the forest, death is not an ending but another form of giving' express?

options.text: 森林里的动物会吃掉死去的树木
options.pinyin: sēnlín lǐ de dòngwù huì chī diào sǐ qù de shùmù
options.text_en: Animals in the forest eat dead trees

options.text: 老树死后躯干腐烂成泥土，继续滋养下一代生命
options.pinyin: lǎo shù sǐ hòu qūgàn fǔlàn chéng nítǔ, jìxù zīyǎng xià yī dài shēngmìng
options.text_en: After old trees die, their trunks decay into soil, continuing to nourish the next generation

options.text: 死去的树木可以被人类用来建造房屋
options.pinyin: sǐ qù de shùmù kěyǐ bèi rénlèi yòng lái jiànzào fángwū
options.text_en: Dead trees can be used by humans to build houses

options.text: 树木死后会变成化石留给后人研究
options.pinyin: shùmù sǐ hòu huì biànchéng huàshí liú gěi hòurén yánjiū
options.text_en: Trees become fossils after death for future generations to study

questions.explanation: "tell whose is whose.' I asked whether" — the passage stakes its claim here; less precise readings miss it.

title: The Museum of Small Things
text_zh: 我一直想建一座博物馆，专门收藏那些被忽视的微小之物。不是古董、不是名画、不是任何有市场价值的东西，而是那些在日常生活中随时可能被丢弃的细碎之物。比如一张被折了角的火车票，上面的日期显示的是某个已经被遗忘的星期二。比如一颗从旧毛衣上掉下来的纽扣，颜色已经和原来的衣服对不上了。比如一片压在书页间的枫叶，薄得近乎透明，脉络清晰得像一幅精密的地图。这些东西之所以值得收藏，不是因为它们本身有什么价值，而是因为每一件背后都藏着一个不被讲述的故事。那张火车票也许是某个人第一次去见网友的凭证；那颗纽扣也许是一位母亲留下的唯一遗物；那片枫叶也许是一对恋人在分手前的最后一次散步中随手捡起的。我们不知道这些故事——也永远不会知道。但正是这种「不知道」赋予了这些物品一种特殊的诗意。它们是沉默的证人，见证过某个人生命中的某个瞬间，然后被遗留在时间的缝隙里。如果有人问我这座博物馆的意义是什么，我会说：它提醒我们，生活不是由那些轰轰烈烈的大事组成的。生活是由无数个不起眼的小瞬间拼凑而成的——那些转瞬即逝的、无法复制的、甚至当时都没有被注意到的瞬间。一座收藏微小之物的博物馆，其实是一座收藏「被遗忘的时间」的博物馆。走进去的人也许什么都看不懂，但他们会安静下来——因为在这些沉默的物件面前，喧嚣显得格外多余。
text_pinyin: Wǒ yīzhí xiǎng jiàn yī zuò bówùguǎn, zhuānmén shōucáng nàxiē bèi hūshì de wēixiǎo zhī wù. Bú shì gǔdǒng, bú shì mínghuà, bú shì rènhé yǒu shìchǎng jiàzhí de dōngxī, ér shì nàxiē zài rìcháng shēnghuó zhōng suíshí kěnéng bèi diūqì de xìsuì zhī wù. Bǐrú yī zhāng bèi zhé le jiǎo de huǒchē piào, shàngmiàn de rìqī xiǎnshì de shì mǒu gè yǐjīng bèi yíwàng de xīngqī'èr. Bǐrú yī kē cóng jiù máoyī shàng diào xià lái de niǔkòu, yánsè yǐjīng hé yuánlái de yīfú duì bú shàng le. Bǐrú yī piàn yā zài shūyè jiān de fēngyè, báo de jìnhū tòumíng, màiluò qīngxī de xiàng yī fú jīngmì de dìtú. Zhèxiē dōngxī zhī suǒyǐ zhíde shōucáng, bú shì yīnwèi tāmen běnshēn yǒu shénme jiàzhí, ér shì yīnwèi měi yī jiàn bèihòu dōu cáng zhe yī gè bù bèi jiǎngshù de gùshì. Nà zhāng huǒchē piào yěxǔ shì mǒu gè rén dì yī cì qù jiàn wǎngyǒu de píngzhèng; nà kē niǔkòu yěxǔ shì yī wèi mǔqīn liú xià de wéiyī yíwù; nà piàn fēngyè yěxǔ shì yī duì liànrén zài fēnshǒu qián de zuìhòu yī cì sànbù zhōng suíshǒu jiǎn qǐ de. Wǒmen bù zhīdào zhèxiē gùshì——yě yǒngyuǎn bú huì zhīdào. Dàn zhèng shì zhè zhǒng「bù zhīdào」fùyǔ le zhèxiē wùpǐn yī zhǒng tèshū de shīyì. Tāmen shì chénmò de zhèngrén, jiànzhèng guò mǒu gè rén shēngmìng zhōng de mǒu gè shùnjiān, ránhòu bèi yíliú zài shíjiān de fèngxì lǐ. Rúguǒ yǒu rén wèn wǒ zhè zuò bówùguǎn de yìyì shì shénme, wǒ huì shuō: tā tíxǐng wǒmen, shēnghuó bú shì yóu nàxiē hōnghōnglièliè de dà shì zǔchéng de. Shēnghuó shì yóu wúshù gè bù qǐyǎn de xiǎo shùnjiān pīncòu ér chéng de——nàxiē zhuǎnshùnjíshì de, wúfǎ fùzhì de, shènzhì dāngshí dōu méiyǒu bèi zhùyì dào de shùnjiān. Yī zuò shōucáng wēixiǎo zhī wù de bówùguǎn, qíshí shì yī zuò shōucáng「bèi yíwàng de shíjiān」de bówùguǎn. Zǒu jìn qù de rén yěxǔ shénme dōu kàn bù dǒng, dàn tāmen huì ānjìng xià lái——yīnwèi zài zhèxiē chénmò de wùjiàn miànqián, xuānxiāo xiǎnde géwài duōyú.
text_en: I have always wanted to build a museum dedicated to collecting overlooked small things. Not antiques, not famous paintings, not anything with market value, but those trivial objects from daily life that could be discarded at any moment. Like a train ticket with a folded corner, its date showing some forgotten Tuesday. Like a button fallen from an old sweater, its color no longer matching the original garment. Like a maple leaf pressed between book pages, thin to near transparency, its veins as clear as a precise map. These things are worth collecting not because they have any inherent value, but because behind each one hides an untold story. That train ticket might be someone's proof of going to meet an online friend for the first time; that button might be the only relic a mother left behind; that maple leaf might have been casually picked up by a couple on their last walk before breaking up. We don't know these stories—and never will. But it is precisely this 'not knowing' that endows these objects with a special poetic quality. They are silent witnesses who observed a certain moment in someone's life, then were left behind in the crevices of time. If someone asked me what this museum means, I would say: it reminds us that life is not composed of grand, dramatic events. Life is pieced together from countless unremarkable small moments—those fleeting, irreplicable, even unnoticed moments. A museum collecting small things is actually a museum collecting 'forgotten time.' People who walk in may understand nothing, but they will grow quiet—because before these silent objects, noise seems especially superfluous.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为这些微小之物的收藏价值来自哪里？
questions.q_en: Where does the author believe the collection value of these small things comes from?

options.text: 它们的年代久远，具有考古价值
options.pinyin: tāmen de niándài jiǔyuǎn, jùyǒu kǎogǔ jiàzhí
options.text_en: They are from ancient times and have archaeological value

options.text: 每一件背后都藏着一个不被讲述的故事
options.pinyin: měi yī jiàn bèihòu dōu cáng zhe yī gè bù bèi jiǎngshù de gùshì
options.text_en: Behind each one hides an untold story

options.text: 它们的材质独特，在市场上很稀有
options.pinyin: tāmen de cáizhì dútè, zài shìchǎng shàng hěn xīyǒu
options.text_en: Their materials are unique and rare on the market

options.text: 它们代表着某个特定历史时期的文化
options.pinyin: tāmen dàibiǎo zhe mǒu gè tèdìng lìshǐ shíqī de wénhuà
options.text_en: They represent the culture of a specific historical period

questions.explanation: "museum dedicated to collecting overlooked small things" — concrete detail pins this down; the passage doesn't leave it abstract.

questions.q_zh: 作者所说的「收藏被遗忘的时间的博物馆」的核心理念是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the core idea of the author's 'museum collecting forgotten time'?

options.text: 提醒人们珍惜时间，不要浪费生命
options.pinyin: tíxǐng rénmen zhēnxī shíjiān, búyào làngfèi shēngmìng
options.text_en: Reminding people to cherish time and not waste life

options.text: 记录历史上被遗忘的重大事件
options.pinyin: jìlù lìshǐ shàng bèi yíwàng de zhòngdà shìjiàn
options.text_en: Recording forgotten major historical events

options.text: 生活由无数不起眼的小瞬间拼凑而成，值得被安静地注视
options.pinyin: shēnghuó yóu wúshù bù qǐyǎn de xiǎo shùnjiān pīncòu ér chéng, zhíde bèi ānjìng de zhùshì
options.text_en: Life is pieced together from countless unremarkable small moments, worthy of quiet contemplation

options.text: 收藏旧物可以帮助人们缓解现代生活的压力
options.pinyin: shōucáng jiùwù kěyǐ bāngzhù rénmen huǎnjiě xiàndài shēnghuó de yālì
options.text_en: Collecting old things can help relieve the pressure of modern life

questions.explanation: "those fleeting, irreplicable, even unnoticed moments" — the passage organizes its evidence around this point.

title: The Geography of Longing
text_zh: 思念是有方向的。当我想念一个人的时候，我的身体会不由自主地朝向某个方位——不是抬头看天，也不是低头看地，而是面朝某个特定的方向，仿佛那个人就在那个方向的尽头等着我。这种方向感不是地理意义上的精确定位，而是一种身体记忆。我的身体记得，从这座城市到那座城市，应该朝哪个方向走。即使我知道那个人可能早已不在那座城市了，我的身体仍然固执地朝向那个方向倾斜。思念还有距离感。想念一个在隔壁城市的人和想念一个在另一个大洲的人，感觉是完全不同的。前者像一根被轻轻拉长的橡皮筋——有弹性，有回弹的希望；后者像一根细线被拉到了极限——绷得那么紧，紧到你害怕它随时会断。最奇特的是思念已经不在世上的人。那种思念没有方向、没有距离，因为你不知道该朝哪里想。它像一团雾气，弥漫在身体的每一个角落，无处不在，又无处可以被指认。你想念他们，但你的思念找不到落点——没有可以寄出的地址，没有可以拨打的号码，没有可以朝之微笑的面孔。于是思念就悬浮在空中，像一封写好了却无法投递的信。我有时候觉得，正是这种「无处安放」的感觉使得对逝去之人的思念变得如此沉重。如果思念可以有一个明确的方向，我们至少可以朝着那个方向走去——即使永远到达不了，走的过程本身也是一种安慰。但当方向消失了，我们只能站在原地，让思念在体内无声地回荡。墓碑给了思念一个方向。一个可以朝之走去的地方，一个可以放下花束、说几句话、然后转身离开的坐标。
text_pinyin: Sīniàn shì yǒu fāngxiàng de. Dāng wǒ xiǎngniàn yī gè rén de shíhòu, wǒ de shēntǐ huì bù yóu zì zhǔ de cháo xiàng mǒu gè fāngwèi——bú shì tái tóu kàn tiān, yě bú shì dī tóu kàn dì, ér shì miàn cháo mǒu gè tèdìng de fāngxiàng, fǎngfú nàge rén jiù zài nàge fāngxiàng de jìntóu děng zhe wǒ. Zhè zhǒng fāngxiàng gǎn bú shì dìlǐ yìyì shàng de jīngquè dìngwèi, ér shì yī zhǒng shēntǐ jìyì. Wǒ de shēntǐ jìde, cóng zhè zuò chéngshì dào nà zuò chéngshì, yīnggāi cháo nǎge fāngxiàng zǒu. Jíshǐ wǒ zhīdào nàge rén kěnéng zǎo yǐ bú zài nà zuò chéngshì le, wǒ de shēntǐ réngrán gùzhí de cháo xiàng nàge fāngxiàng qīngxié. Sīniàn hái yǒu jùlí gǎn. Xiǎngniàn yī gè zài gébì chéngshì de rén hé xiǎngniàn yī gè zài lìng yī gè dàzhōu de rén, gǎnjué shì wánquán bùtóng de. Qiánzhě xiàng yī gēn bèi qīngqīng lā cháng de xiàngpí jīn——yǒu tánxìng, yǒu huítán de xīwàng; hòuzhě xiàng yī gēn xì xiàn bèi lā dào le jíxiàn——bēng de nàme jǐn, jǐn dào nǐ hàipà tā suíshí huì duàn. Zuì qíté de shì sīniàn yǐjīng bú zài shì shàng de rén. Nà zhǒng sīniàn méiyǒu fāngxiàng, méiyǒu jùlí, yīnwèi nǐ bù zhīdào gāi cháo nǎlǐ xiǎng. Tā xiàng yī tuán wùqì, mímàn zài shēntǐ de měi yī gè jiǎoluò, wú chù bú zài, yòu wú chù kěyǐ bèi zhǐrèn. Nǐ xiǎngniàn tāmen, dàn nǐ de sīniàn zhǎo bú dào luòdiǎn——méiyǒu kěyǐ jì chū de dìzhǐ, méiyǒu kěyǐ bōdǎ de hàomǎ, méiyǒu kěyǐ cháo zhī wēixiào de miànkǒng. Yúshì sīniàn jiù xuánfú zài kōng zhōng, xiàng yī fēng xiě hǎo le què wúfǎ tóudì de xìn. Wǒ yǒu shíhòu juéde, zhèng shì zhè zhǒng「wú chù ān fàng」de gǎnjué shǐ de duì shì qù zhī rén de sīniàn biàn de rúcǐ chénzhòng. Rúguǒ sīniàn kěyǐ yǒu yī gè míngquè de fāngxiàng, wǒmen zhìshǎo kěyǐ cháo zhe nàge fāngxiàng zǒu qù——jíshǐ yǒngyuǎn dàodá bù liǎo, zǒu de guòchéng běnshēn yě shì yī zhǒng ānwèi. Dàn dāng fāngxiàng xiāoshī le, wǒmen zhǐ néng zhàn zài yuándì, ràng sīniàn zài tǐnèi wúshēng de huídàng. Yěxǔ zhè jiù shì wèishéme rénmen yào jiàn mùbēi——bú shì wèi le sǐzhě, ér shì wèi le huó zhe de rén. Mùbēi gěi le sīniàn yī gè fāngxiàng, yī gè kěyǐ cháo zhī zǒu qù de dìfāng, yī gè kěyǐ fàng xià huāshù, shuō jǐ jù huà, ránhòu zhuǎnshēn líkāi de zuòbiāo.
text_en: Longing has direction. When I miss someone, my body involuntarily turns toward a certain bearing—not looking up at the sky or down at the ground, but facing a specific direction, as if that person were waiting for me at its far end. This sense of direction is not geographic precision but a kind of body memory. My body remembers which direction to travel from this city to that one. Even when I know the person may have long since left that city, my body still stubbornly tilts in that direction. Longing also has a sense of distance. Missing someone in a neighboring city and missing someone on another continent feel completely different. The former is like a rubber band gently stretched—elastic, with hope of springing back; the latter is like a thin thread pulled to its limit—so taut you fear it could snap at any moment. Most peculiar is longing for someone no longer in this world. That kind of longing has no direction, no distance, because you don't know where to direct your thoughts. It is like a mist, pervading every corner of the body, omnipresent yet impossible to locate. You miss them, but your longing finds no landing point—no address to send to, no number to dial, no face to smile toward. So the longing hangs suspended in air, like a letter written but impossible to deliver. Sometimes I think it is precisely this feeling of 'having nowhere to place it' that makes longing for the departed so heavy. If longing could have a clear direction, we could at least walk toward it—even if we could never arrive, the act of walking would itself be a comfort. But when direction disappears, we can only stand in place, letting longing echo silently within. A gravestone gives longing a direction.

questions.q_zh: 作者认为思念去世之人的感受为什么特别沉重？
questions.q_en: Why does the author believe longing for the deceased feels particularly heavy?

options.text: 因为去世的人永远无法回来
options.pinyin: yīnwèi qùshì de rén yǒngyuǎn wúfǎ huí lái
options.text_en: Because the deceased can never return

options.text: 因为那种思念没有方向，无处安放，找不到落点
options.pinyin: yīnwèi nà zhǒng sīniàn méiyǒu fāngxiàng, wú chù ān fàng, zhǎo bú dào luòdiǎn
options.text_en: Because that longing has no direction, nowhere to be placed, no landing point

options.text: 因为人们通常对去世的人感到愧疚
options.pinyin: yīnwèi rénmen tōngcháng duì qùshì de rén gǎndào kuìjiù
options.text_en: Because people usually feel guilty toward the deceased

options.text: 因为时间会让人逐渐忘记去世的人
options.pinyin: yīnwèi shíjiān huì ràng rén zhújiàn wàngjì qùshì de rén
options.text_en: Because time gradually makes people forget the deceased

questions.explanation: "If longing could have a clear direction" — narrative sequence does the causal work; the reason unfolds in the telling.

questions.q_zh: 根据文章，墓碑的真正意义是什么？
questions.q_en: What is the true meaning of tombstones?

options.text: 纪念死者的生平和功绩
options.pinyin: jìniàn sǐzhě de shēngpíng hé gōngjì
options.text_en: To commemorate the life and achievements of the deceased

options.text: 标记死者的埋葬位置
options.pinyin: biāojì sǐzhě de máizàng wèizhì
options.text_en: To mark the burial location of the deceased

options.text: 给活着的人的思念一个方向和可以走去的坐标
options.pinyin: gěi huó zhe de rén de sīniàn yī gè fāngxiàng hé kěyǐ zǒu qù de zuòbiāo
options.text_en: To give the living's longing a direction and coordinates to walk toward

options.text: 提醒活着的人生命的短暂和珍贵
options.pinyin: tíxǐng huó zhe de rén shēngmìng de duǎnzàn hé zhēnguì
options.text_en: To remind the living of the brevity and preciousness of life

questions.explanation: "but facing a specific direction" — concrete specifics encode the meaning; the abstraction grows from the image.

title: The Apprenticeship of Waiting
text_zh: 现代人几乎丧失了等待的能力。我们等红灯会焦躁不安，等外卖超过三十分钟就开始投诉，等一条消息的回复超过一个小时就开始胡思乱想。我们活在一个「即时满足」的时代——一切都要快，快到你来不及感受等待本身的质地。但等待曾经是一门手艺。在没有电话的年代，人们写信，然后等待回信。那种等待可能持续数周甚至数月。在等待的日子里，生活并没有暂停——人们照常劳作、吃饭、入睡，但心里始终悬着一根细线，连接着某个远方的人。那根细线不是焦虑的，而是温柔的。它让日常的每一个动作都多了一层含义：洗菜的时候会想他最近吃得好不好，晾衣服的时候会抬头看看天气是否也影响着他那边的生活。等待把一个人的存在编织进了另一个人的日常之中，即使他们相隔千里。我的祖母曾经等了祖父三年。他去了远方工作，每隔两三个月寄一封信回来。祖母把每一封信都叠好，放在一个铁盒子里。她说，等信的日子并不难熬，因为等待本身就是一种陪伴——只要你知道那个人会回来，等待就不是空虚的，而是充实的。它像一颗种子埋在土里，你看不见它在生长，但你知道它在那里，在你看不到的地方默默地扎根、发芽。如今我们不再需要等信，但我偶尔会刻意给自己制造一些等待的机会——比如泡一壶茶，然后什么都不做，就坐在那里等水慢慢变色；比如种一盆花，然后每天早上去看它有没有开。这些刻意的等待像是一种修行，提醒我：不是所有好的东西都应该立刻得到。有些东西，正是因为等待，才变得弥足珍贵。
text_pinyin: Xiàndài rén jīhū sàngshī le děngdài de nénglì. Wǒmen děng hóngdēng huì jiāozào bù'ān, děng wàimài chāoguò sānshí fēnzhōng jiù kāishǐ tóusù, děng yī tiáo xiāoxi de huífù chāoguò yī gè xiǎoshí jiù kāishǐ húsī luàn xiǎng. Wǒmen huó zài yī gè「jíshí mǎnzú」de shídài——yīqiè dōu yào kuài, kuài dào nǐ lái bù jí gǎnshòu děngdài běnshēn de zhìdì. Dàn děngdài céngjīng shì yī mén shǒuyì. Zài méiyǒu diànhuà de niándài, rénmen xiě xìn, ránhòu děngdài huíxìn. Nà zhǒng děngdài kěnéng chíxù shù zhōu shènzhì shù yuè. Zài děngdài de rìzi lǐ, shēnghuó bìng méiyǒu zàntíng——rénmen zhàocháng láozuò, chīfàn, rùshuì, dàn xīnlǐ shǐzhōng xuán zhe yī gēn xì xiàn, liánjiē zhe mǒu gè yuǎnfāng de rén. Nà gēn xì xiàn bú shì jiāolǜ de, ér shì wēnróu de. Tā ràng rìcháng de měi yī gè dòngzuò dōu duō le yī céng hányì: xǐ cài de shíhòu huì xiǎng tā zuìjìn chī de hǎo bù hǎo, liàng yīfú de shíhòu huì tái tóu kànkàn tiānqì shìfǒu yě yǐngxiǎng zhe tā nàbiān de shēnghuó. Děngdài bǎ yī gè rén de cúnzài biānzhī jìn le lìng yī gè rén de rìcháng zhī zhōng, jíshǐ tāmen xiānggé qiān lǐ. Wǒ de zǔmǔ céngjīng děng le zǔfù sān nián. Tā qù le yuǎnfāng gōngzuò, měi gé liǎng sān gè yuè jì yī fēng xìn huí lái. Zǔmǔ bǎ měi yī fēng xìn dōu dié hǎo, fàng zài yī gè tiě hézi lǐ. Tā shuō, děng xìn de rìzi bìng bù nán'áo, yīnwèi děngdài běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng péibàn——zhǐyào nǐ zhīdào nàge rén huì huí lái, děngdài jiù bú shì kōngxū de, ér shì chōngshí de. Tā xiàng yī kē zhǒngzǐ mái zài tǔ lǐ, nǐ kàn bú jiàn tā zài shēngzhǎng, dàn nǐ zhīdào tā zài nàlǐ, zài nǐ kàn bú dào de dìfāng mòmò de zhā gēn, fāyá. Rújīn wǒmen bú zài xūyào děng xìn, dàn wǒ ǒu'ěr huì kèyì gěi zìjǐ zhìzào yīxiē děngdài de jīhuì——bǐrú pào yī hú chá, ránhòu shénme dōu bù zuò, jiù zuò zài nàlǐ děng shuǐ mànmàn biàn sè; bǐrú zhòng yī pén huā, ránhòu měi tiān zǎoshang qù kàn tā yǒu méiyǒu kāi. Zhèxiē kèyì de děngdài xiàng shì yī zhǒng xiūxíng, tíxǐng wǒ: bú shì suǒyǒu hǎo de dōngxī dōu yīnggāi lìkè dédào. Yǒuxiē dōngxī, zhèng shì yīnwèi děngdài, cái biàn de mízú zhēnguì.
text_en: Modern people have nearly lost the ability to wait. We grow restless at red lights, complain when food delivery exceeds thirty minutes, and overthink when a message goes unanswered for over an hour. We live in an age of 'instant gratification'—everything must be fast, so fast you have no time to feel the texture of waiting itself. But waiting was once a craft. In the age before telephones, people wrote letters, then waited for replies. That waiting could last weeks or even months. During the waiting days, life did not pause—people went about their work, ate, slept, but a thin thread always hung in their hearts, connecting them to someone far away. That thread was not anxious but tender. It gave every daily action an extra layer of meaning: while washing vegetables, one would wonder whether he had been eating well lately; while hanging laundry, one would look up to see whether the weather was also affecting life on his side. Waiting wove one person's existence into another's daily life, even when they were separated by a thousand miles. My grandmother once waited three years for my grandfather. He went far away to work, sending a letter back every two or three months. Grandmother folded each letter neatly and placed it in a tin box. She said the days of waiting for letters were not hard to endure, because waiting itself is a form of companionship—as long as you know that person will return, waiting is not emptiness but fullness. It is like a seed buried in soil: you cannot see it growing, but you know it is there, quietly taking root and sprouting where you cannot see. Today we no longer need to wait for letters, but I occasionally create deliberate opportunities for waiting—like brewing a pot of tea, then doing nothing, sitting there watching the water slowly change color; like planting a pot of flowers, then checking each morning whether it has bloomed. These deliberate acts of waiting are like a form of practice, reminding me: not all good things should be obtained immediately.

questions.q_zh: 祖母认为等信的日子为什么不难熬？
questions.q_en: Why did the grandmother believe the days of waiting for letters were not hard to endure?

options.text: 因为她有很多其他事情可以做
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā yǒu hěn duō qítā shìqíng kěyǐ zuò
options.text_en: Because she had many other things to do

options.text: 因为信来得很频繁
options.pinyin: yīnwèi xìn lái de hěn pínfán
options.text_en: Because letters came very frequently

options.text: 因为等待本身就是一种陪伴，知道那个人会回来就不空虚
options.pinyin: yīnwèi děngdài běnshēn jiù shì yī zhǒng péibàn, zhīdào nàge rén huì huí lái jiù bù kōngxū
options.text_en: Because waiting itself is companionship, and knowing the person will return means it's not empty

options.text: 因为那个年代的人习惯了慢节奏的生活
options.pinyin: yīnwèi nàge niándài de rén xíguàn le màn jiézòu de shēnghuó
options.text_en: Because people of that era were used to a slow pace of life

questions.explanation: "waiting is not emptiness but fullness" — cause is shown through concrete sequence, not stated as principle.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么要刻意给自己制造等待的机会？
questions.q_en: Why does the author deliberately create opportunities to wait?

options.text: 因为他没有太多事情要做
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā méiyǒu tài duō shìqíng yào zuò
options.text_en: Because he doesn't have much to do

options.text: 作为一种修行，提醒自己有些好东西因为等待才变得珍贵
options.pinyin: zuòwéi yī zhǒng xiūxíng, tíxǐng zìjǐ yǒuxiē hǎo dōngxī yīnwèi děngdài cái biàn de zhēnguì
options.text_en: As a form of practice, reminding himself that some good things become precious through waiting

options.text: 因为他怀念过去没有电话的年代
options.pinyin: yīnwèi tā huáiniàn guòqù méiyǒu diànhuà de niándài
options.text_en: Because he misses the era before telephones

options.text: 因为泡茶和种花是他的爱好
options.pinyin: yīnwèi pào chá hé zhòng huā shì tā de àihào
options.text_en: Because brewing tea and growing flowers are his hobbies

questions.explanation: "like brewing a pot of tea" — negation reveals the reason: what is ruled out clarifies what drives the action.

title: The Dialect of Rain
text_zh: 每一场雨都有自己的方言。落在铁皮屋顶上的雨说的是急促而清脆的短句，像一个性急的说书人；落在柏油路面上的雨说的是含糊的长句，像一个醉了酒的老人在自言自语；落在湖面上的雨几乎不说话——它只是安静地融入水中，连涟漪都是轻声的。我小时候住在乡下，屋顶是瓦片的。雨落在瓦片上的声音是我听过的最美的方言——不急不缓，有节奏但不刻板，像一个有教养的人在用恰到好处的语速讲述一个不紧不慢的故事。那种声音有一种催眠的效果。不是让你昏昏欲睡的那种催眠，而是让你的心慢慢沉下来的那种——沉到一个很深很安静的地方，那里没有焦虑、没有期待、没有需要回应的声音，只有瓦片上的雨声，一滴接着一滴，像时间本身在敲门。后来我搬到了城市。城市里的雨听起来完全不同——它被高楼大厦的玻璃幕墙弹来弹去，被排水管道吞没，被汽车轮胎碾碎，变成了一种嘈杂的、没有个性的噪音。城市的建筑材料不允许雨说它自己的话——钢筋水泥把所有的声音都压缩成了同一种单调的嗡嗡声。我怀念瓦片上的雨。那种雨是有表情的——开始时试探性的几滴像一声轻咳，密集时倾泻而下像一段慷慨激昂的演说，渐渐变小时又像一个人说完了所有想说的话之后意犹未尽的沉默。如今我偶尔在网上搜索「瓦片雨声」的录音来听。戴上耳机的那一刻，我仿佛被传送回了小时候的那间老屋——躺在床上，听着屋顶上的雨用它的方言对我说：没事的，一切都在，你只需要闭上眼睛。
text_pinyin: Měi yī chǎng yǔ dōu yǒu zìjǐ de fāngyán. Luò zài tiěpí wūdǐng shàng de yǔ shuō de shì jícù ér qīngcuì de duǎnjù, xiàng yī gè xìngjí de shuōshūrén; luò zài bǎiyóu lùmiàn shàng de yǔ shuō de shì hánhú de chángjù, xiàng yī gè zuì le jiǔ de lǎorén zài zìyánzìyǔ; luò zài húmiàn shàng de yǔ jīhū bù shuōhuà——tā zhǐ shì ānjìng de róngrù shuǐ zhōng, lián liányī dōu shì qīngshēng de. Wǒ xiǎo shíhòu zhù zài xiāngxià, wūdǐng shì wǎpiàn de. Yǔ luò zài wǎpiàn shàng de shēngyīn shì wǒ tīng guò de zuì měi de fāngyán——bù jí bù huǎn, yǒu jiézòu dàn bù kèbǎn, xiàng yī gè yǒu jiàoyǎng de rén zài yòng qià dào hǎo chù de yǔsù jiǎngshù yī gè bù jǐn bù màn de gùshì. Nà zhǒng shēngyīn yǒu yī zhǒng cuīmián de xiàoguǒ. Bú shì ràng nǐ hūnhūn yùshuì de nà zhǒng cuīmián, ér shì ràng nǐ de xīn mànmàn chén xià lái de nà zhǒng——chén dào yī gè hěn shēn hěn ānjìng de dìfāng, nàlǐ méiyǒu jiāolǜ, méiyǒu qīdài, méiyǒu xūyào huíyìng de shēngyīn, zhǐyǒu wǎpiàn shàng de yǔ shēng, yī dī jiē zhe yī dī, xiàng shíjiān běnshēn zài qiāo mén. Hòulái wǒ bān dào le chéngshì. Chéngshì lǐ de yǔ tīng qǐlái wánquán bùtóng——tā bèi gāolóu dàshà de bōlí mùqiáng tán lái tán qù, bèi páishuǐ guǎndào tūnmò, bèi qìchē lúntāi niǎn suì, biànchéng le yī zhǒng cáozá de, méiyǒu gèxìng de zàoyīn. Chéngshì de jiànzhù cáiliào bù yǔnxǔ yǔ shuō tā zìjǐ de huà——gāngjīn shuǐní bǎ suǒyǒu de shēngyīn dōu yāsuō chéng le tóng yī zhǒng dāndiào de wēngwēng shēng. Wǒ huáiniàn wǎpiàn shàng de yǔ. Nà zhǒng yǔ shì yǒu biǎoqíng de——kāishǐ shí shìtàn xìng de jǐ dī xiàng yī shēng qīng ké, mìjí shí qīngxiè ér xià xiàng yī duàn kāngkǎi jī'áng de yǎnshuō, jiànjiàn biàn xiǎo shí yòu xiàng yī gè rén shuō wán le suǒyǒu xiǎng shuō de huà zhīhòu yì yóu wèi jìn de chénmò. Rújīn wǒ ǒu'ěr zài wǎng shàng sōusuǒ「wǎpiàn yǔ shēng」de lùyīn lái tīng. Dài shàng ěrjī de nà yī kè, wǒ fǎngfú bèi chuánsòng huí le xiǎo shíhòu de nà jiān lǎo wū——tǎng zài chuáng shàng, tīng zhe wūdǐng shàng de yǔ yòng tā de fāngyán duì wǒ shuō: méi shì de, yīqiè dōu zài, nǐ zhǐ xūyào bì shàng yǎnjīng.
text_en: Every rainfall has its own dialect. Rain falling on a tin roof speaks in urgent, crisp short phrases, like an impatient storyteller; rain falling on asphalt speaks in slurred long sentences, like a drunken old man talking to himself; rain falling on a lake surface hardly speaks at all—it simply merges quietly into the water, even its ripples whispered. As a child I lived in the countryside with a tile roof. The sound of rain on tiles was the most beautiful dialect I have ever heard—neither rushed nor slow, rhythmic but not rigid, like a well-mannered person telling an unhurried story at just the right pace. That sound had a hypnotic effect. Not the kind that makes you drowsy, but the kind that lets your heart slowly sink down—down to a deep, quiet place where there is no anxiety, no expectation, no sound requiring response, only the rain on tiles, drop after drop, like time itself knocking at the door. Later I moved to the city. City rain sounds completely different—bounced back and forth by the glass curtain walls of tall buildings, swallowed by drainage pipes, crushed by car tires, it becomes a noisy, characterless racket. The city's building materials do not allow rain to speak its own words—reinforced concrete compresses all sound into the same monotonous hum. I miss the rain on tiles. That rain had expression—the first tentative drops like a soft cough, the dense downpour like a passionate speech, the gradual softening like the lingering silence after someone has said everything they wanted to say. Now I occasionally search online for recordings of 'rain on tiles' to listen to. The moment I put on headphones, I feel transported back to that old house from childhood—lying in bed, listening to the rain on the roof say to me in its dialect: it's all right, everything is here, you only need to close your eyes.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说城市里的雨「没有个性」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say city rain 'has no personality'?

options.text: 因为城市里下雨的时间太短
options.pinyin: yīnwèi chéngshì lǐ xià yǔ de shíjiān tài duǎn
options.text_en: Because it rains for too short a time in cities

options.text: 因为城市的建筑材料把所有雨声压缩成了同一种单调的噪音

title: The Temperature of Words
text_zh: 有些文字是冰冷的。法律条文、使用说明、官方通知——这些文字精确、高效，但读起来像是在触摸金属表面，光滑而冰凉，没有一丝体温。有些文字是滚烫的。革命宣言、情书、悼词——这些文字燃烧着写作者的情感，读的人能感受到纸页背后的心跳，甚至能被那种热度灼伤。但我最珍惜的是那些温温的文字——既不冰冷也不滚烫，像一杯放到刚好可以入口的茶。这种文字不试图说服你什么，也不试图打动你什么。它只是平静地叙述，用一种不急不缓的语气告诉你一些事情——可能是一个普通人的一天，可能是一条街道在四季中的变化，可能是一个孩子第一次发现蚂蚁搬家时的心情。这种文字的温度来自真诚。不是那种刻意展示的真诚——「你看，我多么坦率」——而是一种浑然天成的真诚，像一个人说话时不加修饰的语气，像呼吸一样自然。你读这种文字的时候不会激动，不会落泪，不会拍案而起。你只是会觉得安心，像冬天走进一间有人在的屋子——看不到火炉，但能感受到温暖是从某个角落悄悄散发出来的。我一直相信，最好的写作不是让读者「感受到」什么，而是让读者「安静下来」。一篇好的文章读完之后，心里应该比读之前更安静一些，像一面被搅动的水面慢慢恢复平静。那些追求震撼效果的文字也许更容易被记住，但那些温温的文字才是可以反复阅读的——因为每一次重读，你都能从中感受到一种不同的温度，就像同一杯茶在不同的季节里品尝，味道总会有细微的差异。
text_pinyin: Yǒuxiē wénzì shì bīnglěng de. Fǎlǜ tiáowén, shǐyòng shuōmíng, guānfāng tōngzhī——zhèxiē wénzì jīngquè, gāoxiào, dàn dú qǐ lái xiàng shì zài chùmō jīnshǔ biǎomiàn, guānghuá ér bīngliáng, méiyǒu yī sī tǐwēn. Yǒuxiē wénzì shì gǔntàng de. Gémìng xuānyán, qíngshū, dàocí——zhèxiē wénzì ránshāo zhe xiězuòzhě de qínggǎn, dú de rén néng gǎnshòu dào zhǐyè bèihòu de xīntiào, shènzhì néng bèi nà zhǒng rèdù zhuóshāng. Dàn wǒ zuì zhēnxī de shì nàxiē wēnwēn de wénzì——jì bù bīnglěng yě bù gǔntàng, xiàng yī bēi fàng dào gānghǎo kěyǐ rùkǒu de chá. Zhè zhǒng wénzì bù shìtú shuōfú nǐ shénme, yě bù shìtú dǎdòng nǐ shénme. Tā zhǐ shì píngjìng de xùshù, yòng yī zhǒng bù jí bù huǎn de yǔqì gàosu nǐ yīxiē shìqíng——kěnéng shì yī gè pǔtōng rén de yī tiān, kěnéng shì yī tiáo jiēdào zài sìjì zhōng de biànhuà, kěnéng shì yī gè háizi dì yī cì fāxiàn mǎyǐ bānjiā shí de xīnqíng. Zhè zhǒng wénzì de wēndù lái zì zhēnchéng. Bú shì nà zhǒng kèyì zhǎnshì de zhēnchéng——「nǐ kàn, wǒ duōme tǎnlǜ」——ér shì yī zhǒng húnrán tiānchéng de zhēnchéng, xiàng yī gè rén shuōhuà shí bù jiā xiūshì de yǔqì, xiàng hūxī yīyàng zìrán. Nǐ dú zhè zhǒng wénzì de shíhòu bú huì jīdòng, bú huì luòlèi, bú huì pāi'àn ér qǐ. Nǐ zhǐ shì huì juéde ānxīn, xiàng dōngtiān zǒu jìn yī jiān yǒu rén zài de wūzi——kàn bú dào huǒlú, dàn néng gǎnshòu dào wēnnuǎn shì cóng mǒu gè jiǎoluò qiāoqiāo sànfā chū lái de. Wǒ yīzhí xiāngxìn, zuì hǎo de xiězuò bú shì ràng dúzhě「gǎnshòu dào」shénme, ér shì ràng dúzhě「ānjìng xià lái」. Yī piān hǎo de wénzhāng dú wán zhīhòu, xīnlǐ yīnggāi bǐ dú zhīqián gèng ānjìng yīxiē, xiàng yī miàn bèi jiǎodòng de shuǐmiàn mànmàn huīfù píngjìng. Nàxiē zhuīqiú zhènhàn xiàoguǒ de wénzì yěxǔ gèng róngyì bèi jì zhù, dàn nàxiē wēnwēn de wénzì cái shì kěyǐ fǎnfù yuèdú de——yīnwèi měi yī cì chóng dú, nǐ dōu néng cóng zhōng gǎnshòu dào yī zhǒng bùtóng de wēndù, jiù xiàng tóng yī bēi chá zài bùtóng de jìjié lǐ pǐncháng, wèidào zǒng huì yǒu xìwēi de chāyì.
text_en: Some words are cold. Legal provisions, user manuals, official notices—these words are precise and efficient, but reading them is like touching a metal surface: smooth and cool, without a trace of body heat. Some words are scalding. Revolutionary manifestos, love letters, eulogies—these words burn with the writer's emotion; the reader can feel the heartbeat behind the pages, may even be burned by that heat. But what I treasure most are words that are gently warm—neither cold nor scalding, like a cup of tea cooled to just the right temperature for drinking. These words do not try to persuade or move you. They simply narrate calmly, telling you things in an unhurried tone—perhaps an ordinary person's day, perhaps how a street changes through the seasons, perhaps a child's feeling upon first discovering ants moving house. The warmth of these words comes from sincerity. Not the kind of sincerity deliberately displayed—'Look how candid I am'—but a natural, unforced sincerity, like someone speaking in an unadorned tone, as natural as breathing. When you read these words, you will not be excited, will not shed tears, will not slam the table and rise. You will simply feel at ease, like walking into an occupied room in winter—you cannot see the stove, but you can feel warmth quietly emanating from some corner. I have always believed that the best writing does not make readers 'feel' something but makes readers 'grow quiet.' After reading a good piece of writing, the heart should be quieter than before, like a disturbed water surface slowly returning to calm. Words that pursue shock value may be more easily remembered, but gently warm words are the ones that can be read again and again—because with each rereading, you sense a different temperature, just as the same cup of tea tastes subtly different in different seasons.

title: The Archaeology of an Afternoon Nap
text_zh: 午睡是一种正在消亡的文明。在我的童年记忆中，午睡是一天之中最神圣的仪式。午饭过后，整个村庄都会陷入一种集体的沉寂。大人们回到各自的房间，拉上窗帘，躺在凉席上，用一把蒲扇盖住脸，几分钟之内就进入了梦乡。孩子们被要求安静——不许跑、不许叫、不许发出任何可能惊扰大人的声音。于是我们也躺下来，假装睡着，其实是睁着眼睛盯着天花板上的裂缝，看一只壁虎慢慢地从墙角爬到灯泡旁边。那种午后的安静是有厚度的。不是真空的安静，而是被填满了的安静——填满了蝉鸣、微风吹动竹叶的沙沙声、远处水牛在泥潭里翻身的闷响。这些声音不但没有打破安静，反而成为了安静的一部分，像一块织锦上的暗纹——你不仔细看就看不到，但正是它们让织锦变得完整。如今午睡变成了一种奢侈。办公室里没有躺下的空间，午休时间被压缩到可以忽略不计的长度，人们在格子间里趴在桌上小憩十五分钟，醒来后脸上印着键盘的痕迹。那不是午睡——那是身体在高压之下的短暂崩溃。真正的午睡需要三个条件：一个可以完全平躺的空间、一段不被打扰的时间、以及一种「这个世界可以没有我运转一两个小时」的心安。前两个条件尚且可以创造，第三个条件在当今社会几乎是不可能的。我们已经习惯了随时在线、随时回应、随时保持高效。「放下」变成了一件需要学习的事情。也许重新学会午睡是现代人最需要的考古实践——不是挖掘古代的遗迹，而是挖掘我们自己内心深处那个可以安然入睡的孩子。
text_pinyin: Wǔshuì shì yī zhǒng zhèng zài xiāowáng de wénmíng. Zài wǒ de tóngnián jìyì zhōng, wǔshuì shì yī tiān zhī zhōng zuì shénshèng de yíshì. Wǔfàn guòhòu, zhěnggè cūnzhuāng dōu huì xiànrù yī zhǒng jítǐ de chénjì. Dàrénmen huí dào gèzì de fángjiān, lā shàng chuānglián, tǎng zài liángxí shàng, yòng yī bǎ púshàn gài zhù liǎn, jǐ fēnzhōng zhī nèi jiù jìnrù le mèngxiāng. Háizimen bèi yāoqiú ānjìng——bù xǔ pǎo, bù xǔ jiào, bù xǔ fāchū rènhé kěnéng jīngrǎo dàrén de shēngyīn. Yúshì wǒmen yě tǎng xià lái, jiǎzhuāng shuì zháo, qíshí shì zhēng zhe yǎnjīng dīng zhe tiānhuābǎn shàng de lièfèng, kàn yī zhī bìhǔ mànmàn de cóng qiáng jiǎo pá dào dēngpào pángbiān. Nà zhǒng wǔhòu de ānjìng shì yǒu hòudù de. Bú shì zhēnkōng de ānjìng, ér shì bèi tián mǎn le de ānjìng——tián mǎn le chán míng, wēifēng chuī dòng zhúyè de shāshā shēng, yuǎn chù shuǐniú zài nítán lǐ fānshēn de mèn xiǎng. Zhèxiē shēngyīn bú dàn méiyǒu dǎpò ānjìng, fǎn'ér chéngwéi le ānjìng de yī bùfèn, xiàng yī kuài zhījǐn shàng de ànwén——nǐ bù zǐxì kàn jiù kàn bú dào, dàn zhèng shì tāmen ràng zhījǐn biàn de wánzhěng. Rújīn wǔshuì biànchéng le yī zhǒng shēchǐ. Bàngōngshì lǐ méiyǒu tǎng xià de kōngjiān, wǔxiū shíjiān bèi yāsuō dào kěyǐ hūlüè bù jì de chángdù, rénmen zài gézijiān lǐ pā zài zhuō shàng xiǎo qì shíwǔ fēnzhōng, xǐng lái hòu liǎn shàng yìn zhe jiànpán de hénjì. Nà bú shì wǔshuì——nà shì shēntǐ zài gāoyā zhī xià de duǎnzàn bēngkuì. Zhēnzhèng de wǔshuì xūyào sān gè tiáojiàn: yī gè kěyǐ wánquán píng tǎng de kōngjiān, yī duàn bú bèi dǎrǎo de shíjiān, yǐjí yī zhǒng「zhège shìjiè kěyǐ méiyǒu wǒ yùnzhuǎn yī liǎng gè xiǎoshí」de xīn'ān. Qián liǎng gè tiáojiàn shàngqiě kěyǐ chuàngzào, dì sān gè tiáojiàn zài dāngjīn shèhuì jīhū shì bù kěnéng de. Wǒmen yǐjīng xíguàn le suíshí zàixiàn, suíshí huíyìng, suíshí bǎochí gāoxiào.「Fàng xià」biànchéng le yī jiàn xūyào xuéxí de shìqíng. Yěxǔ chóngxīn xué huì wǔshuì shì xiàndài rén zuì xūyào de kǎogǔ shíjiàn——bú shì wājué gǔdài de yíjì, ér shì wājué wǒmen zìjǐ nèixīn shēnchù nàge kěyǐ ānrán rùshuì de háizi.
text_en: The afternoon nap is a dying civilization. In my childhood memories, the afternoon nap was the most sacred ritual of the day. After lunch, the entire village would fall into a collective silence. Adults returned to their rooms, drew the curtains, lay on bamboo mats, covered their faces with palm-leaf fans, and drifted into dreams within minutes. Children were required to be quiet—no running, no shouting, no making any sound that might disturb the adults. So we lay down too, pretending to sleep, actually staring wide-eyed at cracks in the ceiling, watching a gecko slowly crawl from the wall corner to beside the light bulb. That afternoon quiet had thickness. It was not vacuum silence but filled silence—filled with cicada songs, the rustle of wind stirring bamboo leaves, the muffled sound of a water buffalo turning over in a mud pond in the distance. These sounds did not break the quiet but became part of it, like hidden patterns in brocade—invisible unless you look closely, but it is precisely they that make the brocade complete. Today the afternoon nap has become a luxury. Offices have no space to lie down, lunch breaks are compressed to negligible lengths, people rest their heads on desks in cubicles for fifteen minutes, waking with keyboard marks imprinted on their faces. That is not an afternoon nap—that is the body's brief collapse under high pressure. A true afternoon nap requires three conditions: a space where one can lie completely flat, an uninterrupted period of time, and a peace of mind that 'the world can function without me for an hour or two.' The first two conditions can still be created; the third is nearly impossible in today's society. We have grown accustomed to being always online, always responsive, always efficient. 'Letting go' has become something that must be learned. Perhaps relearning the afternoon nap is the most needed archaeological practice for modern people—not excavating ancient ruins, but excavating the child deep within ourselves who can peacefully fall asleep.

title: The Bookshelf at Midnight
text_zh: 午夜的书架和白天的书架是两件完全不同的家具。白天，它只是一面墙上的储物架——功能明确、秩序井然，每一本书都安静地待在自己的位置上，像一排守纪律的士兵。但到了午夜，当房间里只剩下一盏台灯的光、当窗外的世界已经沉入黑暗，书架就变了。那些书脊上的字在昏暗的灯光下变得模糊，你不再能清楚地辨认每一本书的书名，于是它们开始失去各自的身份，融合成一个整体——一面由文字、纸张和岁月堆砌而成的墙。这面墙有气味。不是新书那种油墨的刺鼻味，而是旧纸张缓慢氧化后散发出的、带着一点甜味的陈旧气息，像一杯放了很久的红酒。我有时候会在午夜站在书架前，随手抽出一本书——不看封面，不看目录，随便翻到某一页开始读。这种阅读方式像是掷骰子——你永远不知道自己会读到什么。可能是一段关于十九世纪巴黎街头的描写，可能是一首翻译得不太好的日本俳句，可能是一篇已经被遗忘了的散文中某个让你突然停下来的句子。这种偶然的相遇是白天的阅读所无法提供的。白天我们带着目的读书——为了学习、为了工作、为了消磨时间。但午夜的阅读没有目的，它是纯粹的漫游。你不是在寻找什么，而是在允许自己被找到。每一本你拥有的书都是某个过去的你留给现在的你的礼物。那个买下这本书的人也许已经和你很不一样了，但那本书仍然在书架上等着你——等着在某个午夜被重新打开，像一扇通往过去的门。推开那扇门，你会遇见曾经的自己。也许那个自己会让你感到陌生，也许会让你感到亲切。但无论如何，那个相遇是安静的——安静得就像午夜的书架本身。
text_pinyin: Wǔyè de shūjià hé báitiān de shūjià shì liǎng jiàn wánquán bùtóng de jiājù. Báitiān, tā zhǐ shì yī miàn qiáng shàng de chǔwù jià——gōngnéng míngquè, zhìxù jǐngrán, měi yī běn shū dōu ānjìng de dāi zài zìjǐ de wèizhì shàng, xiàng yī pái shǒu jìlǜ de shìbīng. Dàn dào le wǔyè, dāng fángjiān lǐ zhǐ shèng xià yī zhǎn táidēng de guāng, dāng chuāng wài de shìjiè yǐjīng chén rù hēi'àn, shūjià jiù biàn le. Nàxiē shūjǐ shàng de zì zài hūn'àn de dēngguāng xià biàn de móhu, nǐ bú zài néng qīngchǔ de biànrèn měi yī běn shū de shūmíng, yúshì tāmen kāishǐ shīqù gèzì de shēnfèn, rónghé chéng yī gè zhěngtǐ——yī miàn yóu wénzì, zhǐzhāng hé suìyuè duīqì ér chéng de qiáng. Zhè miàn qiáng yǒu qìwèi. Bú shì xīn shū nà zhǒng yóumò de cìbí wèi, ér shì jiù zhǐzhāng huǎnmàn yǎnghuà hòu sànfā chū de, dài zhe yīdiǎn tián wèi de chénjiù qìxī, xiàng yī bēi fàng le hěn jiǔ de hóngjiǔ. Wǒ yǒu shíhòu huì zài wǔyè zhàn zài shūjià qián, suíshǒu chōu chū yī běn shū——bú kàn fēngmiàn, bú kàn mùlù, suíbiàn fān dào mǒu yī yè kāishǐ dú. Zhè zhǒng yuèdú fāngshì xiàng shì zhì tóuzi——nǐ yǒngyuǎn bù zhīdào zìjǐ huì dú dào shénme. Kěnéng shì yī duàn guānyú shíjiǔ shìjì Bālí jiētóu de miáoxiě, kěnéng shì yī shǒu fānyì de bú tài hǎo de Rìběn páijù, kěnéng shì yī piān yǐjīng bèi yíwàng le de sǎnwén zhōng mǒu gè ràng nǐ tūrán tíng xià lái de jùzi. Zhè zhǒng ǒurán de xiāngyù shì báitiān de yuèdú suǒ wúfǎ tígōng de. Báitiān wǒmen dài zhe mùdì dúshū——wèi le xuéxí, wèi le gōngzuò, wèi le xiāomó shíjiān. Dàn wǔyè de yuèdú méiyǒu mùdì, tā shì chúncuì de mányóu. Nǐ bú shì zài xúnzhǎo shénme, ér shì zài yǔnxǔ zìjǐ bèi zhǎo dào. Měi yī běn nǐ yōngyǒu de shū dōu shì mǒu gè guòqù de nǐ liú gěi xiànzài de nǐ de lǐwù. Nàge mǎi xià zhè běn shū de rén yěxǔ yǐjīng hé nǐ hěn bù yīyàng le, dàn nà běn shū réngrán zài shūjià shàng děng zhe nǐ——děng zhe zài mǒu gè wǔyè bèi chóngxīn dǎkāi, xiàng yī shàn tōng wǎng guòqù de mén. Tuī kāi nà shàn mén, nǐ huì yùjiàn céngjīng de zìjǐ. Yěxǔ nàge zìjǐ huì ràng nǐ gǎndào mòshēng, yěxǔ huì ràng nǐ gǎndào qīnqiè. Dàn wúlùn rúhé, nàge xiāngyù shì ānjìng de——ānjìng de jiù xiàng wǔyè de shūjià běnshēn.
text_en: A bookshelf at midnight and a bookshelf during the day are two entirely different pieces of furniture. By day, it is simply a storage shelf on a wall—clearly functional, neatly ordered, each book sitting quietly in its place like a row of disciplined soldiers. But at midnight, when only a desk lamp's glow remains in the room, when the world outside the window has sunk into darkness, the bookshelf transforms. The characters on those spines become blurred in the dim light; you can no longer clearly identify each book's title, and so they begin to lose their individual identities, merging into a whole—a wall built of words, paper, and years. This wall has a scent. Not the pungent ink smell of new books, but the slightly sweet, aged aroma that old paper releases as it slowly oxidizes, like a glass of wine left for a long time. Sometimes at midnight I stand before the bookshelf and randomly pull out a book—without looking at the cover, without checking the table of contents, opening to any page and starting to read. This reading method is like rolling dice—you never know what you will encounter. Perhaps a passage describing nineteenth-century Parisian streets, perhaps a not-quite-well-translated Japanese haiku, perhaps a sentence in some forgotten essay that makes you suddenly stop. This accidental encounter is something daytime reading cannot provide. During the day we read with purpose—for learning, for work, to pass time. But midnight reading has no purpose; it is pure wandering. You are not searching for something; you are allowing yourself to be found. Every book you own is a gift from some past version of yourself to the present you. The person who bought that book may already be very different from who you are now, but the book still waits on the shelf—waiting to be reopened some midnight, like a door to the past. Push that door open, and you will meet your former self. Perhaps that self will feel like a stranger; perhaps it will feel familiar. But either way, the encounter is quiet—as quiet as the midnight bookshelf itself.

questions.q_zh: 作者为什么说午夜的书架和白天的书架是「两件完全不同的家具」？
questions.q_en: Why does the author say a midnight bookshelf and a daytime bookshelf are 'two entirely different pieces of furniture'?

options.text: 因为夜晚的温度变化会导致书架变形
options.pinyin: yīnwèi yèwǎn de wēndù biànhuà huì dǎozhì shūjià biànxíng
options.text_en: Because nighttime temperature changes cause the bookshelf to warp

options.text: 因为在昏暗的灯光下书脊模糊，书架从有序的储物架变成了由文字和岁月堆砌的整体
options.pinyin: yīnwèi zài hūn'àn de dēngguāng xià shūjǐ móhu, shūjià cóng yǒuxù de chǔwù jià biànchéng le yóu wénzì hé suìyuè duīqì de zhěngtǐ
options.text_en: Because in dim light the spines blur and the shelf transforms from ordered storage into a whole built of words and years

options.text: 因为午夜的书架上会放不同的书
options.pinyin: yīnwèi wǔyè de shūjià shàng huì fàng bùtóng de shū
options.text_en: Because different books are placed on the midnight shelf

options.text: 因为人在夜晚的视觉和记忆力会衰退
options.pinyin: yīnwèi rén zài yèwǎn de shìjué hé jìyìlì huì shuāituì
options.text_en: Because vision and memory decline at night

questions.explanation: "the day are two entirely different pieces" — narrative sequence does the causal work; the reason unfolds in the telling.

options.pinyin: yī zhǒng xūyào xuéxí de zhīshì huò jìnéng
options.text_en: A type of knowledge or skill to learn

options.text: 过去的自己留给现在的自己的礼物

options.text_en: A gift from a past self to the present self

options.text_en: The author's aesthetic taste and social status

options.text_en: A life stage that has already ended

title: The Etiquette of Solitude
text_zh: 独处是需要礼仪的。不是对别人的礼仪，而是对自己的礼仪。很多人害怕独处，因为他们不知道一个人的时候应该怎样和自己相处。他们会打开电视当作背景音，会刷手机填满每一个空闲的缝隙，会找各种理由出门——仿佛独处是一种需要被填满的空虚，而不是一种值得被经营的状态。但如果你学会了独处的礼仪，一个人的时间可以变得无比丰盈。首先是空间的礼仪。一个人住的房间不应该因为只有自己住就变得潦草。床铺要整理，桌面要干净，杯子用完要洗——不是为了等谁来检查，而是为了让自己住在一个值得被尊重的空间里。这是你对自己最基本的礼貌。然后是时间的礼仪。独处的时间不应该全部花在「消遣」上。消遣是一种对时间的浪费伪装成对时间的使用。真正的独处需要一些留白——什么都不做的时间，让思绪自由飘荡的时间，让身体仅仅因为存在本身就感到满足的时间。最后是饮食的礼仪。一个人吃饭不意味着可以站着吃、蹲着吃、对着电脑屏幕吃。给自己做一顿认真的饭，把它装在干净的盘子里，坐下来，慢慢吃完。这不是矫情，这是你告诉自己：即使只有你一个人，你的存在也值得被好好对待。我认识一位老先生，他一个人住了二十年。每天早上他都会为自己泡一壶茶，用的是景德镇的青花瓷茶具。我问他为什么一个人喝茶还用这么好的茶具。他说：「用好茶具喝茶不是为了给别人看。茶的味道和你用什么杯子无关，但你对待自己的态度，和你用什么杯子有关。」
text_pinyin: Dúchǔ shì xūyào lǐyí de. Bú shì duì biérén de lǐyí, ér shì duì zìjǐ de lǐyí. Hěn duō rén hàipà dúchǔ, yīnwèi tāmen bù zhīdào yī gè rén de shíhòu yīnggāi zěnyàng hé zìjǐ xiāngchǔ. Tāmen huì dǎkāi diànshì dāng zuò bèijǐng yīn, huì shuā shǒujī tián mǎn měi yī gè kòngxián de fèngxì, huì zhǎo gè zhǒng lǐyóu chūmén——fǎngfú dúchǔ shì yī zhǒng xūyào bèi tián mǎn de kōngxū, ér bú shì yī zhǒng zhíde bèi jīngyíng de zhuàngtài. Dàn rúguǒ nǐ xué huì le dúchǔ de lǐyí, yī gè rén de shíjiān kěyǐ biàn de wúbǐ fēngyíng. Shǒuxiān shì kōngjiān de lǐyí. Yī gè rén zhù de fángjiān bù yīnggāi yīnwèi zhǐyǒu zìjǐ zhù jiù biàn de liáocǎo. Chuángpù yào zhěnglǐ, zhuōmiàn yào gānjìng, bēizi yòng wán yào xǐ——bú shì wèi le děng shéi lái jiǎnchá, ér shì wèi le ràng zìjǐ zhù zài yī gè zhíde bèi zūnzhòng de kōngjiān lǐ. Zhè shì nǐ duì zìjǐ zuì jīběn de lǐmào. Ránhòu shì shíjiān de lǐyí. Dúchǔ de shíjiān bù yīnggāi quánbù huā zài「xiāoqiǎn」shàng. Xiāoqiǎn shì yī zhǒng duì shíjiān de làngfèi wèizhuāng chéng duì shíjiān de shǐyòng. Zhēnzhèng de dúchǔ xūyào yīxiē liúbái——shénme dōu bù zuò de shíjiān, ràng sīxù zìyóu piāodàng de shíjiān, ràng shēntǐ jǐnjǐn yīnwèi cúnzài běnshēn jiù gǎndào mǎnzú de shíjiān. Zuìhòu shì yǐnshí de lǐyí. Yī gè rén chīfàn bù yìwèi zhe kěyǐ zhàn zhe chī, dūn zhe chī, duì zhe diànnǎo píngmù chī. Gěi zìjǐ zuò yī dùn rènzhēn de fàn, bǎ tā zhuāng zài gānjìng de pánzi lǐ, zuò xià lái, mànmàn chī wán. Zhè bú shì jiáoqíng, zhè shì nǐ gàosu zìjǐ: jíshǐ zhǐyǒu nǐ yī gè rén, nǐ de cúnzài yě zhíde bèi hǎohǎo duìdài. Wǒ rènshí yī wèi lǎo xiānshēng, tā yī gè rén zhù le èrshí nián. Měi tiān zǎoshang tā dōu huì wèi zìjǐ pào yī hú chá, yòng de shì Jǐngdézhèn de qīnghuā cí chájù. Wǒ wèn tā wèishéme yī gè rén hē chá hái yòng zhème hǎo de chájù. Tā shuō:「Yòng hǎo chájù hē chá bú shì wèi le gěi biérén kàn. Chá de wèidào hé nǐ yòng shénme bēizi wúguān, dàn nǐ duìdài zìjǐ de tàidù, hé nǐ yòng shénme bēizi yǒuguān.」
text_en: Solitude requires etiquette. Not etiquette toward others, but etiquette toward oneself. Many people fear being alone because they don't know how to get along with themselves. They turn on the television as background noise, scroll through phones to fill every idle gap, find all kinds of excuses to go out—as if solitude were an emptiness needing to be filled rather than a state worth cultivating. But if you learn the etiquette of solitude, time alone can become immeasurably rich. First is the etiquette of space. A room inhabited by one person should not become sloppy just because only you live there. The bed should be made, the desk kept clean, cups washed after use—not because someone might come to inspect, but to live in a space that deserves respect. This is your most basic courtesy to yourself. Then is the etiquette of time. Time alone should not be entirely spent on 'amusement.' Amusement is the waste of time disguised as the use of time. True solitude needs some blank space—time for doing nothing, time for letting thoughts drift freely, time for the body to feel satisfied simply by existing. Finally is the etiquette of eating. Eating alone does not mean you can eat standing up, squatting, or facing a computer screen. Make yourself a proper meal, put it on a clean plate, sit down, and eat slowly. This is not being fussy; it is telling yourself: even when you are alone, your existence deserves to be treated well. I know an elderly gentleman who has lived alone for twenty years. Every morning he brews himself a pot of tea, using Jingdezhen blue-and-white porcelain tea ware. I asked why he used such fine tea ware when drinking tea alone. He said: 'Using fine tea ware is not for others to see. The taste of tea has nothing to do with what cup you use, but your attitude toward yourself has everything to do with it.'

questions.q_zh: 作者认为「消遣」和真正的独处之间的区别是什么？
questions.q_en: What distinction does the author draw between 'amusement' and true solitude?
